00 \u2014 Concurrency Overview.content:
# Concurrency & Synchronization

> **Hardware-first.** When two CPU cores execute instructions that touch the same memory, the hardware's cache coherence protocol, store buffers, and instruction reordering determine what actually happens. This note starts at the silicon — the assembly interleaving, the `LOCK CMPXCHG`, the MESI state transitions — and builds upward to mutexes, atomics, and lock-free structures.

---

| Abstraction | Hardware Mechanism |
|-------------|-------------------|
| **Data race** | Two cores' load-modify-store sequences interleave on the same cache line |
| **Mutex (fast path)** | Single `LOCK CMPXCHG` on a 32-bit futex word — ~20 cycles, no kernel |
| **Mutex (slow path)** | `FUTEX_WAIT` syscall → kernel sleep queue → context switch → wake on `FUTEX_WAKE` |
| **Atomic increment** | `LOCK XADD` — locks cache line, reads, adds, writes — ~15–30 cycles |
| **CAS loop** | `LOCK CMPXCHG` + conditional retry — universal lock-free primitive |
| **LL/SC (ARM)** | Hardware monitors address between load-linked and store-conditional |
| **Memory ordering** | Store buffer drain + memory barriers (`MFENCE`, `dmb`, `ldar`/`stlr`) |
| **False sharing** | MESI protocol invalidates entire 64-byte cache line on any write to it |
| **Spinlock** | `LOCK XCHG` in a loop — burns core but avoids sleep overhead |
| **Condition variable** | `FUTEX_WAIT` on internal futex, `FUTEX_WAKE` on notify, predicate re-check on wake |
| **Lock-free structure** | CAS loops everywhere; helping; ABA protection via tagged pointers |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
When two threads modify the same variable at the same time, the interleaving of their instructions can produce wrong results — this is a data race. A mutex is a lock: only one thread can hold it at a time. If another thread tries to lock an already-locked mutex, it goes to sleep until the first thread unlocks it. Under the hood, the fast path uses an atomic compare-and-swap instruction (no kernel involvement, ~20 cycles). The slow path makes a `futex` system call to put the thread to sleep. Atomics use special CPU instructions like `LOCK CMPXCHG` that guarantee the read-modify-write cycle is indivisible — no other core can interfere.

### Technical
A data race occurs when two threads access the same memory location without synchronization and at least one is a write. The `LOCK` prefix on x86 asserts a bus lock (or cache lock on modern CPUs via MESI) for the duration of the instruction, ensuring atomicity. Futex (`FUTEX_WAIT`/`FUTEX_WAKE`) is the kernel primitive underlying `std::mutex`: the fast path uses `LOCK CMPXCHG` to acquire without a syscall; contention triggers `FUTEX_WAIT` to block the thread in the kernel. ARM uses LL/SC (`ldaxr`/`stlxr`) — the store-conditional fails if any other core touched the address between the load and store. Memory ordering (relaxed, acquire, release, seq_cst) controls how the CPU's store buffer and cache coherence allow reordering across cores. Spinlocks busy-wait via `LOCK CMPXCHG` in a loop — faster than a syscall for very short critical sections (`<` ~1,000 cycles), but wasteful of CPU and power otherwise.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** In the Hardware Abstraction table above, Mutex (fast path) and Atomic Increment both use `LOCK`-prefixed instructions. What's the key difference in what they achieve? Why can't a mutex be implemented with just `LOCK XADD`?

2. **Code:** Write a program where two threads each increment a shared `int` 1,000,000 times — once with `std::mutex`, once with `std::atomic`<`int`>`. Measure wall-clock time for both. Which is faster and by how much? Explain the cycle counts from this note.

3. **Analysis:** A spinlock burns CPU cycles while a mutex sleeps. At what critical section duration does one become cheaper than the other? Using the cycle counts from the Hardware Abstraction table, calculate the break-even point.

4. **Real-world:** You're building a game server handling 64 concurrent players. Each player update takes 50 µs and must be serialized. Should you use one global mutex, per-player mutexes, or a lock-free queue? Justify using the latency numbers from this note.

5. **Design:** The table lists LL/SC (ARM) as distinct from CAS (x86). Design a lock-free counter that works on both architectures. What abstraction does `std::atomic` use to paper over this difference?

---

## See Also

- [[01 — Processes & Threads]] — How threads are created, scheduled, and context-switched
- [[04 — Data Races & Mutexes]] — Deep dive into assembly interleaving and futex mechanics
- [[05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — Cache lines, false sharing, and MESI coherence (hardware side)
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — Cores, caches, MESI protocol, store buffers, memory barriers
- [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]] — How memory is shared across cores
- [[00 — Function Calls Overview|Function Calls & Stack Frames]] — How each thread gets its own stack
- [[09 — Practical Concurrency]] — Practical UE threading patterns

---

## Folder Map

| # | Note | Quiz | What |

| 01 | Processes & Threads | ✓ | Process vs thread, fork vs pthread_create |
| 02 | The OS Scheduler | ✓ | Time slices, preemption, context switching |
| 03 | SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools | ✓ | Hyperthreading, CPU pinning, work stealing |
| 04 | Data Races & Mutexes | ✓ | Assembly interleaving, futex, lock_guard |
| 05 | Deadlock, Livelock & Starvation | ✓ | Circular wait, lock ordering |
| 06 | Atomics & Memory Ordering | ✓ | CMPXCHG, CAS loop, acquire/release |
| 07 | Synchronization Patterns | ✓ | Spinlock vs mutex vs RWLock — which when |
| 08 | Lock-Free Programming | ✓ | Treiber stack, ABA, Michael-Scott queue |
| 09 | Practical Concurrency | ✓ | Per-thread counters, per-bucket hash, UE |
| 10 | Quick Reference | — | Problem → solution cheat sheet |
| — | Condition Variables & Semaphores | ✓ | wait/notify, Mesa vs Hoare, P/V |
| — | Deadlock — The Four Conditions | ✓ | Coffman conditions deep dive |
| — | Scheduling — MLFQ | ✓ | MLFQ → lottery → stride → CFS |

01 \u2014 Processes & Threads.content:
# Processes & Threads

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] learning path. Read the overview first.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> Unknown acronym? Check the [[Glossary]].
>
> You should understand virtual memory and the CPU scheduler concept ([[04 — CPU Architecture]] and [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]]).


You have an 8-core CPU. Your game needs to simulate physics, render 3D graphics, stream audio, poll input devices, animate characters, run AI behavior trees, and stream assets from disk — all at 60 frames per second. That's 16.7 milliseconds per frame. A single thread can do exactly one thing at any instant. How does one program appear to do everything \"simultaneously\"?

The answer requires understanding what a CPU core actually does when it \"runs\" code, how the OS creates the illusion that hundreds of programs are running at once when only 8 cores exist, and what it physically costs to switch between them. Every game engine architecture decision — task graphs, worker threads, fiber systems, frame budgets — descends from these hardware and OS realities.

Let's start from the bottom: what happens when you launch a program.

---

### The Difference Between an Executable and a Process

An executable file sitting on disk (`MyGame.exe`, `/usr/bin/firefox`) is a static blueprint — a sequence of machine instructions, bundled with data sections, symbol tables, and metadata. It does nothing by itself. A **process** is that blueprint *in flight*: the executable loaded into memory, with CPU time allocated to it, actively executing instructions.

When you double-click `MyGame.exe`, the OS loader performs a carefully choreographed sequence:

```
1. READ: Read the executable file header (PE on Windows, ELF on Linux)
   → Parse: what sections exist? (.text, .data, .bss, .rdata)
   → Where should they be loaded in virtual memory?
   → What dynamic libraries does it need? (kernel32.dll, libc.so, etc.)

2. CREATE: Create a new virtual address space
   → Allocate a top-level page table (PML4 on x86-64)
   → This is a PRIVATE sandbox — no other process can touch this memory

3. LOAD: Map the executable sections into the virtual address space
   → .text (code): mapped read+execute, backed by the .exe file on disk
   → .data (initialized globals): mapped read+write, backed by the .exe file
   → .bss (zero-initialized globals): mapped read+write, backed by the zero page
   → Stack: allocated (typically 1-8 MB of guard-page-protected virtual space)
   → Heap: a small initial reservation, grows on demand via sbrk/mmap

4. LOAD DYNAMIC LIBRARIES: For each dependency (e.g., kernel32.dll, vcruntime)
   → Check if already loaded by another process (share the same physical pages!)
   → If not: map its sections into this process's address space
   → Resolve symbols (function pointers → actual addresses)

5. CREATE THE MAIN THREAD: The first thread of execution
   → Allocate a kernel Thread Control Block (TCB)
   → Set its program counter = the process's entry point (main/WinMain)
   → Set its stack pointer = top of the allocated stack
   → Mark it RUNNABLE — the scheduler will dispatch it soon

6. RETURN TO CALLER: The CreateProcess()/fork()+exec() call returns
   → The new process now exists, the main thread is in the run queue
```

### The Process Control Block (PCB) — The OS's Dossier on Your Program

Every process has a **Process Control Block (PCB)** — a kernel data structure (`task_struct` on Linux, `EPROCESS` on Windows) that contains everything the OS needs to manage that process. This is NOT in the process's own virtual address space — it lives in kernel memory, invisible to the process itself.

```
Process Control Block contents:
├── pid              (unique process ID)
├── ppid             (parent process ID)
├── state            (RUNNING, INTERRUPTIBLE, UNINTERRUPTIBLE, ZOMBIE, STOPPED)
├── priority         (scheduling priority: -20 to +19 nice on Linux, 0-31 on Windows)
├── cpu_time         (total CPU time consumed by all threads)
├── mm_struct*       (pointer to memory descriptor: page tables, VMAs, brk limit)
├── files_struct*    (pointer to file descriptor table: open files, sockets, pipes)
├── fs_struct*       (root directory, current working directory, umask)
├── signal_struct*   (pending signals, signal handlers, blocked signal mask)
├── cred             (UID, GID, supplementary groups, capabilities)
├── thread_group     (list of all threads belonging to this process)
└── stats            (page fault counts, I/O counters, context switch count)
```

> [!info] Analogy — The OS as a Property Manager
> The OS is like a property management company that owns an apartment building (the computer). Each process is a tenant renting an apartment. The PCB is the tenant's lease agreement — it records which apartment (address space), who lives there (threads), what keys they have (file descriptors), what rules they must follow (security context), and whether they're currently home or away (process state). The landlord (OS) can check any lease at any time. Tenants cannot read each other's leases or enter each other's apartments — that's isolation. If a tenant breaks the rules too badly (SIGSEGV), they get evicted. If the building is full (out of RAM), the landlord asks someone to move stuff into storage (swap).

### What a Process Owns — The Resource List

| Resource | Description | Physical Reality |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Virtual address space** | Private 4 GB (32-bit) or 256 TB (64-bit) sandbox | A page table hierarchy in DRAM; the CR3 register points to it |
| **Executable code (.text)** | The program's machine instructions | Physical RAM frames, shared across processes running the same binary or shared library |
| **Heap** | Dynamic memory from `new`/`malloc` | Anonymous physical pages, allocated on first write (demand paging) |
| **Stack(s)** | One per thread, ~1-8 MB each (virtual; physically allocated on demand) | Guard pages at the bottom; stack overflow triggers SEGFAULT via guard page hit |
| **File descriptor table** | Open files, sockets, pipes, eventfds | An array of pointers to kernel `struct file` objects; indices are the \"fd\" integers |
| **Signal handlers** | Functions to call on SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGSEGV, etc. | Function pointers stored in the PCB; kernel invokes them by doctoring the thread's user-space stack |
| **Environment variables** | `PATH`, `HOME`, `USER`, etc. | A block of strings in the process's address space, pointed to by `environ` |
| **Security context** | UID, GID, capabilities, AppArmor/SELinux profile | Integers and bitmasks in the PCB; checked on every syscall that accesses a resource |

### Process Creation — fork() vs CreateProcess()

**Unix/Linux:** The only way to create a new process is `fork()`. The kernel clones the calling process — same memory, same file descriptors, same everything — then the child typically calls `execve()` to replace its memory with a new program.

```cpp
pid_t pid = fork();
if (pid == 0) {
    // CHILD: identical copy of parent's memory (copy-on-write!)
    // fd table, signal handlers, environment — all duplicated
    execl(\"/usr/bin/myprogram\", \"myprogram\", \"--verbose\", NULL);
    // exec REPLACES the entire address space with myprogram's image
    // If exec succeeds, this line is never reached
    perror(\"exec failed\");
    _exit(1);
} else if (pid > 0) {
    // PARENT: continues here
    int status;
    waitpid(pid, &status, 0);  // Wait for child to finish
} else {
    // fork failed (out of memory, process limit reached, etc.)
}
```

**Why `fork()` doesn't actually copy all memory:** It uses **copy-on-write (COW)**. After `fork()`, both parent and child share the SAME physical pages, marked read-only in both page tables. When either process writes to a page, the MMU triggers a fault, the kernel copies that page to a new physical frame, marks the new copy writable, and lets the write proceed. If the child calls `exec()` immediately (the common case), those pages were never actually copied — the COW overhead was never paid.

**Windows:** `CreateProcess()` creates a new process from an executable, without the intermediate clone step. It's more direct but less flexible for patterns like \"fork a worker that shares my memory.\"

```cpp
PROCESS_INFORMATION pi;
STARTUPINFO si = { sizeof(si) };
CreateProcess(
    L\"C:\\\\Game\\\\MyGame.exe\",  // Executable path
    L\"--level test\",          // Command line
    NULL, NULL, FALSE,         // Security, inheritance
    0, NULL, NULL,             // Creation flags, environment, directory
    &si, &pi                   // Startup info, process info (out)
);
// pi.hProcess — handle to the new process
// pi.hThread  — handle to its main thread
// pi.dwProcessId — the new PID
```

---

### The Hardware Reality: A CPU Core Executes One Instruction Stream

A CPU core has exactly one **program counter** (RIP on x86-64, PC on ARM) — a register that holds the memory address of the next instruction to execute. It has one set of general-purpose registers (RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, RBP, R8-R15 on x86-64). It has one stack pointer (RSP). It fetches an instruction from the address in RIP, decodes it, executes it, updates RIP, and repeats — billions of times per second.

A **thread** is the OS abstraction that represents one such instruction stream. It's the thing that actually occupies a CPU core and executes code. Every process starts with one thread (the \"main thread\"), but can create more. When people say \"my program runs on 8 threads,\" they mean the OS scheduler has 8 independent instruction streams for that process, and (ideally) the hardware has 8 physical cores to run them on simultaneously.

### What Belongs to a Thread — Private State

Each thread has its own:

| Resource | Physical Reality | Why Private? |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Stack** | A region of virtual memory, 1-8 MB, with guard pages | Local variables, return addresses, function call frames. Thread A calling `foo()` must not corrupt Thread B's call stack. |
| **Program Counter (RIP)** | A CPU register | Each thread is at a different point in the code. Thread A is in `RenderFrame()`, Thread B is in `UpdatePhysics()`. |
| **Stack Pointer (RSP)** | A CPU register | Each thread's stack is at a different depth. |
| **General-Purpose Registers** | RAX-R15, XMM0-XMM15, etc. | Thread A's `for` loop counter is in R8; Thread B's is in R8 too — but a different physical instance, restored during context switch. |
| **Thread-Local Storage (TLS)** | A per-thread memory region accessed via FS/GS segment register | `thread_local int myData` — each thread gets its own copy, useful for per-thread allocators, random number generators, or error codes. |
| **Signal mask** | Bitmask in the TCB | Threads can independently block/unblock signals (e.g., only one thread handles SIGINT). |
| **errno** | A per-thread variable (actually a macro expanding to a TLS access) | If Thread A's `fopen()` sets errno to ENOENT and Thread B reads errno, Thread B must NOT see Thread A's error. |

### What Threads Share — Common State

All threads within a process share:

| Resource | Physical Reality | Consequence |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Code (.text)** | Same physical pages mapped into the shared virtual address space | All threads execute the same functions. No per-thread code duplication. |
| **Heap** | Same virtual address space → `new`/`malloc` return addresses visible to all threads | `global_ptr = new int(42)` in Thread A — Thread B can read `*global_ptr`. This is both powerful (shared state) and dangerous (data races). |
| **Global variables** | Reside in .data/.bss sections, shared address space | Any thread can read/write any global. Synchronization required. |
| **File descriptors** | The fd table is per-process, not per-thread | If Thread A calls `close(fd)`, Thread B's next `read(fd)` returns EBADF. |
| **Virtual address space** | Same page tables (same CR3 value) | A pointer valid in Thread A is valid in Thread B. No TLB flush on thread switch. |

> [!info] Analogy — A Restaurant Kitchen (Revisited)
> The process is the restaurant: it has a building (address space), equipment lease (PCB), pantry and walk-in freezer (heap and globals), and a set of keys to the loading dock (file descriptors). Threads are the chefs. Each chef has their own cutting board and recipe stand (stack), is on a different step of their current recipe (program counter), and holds different knives in their hands (registers). But all chefs share the same pantry — if Chef A uses the last onion, Chef B can't make French onion soup. All chefs share the same order tickets (global task queue). If two chefs try to grab the same ticket simultaneously, chaos ensues. That's a data race.

### The Thread Control Block (TCB) — The OS's Dossier on a Thread

Just as each process has a PCB, each thread has a **Thread Control Block (TCB)** stored in kernel memory. On Linux, threads are actually implemented as \"lightweight processes\" — each thread gets its own `task_struct` that links back to the thread group leader (the main thread's `task_struct`).

```
Thread Control Block contents:
├── tid              (Thread ID, unique system-wide)
├── state            (RUNNING, READY, BLOCKED, etc.)
├── priority         (Scheduling priority, may differ from process base priority)
├── cpu_time         (CPU time consumed by THIS thread)
├── saved_context    (THE CRITICAL PART — saved register state when not running):
│   ├── rip          (Program Counter — where to resume)
│   ├── rsp          (Stack Pointer)
│   ├── rbp          (Base Pointer)
│   ├── rax, rbx, rcx, rdx, rsi, rdi, r8..r15  (General purpose registers)
│   ├── xmm0..xmm15  (SSE/AVX registers — lazy save/restore to reduce cost)
│   ├── rflags       (CPU flags register)
│   └── fs_base, gs_base  (TLS segment bases)
├── stack            (Kernel stack pointer — each thread has a small kernel stack)
├── blocked_on       (What is this thread waiting for? Lock? I/O? Sleep?)
├── pending_signals  (Signals queued for this thread)
└── tg               (Pointer to the thread group / owning process)
```


## Exercises

1. **Trace a process launch:** On your machine, run a program and use `ps aux` (Linux) or Task Manager (Windows) to find its PID. How many threads does it have? What are they doing?

2. **fork() without exec():** Write a program that calls `fork()` and prints "Parent" or "Child" based on the return value. Add a global variable. Modify it in the child. Read it in the parent. Does the parent see the change? Why or why not?

3. **Thread-local storage:** Write a program with a `thread_local int counter` that increments across multiple threads. Are the values shared or per-thread? What does the `FS`/`GS` segment register have to do with this?

4. **Compare CreateProcess vs fork+exec:** What can `fork()` do that `CreateProcess()` cannot? Why would a game engine ever need `fork()`?

5. **Design a task system:** Your game has physics, rendering, audio, and AI on separate threads. Which resources must be PRIVATE per thread? Which should be SHARED? Justify each decision.

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A program on disk is a recipe. A process is the kitchen with the recipe being cooked — counters (memory), pantry (heap), a chef reading line by line (main thread). Multiple chefs in the same kitchen (threads) share counters and pantry, each on a different recipe step, each with their own cutting board (stack). The restaurant manager (OS) checks every few milliseconds who's making progress.

### Technical
A process is a running program instance, represented by `task_struct` (Linux) or `EPROCESS` (Windows) in kernel memory. It owns a virtual address space (page table hierarchy rooted at CR3), file descriptors (`struct file*` array), signal handlers, and security credentials. A thread is a schedulable execution context — its own RIP, RSP, RAX-R15, XMM0-15, and TLS via FS/GS base. All threads share the same virtual address space: a pointer valid in Thread A is valid in Thread B — no TLB flush on thread switch. This shared address space is both the power (zero-copy shared memory) and the danger (data races on globals).

## See Also

- [[02 — The OS Scheduler]] — Next in sequence
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — The hardware executing the threads
- [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]] — How each process gets its own address space

02 \u2014 The OS Scheduler.content:
# The OS Scheduler

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[01 — Processes & Threads]] — understand what a thread IS before learning how the OS schedules them.


### The Fundamental Problem: More Threads Than Cores

At any moment on a typical desktop:
- 8 physical CPU cores (16 with hyperthreading → 16 logical cores)
- 200+ threads across all running processes (Chrome alone might have 60+: one per tab, GPU thread, compositor, network threads, etc.)
- Only 8 threads can physically execute at any given instant

The **scheduler** is the kernel component that answers, roughly 100-1,000 times per second: \"Which 8 threads get the CPU for the next few milliseconds?\" It must balance fairness (no thread starves), responsiveness (interactive apps feel snappy), and throughput (CPU-bound work actually finishes).

### How the Scheduler Actually Works — Timer Interrupts

The scheduler is not a thread. It has no stack. It's **kernel code invoked by hardware timer interrupts**. Here's the flow:

```
1. HARDWARE TIMER INTERRUPT fires (programmable interval timer, typically 1-10 ms)
   → The interrupt is a physical electrical signal on the CPU's LAPIC (Local APIC) pin
   → The CPU stops whatever it's doing, saves RIP/RSP/RFLAGS onto the current stack,
     switches to the kernel's interrupt stack, and jumps to the interrupt handler

2. KERNEL INTERRUPT HANDLER runs:
   a. Save the remaining registers of the interrupted thread into its TCB
   b. Call the scheduler function (schedule() on Linux)
   c. The scheduler picks the next thread:
      - Update vruntime (Linux CFS) or priority boost (Windows)
      - Walk the runqueue (red-black tree on Linux CFS, multi-level queue on Windows)
      - Select the \"leftmost\" (most deserving) runnable thread
   d. If the selected thread is the same → just return (no context switch needed)
   e. If different → context switch

3. CONTEXT SWITCH (see Part 4 for detailed walkthrough)

4. NEW THREAD RESUMES — from its perspective, nothing happened
   → It was paused mid-instruction; now that instruction completes
```

> [!info] Analogy — The Traffic Light System
> The OS scheduler is like a city's traffic light system. Cars (threads) want to use intersections (CPU cores). A central controller (the scheduler) cycles through which direction gets green. Each direction gets a fixed time window (time slice/quantum). Emergency vehicles (high-priority threads) get signal preemption — lights change for them. If a car breaks down (blocks on I/O), it's towed out of the intersection so others can proceed. The controller makes decisions based on a timer (the heartbeat interrupt) and a set of rules (scheduling policy). Cars don't get to vote — they just go when it's green.

### Scheduling Policies — Linux CFS and Windows NT Scheduler

**Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler):**
The dominant Linux scheduler from 2007 to 2023 (superseded by EEVDF in Linux 6.6). Core idea: every runnable thread should get a fair share of CPU time, with weights for priority.

- Each thread has a **vruntime** (virtual runtime) — the amount of CPU time it has consumed, weighted by its priority. Lower priority threads accumulate vruntime *faster* (each nanosecond of CPU time counts as more vruntime).
- Runnable threads are stored in a **red-black tree** sorted by vruntime.
- The scheduler always picks the thread with the **lowest vruntime** (the one that's been treated most unfairly so far).
- **Nice values** (-20 to +19) adjust the weight. A thread with nice=-20 (highest priority) gets ~10× the CPU share of a thread with nice=19 (lowest priority). The weight multiplier ensures fairness: a nice=0 thread gets 1024 units; nice=5 gets 335; nice=-5 gets 3121.
- **No fixed time slices** per se. Instead, the scheduler calculates a **target latency** — the period over which all runnable threads should get at least one turn. Default: 6 ms. If there are N runnable threads, each gets 6/N ms before preemption (with a minimum granularity of 0.75 ms to prevent thrashing).

**Windows NT Scheduler:**
A priority-based, preemptive scheduler with 32 priority levels (0-31):

- 0: Zero-page thread (background memory zeroing)
- 1-15: Dynamic range (normal threads — priority can be boosted temporarily)
- 16-31: Real-time range (fixed — thread must have `SeIncreaseBasePriorityPrivilege`)
- Threads at higher priority **always** run before threads at lower priority — no fairness guarantee
- Within the same priority level: round-robin with time quanta
- **Priority boosts:** Threads that were blocked on I/O or waiting for UI events get temporary priority boosts when they wake up (so interactive apps feel responsive). The boost decays over time.
- **Quantum:** Varies by Windows version and edition. Desktop: 2 clock intervals (~30 ms for client, ~180 ms for server). Short quantum → responsive. Long quantum → throughput.

### Thread States — The Lifecycle

```
                         ┌──────────────┐
     Thread created ───→ │    READY     │  ← \"I could run, if the scheduler picks me\"
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │ scheduler dispatches thread
                                ▼
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │   RUNNING    │  ← \"I'm on a CPU core RIGHT NOW\"
                         └──┬───┬───┬───┘
                            │   │   │
              time slice    │   │   │  thread exits/completes
              expires       │   │   │
                            │   │   └────────────────────→ TERMINATED
                            │   │
                            │   │  waiting for: I/O, lock, sleep, page fault
                            │   │
                            ▼   ▼
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │   BLOCKED    │  ← \"I can't run until something happens\"
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │ I/O complete / lock acquired / sleep done
                                ▼
                              READY  (re-enters the run queue)
```

**The trap:** A thread can bounce between RUNNING and BLOCKED thousands of times per second. Each transition involves a context switch. If a thread blocks on a mutex for 50 µs, it might get switched out and switched back in — two context switches for 50 µs of waiting. This is why spinlocks exist: sometimes it's cheaper to burn CPU cycles spinning than to pay two context switches.

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Mutex is explained in detail in [[04 — Data Races & Mutexes]]. This note only covers what is necessary for the current topic.


---

### The Physical Reality: A Context Switch Is Register Save/Restore

A **context switch** is the mechanism by which the CPU stops running Thread A and starts running Thread B. It is the single most expensive operation the OS performs on your behalf — not because the code is complex (it's a few hundred assembly instructions), but because of the **indirect costs**: cache eviction, TLB pressure, and pipeline flushes.

### Step-by-Step: What Happens During a Context Switch

```
=== PHASE 1: SAVE THE OUTGOING THREAD ===

1. Timer interrupt fires. CPU hardware saves:
   - RFLAGS (condition codes, interrupt enable bit, I/O privilege level)
   - RIP (Program Counter — address of the NEXT instruction Thread A would execute)
   - CS (Code Segment selector — ring 0 vs ring 3)
   - RSP (Stack Pointer, switched to kernel stack)
   → These are pushed onto Thread A's KERNEL stack by the CPU hardware

2. Interrupt handler (kernel) saves remaining registers onto kernel stack:
   - RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, RBP
   - R8, R9, R10, R11, R12, R13, R14, R15
   - FS_BASE, GS_BASE (TLS segment bases)

3. Kernel checks: does Thread A use floating-point/SIMD?
   → If yes (TS flag set in CR0): save XMM0-XMM15 and MXCSR
   → Intel/AMD: use XSAVE/XRSTOR instructions to save the full FPU/SIMD state
   → This is LAZY: if Thread A hasn't used FPU, the kernel skips saving it
     (CR0.TS = \"Task Switched\" flag tracks this)

4. Store RSP (kernel stack pointer) into Thread A's TCB.saved_context.sp
   → This records where all the saved registers are on Thread A's kernel stack

=== PHASE 2: PICK THE NEXT THREAD ===

5. Scheduler selects Thread B (highest priority / lowest vruntime / next in queue)
6. If Thread B belongs to a DIFFERENT process:
   a. Load Thread B's process's page table base into CR3
   b. → THIS FLUSHES ALL TLB ENTRIES (except global pages marked with PGE flag)
   c. → Every subsequent memory access will TLB-miss until the working set is reloaded
   d. Switch to the process's kernel stack
   e. Update the CPU's FS/GS base if the process uses a different TLS segment layout

=== PHASE 3: RESTORE THE INCOMING THREAD ===

7. Load Thread B's kernel stack pointer from its TCB
8. Pop the saved registers from Thread B's kernel stack:
   - R15..R8, RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, RBX, RAX, RBP
9. Restore FS_BASE, GS_BASE for Thread B's TLS
10. Pop RFLAGS from Thread B's kernel stack
11. Pop RIP from Thread B's kernel stack
    → The CPU now jumps to this address — Thread B resumes
12. Pop RSP (switch to Thread B's user-space stack)
13. IRETQ instruction: return from interrupt, switch ring 0 → ring 3

=== Thread B is now running as if nothing happened ===
```

### The Real Cost — Direct and Indirect

| Cost Category | Approximate Latency | Why |
|---------------|-------------------|-----|
| **Register save/restore** | ~0.1-0.2 µs (200-400 cycles at 2 GHz) | ~30 × 8-byte push/pop operations. Fast. |
| **Kernel mode transition** | ~0.1 µs | The interrupt itself (save RFLAGS, RIP, CS, RSP) |
| **Scheduler decision** | ~0.1-0.5 µs | Walking the runqueue tree, calculating vruntime |
| **TLB flush (cross-process only)** | ~0.1-0.5 µs direct + **massive indirect** | CR3 write is fast. The INDIRECT cost is TLB refill on next memory accesses. |
| **Cache warmth loss** | **~5-50 µs indirect** | Thread B's data was evicted from L1/L2/L3 while Thread A ran. First accesses post-switch are cache misses → DRAM latency. |
| **Pipeline flush** | ~0.02-0.05 µs | The CPU's ~200-instruction pipeline was filled with Thread A's instructions — all discarded. |
| **Total (same process, warm caches)** | **~1-3 µs** | Best case |
| **Total (different process, cold caches)** | **~5-15 µs** | With cache warmup penalty |

> [!warning] The Hidden Killer — Cache Warmup After Context Switch
> The 1-3 µs number that textbooks quote is just the register save/restore. The REAL cost is what happens next: Thread B's working set was in the CPU caches when it last ran, but while Thread A was running (for its 10 ms time slice), Thread A's data evicted Thread B's data from L1, L2, and probably L3. Thread B's first 100-1,000 memory accesses after the switch are:
> - L1 miss → L2 miss → L3 miss → DRAM access (100 ns each)
> - 100 cache misses × 100 ns = **10 µs** of stall time
> - If the working set is large, cache warmup can take **50-100 µs**
> 
> This is why CPU affinity (pinning threads to dedicated cores) improves performance so dramatically: Thread B's data STAYS HOT in core 3's caches because nothing else runs on core 3.

### Thread Switch vs Process Switch — Why Threads Are Cheaper

```
Thread A → Thread B (same process):
  - Same CR3 (same page tables)
  - NO TLB flush
  - FS/GS base might change (different TLS), but that's just two MSR writes

Thread A → Thread B (different process):
  - CR3 change → FULL TLB flush (unless PCID/Tagged TLB is used)
  - Every subsequent memory access: TLB miss → page table walk (~50-300 ns)
  - Until TLB is repopulated with Thread B's working set entries
  - L1/L2/L3 are all cold for Thread B (filled with Thread A's data)
```

This is why threads are the concurrency primitive of choice for most applications: they share the address space, so context switches are cheaper and shared-data communication is pointer-based rather than IPC-based.

---

### The Quantum — How Long You Get the CPU

A **time slice** (or **quantum**) is the maximum uninterrupted time a thread gets before the scheduler considers preempting it. In 2025:

| OS | Default Quantum | Notes |
|----|----------------|-------|
| Linux CFS (desktop) | ~6 ms / N runnable threads (min 0.75 ms) | Dynamic, depends on the number of runnable threads |
| Windows 10/11 (client) | ~30-60 ms | 2-3 clock intervals at 15.6 ms per interval |
| Windows Server | ~180 ms | Longer quantum → better throughput for server workloads |
| Real-time Linux (PREEMPT_RT) | Until a higher-priority thread wakes up | Preempt immediately, no minimum quantum |

**The tradeoff:**

| Short Quantum | Long Quantum |
|--------------|-------------|
| ✅ UI feels responsive (interactive threads get frequent turns) | ✅ Better throughput (fewer context switches → less overhead) |
| ❌ More context switches → 1-5% overhead for frequent switches | ❌ Laggy — a CPU-bound thread can hog the core for 100+ ms |
| ❌ Cache thrashing if threads bounce too often | ❌ Poor for real-time (audio buffer might underrun) |

### How This Creates the Illusion of Simultaneous Execution

On a single-core machine with 10 active threads and a 10 ms quantum:

```
Timeline (1 second):
  0-10 ms:  Thread A runs
  10-20 ms: Thread B runs
  20-30 ms: Thread C runs
  ...
  90-100 ms: Thread J runs
  100-110 ms: Thread A runs again
  ...
  990-1000 ms: Thread J runs for the 10th time

Each thread got 100 ms of CPU time over 1 second = appears to run at 10% speed
But to a human: 100 context switches per second. The 10 ms gaps are imperceptible.
The user perceives: \"Everything is running at once!\"
The hardware truth: At any SINGLE INSTANT, one thread executes on one core.
```

With 8 physical cores: 8 threads truly run in parallel. The other 192 threads timeshare the 8 cores. The interleaving is so fast (sub-millisecond) that even interactive applications feel smooth — as long as the total CPU demand doesn't exceed 8 cores' worth.

---


## Exercises

1. **Why CFS uses a red-black tree:** Explain why the Linux CFS scheduler uses a red-black tree sorted by vruntime rather than a simple round-robin queue. What property of the tree makes "pick the thread with least vruntime" an O(log N) operation instead of O(N)?

2. **Quantum and frame budget:** Your game runs at 60 FPS (16.7 ms per frame). The Windows scheduler gives each thread a ~30 ms quantum. If your render thread and physics thread both do 8 ms of work and then block waiting for the GPU, how many context switches happen per frame? Is this a problem?

3. **Design a priority scheme:** You're building a game with four thread types: audio (must never glitch), rendering (must hit 60 FPS), physics (can lag slightly), and asset streaming (background). Assign Windows priority levels (0-31) and Linux nice values (-20 to +19) to each. Justify your choices.

4. **Cache warmup measurement:** Write pseudocode to measure the cost of a context switch. Hint: have Thread A write to a large array, yield, then Thread B reads it. Compare the read latency when Thread B runs on the same core vs a different core. What does this tell you about cache warmup?

5. **Preempt or not?** A real-time audio callback must complete within 1 ms. Should the OS preempt it to run a higher-priority thread? If not, what Linux configuration enables this? (Hint: look up PREEMPT_RT.)

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
The OS scheduler is a traffic cop at a busy intersection. Hundreds of cars (threads) want to use 8 lanes (CPU cores). The cop gives each car a few seconds of green light (time slice), then rotates. Emergency vehicles (high-priority threads) get immediate right-of-way. If a car breaks down (blocks on I/O), it's towed to the shoulder so other cars can proceed. The cop checks a timer every few milliseconds (the hardware interrupt) and makes a decision: same car gets more time, or next car in line.

### Technical
The kernel scheduler is invoked by a hardware timer interrupt (programmable interval timer connected to the LAPIC). On interrupt, the CPU saves the current thread's registers to its TCB, the kernel's `schedule()` function selects the next runnable thread (lowest vruntime on Linux CFS, highest dynamic priority on Windows NT), and if different, a context switch restores the target thread's register state. The switch writes CR3 if the target is in a different process (TLB flush + cache cold start). CFS uses a red-black tree keyed by vruntime for O(log N) selection; Windows uses 32 priority queues with priority boosting for I/O-bound threads to maintain UI responsiveness.

## See Also

- [[03 — SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools]] — Next in sequence
- [[Scheduling — MLFQ & Proportional Share]] — Deep dive on scheduling algorithms
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — Context switching at the hardware level

03 \u2014 SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools.content:
# SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[02 — The OS Scheduler]] — understand scheduling and context switching first.


### The Physical Trick: Duplicate Architectural State, Share Execution Units

A modern CPU core has many execution units that rarely operate at 100% utilization simultaneously:

- **Integer ALUs** (4-6 per core): arithmetic, logic, address calculation
- **Floating-point / SIMD units** (2-4 per core): FMAs, vector operations
- **Load/store units** (2-4 per core): memory reads/writes
- **Branch predictor**: guesses which way a branch will go

In any given clock cycle, a single thread typically uses only 30-50% of these units. The rest sit idle — maybe the thread is doing integer math and the FPU sits idle, or the thread is waiting on a cache miss and ALL execution units are stalled.

**SMT (Simultaneous MultiThreading)**, branded as **Hyper-Threading** by Intel, exploits this slack. The physical core maintains **two complete sets of architectural state** (two sets of general-purpose registers, two program counters, two sets of control registers, two APIC IDs) but shares all execution units, caches, and memory bandwidth:

```
Physical Core 0
├── Logical Core 0 (Thread A's registers, RIP, RFLAGS, APIC ID)
│   └──┐
│      ├── Shared: L1 I-Cache, L1 D-Cache, L2 Cache
│      ├── Shared: Execution Units (ALUs, FPUs, Load/Store, Branch Predictor)
│      ├── Shared: TLB (L1 DTLB, L1 ITLB, L2 STLB)
│      ├── Shared: Memory bandwidth (to L3 and DRAM)
│   ┌──┘
├── Logical Core 1 (Thread B's registers, RIP, RFLAGS, APIC ID)
```

The CPU's front-end (instruction fetch/decode) alternates between the two threads, and the back-end (scheduler, execution units) interleaves instructions from both threads at the pipeline level. If Thread A stalls waiting for a cache miss, the hardware seamlessly fills those pipeline slots with Thread B's instructions.

### Performance Reality — It's NOT 2× Performance

| Workload Type | SMT Throughput Gain | Why |
|--------------|---------------------|-----|
| **Integer-heavy, low cache miss** | +5-20% | Both threads contend for ALUs; little slack to exploit |
| **Floating-point heavy (different types)** | +15-30% | Thread A uses FMUL, Thread B uses FADD — different units |
| **Memory-bound (lots of cache misses)** | +20-40% | Thread A stalls on DRAM; Thread B fills the pipeline seamlessly |
| **Both threads heavy FPU, same instruction mix** | +0-10% | Both contend for the FPU; minimal slack |
| **Worst case: resource contention** | -5-10% | Both threads evict each other's cache lines → more cache misses than dedicated cores |

> [!important] The SMT Trap — Logical Cores Aren't Physical Cores
> `std::thread::hardware_concurrency()` returns **logical** cores (e.g., 16 on an 8-core HT chip). If you create 16 compute-bound threads (e.g., a parallel `for` loop doing heavy math), you're asking for 16× the physical throughput on 8 cores. Reality: you'll get maybe **10-12×** the single-thread performance, and individual thread performance will DROP because each logical core competes for shared resources.
>
> ```cpp
> unsigned int n = std::thread::hardware_concurrency();  // Returns 16
> // THE TRAP: using all 16 for CPU-bound work
> // THE BETTER APPROACH: use physical core count for compute, logical count for I/O
> unsigned int physical = n / 2;  // Assuming HT (2 logical per physical)
> ``

**The practical rule:** For CPU-bound workloads (physics, pathfinding, rendering), use `physical_core_count` threads. For I/O-bound or mixed workloads (asset streaming, network handling), you can oversubscribe — threads spend most of their time blocked on I/O, so the logical cores efficiently multiplex the ready threads.

---

### Why the Scheduler Migrates Threads

The scheduler normally moves threads between cores freely. If core 3 is idle and core 7 has a runnable thread, the scheduler will migrate that thread to core 3 to keep all cores busy. This is load balancing — it maximizes total system throughput.

### The Problem: Cache Locality Loss

A thread that runs on core 3 builds up **cache warmth**: its stack, heap objects, global data, and code are loaded into core 3's private L1 and L2 caches. If the scheduler migrates the thread to core 7:

- Core 7's L1/L2 caches are COLD for this thread's data
- Every memory access: L1 miss → L2 miss → probe L3 (shared, but still ~40 ns) → maybe DRAM
- Meanwhile, core 3's caches still hold the thread's data, but nobody is using them
- The thread experiences **~10-50 µs of cache warmup latency** after migration

For latency-sensitive threads (audio, render thread, game loop), this migration jitter causes frame hitches. The solution: **CPU affinity**.

### Setting Affinity — Pinning Threads to Cores

```cpp
// Linux: pin to core 2 only
cpu_set_t cpuset;
CPU_ZERO(&cpuset);
CPU_SET(2, &cpuset);  // Only allow core 2
pthread_setaffinity_np(pthread_self(), sizeof(cpu_set_t), &cpuset);

// Windows: pin to core 2 only
DWORD_PTR mask = (1ULL << 2);
SetThreadAffinityMask(GetCurrentThread(), mask);

// C++11: no standard API, but platform-specific
// You need native handles: pthread_self() or GetCurrentThread()
```

### The Affinity Trap — Over-Constraining the Scheduler

```cpp
// THE TRAP: pin ALL threads to specific cores
// What if a pinned thread blocks on I/O? Core 2 sits IDLE.
// What if core 5 is busy but core 3 is free? Too bad — threads are pinned.
// You've removed the scheduler's ability to load-balance.

// THE RIGHT WAY: pin only latency-critical threads to dedicated cores
// Game thread → core 0 (locked)
// Render thread → core 1 (locked)
// Audio thread → core 2 (locked)
// Worker pool (6 threads) → cores 3-8 (float freely within this set)
// Leave remaining cores for OS and background tasks
```

In Unreal Engine, the game thread and render thread are typically pinned to separate cores. The `FTaskGraph` workers are allowed to float within a designated set of cores. This gives latency-critical threads stable cache warmth while maintaining load balancing for compute workers.

---

### The True Cost of Creating a Thread

```cpp
// THE TRAP: Create a thread, do a tiny amount of work, destroy it
for (int i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {
    std::thread t([i]() {
        ProcessSmallItem(i);  // Takes 5 µs
    });
    t.join();  // Wait for it to finish
}
// Thread creation: ~10-50 µs (kernel allocations + syscall)
// Thread destruction: ~5-10 µs (kernel cleanup)
// Useful work: 5 µs
// OVERHEAD PER ITEM: 15-50 µs
// Total: 100,000 × 20 µs average = 2 full seconds of overhead
// For 0.5 seconds of actual work. 80% overhead!
```

What the kernel must do to create a thread:

```
pthread_create() / CreateThread():
1. Allocate kernel stack (typically 16 KB for kernel use)
2. Allocate user stack (default: 1-8 MB of virtual address space; physically allocated on demand)
3. Create guard pages at bottom of stack (PROT_NONE → SEGFAULT on overflow)
4. Allocate and initialize a TCB (task_struct on Linux, ETHREAD on Windows)
5. Assign a unique TID
6. Set up initial register state (RSP = top of stack, RIP = thread entry function)
7. Initialize TLS area (thread-local storage variables)
8. Add thread to the process's thread group list
9. Insert the TCB into the scheduler's runqueue
10. If the new thread has higher priority than the caller: trigger immediate context switch

Total syscall + kernel work: ~10-50 µs (highly variable, depends on kernel load)
```

### How a Thread Pool Solves This

A **thread pool** pre-creates a fixed number of long-lived worker threads. Work is submitted as **tasks** (function objects), and idle workers pull tasks from a shared queue:

```
                      ┌──────────────────────┐
     Submit(task) →   │     Task Queue       │  (concurrent queue: mutex + condition variable,
                      └──────────┬───────────┘   or lock-free MPMC queue)
                                 │ workers dequeue
              ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
              ▼                  ▼                  ▼
        ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐
        │ Worker 0 │       │ Worker 1 │       │ Worker 2 │  ... (N threads)
        └──────────┘       └──────────┘       └──────────┘
              │                  │                  │
              └──────────────────┴──────────────────┘
                     │ Thread lives forever (or until pool shutdown)
                     │ Spends most of time BLOCKED on \"queue empty\" condition
                     │ When a task arrives: wakes up, runs task, goes back to waiting
```

Benefits:
- **Zero creation overhead per task** — threads already exist
- **Bounded concurrency** — N threads = N concurrent tasks (prevents oversubscription)
- **Load balancing** — if one task finishes early, the worker immediately grabs the next
- **Predictable memory** — stack space is allocated once, not repeatedly

### Work-Stealing — The Advanced Version

In a basic thread pool with a single shared queue, the queue becomes a contention point. Every worker must lock the mutex to dequeue — and at high throughput, workers spend more time contending for the queue than doing work.

**Work-stealing** solves this: each worker has its **own queue** (typically a lock-free deque). When a worker finishes a task, it pops from its own queue (no contention). When its queue is empty, it **steals** from another worker's queue (rare operation, contention amortized). This is how Unreal Engine's `FTaskGraph`, Intel's TBB, and C++ `std::execution` (P2300) work under the hood.

```cpp
// Conceptual work-stealing worker loop
void WorkerLoop(int workerId) {
    while (!shutdown) {
        Task* task = nullptr;
        
        // 1. Try to pop from own queue (FAST, no contention)
        task = localQueue.pop();
        
        // 2. If empty, try to steal from another worker
        if (!task) {
            for (int victim = 0; victim < numWorkers; victim++) {
                if (victim == workerId) continue;
                task = workers[victim].localQueue.steal();  // Steal from the OPPOSITE end
                if (task) break;
            }
        }
        
        // 3. Still nothing? Wait on a condition variable
        if (!task) {
            waitForWork();
            continue;
        }
        
        // 4. Execute the task
        task->Execute();
        
        // 5. If task spawned child tasks, they go to THIS worker's queue
        //    (locality: children are likely to access same data → cache-friendly)
    }
}
```

### Thread Pool Sizing — The Hardware Rule

```cpp
// For CPU-bound work (physics, compression, rendering):
size_t numThreads = std::thread::hardware_concurrency();  // Logical cores
// BUT: if the work is truly compute-bound and memory-bound, oversubscription hurts
size_t numThreads = physicalCoreCount;  // Better: physical cores only

// For I/O-bound work (asset loading, network requests, file I/O):
size_t numThreads = std::thread::hardware_concurrency() * 2;  // or even 4×
// Threads spend most time BLOCKED (waiting on disk/network)
// More threads = more concurrent I/O requests inflight = better throughput

// For mixed workloads (typical game engine):
// Separate pools for compute (physical cores) and I/O (oversubscribed)
```

> [!info] Analogy — A Taxi Stand
> A thread pool is a taxi stand. Creating a new thread per task is like building a new car from scratch and hiring a new driver for every passenger — most of the time is spent on construction, not driving. A thread pool has a fixed fleet of cars and drivers idling at the stand. Passengers (tasks) line up. The next available driver takes the next passenger. When the ride ends, the driver returns to the stand. The cars (stacks) never get scrapped. Work-stealing is like drivers at a multi-line taxi stand: when their line is empty, they peek at neighboring lines and take passengers from the longest one.

---

### The Spectrum of Execution Abstractions

```
User-Space (runtime/language managed)     Kernel-Space (OS managed)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Coroutines  →  Fibers  →  Green Threads  →  OS Threads  →  Processes
    │              │             │                │
    │  Cooperative │   Sometimes │   Preemptive   │
    │  (explicit   │  preemptive │   by timer     │
    │   yield)     │             │   interrupts   │
    │              │             │                │
    └─ C++20 coroutines          │                │
       (co_await)                └─ Go goroutines │
                                    Erlang processes
```

### Green Threads — The OS Doesn't Know They Exist

A **green thread** (also called a **fiber** or **user-space thread**) is a schedulable execution context managed entirely by a runtime library in user space. The OS kernel has NO idea the green threads exist — it sees only one OS thread, but the runtime multiplexes many green threads onto that one OS thread.

| | OS Threads | Green Threads / Fibers |
|---|---|---|
| **Managed by** | OS kernel (scheduler, timer interrupts) | Runtime library in user space |
| **Preemption** | Yes — timer interrupts force switches | Usually cooperative (must explicitly yield) |
| **Context switch cost** | ~1-5 µs (kernel transition + register save/restore) | ~10-100 ns (just swap stack pointer + a few registers) |
| **Parallelism** | True — multiple OS threads on multiple cores | Concurrent but NOT parallel (one OS thread = one core max) |
| **Blocking I/O** | Blocks only that OS thread | Blocks ALL green threads on that OS thread |
| **Memory overhead** | ~1 MB stack per thread (virtual; physical pages on demand) | ~4-16 KB stack per fiber (adjustable) |
| **Examples** | `std::thread`, `pthread_create` | Go goroutines, Erlang processes, C++20 coroutines |

**Why green thread switching is 100-1000× faster:**

```
OS Thread Context Switch:
  1. Timer interrupt → ring 0 transition
  2. Save ALL registers (GPRs, XMM, FPU state potentially)
  3. Kernel scheduler code runs (hundreds of instructions)
  4. Maybe flush TLB (cross-process)
  5. Restore ALL registers
  6. Ring 3 transition
  Total: ~1-5 µs, ~5,000-25,000 cycles at 5 GHz

Green Thread / Fiber Switch:
  1. Save callee-saved registers to current fiber's stack (~7 registers)
  2. Save stack pointer
  3. Load next fiber's stack pointer
  4. Restore callee-saved registers from next fiber's stack
  5. Return (ret instruction pops RIP from new stack)
  Total: ~10-30 ns, ~50-150 cycles at 5 GHz
```

### The Green Thread Trap — Blocking I/O Kills Everything

```cpp
// THE TRAP: one fiber calls a blocking read
// All fibers share ONE OS thread
fiber_read(fd, buf, size);  // Internally calls the blocking read() syscall
// NOW THE OS THREAD IS BLOCKED
// → ALL OTHER FIBERS ARE STUCK — they can't run
// → Even if other fibers are \"ready,\" the OS thread is sleeping
// → The fiber scheduler never gets control to switch to them

// THE FIX: use non-blocking I/O + an event loop
// Instead of read(), use epoll/kqueue/IOCP to wait for \"fd is readable\"
// When nothing is ready, the event loop switches to another fiber
// This requires ALL I/O in the system to be non-blocking
```

This is why Go (which uses green threads called goroutines) has an entire runtime with a custom network poller and non-blocking I/O wrappers — a goroutine that calls `file.Read()` doesn't actually call the blocking `read()` syscall. The Go runtime intercepts the call, registers the fd with the netpoller, parks the goroutine, and switches to another goroutine. When the fd is ready, the original goroutine wakes up. The OS thread never blocks.

### C++20 Coroutines — Stackless, Compiler-Generated State Machines

C++20 coroutines (`co_await`, `co_yield`, `co_return`) are **stackless coroutines**, which means:

- The coroutine state (local variables that live across suspension points) is allocated on the HEAP, not the stack
- The compiler transforms the coroutine function into a state machine with numbered resume points
- `co_await` compiles to: save current state number, jump to caller (or awaiter), return
- Resuming jumps back to the saved state number, restores local variables from the heap-allocated frame

```cpp
Task<int> ReadDataAsync(std::string path) {
    auto file = co_await AsyncOpen(path);       // Suspend here, resume when open completes
    auto data = co_await AsyncRead(file, 1024); // Suspend here, resume when read completes
    co_return data;                              // All done
}
// This reads like synchronous code but suspends without blocking an OS thread.
// No stack per coroutine. Each suspended coroutine is ~100-200 bytes of heap overhead.
```

Game engines use similar patterns for async asset loading — a linear sequence of \"open file → read header → read data → decompress → upload to GPU\" that reads like synchronous code but yields control whenever it would block on I/O.

---


## Exercises

1. **SMT throughput:** Your game has two threads: one does pure integer math, the other does pure floating-point. Running on a single core with SMT (2 logical cores), will they get near-2x speedup? What if both do floating-point? Explain in terms of shared execution units.

2. **Cache affinity measurement:** Write a benchmark that pins Thread A to core 3, runs a workload, unpins it, then runs the same workload with the OS free to migrate. Measure the difference. What explains the gap?

3. **Thread pool sizing:** You have an 8-core CPU (16 logical with SMT). Your game uses a thread pool for async work. What pool size maximizes throughput and why? (Hint: consider SMT's partial core sharing vs full physical cores for latency-sensitive tasks.)

4. **Work-stealing design:** Implement a simple work-stealing deque. One side does LIFO push/pop (local), the other does FIFO steal (remote). Why is this asymmetric? What happens if every thread tries to steal simultaneously?

5. **Green threads vs OS threads:** Go's goroutines multiplex thousands of goroutines onto a few OS threads. Write a small program using C++20 coroutines that does the same — schedule 1000 "tasks" onto 4 OS threads. What happens when a task blocks on I/O?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
SMT (hyperthreading) is like a fast-food cook handling two orders at once. While one burger is on the grill (execution unit busy), the cook assembles a second order (second thread uses idle units). CPU affinity is like reserving your favorite parking spot — your car (thread's data) stays there, nobody else uses it, so everything is exactly where you left it. A thread pool is like a taxi stand — instead of buying a new car (creating a thread) for every trip, you reuse the same cars from the stand. Work-stealing: if one taxi driver finishes early, they grab a passenger from a busier driver's queue.

### Technical
SMT duplicates the architectural state (registers, PC) while sharing execution units (ALUs, FPUs, load/store units). The CPU presents 2 logical cores per physical core. A thread pinned via `sched_setaffinity` (Linux) or `SetThreadAffinityMask` (Windows) stays on its assigned core — its L1/L2 cache stays warm, avoiding the 5-50 µs cold-start penalty of migration. Thread pools amortize the ~10 µs cost of `pthread_create` by recycling threads; work-stealing deques (Chase-Lev, as used in Intel TBB and UE5's TaskGraph) use asymmetric LIFO/FIFO access — local end uses LIFO for cache locality, remote steals use FIFO to grab oldest (presumably largest-grained) work.

## See Also

- [[04 — Data Races & Mutexes]] — Next in sequence
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — SMT at the microarchitectural level

04 \u2014 Data Races & Mutexes.content:
# Data Races & Mutexes

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[03 — SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools]] — understand threads, context switching, and the CPU memory model ([[05 — Memory Hierarchy]]).
Modern CPUs have multiple cores, each with its own L1/L2 caches, sharing a common L3 cache and memory bus. When Core 0 and Core 1 both execute instructions touching address `0x1234`, the hardware's **cache coherence protocol** (MESI/MESIF/MOESI) ensures they eventually see a consistent view. But \"eventually\" leaves enormous gaps between what one core wrote and what another core reads.
> [!note] Why This Matters
> If you've ever seen a 10ms hitch in your game, it was probably a mutex contention, a false sharing cache ping-pong, or a priority inversion. This note explains why.


### What Is a Data Race — At the Assembly Level

Consider two threads running on different cores, both executing:

```cpp
int counter = 0;  // At memory address 0x7FFF1234

void Increment() {
    counter++;  // Looks atomic in C++. It is NOT.
}
```

The C++ statement `counter++` compiles to **three separate instructions** on x86-64:

```asm
mov    eax, [counter]     ; 1. LOAD:  read counter from memory into register
add    eax, 1             ; 2. MODIFY: add 1 in the register
mov    [counter], eax     ; 3. STORE: write the new value back to memory
```

Each of these is a separate instruction — and between any two, the CPU may handle an interrupt, the OS may preempt the thread, or another core may write to the same address.

### The Fatal Interleaving

```
Core 0 (Thread 1)          Core 1 (Thread 2)         counter (at 0x7FFF1234)
─────────────────────      ─────────────────────      ──────────────────────
mov  eax, [counter]        ...                       eax0 = 0
add  eax, 1                ...                       eax0 = 1
-- PREEMPTED --            mov  eax, [counter]       eax1 = 0  ← STILL 0!
                           add  eax, 1               eax1 = 1
                           mov  [counter], eax       counter = 1  ← writes 1
mov  [counter], eax        ...                       counter = 1  ← ALSO writes 1
```

Both cores loaded 0, both added 1, both stored 1. **Two increments produced a net change of +1.** This is a **lost update** — the most basic data race.

The root cause: the three-instruction sequence is not **atomic** (indivisible). The hardware provides no guarantee that another core won't observe or modify `counter` between the LOAD and STORE.

### The C++ Definition of a Data Race

Per the C++ standard ([intro.races]), a data race occurs when:

1. Two or more threads access the same memory location **concurrently**
2. At least one access is a **write** (modification)
3. The accesses are **not atomic** — no `std::atomic`, no `volatile` (volatile doesn't make access atomic)
4. There is **no happens-before** relationship ordering them — no mutex, no `std::atomic` with `memory_order` stronger than relaxed, no thread join before the access

The result is **undefined behavior** per the C++ standard — the compiler is allowed to assume races don't happen, which can cause optimizations that make the bug even worse (e.g., hoisting a load out of a loop, making a race that would \"usually work\" fail deterministically).

---

A **mutex** (mutual exclusion) ensures that only one thread executes a critical section at a time. At the hardware level, it converts the three-instruction `counter++` into an effectively atomic operation by preventing interleaving.

```cpp
#include <mutex>  // std::mutex, std::lock_guard — thread synchronization primitives
std::mutex m;
int counter = 0;

void SafeIncrement() {
    m.lock();        // Hardware atomic operation serializes access
    counter++;       // Now safe — no other thread can be here
    m.unlock();
}
```

### How a Mutex Actually Works — The Futex (Linux)

On Linux, `std::mutex` is built on the **futex** (fast userspace mutex) system call. The futex is a 32-bit integer in userspace, with a kernel wait queue hanging off it. The design's brilliance: the **uncontended** path requires zero syscalls.

#### Futex Word States

| Value | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `0` | Unlocked |
| `1` | Locked, no waiters |
| `2` | Locked, one or more threads waiting |

#### Fast Path — Uncontended Acquisition (No Kernel Involvement)

```
Thread wants to lock:
  1. Atomic CAS on futex_word: if 0 → set to 1
  2. SUCCESS → lock acquired
  3. Total cost: ~20 CPU cycles (one LOCK CMPXCHG)
  4. No syscall. No kernel. No mode switch.
```

This is the common case — mutexes are usually uncontended.

#### Slow Path — Contended (Kernel Involvement via `FUTEX_WAIT`)

```
Thread wants to lock:
  1. Atomic CAS: futex_word is 1 → FAIL (someone holds the lock)
  2. Atomic XCHG: set futex_word to 2 (indicates \"waiters exist\")
  3. Call futex(FUTEX_WAIT, &futex_word, 2) → SYSCALL
     - Kernel: put thread on wait queue for this futex address
     - Kernel: schedule another thread (context switch)
     - Thread is now SLEEPING, consuming zero CPU

When lock holder unlocks:
  1. Atomic decrement: futex_word was 1 → set to 0 → DONE (no waiters)
     -- OR --
  2. futex_word was 2 → set to 0
  3. Call futex(FUTEX_WAKE, &futex_word, 1) → SYSCALL
     - Kernel: dequeue one waiter, mark it runnable
     - Waiter wakes up, retries CAS, acquires lock
```

#### Why This Design Wins — Hardware Perspective

| Scenario | What Happens | Cycle Cost |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Lock free, grab on first try | Single atomic CAS | ~20–30 cycles |
| Lock held briefly, spin a few times then acquire | A few CAS attempts | ~100–200 cycles |
| Lock held long, go to sleep | `FUTEX_WAIT` syscall + context switch | ~2,000–10,000+ cycles |

The futex's fast path is the same cost as an atomic increment. Only contention invokes the kernel. This is why well-designed concurrent systems minimize lock hold times — keeping the mutex in the fast path.

> [!note]
> **Windows `CRITICAL_SECTION`:** Similar two-phase design. The lock is a userspace integer. Uncontended acquisition uses `InterlockedCompareExchange`. Contended acquisition uses `WaitForSingleObject` on a kernel event. The fast path is ~20 cycles; the slow path involves a kernel transition.

### RAII Locking — `std::lock_guard` and `std::unique_lock`

Never call `lock()` and `unlock()` manually. An exception between them leaves the mutex locked — deadlock for every other thread.

```cpp
// BAD: exception in DoWork() → unlock() never called → DEADLOCK
m.lock();
DoWork();
m.unlock();

// GOOD: lock_guard destructor always calls unlock()
{
    std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(m);
    DoWork();  // throws? lock.~lock_guard() runs → unlock
}

// BETTER: unique_lock for deferred lock, early unlock, condition variables
{
    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(m, std::defer_lock);
    // ... prepare work ...
    lock.lock();
    DoWork();
    lock.unlock();  // Release early if desired
    DoMoreWork();   // Outside lock
}
```

| | `lock_guard` | `unique_lock` |
|---|---|---|
| **Overhead** | Minimal (just a reference) | Slightly more (tracks owned state) |
| **Lock/unlock** | Lock on construction, unlock on destruction | Lock, unlock, try_lock anytime |
| **Deferred locking** | No | Yes — `std::defer_lock` |
| **Movable** | No | Yes |
| **Condition variable** | No | Yes — `cv.wait()` requires `unique_lock` |

---


## Exercises

1. **Lost update trace:** Two threads each call `counter++` 100,000 times. Without synchronization, the final value is not 200,000. Write a program that demonstrates this. Explain the exact assembly interleaving that causes the lost update at least once.

2. **Futex state machine:** Draw the state transitions of a futex word: 0 → 1 (fast path lock), 1 → 2 (slow path with waiters), 2 → 0 (unlock with wake). At each transition, what CPU instruction executes and is a kernel call involved?

3. **lock_guard vs unique_lock:** Write two functions — one using `std::lock_guard`, one using `std::unique_lock`. In what situation can `lock_guard` NOT be used but `unique_lock` CAN? (Hint: condition variables.)

4. **Measure mutex overhead:** Benchmark an uncontended mutex lock/unlock (fast path, ~20 cycles) vs a contended one (slow path, FUTEX_WAIT, ~2000+ cycles). At what contention level does a spinlock become cheaper than a mutex? Write the benchmark and report your numbers.

5. **Mutex or atomic?** You have a shared `int playerCount` incremented every frame by one thread and read by 100 rendering threads. Should you use `std::mutex`, `std::atomic<int>, or `std::shared_mutex`? Justify with cycle counts from this note.

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Two chefs reaching for the same onion at the same time — one grabs it and starts chopping, the other grabs empty air because the onion is already gone. That's a data race. A mutex is a token: only the chef holding the "cooking now" token can touch the onion. The other chef waits. If the wait is short (a few knife strokes), the waiting chef just spins in place (spinlock — burns energy but no waiting in line). If it's long (deep-frying), the waiting chef sits down and reads a magazine until tapped on the shoulder (futex — sleeps, zero CPU cost, but costs time to sit down and stand back up).

### Technical
A data race occurs when two cores' load-modify-store sequences interleave on the same cache line — the `counter++` at C++ expands to `mov [counter], eax; add eax, 1; mov [counter], eax` in x86, and another core can interleave between any pair. `std::mutex` wraps a futex (Linux) or `CRITICAL_SECTION` (Windows): the fast path uses a single `LOCK CMPXCHG` on a 32-bit futex word (0→1, ~20 cycles, no kernel). Contention triggers `FUTEX_WAIT` (futex word set to 2, thread placed on kernel wait queue, context switch). On unlock, `FUTEX_WAKE` dequeues a waiter. `std::lock_guard` provides RAII scoped locking (constructor locks, destructor unlocks — exception-safe). `std::unique_lock` adds deferred locking and unlock/lock cycles for condition variable integration.

## See Also

- [[05 — Deadlock, Livelock & Starvation]] — Next in sequence
- [[06 — Atomics & Memory Ordering]] — The alternative to mutexes
- [[Condition Variables & Semaphores]] — Blocking until a condition, not just exclusion

06 \u2014 Atomics & Memory Ordering.content:
# Atomics & Memory Ordering

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[04 — Data Races & Mutexes]] — understand what mutexes solve before learning the hardware-level alternative.

## Why This Matters

Your multiplayer game ships on PC first. It runs flawlessly. Then the Switch port arrives — and players report inventory items duplicating, health bars flickering with stale values, and crashes that repro once every hundred matches. You audit the code and find `std::atomic` everywhere. "But I used atomics!" you protest. You did — with `memory_order_relaxed` on everything. On x86, the hardware's strong ordering silently saved you. On ARM (Switch, smartphones, Apple Silicon), the CPU reordered your relaxed stores and loads in ways that broke every invariant. Your code was never correct; x86 was just too polite to tell you.

This note teaches you what actually happens when a CPU executes an atomic operation — the `LOCK` signal on the memory bus, the cache line negotiations of the MESI protocol, and the store buffers that reorder your carefully sequenced instructions. After this note, you will understand exactly what `memory_order_acquire`, `release`, and `seq_cst` do at the hardware level, why x86 lets you get away with things ARM won't, and how to write concurrent code that is correct on every platform — not just the one you test on.

---

An **atomic operation** is one that executes as a single, indivisible unit. No other core can observe a half-completed state. At the hardware level, this means the CPU provides instructions that **lock the memory bus or cache line** for the duration of the operation.

### CMPXCHG — The Universal Atomic Primitive (x86)

The x86 `CMPXCHG` instruction (Compare and Exchange) is the foundation of all lock-free programming on x86/x86-64:

```asm
; CMPXCHG dest, src
; Atomically performs:
;   temp = dest
;   IF (RAX == temp):
;       dest = src
;       ZF = 1     (success)
;   ELSE:
;       RAX = temp
;       ZF = 0     (failure)
```

Without the `LOCK` prefix, `CMPXCHG` is atomic only on a single core (it can't be interrupted mid-instruction, but another core can access the same memory). With `LOCK`, the CPU asserts a **lock signal** on the memory bus (or uses cache locking on modern CPUs), preventing any other core or DMA device from accessing that memory location until the instruction completes:

```asm
lock cmpxchg [counter], edx   ; Atomic compare-and-swap across ALL cores
```

### How `LOCK` Works — Three Generations of Hardware

| Era | Mechanism | Cost |
|------|-----------|------|
| **Old (486/Pentium)** | Assert physical `LOCK#` pin on the memory bus. ALL memory access blocked system-wide. | ~50–100 cycles |
| **Modern (Core 2+)** | Cache lock: lock ONLY the target cache line in L1. Other cores can access other cache lines freely. | ~20–40 cycles for L1 hit |
| **Skylake+** | Cache lock with reduced coherence traffic | ~15–30 cycles |

The key hardware improvement: modern CPUs don't lock the entire bus — they lock only the specific cache line (64 bytes) in their L1 cache. The MESI protocol handles notifying other cores that the line is locked.

### The CAS Loop — How Atom Actually Works

`std::atomic<int>::fetch_add(1)` compiles to a CAS loop on x86:

```cpp
// Conceptual C++ for what the hardware does:
int old = counter.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);     // Read current value
int desired;
do {
    desired = old + 1;                                  // Compute new value
} while (!counter.compare_exchange_weak(old, desired)); // Try CAS; if fail, old is updated
```

The corresponding assembly (simplified):

```asm
.Lloop:
    mov    eax, [counter]           ; Load current value
    lea    edx, [rax + 1]          ; Compute desired = old + 1
    lock cmpxchg [counter], edx    ; If [counter]==eax, [counter]=edx; else eax=[counter]
    jne    .Lloop                   ; If ZF=0 (CAS failed), retry with new eax
```

> [!tip]
> **`fetch_add` is actually a single instruction on x86:** `lock xadd [counter], eax`. The compiler doesn't always need a CAS loop — `fetch_add`, `fetch_sub`, `exchange` have dedicated atomic instructions. `compare_exchange` is the universal fallback.

### ARM64 / RISC-V — Load-Linked / Store-Conditional

RISC architectures don't have `CMPXCHG`. Instead, they use a two-instruction pattern:

```asm
; ARM64: atomically increment [x1]
retry:
    ldaxr  w0, [x1]        ; Load-Linked: load [x1], mark address as \"watched\"
    add    w0, w0, #1       ; Compute new value
    stlxr  w2, w0, [x1]    ; Store-Conditional: store ONLY if no other core wrote to [x1]
    cbnz   w2, retry        ; w2=1 means store failed → retry
```

The CPU's **cache coherence hardware monitors** the address between `ldaxr` and `stlxr`. If any core writes to that address (or if an interrupt occurs, or if the cache line is evicted), the `stlxr` fails. This is the hardware guarantee that makes lock-free algorithms work on ARM.

| Property | x86 CMPXCHG | ARM LL/SC |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| **Approach** | One instruction atomically does it all | Two instructions + retry loop |
| **ABA problem** | Vulnerable (A→B→A: CAS sees A, succeeds incorrectly) | LL/SC detects ANY write between load and store, including A→B→A |
| **Forward progress** | Guaranteed (lock cmpxchg always succeeds if value matches) | Can livelock (constant interference from other cores) |
| **Memory ordering** | Implicitly strong (total store order) | Explicit — `ldaxr` = acquire, `stlxr` = release |

### What `std::atomic<T>` Actually Provides

```cpp
`std::`atomic<int>`` counter{0};

counter.store(42);                       // Atomic write
int x = counter.load();                  // Atomic read
int old = counter.fetch_add(1);          // Atomic read-modify-write
counter++;                               // Same as fetch_add(1)
bool ok = counter.compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);  // CAS
```

**Guarantees:**
- ✅ Atomicity: operations are indivisible — no torn reads/writes
- ✅ Well-defined memory ordering (controlled via `memory_order`)
- ✅ Lock-free for `int`, `bool`, pointer types on all modern platforms (`counter.is_lock_free()`)

**Does NOT guarantee:**
- ❌ Individual atomic ops don't make a sequence atomic: `if (counter < 10) counter++;` — another thread can slip between the `load` and `fetch_add`
- ❌ Atomic counter doesn't make the data it indexes thread-safe — if `counter` tracks how many items are in a non-atomic array, the array accesses still race

---

> [!abstract] Deep Dive — Can skip on first read — When the CPU Reorders Your Code

### The Hardware Reality: Store Buffers and Out-of-Order Execution

Modern CPUs don't execute instructions in program order. They have:

- **Store buffers:** When a core executes a store, the value goes into a store buffer — it's visible to the local core immediately but may not be visible to other cores for dozens of cycles.
- **Out-of-order execution:** The CPU reorders independent instructions to keep pipelines full.
- **Speculative execution:** Instructions execute before branches are resolved; results may be discarded.

This means that even with atomic operations, one thread may see writes in a **different order** than they were executed.

### The Producer-Consumer Failure (Relaxed Ordering)

```cpp
`std::`atomic<int>`` data{0};
`std::`atomic<bool>`` ready{false};

// Thread 1 (Producer):
void Producer() {
    data.store(42, std::memory_order_relaxed);   // Write data
    ready.store(true, std::memory_order_relaxed); // Signal readiness
}

// Thread 2 (Consumer):
void Consumer() {
    while (!ready.load(std::memory_order_relaxed)); // Wait for signal
    int value = data.load(std::memory_order_relaxed); // Read data
    // value might NOT be 42! Even though Producer wrote data first!
}
```

**Why this fails at the hardware level:**

Core 0's store buffer may reorder the two stores. Or Core 1's load queue may reorder the two loads. Or the cache coherence interconnect may deliver the invalidation for `ready` before the invalidation for `data`. With `relaxed`, the hardware provides **zero ordering guarantees** — each atomic operation is individually atomic, but their relative order is undefined.

```
Core 0's store buffer:           Core 1's cache:
  [data=42] ← in buffer              [data=0] ← still sees old value!
  [ready=true] ← in buffer           [ready=true] ← this invalidation arrived first
  
Core 1 reads: ready=true ✓ → reads data: 0 ✗ (wrong!)
```

### The Fix: Acquire-Release

```cpp
// Thread 1 (Producer):
data.store(42, std::memory_order_release);    // All previous writes \"released\" here
ready.store(true, std::memory_order_release); // Signal AFTER data is visible

// Thread 2 (Consumer):
while (!ready.load(std::memory_order_acquire)); // Everything after this sees released state
int value = data.load(std::memory_order_relaxed); // GUARANTEED: value == 42
```

**The hardware guarantee:** `release` on `ready` ensures that all memory writes before the release are visible to any thread that subsequently does an `acquire` on the same atomic variable and sees the released value. The CPU inserts a **memory barrier** (`MFENCE` or equivalent) that drains the store buffer before the release store becomes visible.

### The Four C++ Memory Orderings

| Order | Guarantee | x86 Hardware Cost | ARM Hardware Cost |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| `relaxed` | Atomicity only. No ordering. | Free (~1 cycle, same as non-atomic) | Free |
| `acquire` | Load: no reads/writes after can reorder before this load. | Free (x86 loads are implicitly acquire) | `ldar` instruction (~2 cycles) |
| `release` | Store: no reads/writes before can reorder after this store. | Free (x86 stores are implicitly release) | `stlr` instruction (~2 cycles) |
| `seq_cst` | Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst ops. | `MFENCE` after store (~20–30 cycles) | `dmb` barrier (~10–30 cycles) |

> [!tip]
> **Why x86 is \"easy mode\" for memory ordering:** x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — all stores appear in a consistent order to all cores. `acquire` and `release` are free because the hardware already provides those guarantees.
