Phase 3: the actors (kernel/OS) take the stage — building the set (virtual memory), positioning props (device drivers), and warming up their voices. Phase 4: the audience arrives (userspace/your program) and the show begins.

### Technical
Power-on triggers the reset vector at `0xFFFFFFF0` (x86) — a hardwired jump to firmware. The CPU starts in 16-bit real mode (8086-compatible, 1MB addressable). UEFI transitions to 32-bit protected mode or 64-bit long mode during DXE phase. The bootloader is loaded from the ESP (FAT32 partition identified by GPT type GUID `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`). UEFI hands control via `ExitBootServices()` — after this, the firmware runtime services are unavailable. The kernel initializes: GDT (segment descriptors), IDT (interrupt handlers), TSS (task state for ring transitions), page tables (identity map → full virtual memory), and then subsystem by subsystem. The ELF loader (`fs/binfmt_elf.c` in Linux) maps segments using `mmap()` with appropriate protections. `_start` from `crt1.o` calls `__libc_start_main` which initializes the C++ runtime (calls functions in `.init_array`) before calling `main`.

## See Also

- [[03 — Assembly Language]] — The boot sector is written in assembly
- [[RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]] — Linux kernel boot and memory map
- [[../System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]] — What the kernel does after boot
- [[../System Software/03 — Linking & Executables]] — How the OS loads your executable

12 \u2014 GPU Architecture.content:
# GPU Architecture

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Your CPU has 8 cores running at 5 GHz. Your GPU has 10,000+ cores running at 2.5 GHz. The GPU is "slower" per core yet renders 4K at 120 FPS. How? Because graphics is not one hard problem — it's 8 million tiny problems (pixels) that are all identical. The GPU is a specialized machine that trades single-thread performance for **massive parallelism**: do the same operation on millions of data points simultaneously. Understanding the GPU's architecture — warps, wavefronts, shared memory, occupancy, the rendering pipeline — is understanding why your game runs at 15 FPS on integrated graphics and 144 FPS on a discrete card.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — understand CPU cores, caches, and pipelining before learning how the GPU is different.
> [[05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — DDR vs GDDR, bandwidth vs latency, cache coherence.

---

## Why Not Just More CPU Cores?

A CPU core (Golden Cove in Intel Raptor Lake, Zen 4 in AMD) is a marvel of single-thread performance:
- Out-of-order execution: reorders instructions to keep pipelines full.
- Branch prediction: guesses which way `if` statements go before the condition resolves.
- Speculative execution: executes instructions before knowing if they're needed.
- Large caches: 2 MB L2, 32 MB L3 shared.
- Complex instruction set: AVX-512, AES-NI, virtualization extensions.

Total transistor budget per core: ~50-100 million. 8 such cores: ~500-800 million transistors for compute. The remaining 5+ billion transistors on a modern CPU die? Mostly cache and I/O.

A GPU streaming multiprocessor (SM) / compute unit (CU) is a **simplified, replicated processor**:
- No branch prediction (or very simple).
- No out-of-order execution (in-order, deterministic).
- Tiny caches per SM (128 KB L1 shared across 128 threads).
- Simple instruction set: mostly fused multiply-add (FMA), load/store, texture sampling.

Total transistor budget per SM: ~2-3 million. An RTX 4090 has **128 SMs** × 128 CUDA cores each = **16,384 cores**. The same transistor budget spent on 128 simple processors instead of 8 complex ones.

> [!important] The Key Insight: SIMT
> GPU cores are NOT independent. They execute in **lockstep groups**: 32 threads on NVIDIA (a **warp**), 64 threads on AMD (a **wavefront**). All threads in a warp execute the same instruction at the same time, on different data. This is **SIMT**: Single Instruction, Multiple Threads. One instruction fetch, one decode, 32 executions. The instruction cache and decoder are shared across all 32 lanes — enormous power and area savings.

---

## Warps and Wavefronts — The Fundamental Execution Unit

```cuda
// CUDA kernel — 256 threads total
__global__ void AddArrays(float* a, float* b, float* c, int n) {
    int idx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;
    if (idx < n) {
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx];  // FMA: 1 cycle for all 32 threads in warp
    }
}
```

When you launch this kernel with 256 threads, the GPU scheduler:
1. Groups threads into **warps** of 32 (8 warps for 256 threads).
2. Assigns warps to SMs.
3. Each SM picks a warp, fetches one instruction, broadcasts it to all 32 lanes.
4. All 32 threads execute `a[idx] + b[idx]` simultaneously — 32 adds in parallel.
5. The SM can interleave multiple warps: while warp 0 waits for memory, warp 1 executes.

```
   Warp 0 (threads 0-31):    [=====memory wait=====]
   Warp 1 (threads 32-63):       [==execute==]
   Warp 2 (threads 64-95):           [==execute==]
   Warp 3 (threads 96-127):              [==execute==]
   Warp 0 (resumes):                          [==execute==]
```

This is **latency hiding**: the GPU doesn't care that memory takes 400 cycles. It just switches to another warp. With enough active warps, the execution units are always busy.

### The Divergence Trap

```cuda
if (threadIdx.x % 2 == 0) {
    c[idx] = a[idx] * 2;   // Even threads take this path
} else {
    c[idx] = a[idx] / 2;   // Odd threads take this path
}
```

All 32 threads in a warp share one instruction pointer. When half take `if` and half take `else`, the warp **serializes**: first execute the `if` path with even threads active (odd threads masked off), then execute the `else` path with odd threads active (even threads masked off). **Performance: 50% of peak.** This is warp divergence.

> [!warning] Divergence is the #1 GPU performance killer
> Avoid data-dependent branching within a warp. Prefer branchless code (`cond ? a : b` compiled to `select`/`cmov`), sorting data so adjacent threads take the same path, or restructuring algorithms to keep control flow uniform.

---

## GPU Memory Hierarchy — The Bandwidth Engine

| Level | Size (RTX 4090) | Latency | Bandwidth | Per |
|-------|-----------------|---------|-----------|-----|
| **Register file** | 256 KB per SM | ~0 cycles | ~40 TB/s | SM |
| **Shared memory / L1** | 128 KB per SM | ~20 cycles | ~20 TB/s | SM |
| **L2 cache** | 72 MB | ~200 cycles | ~5 TB/s | Chip |
| **VRAM (GDDR6X)** | 24 GB | ~400 cycles | 1,008 GB/s | Chip |
| **PCIe 4.0 ×16** | — | ~5 µs | 32 GB/s | Host→GPU |
| **System RAM** | 32-64 GB | ~10 µs | 50-100 GB/s | CPU |

> [!tip] The GPU is a bandwidth-first design
> An RTX 4090 has 1,008 GB/s of VRAM bandwidth. A high-end CPU has ~50-100 GB/s to system RAM. The GPU has 10-20× more bandwidth because graphics is inherently bandwidth-bound: 8 million pixels × 4 bytes (RGBA) × 3 buffers (color, depth, normal) × 120 FPS = 11.5 GB/s JUST for framebuffer traffic. Add textures (4K × 4K × 4 bytes = 64 MB per texture, hundreds of textures per frame), vertex buffers, and compute intermediate buffers — easily 200+ GB/s. The GPU's entire architecture is designed to keep the memory bus saturated.

### Shared Memory — Programmer-Managed L1

CUDA/HLSL expose **shared memory**: an explicitly managed scratchpad, not an automatic cache. You decide what data goes there.

```cuda
__global__ void MatrixMul(float* A, float* B, float* C, int N) {
    __shared__ float tileA[32][32];  // Stored in shared memory — 1 cycle access
    __shared__ float tileB[32][32];

    // Cooperative load: all threads in block load a tile from VRAM
    tileA[threadIdx.y][threadIdx.x] = A[row * N + tile * 32 + threadIdx.x];
    tileB[threadIdx.y][threadIdx.x] = B[(tile * 32 + threadIdx.y) * N + col];
    __syncthreads();  // Barrier: all threads must finish loading before computing

    // Now compute from shared memory — 200× faster than VRAM
    for (int k = 0; k < 32; k++) {
        sum += tileA[threadIdx.y][k] * tileB[k][threadIdx.x];
    }
}
```

This is the critical pattern: cooperatively load a tile from VRAM (slow, 400 cycles) into shared memory (fast, 20 cycles), synchronize, then compute entirely from shared memory. Without this tiling, every FMA would fetch from VRAM — the GPU would spend 95% of time waiting for memory.

---

## Occupancy — How Many Warps Can Hide Latency?

**Occupancy** = active warps per SM ÷ maximum warps per SM.

An RTX 4090 SM supports up to 64 warps (2,048 threads). If your kernel uses 64 registers per thread, the SM's 65,536 registers can hold 1,024 threads = 32 warps. Occupancy = 32/64 = 50%.

Higher occupancy = more warps to switch between while waiting for memory = better latency hiding. BUT: higher occupancy also means fewer registers per thread, which can cause **register spilling** to VRAM (disastrous). The art of GPU optimization is finding the occupancy sweet spot.

```
Occupancy Calculator:
  Register usage per thread: 32
  Shared memory per block:    8 KB
  Threads per block:          256 (8 warps)

  Max warps by registers:     65536 / (32 regs × 32 threads) = 64 warps → OK
  Max warps by shared mem:    (128 KB / 8 KB per block) × 8 warps per block = 128 warps → OK
  Max warps by SM limit:      64

  Occupancy: 64/64 = 100%
```

---

## Thread Scheduling — How the GPU Keeps 10,000 Cores Busy

### NVIDIA: GigaThread Engine

The GigaThread engine is a hardware scheduler on the GPU die. Its job:
1. Receive kernel launch from the CPU (via CUDA driver / graphics API).
2. Distribute thread blocks across SMs (load balancing: fill SMs evenly).
3. Within each SM, the **warp scheduler** picks a ready warp every cycle (round-robin or priority-based).
4. Issue one or two instructions from that warp to the execution units.

Each SM has 4 warp schedulers, each managing 16 warps. Every cycle, each scheduler picks one ready warp. With enough warps, the execution units never stall.

### AMD: ACE (Asynchronous Compute Engine)

AMD's approach differs: multiple **ACE** units independently manage command streams. Each ACE can dispatch compute shaders while the graphics command processor handles the rendering pipeline. This enables **async compute**: execute compute shaders (e.g., particle simulation, post-processing) concurrently with the graphics pipeline, utilizing SMs that would otherwise idle during fixed-function stages.

```
NVIDIA: Central GigaThread → distributed to SMs → warp schedulers per SM
AMD:    Multiple ACEs + Graphics Command Processor → independent queues → CUs
```

---

## The GPU Core — What's Inside One SM/CU

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) — RTX 4090           │
│                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────┐ │
│  │Warp Sched│  │Warp Sched│  │Warp Sched│  │Warp │ │
│  │  (16w)   │  │  (16w)   │  │  (16w)   │  │Sched│ │
│  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──┬──┘ │
│       │             │             │           │     │
│  ┌────▼─────────────▼─────────────▼───────────▼──┐ │
│  │          128 CUDA Cores (FP32/INT32)           │ │
│  │          64 FP32 + 64 INT32 per scheduler       │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  64 Tensor Cores (FP16 matrix FMA)             │ │
│  │  4 RT Cores (ray-triangle intersection)        │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  128 KB L1 Data Cache / Shared Memory          │ │
│  │  256 KB Register File (16,384 × 32-bit regs)   │ │
│  │  4 Texture Units (TMUs)                        │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each CUDA core is simple: a fused multiply-add (FMA) unit that computes `a*b + c` in one cycle, plus a small register file. No branch predictor, no reorder buffer, no speculative execution. The simplicity enables massive replication.

### Specialized Units

- **Tensor Cores:** Matrix multiply-accumulate for AI inference (DLSS). One instruction computes a 4×4×4 matrix multiply. ~8× faster than CUDA cores for matmul workloads.
- **RT Cores:** Hardware ray-triangle intersection and BVH traversal. One instruction tests a ray against a bounding volume or triangle. ~10× faster than compute shader path tracing.
- **TMUs (Texture Mapping Units):** Hardware texture sampling with bilinear/trilinear/anisotropic filtering. Free for the shader — one instruction, one filtered texel.
- **ROPs (Render Output Units):** Fixed-function pixel output: depth testing, blending, MSAA resolve. Not exposed to shaders — pure hardware at the end of the pipeline.

---

## The Graphics Pipeline — From Vertices to Pixels

```
Vertex Shader
    │   Input: vertex positions, normals, UVs
    │   Output: transformed vertices in clip space
    ▼
Hull Shader (optional, for tessellation)
    │   Determines tessellation factors
    ▼
Tessellator (fixed-function)
    │   Generates new vertices based on factors
    ▼
Domain Shader (optional)
    │   Computes positions of tessellated vertices
    ▼
Geometry Shader (optional, rarely used — slow)
    │   Can emit/discard primitives
    ▼
Rasterizer (fixed-function)
    │   Converts triangles to pixel fragments. Interpolates attributes.
    ▼
Pixel Shader (Fragment Shader)
    │   Input: interpolated attributes per pixel
    │   Output: color, depth
    │   THIS is where most GPU time is spent
    ▼
Output Merger (fixed-function)
    │   Depth test, stencil test, blending, MSAA resolve
    │   Writes to render targets
    ▼
Framebuffer / Render Target
```

### Compute Shaders — Skip the Pipeline

Compute shaders bypass the entire fixed-function pipeline. They read and write arbitrary buffers (UAVs in DirectX, storage buffers in Vulkan). Use cases:
- Particle simulation (positions, velocities stored in buffers).
- Post-processing (bloom, tone mapping, motion blur).
- Culling (GPU decides which objects are visible, writes draw list).
- AI inference (matrix multiplies on tensor cores).

```hlsl
// HLSL compute shader — particle update
[numthreads(256, 1, 1)]
void UpdateParticles(uint3 id : SV_DispatchThreadID) {
    Particle p = ParticleBuffer[id.x];
    p.Position += p.Velocity * DeltaTime;
    p.Velocity += Gravity * DeltaTime;
    ParticleBuffer[id.x] = p;
}
```

256 threads, each updating one particle. No vertices, no triangles, no rasterizer. Just read-buffer, compute, write-buffer.

---

## PCIe Bottleneck and DirectStorage

The GPU renders frames from data in VRAM. But that data originates on disk, passes through system RAM, and crosses the PCIe bus to VRAM.

```
SSD → [DMA] → System RAM → [CPU copy?] → PCIe → VRAM → GPU
```

Traditional path: CPU reads file into RAM, decompresses, copies to GPU-visible buffer, GPU DMA-transfers across PCIe. CPU is the middleman for EVERY byte.

**DirectStorage / RTX IO:** The GPU reads directly from an NVMe SSD into VRAM, with GPU-side decompression.
```
SSD → [PCIe] → GPU decompress → VRAM
```
No CPU involvement. No system RAM staging. The GPU's massive parallelism excels at decompression (tens of thousands of parallel DEFLATE/GDeflate decoders).

### UE5's RHI — Abstracting the GPU

Unreal's **Rendering Hardware Interface (RHI)** is an abstraction layer over DirectX 12, Vulkan, and Metal:
```cpp
// UE5 RHI — same code runs on all platforms
RHICmdList.SetComputeShader(UpdateParticlesShader);
RHICmdList.SetUAVParameter(UpdateParticlesShader, ParticleBufferUAV);
RHICmdList.DispatchComputeShader(NumThreadGroups, 1, 1);
```

The RHI translates this to the platform's native API. You write RHI calls (or higher-level `FRDGBuilder` render graph); UE generates D3D12/Vulkan/Metal commands. The GPU architecture differences (NVIDIA warps vs AMD wavefronts, shared memory sizes, binding models) are handled by the RHI.

---

## Exercises

1. **Warp divergence measurement:** Write a CUDA kernel (or use Godbolt's CUDA output) with an `if (threadIdx.x % 2 == 0)` branch. Analyze: what percentage of lanes are active per path? Now rewrite using branchless selection (`c[idx] = (threadIdx.x % 2 == 0) ? a[idx] * 2 : a[idx] / 2`). How many instructions does each version produce? Why does the branchless version avoid serialization?

2. **Occupancy calculation:** An SM supports 2,048 threads (64 warps), 65,536 registers, and 128 KB shared memory. Your kernel uses 48 registers per thread and 16 KB shared memory per block of 256 threads. Calculate maximum threads per SM limited by registers, by shared memory, and by thread cap. What is the limiting factor? What occupancy does it achieve?

3. **Shared memory tiling analysis:** The matrix multiply example loads 32×32 tiles. If VRAM bandwidth is 1 TB/s and shared memory bandwidth is 20 TB/s, what fraction of total memory accesses hit shared memory (vs VRAM) in the compute loop? If each thread computes one output element (requiring 64 float loads from VRAM without tiling), how many loads per thread does tiling reduce this to? What is the arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per byte loaded) with and without tiling?

4. **GPU vs CPU for a scatter operation:** You need to apply a random permutation to an array of 10 million floats: `output[perm[i]] = input[i]`. On CPU (single core, 5 GHz), this is a simple loop — but the random access pattern defeats prefetching. On GPU, 10,000 threads each write to a random location — but this causes **global memory scatter**, which is the GPU's worst-case access pattern (no coalescing). Estimate throughput for both. Why might the CPU win this particular workload despite having 1/1000th the "cores"?

5. **Design a GPU particle system:** Design a compute-shader-based particle system with 1 million particles. Each particle has position (float3), velocity (float3), lifetime (float), and color (float4) = 44 bytes. (a) What total VRAM does the particle buffer need? (b) How many thread groups of 256 threads do you dispatch? (c) You also need a "dead list" to recycle expired particles — design a scheme using atomic counters and a free-list buffer. (d) Estimate bandwidth: at 60 FPS, how many GB/s does updating all particles consume (read 44 bytes, write 44 bytes per particle)?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Your CPU is a team of 8 Olympic gymnasts — each can do incredibly complex, independent routines simultaneously. Your GPU is 10,000 kindergarteners who can all clap in unison. One gymnast beats one kindergartener at ANY task. But if the task is "clap 8 million times" (render 8 million pixels), the kindergarteners win — not because they're faster individually, but because there are so many of them that the work finishes in a fraction of the time. The GPU's secret is **lockstep**: 32 kindergarteners hold hands (a warp) and all clap at exactly the same moment. One instruction, 32 claps. Then the teacher (warp scheduler) moves to the next group. While one group waits for crayons (memory), another group is clapping. With enough groups, the teacher never pauses — that's occupancy. The GPU also has specialized helpers: the art room (texture units) for painting, the math club (tensor cores) for matrix multiplication, and the crossing guard (ROPs) who makes sure colors are written in the right order.

### Technical
The GPU employs the SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) execution model: threads are grouped into warps (NVIDIA, 32 threads) or wavefronts (AMD, 64 threads) that execute in lockstep sharing one instruction fetch/decode. An SM (Streaming Multiprocessor) contains 128 CUDA cores plus specialized units (tensor cores, RT cores, TMUs, shared memory/L1). The warp scheduler interleaves multiple warps to hide memory latency; occupancy — the ratio of active to maximum warps — determines latency-hiding capacity. The memory hierarchy is bandwidth-optimized: register file (~40 TB/s per SM), shared memory (~20 TB/s), L2 (72 MB, ~5 TB/s), and VRAM/GDDR6X (24 GB, 1,008 GB/s). Programmer-managed shared memory enables cooperative tiling patterns that reduce VRAM traffic 200×. The graphics pipeline (vertex→hull→tessellator→domain→geometry→rasterizer→pixel→output merger) mixes programmable shaders with fixed-function hardware. Compute shaders bypass the pipeline entirely for arbitrary parallel computation. Warp divergence — when threads within a warp take different branch paths — serializes execution and halves throughput per divergent branch. PCIe bandwidth (32 GB/s Gen4×16) is the bottleneck between CPU and GPU; DirectStorage/RTX IO bypasses the CPU by streaming decompressed data directly from NVMe SSD to VRAM on the GPU.

## See Also

- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — Why CPUs take the opposite design approach (few complex cores vs many simple cores)
- [[05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — Cache coherence (CPUs have it, GPUs don't), DDR vs GDDR tradeoffs
- [[Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe|NVMe, SATA & PCIe]] — PCIe bandwidth and how DirectStorage exploits NVMe parallelism
- [[../../Game Math/UE Math/FVector, FRotator, FQuat — The Core Types|UE Math Types]] — The vector/matrix math that shaders compute

13 \u2014 Interrupts & Exceptions.content:
# Interrupts & Exceptions

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Every key press, every mouse movement, every network packet, every frame rendered starts with an interrupt. Without interrupts, your CPU would have to constantly ask "did the player press a key yet? how about now? now?" — burning 100% of a core on busy-waiting. Interrupts are the hardware mechanism that lets the CPU sleep or do other work, then be **alerted** exactly when something needs attention. Understanding interrupts means understanding how your game communicates with input devices, audio hardware, the GPU, the network card, the disk, and the OS scheduler itself. This note traces the full path from a physical key press to the character appearing on screen.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — understand registers (RIP, RSP, RFLAGS), the instruction cycle, and kernel vs user mode (ring 0 vs ring 3).
> [[../System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics|Function Call Hardware]] — the stack, `call`/`ret`, and how the CPU saves its place.

---

## The Problem Interrupts Solve — Polling vs Notification

**Polling:** The CPU repeatedly checks a status register. "Is there keyboard data? No. Is there keyboard data? No. Is there keyboard data? Yes — process it."

```cpp
// Polling — CPU does nothing else while waiting
while (!KeyboardHasData()) { /* burn cycles */ }
ProcessKey(KeyboardRead());
```

At 5 GHz, a polling loop checks millions of times per second. 99.9999% of those checks are wasted. The CPU can't do anything else — polling consumes a full core.

**Interrupts:** The hardware signals the CPU when data is ready. The CPU stops what it's doing, handles the data, then resumes.

```cpp
// The CPU runs game logic, rendering, physics...
// When the keyboard has data, the CPU is INTERRUPTED:
//   1. Save current state
//   2. Run keyboard ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)
//   3. Restore state
//   4. Continue game logic as if nothing happened
```

The CPU spends ~0.01% of its time handling keyboard input. The other 99.99% runs your game. This is the efficiency of interrupts.

---

## The Interrupt Hardware Chain — From Key Press to ISR

Here is every hardware step when you press the 'W' key:

```
Step 1: Physical Key Press
  Keyboard membrane contact closes → electrical signal → keyboard microcontroller
  (a tiny CPU inside the keyboard itself) scans its key matrix, identifies row+column
  → Key code: 0x1A (scancode for 'W')

Step 2: USB HID Report
  Microcontroller formats a USB HID (Human Interface Device) report packet:
  [Report ID | Modifier Keys | Reserved | Keycode 1 | Keycode 2 | ...]
  Sends packet over USB at 1.5 Mbps (low-speed USB) or 12 Mbps (full-speed)

Step 3: USB Host Controller
  USB controller on motherboard receives packet via differential signaling (D+/D- lines)
  Stores packet in its internal DMA buffer
  USB controller raises an interrupt → asserts its IRQ line

Step 4: Interrupt Routing — I/O APIC
  The USB controller is connected to a specific input pin on the I/O APIC
  (Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller)
  I/O APIC looks up that pin in its Redirection Table:
    IRQ 23 → vector 0x43 → destination: CPU core 2, LAPIC ID 2
  I/O APIC sends an interrupt message over the system bus (or APIC bus)

Step 5: Local APIC (per CPU core)
  Core 2's Local APIC receives the interrupt message
  Checks: is the interrupt priority higher than what this core is currently doing?
    Yes (keyboard: priority 0x40 > current task: priority 0x00)
  Local APIC signals the CPU core: "INTERRUPT, vector 0x43!"

Step 6: CPU Acknowledges
  CPU completes the current instruction (doesn't abort mid-instruction)
  Pushes RFLAGS, CS (code segment), and RIP onto the stack
  Clears IF (Interrupt Flag) — disables further maskable interrupts
  Reads the Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) entry for vector 0x43:
    IDT[0x43] → { handler_address, segment_selector, flags, IST }
  Loads CS:RIP from the IDT entry → jumps to the interrupt handler
```

> [!tip] The IDT — The CPU's Interrupt Phonebook
> The IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table) is an array of up to 256 entries in memory. The CPU's `IDTR` register holds its base address. Entry format (x86-64):
> ``
> Bits 0-15:   Handler address [0:15]
> Bits 16-31:  Code segment selector
> Bits 32-36:  IST (Interrupt Stack Table index, 0 = use current stack)
> Bits 37-39:  Reserved
> Bits 40-43:  Gate type (0xE = interrupt gate, 0xF = trap gate)
> Bits 44-47:  Reserved
> Bits 48-63:  Handler address [16:31]
> Bits 64-95:  Handler address [32:63]
> Bits 96-127: Reserved
> ``
> The CPU reads this 128-bit entry, constructs the target address, and jumps. This all happens in **hardware** — no OS code runs between the LAPIC signal and the first instruction of the handler.

---

## Types of Interrupts

| Type | Source | Maskable? | Example |
|------|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Hardware interrupt** | External device (IRQ) | Yes (via IF flag) | Keyboard, mouse, NIC, disk, GPU |
| **Software interrupt** | `INT n` instruction | No | System calls (`INT 0x80` on old Linux, now `SYSCALL`) |
| **Exception (fault)** | CPU detects error | No | Page fault (#PF, vector 14), division by zero (#DE, vector 0) |
| **Exception (trap)** | CPU after instruction | No | Breakpoint (`INT 3`, debugger single-step) |
| **Exception (abort)** | Unrecoverable error | No | Machine check (hardware failure), double fault |
| **NMI** | Non-maskable interrupt | NO | Memory ECC error, hardware watchdog, magic SysRq |
| **IPI** | Inter-processor interrupt | Yes | One CPU core signaling another (TLB shootdown, reschedule) |

### Exceptions vs Interrupts

- **Interrupts** are **asynchronous** — they can occur between any two instructions, unrelated to the executing code.
- **Exceptions** are **synchronous** — they are caused by the instruction being executed.

```cpp
int x = 10 / 0;           // Exception: division by zero — caused BY this instruction
ProcessKey(kb.ReadKey()); // Interrupt: keyboard data — could arrive at any moment
```

### The Timer Interrupt — The Scheduler's Heartbeat

Every CPU core has a **local APIC timer** that fires an interrupt at a configurable interval. On Linux, this is typically 250 Hz (every 4 ms), configurable via `CONFIG_HZ` (100, 250, 300, 1000).

When the timer interrupt fires:
1. CPU vectors to the timer ISR.
2. ISR calls `scheduler_tick()` — the OS scheduler's periodic entry point.
3. Scheduler checks: has the current thread run long enough? (CFS time slice expired?)
4. If yes: set `need_resched` flag.
5. On return from interrupt, if `need_resched` is set, the scheduler runs and potentially context-switches to another thread.

```
Timer interrupt every 4 ms:
  Frame 1 (16.7 ms at 60 FPS): ~4 timer ticks
  Each tick: chance to preempt and schedule another thread

Without timer interrupts: cooperative multitasking only —
  threads must voluntarily yield. One infinite loop = system frozen.
```

> [!tip] Tickless (dyntick) kernels
> Modern Linux supports `CONFIG_NO_HZ` — if a core is idle or running a single CPU-bound thread, the kernel disables the timer tick. The core sleeps until the next scheduled event or an external interrupt. On a game server running one dedicated process, tickless mode eliminates 250 useless interrupts per second per core.

---

## Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) — Top Half

The ISR is the function pointed to by the IDT entry. It runs in **interrupt context** — a restricted environment:

- No sleeping (no blocking, no mutexes, no page faults).
- No calling functions that might block.
- Runs with interrupts disabled (IF=0) unless explicitly re-enabled.
- Must be **fast** — the CPU can't handle other interrupts while the ISR runs.

```c
// Linux kernel ISR skeleton
static irqreturn_t keyboard_isr(int irq, void *dev_id) {
    // TOP HALF: acknowledge the interrupt, do minimal work
    u8 scancode = inb(0x60);           // Read scancode from keyboard port
    // ... minimal processing: translate scancode, queue event
    return IRQ_HANDLED;                // Tell the kernel we handled it
}
```

### Bottom Half — Deferred Work

Expensive work (USB packet parsing, network stack processing, disk I/O scheduling) is deferred to **bottom half** mechanisms that run with interrupts enabled:

| Mechanism | Context | Sleep OK? | Latency |
|-----------|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Softirq** | Interrupt context (kernel thread) | No | Lowest (runs immediately after ISR) |
| **Tasklet** | Interrupt context | No | Low (runs after softirqs) |
| **Workqueue** | Process context (kernel thread) | **Yes** | Higher (scheduled by scheduler) |

```c
// Top half: ISR — minimal work
irqreturn_t nic_isr(int irq, void *dev) {
    // Ack interrupt, schedule bottom half
    schedule_tasklet(&nic_rx_tasklet);  // "There's a packet, deal with it later"
    return IRQ_HANDLED;
}

// Bottom half: tasklet — parse packets, enqueue to network stack
void nic_rx_tasklet(unsigned long data) {
    while (nic_has_packets()) {
        struct packet* pkt = nic_read_packet();
        netif_rx(pkt);  // Enqueue to Linux network stack
    }
}
```

---

## Interrupt Latency — How Long from Signal to Handler?

Total interrupt latency = hardware latency + OS overhead:

| Component | Typical Latency | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------|
| Device asserts IRQ | ~0 ns | Electrical signal propagation |
| I/O APIC routing | ~50 ns | Table lookup + bus message |
| LAPIC priority check | ~20 ns | Comparator circuit |
| CPU finishes current instruction | ~0.3-50 ns | Varies: simple instruction vs REP-string or locked CMPXCHG |
| CPU pushes state, loads IDT entry | ~10-20 ns | Microcode sequence |
| Pipeline restart cost | ~10-30 cycles (2-6 ns) | Flush + refill |
| ISR first instruction | T=0 | Handler starts executing |
| **Total hardware latency** | **~100-150 ns** | From IRQ assertion to ISR start |

The OS then adds:
- ISR prologue (save registers): ~20 ns
- Actual ISR work: device-specific (keyboard: ~50 ns; disk: 10 µs; NIC: 1-50 µs)
- Bottom half processing: microseconds to milliseconds

> [!warning] Interrupt Storms
> If a misbehaving device asserts its IRQ line continuously without being acknowledged, the CPU enters an **interrupt storm**: it services the ISR, returns, immediately receives the same interrupt again, ad infinitum. The system becomes unresponsive — 100% CPU in interrupt context. Modern OSes detect storms and disable the offending IRQ line.

---

## MSI/MSI-X — Message-Signaled Interrupts

Traditional interrupts use dedicated physical IRQ lines (wires on the motherboard). This limits the number of interrupts and requires complex routing through the PIC/APIC.

**MSI (Message Signaled Interrupts)** replaces the physical wire with a PCIe memory write. The device writes to a special address; the PCIe root complex recognizes this as an interrupt and routes it to the appropriate LAPIC.

**MSI-X** extends MSI by allowing each device to have **multiple interrupt vectors** — up to 2,048. An NVMe SSD can assign one interrupt vector per I/O queue: queue 0 completion fires vector 42 on core 0, queue 1 completion fires vector 43 on core 1. No shared IRQs, no interrupt handler having to figure out "which queue was this for?"

```
Legacy:  Device → IRQ line → I/O APIC → LAPIC → CPU
         All queues share one IRQ → ISR must iterate all queues

MSI-X:   Device → PCIe memory write (address: 0xFEE00000 + vector*16) → LAPIC → CPU
         Queue 0 → vector 42 → core 0
         Queue 1 → vector 43 → core 1
         Each queue has a dedicated handler on a specific core — zero contention
```

This is how modern NVMe drives, 100 GbE NICs, and GPUs achieve millions of IOPS — independent interrupt vectors per hardware queue, routed to specific CPU cores.

---

## Interrupt Coalescing — Too Many Interrupts Is Also Bad

A 100 GbE NIC receiving minimum-size (64-byte) packets at line rate: 148.8 million packets per second. One interrupt per packet at 150 ns hardware latency per interrupt = 22 seconds of interrupt overhead per second. Impossible.

**Interrupt coalescing:** Batch interrupts. Wait until N packets arrive OR T microseconds elapse, THEN fire one interrupt.

```
No coalescing:  1,000,000 packets/sec → 1,000,000 interrupts/sec → CPU at 100%
Coalescing:      1,000,000 packets/sec → ~5,000 interrupts/sec   → CPU at 2%
```

The tradeoff: higher latency per packet. Game networking code typically sets low coalescing thresholds (maybe 5 packets or 50 µs); bulk data transfers use high thresholds (100 packets or 500 µs).

---

## UE's Tick System vs "Real" Interrupts

Unreal Engine's `FTickableGameObject::Tick()` is NOT an interrupt. It's a **polled callback** — the engine loop iterates over all tickable objects and calls `Tick()` every frame.

```cpp
class UMyComponent : public UActorComponent {
    virtual void TickComponent(float DeltaTime, 
                                ELevelTick TickType,
                                FActorComponentTickFunction* ThisTickFunction) override {
        // This is NOT an interrupt. It runs at a predictable time, every frame.
        // No hardware signaling. No context switch. Just a function call in the game thread.
        CheckForPlayerInput();  // Polls input state — the actual keyboard interrupt
                                 // already happened and queued the input data
    }
};
```

The actual input path: keyboard interrupt → OS ISR → OS input queue → engine's `FWindowsApplication::ProcessMessage()` (pumped in the engine loop) → player controller → your `TickComponent()`.

The interrupt happened microseconds after the key press. Your `Tick()` might run 10 ms later (next frame). This is the input latency chain — and why games feel "laggy" above ~50 ms end-to-end input latency.

---

## Exercises

1. **Interrupt path trace — keyboard to game:** Trace the complete path of a key press from physical contact to `TickComponent()` reading the input. At each step, identify: hardware vs software, latency (~ns or ~ms), and whether the step is interrupt-driven or polled. Where is the largest latency contributor? How could you reduce it?

2. **IDT entry decode:** Given the hex IDT entry `0x0010_0000_0000_816E` (simplified — real entries are 128 bits), decode: the gate type, the descriptor privilege level (DPL), and whether the interrupt gate is present. If DPL=0 and the CPU is in ring 3 (user mode) when the interrupt fires, what happens? Why can't user-mode code handle hardware interrupts directly?

3. **Interrupt storm simulation:** A buggy network driver's ISR reads a status register but forgets to acknowledge the interrupt (write to the interrupt-acknowledge register). The IRQ line stays asserted. Trace what happens: ISR returns → CPU re-enables interrupts → IRQ line still high → CPU immediately vectors to ISR again → infinite loop. How many cycles does one loop iteration take? If this runs on one core at 5 GHz, what percentage of that core is consumed? Can the OS detect and recover?

4. **MSI-X vector assignment:** Design the MSI-X configuration for a game server's 100 GbE NIC with 16 RX queues. Assign: (a) which CPU cores handle which queues (consider NUMA — you want each queue on a core local to its memory), (b) interrupt vectors per queue, (c) coalescing parameters (packet count and time threshold) for the game traffic queue vs the file transfer queue. Justify your choices with latency and throughput numbers.

5. **Tick vs interrupt latency analysis:** In UE5, input latency is the time from key press to the engine processing that input. At 60 FPS (16.7 ms per frame), the worst case is ~16.7 ms (key press right after the engine processed input, waits until next frame). Design a system that reduces this: could you use a hardware interrupt to wake the engine thread early? What are the tradeoffs? Why does UE poll input rather than using an interrupt-driven callback?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Interrupts are the CPU's doorbell. Without them, the CPU would have to stand at the door staring through the peephole, asking "anyone there?" a billion times per second — that's polling, and it wastes the entire CPU. With interrupts, the CPU can sit at its desk doing real work (running your game). When the keyboard has data, it rings the doorbell. The CPU puts down its pencil, writes a quick note about where it was (pushes RIP onto the stack), answers the door (runs the ISR), processes the keypress in microseconds, then returns to exactly where it left off. The doorbell system has layers: devices ring the I/O APIC (the apartment building's front desk), which routes the call to the correct CPU core's Local APIC (each apartment's personal doorbell). MSI/MSI-X is like giving each device its own dedicated ringtone so the CPU knows who's calling before answering. Interrupt coalescing is the doorman collecting parcels: instead of ringing for every single package, wait until there are 5 or 50 milliseconds have passed, then ring once. Timer interrupts are the CPU's alarm clock that wakes it every 4 ms to check if it's time to switch tasks — the OS scheduler depends on this.

### Technical
An interrupt is an asynchronous hardware signal that causes the CPU to save its current execution context (RFLAGS, CS, RIP pushed to stack) and vector to a handler via the IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table, 256 entries referenced by the IDTR register). Hardware interrupts originate from devices asserting IRQ lines → I/O APIC (maps IRQ to vector + destination LAPIC ID) → destination core's Local APIC (priority arbitration) → CPU. The ISR runs in interrupt context (interrupts disabled, no sleeping, no blocking); expensive work is deferred to bottom halves (softirqs/tasklets/workqueues). MSI-X replaces physical IRQ lines with PCIe memory writes to the LAPIC's address range (0xFEE00000), enabling per-queue interrupt vectors with zero routing contention. Interrupt coalescing reduces interrupt rate at the cost of per-packet latency by batching. The timer interrupt (programmable via LAPIC timer, typically 250 Hz on Linux) provides the scheduler heartbeat; tickless kernels suppress it when a core is idle. Exceptions (page faults, division by zero, breakpoints) are synchronous — caused by the current instruction — and use the same IDT mechanism. The interrupt flag (IF) in RFLAGS controls whether maskable interrupts are recognized; NMI (non-maskable interrupt) bypasses this. Interrupt latency from IRQ assertion to ISR first instruction is ~100-150 ns on modern hardware.

## See Also

- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — The CPU's interrupt handling microcode, privilege rings (ring 0 for ISR), and the IDTR register
- [[../System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics|Function Call Hardware]] — How `call`/`ret` differ from interrupt entry/exit (IRET)
- [[../System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel|Syscalls & Kernel]] — System calls as software interrupts (old `INT 0x80`) vs `SYSCALL`/`SYSENTER`
- [[../../C++/03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Why ISRs can't use the heap (interrupt context restrictions)
- [[Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]] — MSI-X and how NVMe exploits per-queue interrupt vectors

14 \u2014 Virtualization & Containers.content:
# Virtualization & Containers

> [!note] Why This Matters (Engine Context)
> Your game server runs on a VM in the cloud. Your build pipeline uses Docker containers for reproducible compilation. Your console devkit runs a hypervisor to switch between debug and retail OS images. Virtualization and containers are the invisible layer underneath modern game infrastructure. Understanding VT-x, extended page tables, VM exits, and cgroups isn't just "ops knowledge" — it explains why your server has 5% CPU stolen by the hypervisor, why Docker builds are faster on some machines, and what "bare metal" actually means for dedicated game servers.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — rings, privileged instructions, the instruction cycle.
> [[05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — page tables, TLB, virtual memory.

---

## The Problem — One Machine, Many OSes

A physical server has one set of hardware. Running multiple operating systems on it requires some way to share the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. Two approaches emerged:

- **Virtual Machines (VMs):** simulate complete virtual hardware. Each VM runs its own OS kernel. Strong isolation, heavier overhead.
- **Containers:** share the host OS kernel. Each container has its own filesystem, network, and process namespace. Lighter isolation, near-native performance.

---

## Hypervisors — The Virtual Machine Monitor

A hypervisor manages VMs. Two types:

### Type 1 (Bare-Metal)

Runs directly on hardware. No host OS underneath. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (when enabled as the root partition), Xen, KVM (Linux kernel module).

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VM 1 (Windows)  │  VM 2 (Linux)         │  ← Guest OSes
├──────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│              Hypervisor                   │  ← Runs on bare metal
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Physical Hardware               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Type 2 (Hosted)

Runs as an application on a host OS. Examples: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, QEMU (without KVM).

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VM 1              │  VM 2               │
├────────────────────┴─────────────────────┤
│          Hypervisor Application           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Host OS (Windows/Linux)          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Physical Hardware                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Game servers typically run on Type 1 (bare-metal KVM or Hyper-V) for minimal overhead. Dev workstations use Type 2 for testing.

---

## Hardware Virtualization — VT-x & AMD-V

Before hardware virtualization (pre-2006), x86 was difficult to virtualize because some privileged instructions behave differently in ring 0 vs ring 3 without trapping. VMware used binary translation — scanning guest code and rewriting problematic instructions.

**Intel VT-x (2005) and AMD-V (2006)** added hardware support:

- **VMX root mode:** the hypervisor runs here. Full control.
- **VMX non-root mode:** guest OSes run here. Certain operations trigger **VM exits**.
- **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure):** per-VM data structure controlling what causes VM exits.

### What Happens When a Guest Executes a Privileged Instruction

```
1. Guest OS (ring 0, non-root) executes: MOV CR3, new_page_table
   → This is a privileged instruction. In non-root mode, the CPU checks the VMCS.

2. VMCS says: "MOV CR3 causes VM exit" → CPU performs a VM EXIT:
   a. Saves guest CPU state to VMCS guest-state area
   b. Loads hypervisor state from VMCS host-state area
   c. Transitions to VMX root mode
   d. Jumps to hypervisor's VM exit handler

3. Hypervisor examines exit reason (EXIT_REASON_CR3_ACCESS):
   a. Validates: is this guest allowed to change page tables?
   b. If allowed: sets up shadow page tables or EPT
   c. Updates VMCS to point to the correct page tables

4. Hypervisor executes VMRESUME:
   a. CPU loads guest state from VMCS
   b. Transitions to VMX non-root mode
   c. Guest resumes at the instruction AFTER MOV CR3
```

**VM exit cost:** modern CPUs take ~500-1000 cycles for a round-trip VM exit + resume. A guest doing heavy I/O (many port I/O or MMIO accesses) can spend significant time in VM exits. This is why para-virtualized I/O drivers (virtio) batch operations to reduce exit frequency.

---

## Memory Virtualization — Extended Page Tables (EPT)

Without hardware support, the hypervisor must maintain **shadow page tables** — intercept every guest page table update and translate guest-physical to host-physical addresses. This is slow and complex.

**Intel EPT (Extended Page Tables) / AMD NPT (Nested Page Tables):** a second layer of address translation.

```
Guest Virtual Address
    │  Guest Page Table (managed by guest OS)
    ▼
Guest Physical Address (what the guest THINKS is physical memory)
    │  EPT / NPT (managed by hypervisor)
    ▼
Host Physical Address (actual DRAM location)
```

With EPT, the guest can modify its own page tables freely (no VM exit). The hardware walks BOTH the guest page table and the EPT on every TLB miss. A TLB miss now walks 4 guest levels + 4 EPT levels = up to 24 memory accesses in the worst case (4-level paging × 2). This is why TLB miss cost is higher in virtualized environments.

---

## Paravirtualization — The Guest Knows It's Virtualized

Instead of trapping every privileged instruction, **paravirtualization** modifies the guest OS to make explicit hypercalls:

```c
// Full virtualization: guest does MOV CR3 → VM exit → hypervisor handles it
// Paravirtualization: guest calls hypercall directly
hypercall(HC_UPDATE_PAGE_TABLE, new_table_phys_addr);
// No VM exit surprise — the guest KNOWS it's calling the hypervisor
```

Virtio drivers use this approach for disk and network I/O: the guest queues requests in shared memory rings and makes one hypercall to notify the hypervisor, rather than trapping on every I/O port access.

---

## Docker vs VMs — Containers Aren't Lightweight VMs

| Property | VM | Docker Container |
|----------|-----|------------------|
| OS kernel | Separate per VM | Shared with host |
| Isolation boundary | Hypervisor (hardware-enforced) | Linux kernel (cgroups, namespaces) |
| Startup time | Seconds (boot OS) | Milliseconds (create namespaces) |
| Memory overhead | ~200-500 MB (guest OS) | ~0 MB (shared kernel) |
| Security | Strong (VM escape is rare) | Weaker (kernel is shared — container escape is a kernel exploit) |
| GPU access | PCI passthrough or virtual GPU | Host GPU via device mapping |
| Disk image | GBs (full OS) | MBs (application + dependencies) |

### How Docker Works — cgroups + namespaces

Docker uses two Linux kernel features:

**Namespaces** — limit what a process can SEE:
- `PID namespace`: container sees its own process tree (PID 1 is the container's init)
- `NET namespace`: container has its own network interfaces and IP
- `MNT namespace`: container has its own filesystem root
- `UTS namespace`: container has its own hostname
- `IPC namespace`: container has its own System V IPC

**cgroups (Control Groups)** — limit what a process can USE:
- `cpu.shares`: proportional CPU allocation (container A gets 2× more CPU than B)
- `memory.limit_in_bytes`: hard memory cap (OOM killer triggered inside container)
- `blkio.throttle.read_bps`: disk I/O bandwidth limit

```
Container process
    │  PID namespace: sees PID 1
    │  NET namespace: sees eth0 with 172.17.0.5
    │  MNT namespace: sees / as its Docker image root
    │  cgroup: capped at 512 MB RAM
    ▼
Host kernel (shared) — one kernel, many containers
```

---

## GPU Virtualization — Gaming Relevance

GPU virtualization is challenging because GPUs have massive state (GBs of VRAM, thousands of hardware contexts). Approaches:

- **PCI Passthrough:** assign an entire physical GPU to one VM. Best performance, no sharing. Used for cloud gaming (one GPU = one player).
- **vGPU (NVIDIA GRID / AMD MxGPU):** partition GPU resources across multiple VMs. GPU driver in the hypervisor slices VRAM and compute units.
- **Paravirtualized GPU (virtio-gpu):** guest sends rendering commands to host via shared memory. Host GPU renders and sends back framebuffer.
- **Docker GPU access:** mount host GPU device into container. Container process talks directly to host GPU driver. Near-native performance.

UE's Swarm build system and render farm tools use Docker with GPU passthrough for distributed lightmass baking.

---

## The "Noisy Neighbor" Problem

In cloud environments, multiple VMs share physical hardware. A "noisy neighbor" VM doing heavy I/O or memory access can degrade other VMs on the same machine:

- **CPU steal time:** the hypervisor scheduler gives a vCPU fewer cycles than requested. Guest sees time passing but no progress — reported as `%steal` in `top`.
- **Cache pollution:** one VM's workload evicts another VM's data from shared L3 cache.
- **Memory bandwidth contention:** multiple VMs competing for the same memory channels on multi-socket systems.

Dedicated game servers (bare metal or single-tenant VMs) avoid the noisy neighbor problem entirely, at higher cost.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Virtualization is running a computer inside a computer. The hypervisor is the landlord — it owns the building (hardware) and rents out apartments (VMs) to tenants (guest OSes). Type 1 is the landlord living on-site in the basement. Type 2 is the landlord who also rents an apartment from a different landlord. Hardware virtualization (VT-x) gives the CPU a "landlord mode" and a "tenant mode" — when a tenant tries to do something landlord-only (change page tables), the CPU yanks them into landlord mode (VM exit), the landlord handles it (~500-1000 cycles), then puts the tenant back. Extended Page Tables (EPT) add a second address translation layer — the tenant manages their own page tables without bothering the landlord. Docker containers aren't apartments — they're room dividers in the landlord's own apartment. They share the same kitchen (kernel) but each has their own room (namespace) with a budget (cgroup). Containers share the kernel, so they're lighter but less isolated than VMs.

### Technical
A hypervisor manages VMs using hardware virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V). VT-x introduces VMX root (hypervisor) and non-root (guest) modes. Privileged operations in non-root mode trigger VM exits (~500-1000 cycle round-trip): the CPU saves guest state to VMCS, loads hypervisor state, and transfers control. Extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT) provide a second translation layer (guest virtual → guest physical → host physical), allowing guest-managed page tables without VM exits at the cost of doubling TLB miss latency (up to 24 memory accesses). Paravirtualization uses explicit hypercalls instead of trap-and-emulate. Docker uses Linux cgroups (resource limits) and namespaces (view isolation) to partition a shared kernel. GPU virtualization ranges from PCI passthrough (full GPU per VM) to vGPU partitioning to container device mounting. The noisy neighbor problem arises from shared resource contention (CPU steal, cache pollution, memory bandwidth) in multi-tenant environments.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** A VM exit costs 500-1000 cycles. If a guest OS executes a `CPUID` instruction (which always causes a VM exit) 1,000 times per second, what percentage of a 3 GHz vCPU is consumed by VM exits alone? Why does paravirtualization (using hypercalls instead of trap-and-emulate) reduce this overhead?

2. **Code:** Set up a Docker container limited to 256 MB RAM via `--memory=256m`. Write a C program that allocates 300 MB inside the container. What happens when it tries to touch the 257th MB? What kernel mechanism enforces the limit, and what signal does the process receive?

3. **Analysis:** EPT doubles the page walk from 4 to 24 memory accesses in the worst case. If each DRAM access costs 100 ns, what's the worst-case TLB miss penalty with EPT vs without? Your game VM's working set is 128 MB. With 4 KB pages, how many TLB entries are needed? Can the 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover the working set with 4 KB pages? With 2 MB huge pages?

4. **Real-world:** You're running a dedicated game server on a cloud VM. You notice 15% CPU steal time. What does this mean in practice — if your server needs 16.67 ms per frame, how much actual CPU time does it get? What cloud configuration option eliminates steal time entirely?

5. **Design:** Docker containers share the kernel. Design a container breakout attack: how could a process inside a container with `--privileged` flag access the host filesystem? What specific kernel feature (namespaces, cgroups, seccomp) prevents this in non-privileged containers?

## See Also

- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — Privilege rings (ring 0 vs ring 3) and why virtualization needs VMX root mode
- [[05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — TLB and cache behavior under EPT double translation
- [[07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]] — Page table walking that EPT adds a second layer to
- [[13 — Interrupts & Exceptions]] — How VM exits are a form of hardware exception

Hardware \u2014 Chapter Summary.content:
# Hardware — Chapter Summary

> [!info] Prerequisites
> You should have read the Hardware notes (01–16) before reading this summary. This note synthesizes everything — it's not a substitute for the individual notes.

## Why This Chapter Matters

Hardware is the foundation. Every line of C++ you write — every `std::vector`, every pointer dereference, every mutex lock — executes on real silicon. Understanding what happens at the transistor level isn't academic curiosity; it's how you predict performance, debug crashes, and avoid the traps that cost milliseconds at scale.

This chapter took you from a single MOSFET transistor all the way to a multi-core CPU executing billions of instructions per second. Here's what you learned.

---

## The Hardware Stack

| Layer | Notes | Key Insight |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **Physics** | 01 — Transistors & Logic Gates | NAND gates are universal — every computation reduces to them |
| **Numbers** | 02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness | Two's complement makes addition work for negatives; endianness determines byte order |
| **Assembly** | 03 — Assembly Language | The CPU's native language — registers, instructions, addressing modes |
| **CPU Core** | 04 — CPU Architecture | Pipeline, branch prediction, out-of-order execution, SIMD |
| **Memory** | 05 — Memory Hierarchy | Caches exploit locality; the memory wall is the defining performance constraint |
| **Allocation** | 06 — Memory Allocation | malloc/new → sbrk/mmap → kernel page allocator |
| **Virtual Memory** | 07 — VM Full Picture | Page tables map virtual→physical; TLB caches translations |
| **Storage** | 08 — RAID, Storage 00–04 | From spinning rust to NVMe — the I/O gap and how the OS papers over it |
| **I/O** | 09 — DMA & Bus Mastering | Devices read/write RAM without CPU involvement |
| **Networking** | 10 — TCP/UDP/Sockets | The kernel's network stack: NIC → driver → protocol → socket |
| **Boot** | 11 — Boot Process | BIOS/UEFI → bootloader → kernel → init |
| **GPU** | 12 — GPU Architecture | Massively parallel SIMT — thousands of cores, warps/wavefronts |
| **Interrupts** | 13 — Interrupts & Exceptions | Hardware signals that preempt the CPU, enabling I/O without polling |
| **Virtualization** | 14 — Virtualization & Containers | VT-x/EPT, Docker namespaces/cgroups, GPU passthrough |
| **Drivers** | 15 — Kernel Modules & Drivers | The glue between generic kernel APIs and specific hardware |
| **Security** | 16 — Hardware Security | Spectre, Meltdown, Rowhammer — the hardware itself is the attack surface |

---

## The Two Big Ideas

### 1. Latency Is Everything

| Operation | Latency | CPU Cycles (3 GHz) |
|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| L1 cache hit | ~1 ns | 3 |
| L2 cache hit | ~4 ns | 12 |
| L3 cache hit | ~12 ns | 36 |
| DRAM access | ~100 ns | 300 |
| NVMe read | ~50 µs | 150,000 |
| HDD seek | ~10 ms | 30,000,000 |

Every level of the memory hierarchy exists to hide the next level's latency. Caches, prefetchers, TLBs, branch predictors — all of them are latency-hiding mechanisms. The CPU can execute 400+ instructions while waiting for one DRAM access.

### 2. Everything Is an Abstraction Over Physics

- Virtual memory is an abstraction over DRAM pages + disk blocks
- A filesystem is an abstraction over raw NAND pages in an SSD
- A TCP connection is an abstraction over unreliable IP packets
- A thread is an abstraction over a CPU core + register state
- A GPU draw call is an abstraction over thousands of SIMT threads

Understanding the layer below tells you what can go wrong — and how to measure it.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Your computer is a tower of translators. At the bottom: transistors switching on and off, forming logic gates. One level up: those gates become an ALU and registers — a CPU. Above that: the CPU runs assembly instructions. Above that: a compiler translates C++ into assembly. Above that: the OS manages memory, files, and networks. Above that: your game engine. At every level, the layer below is "just" an implementation detail — until it's not. When your frame drops from 60 to 2 FPS, it's because one of those lower layers hit a limit. Understanding hardware means knowing exactly which layer is the bottleneck and why.

### Technical

The modern computing stack is a hierarchy of state machines, each implemented on top of the one below. Transistors (FETs) implement CMOS logic gates which implement sequential circuits (flip-flops, registers) which implement the CPU datapath and control unit. The ISA (x86-64, ARM64) defines the architectural state (registers, flags, memory model) that the microarchitecture must maintain. The microarchitecture (pipeline, OoO engine, cache hierarchy) implements the ISA with speculative execution and caching to hide memory latency. The OS kernel (ring 0) virtualizes the CPU (threads), memory (page tables + swap), and I/O (drivers + interrupts). User-space (ring 3) builds on syscalls: compilers (LLVM/GCC) translate high-level languages through IR → assembly → machine code; linkers resolve symbols into ELF/PE executables; the dynamic linker (ld.so) resolves shared library symbols at load time via PLT/GOT. Game engines sit at the top of this stack but must understand every layer: cache misses cause frame drops, TLB misses stall the render thread, page faults during asset streaming hitch gameplay, and GPU driver bugs crash the entire system.

---

## Exercises

1. **Cross-layer diagnosis:** Your game drops from 60 FPS to 15 FPS during a loading screen. List every layer of the hardware stack and identify which layers could be the bottleneck. For each, describe what measurement tool you'd use (`perf stat`, `iostat`, `strace`, GPU profiler, etc.) and what the diagnosis would look like.

2. **Latency budgeting:** A frame at 60 FPS has a 16.67 ms budget. Allocate this budget across: CPU game logic (3 ms), rendering command generation (4 ms), GPU rendering (8 ms), and OS overhead (1.67 ms). If a single DRAM access takes 100 ns and your game loop makes 5×10⁷ memory accesses, how many cache misses can you afford before blowing the CPU budget? Assume L3 miss = 100 ns DRAM, L3 hit = 12 ns.

3. **The stack in reverse:** Trace a single GPU draw call (`DrawIndexedPrimitive`) from the game engine down through the hardware stack. Start in UE's RHI layer, through the UMD, through the KMD, through the PCIe bus, into the GPU's command processor. At each layer, what information is added or stripped?

4. **Virtual memory walk:** You allocate a 1 GB array with `new`. Walk through every hardware and OS layer involved: the C++ `new` operator, malloc's free-list search, the sbrk/mmap syscall, the kernel's VMA creation, the page fault on first access, the TLB miss, the page table walk, and the DRAM row activation. How many total steps from `new` to first byte read?

5. **Design:** You're building a profiling tool that measures per-frame hardware counters. Which PMU (Performance Monitoring Unit) events would you track to correlate FPS drops with hardware bottlenecks? List 5 specific events (e.g., `LLC-load-misses`, `dtlb-load-misses`) and explain what each tells you.

## See Also

- [[../From Transistor to Running Program]] — The full-stack map connecting all chapters
- [[../System Software/System Software — Chapter Summary]] — The software side of the stack
- [[../Concurrency/Concurrency — Chapter Summary]] — Multi-core and synchronization
- [[Learning Path]] — The recommended reading order

00 \u2014 The Internet Stack.content:
# Networking 00 — The Internet Stack

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[../01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] — bits, bytes, and endianness for headers.
> [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — transport layer overview (read first for context).

## Why This Matters

Every time you browse a website, the data passes through at least 5 layers of protocols before it reaches the server and 5 more coming back. Understanding what happens at each layer — and where the overhead goes — is how you diagnose latency, packet loss, and why "the internet is slow."

---

## The Two Models

### OSI Model (7 layers — academic reference)

| Layer | Name | What It Carries | Example |
|-------|------|----------------|---------|
| 7 | Application | Your data | HTTP, DNS, game packets |
| 6 | Presentation | Data format | TLS encryption, JSON, FArchive |
| 5 | Session | Connection state | TCP connection, TLS session |
| 4 | Transport | Segments/datagrams | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Packets | IP (IPv4, IPv6) |
| 2 | Data Link | Frames | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11) |
| 1 | Physical | Bits on wire | Copper, fiber, radio |

### TCP/IP Model (4 layers — practical)

| Layer | OSI Equivalent | Protocols |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| Application | 5-7 | HTTP, DNS, TLS, SSH, game protocols |
| Transport | 4 | TCP, UDP, QUIC |
| Internet | 3 | IP, ICMP, ARP |
| Link | 1-2 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |

---

## Encapsulation: The Packet Journey

When your game sends "PlayerPosition(x=100, y=200)" to the server:

```
Application:  [Game Data: "x=100,y=200"]
                  │  TLS encrypts
                  ▼
Application:  [TLS Record: encrypted bytes]
                  │  TCP segments
                  ▼
Transport:    [TCP Header | Data]
                  │  IP encapsulates
                  ▼
Network:      [IP Header | TCP Header | Data]
                  │  Ethernet frames
                  ▼
Link:         [Eth Header | IP Header | TCP Header | Data | Eth Trailer]
                  │  Physical transmission
                  ▼
Physical:     1010110100101110... (electrical/optical/radio signals)
```

At the receiving end, each layer strips its header and passes the payload up. This is why a 100-byte game message can become 154 bytes on the wire (20 IP + 20 TCP + 14 Ethernet headers).

---

## What Each Layer Adds (Overhead)

| Layer | Header Size | Notes |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| Ethernet | 14 bytes + 4 FCS | Destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType |
| IP (v4) | 20-60 bytes | Source/dest IP, TTL, protocol, checksum |
| TCP | 20-60 bytes | Source/dest port, seq, ack, flags, window |
| UDP | 8 bytes | Source/dest port, length, checksum (optional) |
| TLS | 5 bytes + MAC + padding | TLS record header, authentication tag |

Minimal overhead for a TCP packet: 14 + 20 + 20 = **54 bytes**. For a 20-byte game update, that's 270% overhead. This is why games batch updates — sending one 1400-byte packet is far more efficient than 70 20-byte packets.

---

## The Socket API — How Your Program Talks to the Stack

```c
// Create a socket — ask the kernel for a network endpoint
int sock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);  // AF_INET = IPv4, SOCK_STREAM = TCP

// Bind to a port
struct sockaddr_in addr = {.sin_family = AF_INET, .sin_port = htons(8080)};
bind(sock, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));

// TCP: listen and accept
listen(sock, 5);
int client = accept(sock, NULL, NULL);  // Blocks until connection

// UDP: just send and receive
sendto(sock, data, len, 0, (struct sockaddr*)&dest, sizeof(dest));
recvfrom(sock, buf, sizeof(buf), 0, NULL, NULL);
```

Under the hood, the kernel manages everything below the socket: TCP state machine, congestion control, IP routing, ARP resolution, NIC driver interaction. Your program only sees the socket file descriptor.

---

## The Kernel's Network Stack

```
User Space:    Your game → sendto(fd, buf, len) → syscall
                   │
Kernel Space:      ▼
              Socket layer: find the socket, check permissions
                   │
              Transport (TCP/UDP): segment data, manage state
                   │
              Network (IP): add IP header, route lookup, fragment if needed
                   │
              Netfilter (iptables/nftables): firewall rules, NAT
                   │
              Link layer: ARP lookup, add Ethernet header
                   │
              Device driver: DMA to NIC TX ring buffer
                   │
Hardware:     NIC → PHY → cable/wireless
```

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

The internet is a postal system with multiple layers of envelopes. Your game writes a letter (game data). The TCP layer puts it in an envelope with "Letter #42" written on it (sequence number) so the receiver can reassemble them in order. The IP layer puts that in another envelope with the destination address (IP). The Ethernet layer puts THAT in yet another envelope with the immediate next-hop address (MAC). Each router along the way opens the Ethernet envelope, reads the IP address, puts it in a new Ethernet envelope addressed to the next hop, and forwards it. When it arrives at the server, the envelopes are opened in reverse, and the original letter is delivered. If an envelope goes missing, TCP notices (the sequence numbers have a gap) and asks for a resend.

### Technical

The Berkeley Sockets API (`socket`/`bind`/`connect`/`send`/`recv`) is the user-space interface to the kernel's networking subsystem. A socket is associated with a protocol family (`AF_INET`/`AF_INET6`), type (`SOCK_STREAM` for TCP, `SOCK_DGRAM` for UDP), and protocol (0 for default). The kernel maintains a `struct sock` for each socket containing send/receive buffers (`sk_buff` queues), protocol state (TCP control block with seq/ack numbers, congestion window, RTT estimator), and a pointer to the protocol handler (`tcp_prot`/`udp_prot`). On `send()`, data is copied from user space into `sk_buff` structures (socket buffers) which traverse the stack: transport layer adds protocol header → network layer adds IP header and performs route lookup (`fib_lookup`) → netfilter hooks (`NF_INET_LOCAL_OUT`) → neighbour subsystem performs ARP resolution → device driver enqueues the sk_buff onto the NIC's TX ring via DMA. Reception is the reverse: NIC RX interrupt → NAPI polling (`net_rx_action`) → device driver delivers sk_buff → netfilter hooks (`NF_INET_LOCAL_IN`) → IP layer reassembles fragments → transport layer processes protocol (TCP: ACK generation, out-of-order queue, deliver to socket receive queue) → application reads via `recv()`. The `SO_RCVBUF`/`SO_SNDBUF` socket options control buffer sizes, directly affecting throughput via the bandwidth-delay product.

---

## Exercises

1. **Packet overhead:** Your game sends 60 updates per second at 30 bytes each over TCP. Calculate the total bandwidth usage including all headers (Ethernet + IP + TCP). What if you batch into 20 updates per packet (600 bytes data per packet)? What's the bandwidth savings percentage?

2. **Trace a send:** Write a C program that creates a TCP socket, connects to a server, and sends "hello". Use `strace` to trace every syscall. Use `tcpdump -i lo -X` to capture the actual packets. Label every header field in the captured output.

3. **Bandwidth-delay product:** A 1 Gbps link with 50 ms RTT has a BDP of 6.25 MB. If your socket's `SO_RCVBUF` is 256 KB, what's the maximum throughput you can achieve? How big does the buffer need to be to fill the pipe?

4. **Kernel bypass:** Traditional networking copies data user→kernel→DMA. Technologies like DPDK and io_uring bypass the kernel stack. Research DPDK: how does an application get raw access to the NIC? When would you use this for a game server?

5. **Design:** Your game uses UDP for position updates (60 Hz, 30 bytes each) and TCP for chat messages (sporadic, up to 500 bytes). Design the socket setup: should both use separate sockets? What port numbering scheme? How does the server demultiplex incoming packets?

## See Also

- [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — The transport layer overview
- [[01 — IP, Subnets & Routing]] — The network layer underneath
- [[../04 — CPU Architecture]] — How the NIC interrupts the CPU
- [[../13 — Interrupts & Exceptions]] — NIC interrupt handling

01 \u2014 IP, Subnets & Routing.content:
# Networking 01 — IP, Subnets & Routing

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[00 — The Internet Stack]] — the OSI model and encapsulation.
> [[../01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] — binary and bit manipulation for subnet masks.

## Why This Matters

Every packet on the internet has a source and destination IP address. How do those addresses get assigned? How does a packet from your computer in Tokyo find a server in Frankfurt through dozens of intermediate routers? And why does your home router have an address like `192.168.1.1` while Google is `142.250.80.46`?

---

## IPv4 Addressing

An IPv4 address is a **32-bit number**, usually written as four decimal octets:

```
142.250.80.46  =  10001110.11111010.01010000.00101110
```

That's 2³² = ~4.3 billion possible addresses. We ran out in 2011 — enter NAT and IPv6.

---

## Subnetting and CIDR

A subnet mask determines which part of an IP address is the **network** and which is the **host**:

```
IP:      192.168.  1.100
Mask:    255.255.255.  0   (or /24 in CIDR notation)
         └──network──┘ └host┘

Network: 192.168.1.0
Host:              .100
Broadcast: 192.168.1.255
```

CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation: `192.168.1.0/24` means "the first 24 bits are the network prefix." A `/24` network has 256 addresses (254 usable — .0 is network, .255 is broadcast).

| CIDR | Mask | Addresses | Use Case |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| /8 | 255.0.0.0 | 16,777,216 | ISP allocation |
| /16 | 255.255.0.0 | 65,536 | Large enterprise |
| /24 | 255.255.255.0 | 256 | Home/small office |
| /32 | 255.255.255.255 | 1 | Single host |

---

## Private vs Public Addresses

These ranges are reserved for **private networks** — they are NOT routable on the public internet:

| Range | CIDR | Use |
|-------|------|-----|
| 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 | 10.0.0.0/8 | Enterprise |
| 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 | 172.16.0.0/12 | Medium |
| 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 | 192.168.0.0/16 | Home |

Your home router gets ONE public IP from your ISP. All your devices share it via **NAT** (Network Address Translation).

---

## NAT — How 20 Devices Share One IP

```
Your PC (192.168.1.5)                           Google (142.250.80.46)
       │                                               │
       │  src=192.168.1.5:54321, dst=142.250.80.46:443 │
       ▼                                               │
   Home Router ────── rewrites src ──────────────►     │
   (Public IP: 203.0.113.10)                            │
       │  src=203.0.113.10:60001, dst=142.250.80.46:443│
       │                                               │
       │         ◄────── response to 203.0.113.10:60001│
       ▼                                               │
   Router looks up port 60001 → maps to 192.168.1.5:54321
```

The router maintains a NAT table:

| Internal | External | Destination |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| 192.168.1.5:54321 | 203.0.113.10:60001 | 142.250.80.46:443 |
| 192.168.1.7:12345 | 203.0.113.10:60002 | 8.8.8.8:53 |

This is why you can't host a game server behind NAT without port forwarding — incoming connections have no NAT table entry.

---

## Routing — How Packets Find Their Way

Routers use **routing tables** to decide where to forward each packet:

```
Destination     Gateway         Interface    Metric
0.0.0.0/0       203.0.113.1     eth0         10      ← default route
192.168.1.0/24  0.0.0.0         eth1         0       ← local network
10.0.0.0/8      192.168.1.254   eth1         100
```

Longest prefix match wins. When your PC sends a packet to `142.250.80.46`:
1. Check: does 142.250.80.46 match 192.168.1.0/24? No (142 ≠ 192).
2. Check: does it match 10.0.0.0/8? No (142 ≠ 10).
3. Default route 0.0.0.0/0 matches everything → send to gateway 203.0.113.1.

Between ISPs, routers exchange routes via **BGP** (Border Gateway Protocol). The global BGP table contains ~900,000 routes and grows daily.

---

## TTL — Preventing Infinite Loops

Every IP packet has a TTL (Time To Live) field, decremented by each router:

```
TTL=64: Your PC sends
TTL=63: Home router
TTL=62: ISP router 1
...
TTL=1:  Router N
TTL=0:  DROP — send ICMP "Time Exceeded" back to sender
```

This prevents packets from looping forever due to routing misconfiguration. `traceroute` exploits this: send packets with TTL=1,2,3,... and collect the ICMP Time Exceeded messages to map the path.

---

## IPv6 — The Future (That's Been Coming for 20 Years)

IPv6 uses **128-bit addresses**: `2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334`. That's 2¹²⁸ ≈ 3.4×10³⁸ addresses — enough for every atom on Earth to have its own IP.

Key differences from IPv4:
- No NAT needed (every device gets a globally routable address)
- Simplified header (40 bytes fixed vs 20-60 variable)
- No checksum (delegated to lower/upper layers)
- Stateless auto-configuration (SLAAC)
- Mandatory IPsec support

Game servers increasingly support IPv6-only deployments. UE5's socket API handles both via `FIPv4Endpoint` / `FIPv6Endpoint`.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

IP addresses are like postal addresses for computers. A subnet is a neighborhood — all houses on "Elm Street" share the same street name (network prefix), with different house numbers (host). NAT is like an apartment building with one street address — the mail room (router) sorts packages by apartment number (port). Routing is how the post office decides which truck to put your letter on — each post office has a table saying "mail for Europe goes to Truck 3, mail for Asia goes to Truck 7." The TTL is a "return after N failed delivery attempts" stamp — each post office decrements it, and when it hits zero, the letter is returned with "undeliverable."

### Technical

IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses with classless addressing (CIDR, RFC 4632). A subnet mask (e.g., `/24` = `255.255.255.0`) is logically ANDed with the destination IP to extract the network prefix. The kernel's routing table (`ip route show`) is a radix tree (`fib_trie`) indexed by prefix length, using longest-prefix-match (LPM) lookup — O(key_length) = O(32) for IPv4. NAT (specifically NAPT — Network Address and Port Translation) rewrites the source IP and source port in the IP and TCP/UDP headers, maintaining a connection tracking table (`nf_conntrack`). Port forwarding adds a DNAT rule to the netfilter PREROUTING chain, rewriting the destination IP of incoming packets. BGP (RFC 4271) is a path-vector protocol: ASes (Autonomous Systems) advertise prefix reachability with AS_PATH attributes; routers apply policy filters and select the best path based on LOCAL_PREF, AS_PATH length, and MED. IPv6 (RFC 8200) uses 128-bit addresses, a simplified fixed 40-byte header (no header checksum, no fragmentation in routers), extension headers for optional features, and SLAAC (RFC 4862) for stateless address configuration via Router Advertisement messages. Dual-stack deployment runs IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously; the `getaddrinfo()` resolver can return both A (IPv4) and AAAA (IPv6) records.

---

## Exercises

1. **Subnet calculation:** You have the network `10.20.0.0/16`. You need subnets that can each hold 500 hosts. What subnet mask should you use? How many subnets can you create? List the network address, first usable host, last usable host, and broadcast for the first 3 subnets.

2. **NAT traversal:** Design a protocol for two players behind NAT to connect for a peer-to-peer game. Research STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT) and ICE (Interactive Connectivity Establishment). Why does a central server (STUN) help, and what are the cases where P2P still fails?

3. **Routing trace:** Run `traceroute 8.8.8.8` (or `tracert 8.8.8.8` on Windows). Count the hops. What are the first 3 hops? Why is the first hop your home router, the second your ISP, and subsequent hops in different cities/countries? Identify each hop's likely location by its hostname.

4. **BGP hijack:** In 2018, a misconfigured BGP advertisement from a Nigerian ISP briefly routed Google's traffic through China and Russia. Explain how BGP enables this — why is there no cryptographic verification that a prefix advertisement is legitimate? Research RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure).

5. **Design:** Your game server cluster spans 3 data centers (US-East, EU-West, Asia-Pacific). Each has a `/24` public IP block. Design the DNS and routing to direct players to the nearest data center. How does GeoDNS work? What happens if a data center goes offline?

## See Also

- [[00 — The Internet Stack]] — How IP fits into the full stack
- [[02 — TCP Deep Dive]] — The transport layer on top of IP
- [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — Original networking overview

02 \u2014 TCP Deep Dive.content:
# Networking 02 — TCP Deep Dive

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[00 — The Internet Stack]] — the OSI model and TCP's place in it.
> [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — basic TCP overview.

## Why This Matters

TCP is the workhorse of the internet — every web page, every file download, every chat message uses it. But TCP was designed in 1974 for a network with kilobit speeds and millisecond latencies. Today's internet has gigabit speeds and transcontinental round-trips of 200 ms. TCP's congestion control algorithms have evolved dramatically to keep up, and understanding them explains why your download starts slow, speeds up, then occasionally stalls.

---

## The TCP State Machine

Every TCP connection goes through a well-defined lifecycle:

```
                    +---------+  active OPEN
                    |  CLOSED |──────────────┐
                    +---------+               │
                      │  passive OPEN         │
                      ▼                       ▼
                  +---------+            +---------+
                  |  LISTEN |            | SYN_SENT|
                  +---------+            +---------+
                      │  recv SYN            │ recv SYN+ACK
                      ▼                       ▼
                  +-----------+          +-----------+
                  | SYN_RCVD  |          |ESTABLISHED|
                  +-----------+          +-----------+
                      │ recv ACK              │
                      ▼                       │
                  +-----------+               │
                  |ESTABLISHED|◄──────────────┘
                  +-----------+
                      │  close / recv FIN
                      ▼
```
