                  +-----------+
                  | FIN_WAIT1 |  /  CLOSE_WAIT
                  +-----------+
                      │
                      ▼
                  +-----------+
                  | TIME_WAIT |  (2×MSL = 60-120 seconds)
                  +-----------+
                      │
                      ▼
                    CLOSED
```

The `TIME_WAIT` state exists to ensure any delayed duplicate segments from the old connection don't interfere with a new connection using the same ports. This is why you can't immediately rebind a port after closing a server — you need `SO_REUSEADDR`.

---

## Reliability Mechanisms

### 1. Sequence Numbers and Acknowledgments

Every byte has a 32-bit sequence number. The receiver acknowledges the next expected byte:

```
Sender: SEQ=1000, LEN=500    (bytes 1000-1499)
Receiver: ACK=1500            ("I got everything up to byte 1499")
```

If ACK doesn't arrive within RTO, sender retransmits. RTO is calculated dynamically:

```
SRTT = (1 - α) × SRTT + α × RTT_sample       (α = 1/8, smoothed RTT)
RTTVAR = (1 - β) × RTTVAR + β × |SRTT - RTT_sample|  (β = 1/4)
RTO = SRTT + max(G, 4 × RTTVAR)               (G = clock granularity)
```

### 2. Selective Acknowledgment (SACK)

Without SACK, a single lost segment causes retransmission of everything after it (Go-Back-N). With SACK, the receiver can say "I got 1000-1499 and 2000-2499, missing 1500-1999" — only the missing segment is retransmitted.

### 3. Fast Retransmit

If the sender receives 3 duplicate ACKs (same ACK number repeated), it assumes the next segment was lost and retransmits immediately — no need to wait for RTO.

```
Sender sends:   [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]
                     lost!
Receiver ACKs:      ACK 1, ACK 1, ACK 1  ← 3 duplicate ACKs
Sender:             retransmit [2] immediately
```

---

## Congestion Control — The Algorithms

Congestion control prevents senders from overwhelming the network. The sender maintains a **congestion window** (cwnd) — maximum unacknowledged bytes in flight.

### Classic: Reno / CUBIC (Loss-based)

```
Slow Start:        cwnd *= 2 every RTT  (exponential)
Threshold reached: switch to Congestion Avoidance
Congestion Avoidance: cwnd += 1 every RTT (linear)
On packet loss:    cwnd /= 2, enter Fast Recovery
```

### Modern: BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip — Google, 2016)

Instead of reacting to loss, BBR **models the pipe**:

```
ProbeBW:  periodically increase pacing rate to measure available bandwidth
ProbeRTT: periodically reduce to minimum to measure base RTT
Goal:      operate at the optimal point — full bandwidth, minimal queue
```

BBR doesn't require packet loss to slow down — it detects queue buildup from increasing RTT. On paths with bufferbloat (excessive router buffering), BBR can achieve 10-100× better throughput than CUBIC.

---

## Nagle's Algorithm and Delayed ACK — The 200 ms Trap

Nagle's algorithm: don't send a small segment if there's unacknowledged data. Wait until the previous data is ACK'd or you have a full MSS (Maximum Segment Size).

Delayed ACK: the receiver waits up to 200 ms (typically 40 ms on Linux) before sending an ACK, hoping to piggyback it on response data.

**Together, they create the 200 ms stall:**

```
Sender: sends small segment
Receiver: waits 40 ms for data to piggyback ACK on... no data... sends ACK (40 ms lost)
Sender: receives ACK, sends next small segment
Receiver: waits again...
```

**Fix:** Set `TCP_NODELAY` on game sockets to disable Nagle's algorithm. Every byte is sent immediately.

---

## Head-of-Line Blocking

TCP delivers data **in order** to the application. If segment #5 is lost, segments #6-10 sit in the kernel's out-of-order queue until #5 is retransmitted. This is **head-of-line blocking** — one lost packet stalls the entire stream.

This is why HTTP/3 uses QUIC (built on UDP) instead of TCP — QUIC multiplexes independent streams so a lost packet on one stream doesn't block others.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

TCP is like a tracked delivery service. You're shipping boxes labeled #1, #2, #3... The receiver signs for each box. If box #2 goes missing, the receiver keeps signing for box #1, saying "I still need box #2!" After three such complaints, you resend box #2 immediately — no need to wait for the standard reorder timer. Congestion control is like adjusting your shipping rate: if boxes start going missing (packet loss), you slow down. If delivery is smooth, you gradually speed up. BBR is smarter: instead of waiting for lost boxes, it measures delivery time — if boxes start taking longer (queue building up in a router), it slows down BEFORE boxes get lost. Nagle's algorithm is like waiting to fill a full shipping container before sending — efficient for bulk, terrible for real-time. `TCP_NODELAY` says "send each box as soon as it's packed."

### Technical

TCP (RFC 9293) provides a reliable, ordered, byte-stream abstraction over IP. The sender maintains: `snd_una` (oldest unacknowledged byte), `snd_nxt` (next byte to send), and `snd_wnd` (receiver's advertised window). The effective window = min(cwnd, rwnd). Sequence numbers wrap around via 32-bit modular arithmetic with PAWS (Protection Against Wrapped Sequences) using timestamps. Retransmission timeout: Jacobson/Karels algorithm computes RTO from smoothed RTT (SRTT) and RTT variance, with a minimum of 200 ms (Linux default, tunable via `tcp_rto_min`). SACK (RFC 2018) uses TCP options to report non-contiguous blocks of received data. CUBIC (Linux default since 2.6.19) uses a cubic function for window growth: `W(t) = C(t - K)³ + W_max`, where K = ³√(W_max × β / C), making it more aggressive on high-BDP paths. BBR v2 models the delivery rate and RTT via a state machine (Startup, Drain, ProbeBW, ProbeRTT), pacing packets at the estimated bottleneck bandwidth to avoid building queues. DCTCP (Data Center TCP) uses ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) marking instead of loss for congestion feedback, achieving near-zero queue occupancy in datacenter networks. `TCP_NODELAY` (`setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_TCP, TCP_NODELAY, &one, sizeof(one))`) disables Nagle's algorithm; `TCP_QUICKACK` disables delayed ACK on the receiver side. The combination is essential for sub-millisecond game networking.

---

## Exercises

1. **RTO calculation:** You measure RTT samples: 10 ms, 12 ms, 50 ms (spike), 11 ms, 13 ms. Compute SRTT, RTTVAR, and RTO after each sample. When does RTO spike above 200 ms? How does the Jacobson/Karels algorithm smooth out the 50 ms outlier?

2. **BBR vs CUBIC:** Research a benchmark comparing BBR and CUBIC on a 100 Mbps link with 50 ms RTT and 0.1% packet loss. Under CUBIC, what's the maximum throughput? (Hint: Mathis equation: `Rate ≤ MSS/(RTT × √loss)`). What does BBR achieve and why?

3. **Nagle stall reproduction:** Write a client-server pair. The client sends 100 1-byte writes in a loop with Nagle enabled (default). The server reads and echoes. Measure round-trip latency. Now set `TCP_NODELAY` on both sides and measure again. Explain the difference.

4. **HOL blocking:** Your game sends three independent streams over one TCP connection: player positions (60 Hz), chat messages (sporadic), and file transfer (bulk, low priority). A packet is lost on the file transfer stream. What happens to the player position updates? How would QUIC (HTTP/3) solve this?

5. **Design:** Design a reliable message protocol on top of UDP for your game. You need: ordered delivery for critical events (RPCs), unordered delivery for position updates, and reliability only for RPCs. What sequence numbering and acknowledgment scheme do you use? Compare to ENet and RakNet.

## See Also

- [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — Original TCP overview
- [[03 — UDP & Game Protocols]] — What games use instead of TCP
- [[01 — IP, Subnets & Routing]] — The layer underneath TCP

03 \u2014 UDP & Game Protocols.content:
# Networking 03 — UDP & Game Protocols

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — TCP vs UDP overview.
> [[02 — TCP Deep Dive]] — understand why TCP's guarantees hurt real-time performance.

## Why This Matters

TCP guarantees reliability and ordering. Games don't want either — at least not unconditionally. A position update from 50 ms ago is worse than useless (it's misleading). A retransmitted packet arriving late causes more harm than a dropped packet. This is why every multiplayer game engine — Unreal, Unity, Source, id Tech — uses UDP at the transport layer and builds reliability on top only where needed.

---

## UDP — The Anti-TCP

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Connection | Yes (3-way handshake) | No (connectionless) |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | In-order | Any order |
| Flow control | Yes (rwnd) | No |
| Congestion control | Yes (cwnd) | No |
| Header size | 20-60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Head-of-line blocking | Yes | No |
| Use case | Web, files, chat | Games, VoIP, DNS, streaming |

UDP is a thin wrapper around IP. It adds ports and an optional checksum. That's it. Everything else — reliability, ordering, flow control — is YOUR problem. And that's the point.

---

## Why Games Use UDP

### 1. Old Data Is Useless

If a "PlayerPosition(x=100)" packet is lost, you don't want it retransmitted — the player is now at x=105. A late retransmission would cause the player to snap backward. Games just send the next update.

### 2. Ordering Is Optional

Position updates don't need to arrive in order. If packet #42 (x=100) arrives after packet #43 (x=105), just ignore #42 — it's stale. For RPCs ("CastSpell(fireball)"), ordering matters, but you can implement it selectively.

### 3. No Head-of-Line Blocking

On TCP, one lost packet blocks everything behind it. On UDP, each packet is independent. If a position update is lost, the next one still arrives immediately.

### 4. No Congestion Control Fighting

TCP's congestion control reduces sending rate on packet loss. In a game, packet loss is normal (Wi-Fi interference, congested routers). TCP would throttle your game into unplayability. UDP just keeps sending.

---

## Building Reliability on Top of UDP

Game engines implement custom reliability layers:

### Pattern 1: Sequence Numbers + ACK

```
Each packet: [seq_number | flags | payload]
Flags: RELIABLE, UNRELIABLE, ORDERED

Sender:                       Receiver:
  send(seq=1, RELIABLE, data)   → receive, send ACK(1)
  send(seq=2, UNRELIABLE, pos)  → receive, no ACK needed
  send(seq=3, RELIABLE, data)   → lost
  (no ACK for 3 after N ms)     → resend seq=3
```

### Pattern 2: Sliding Window (for large transfers)

When sending map data or assets, you need throughput. A simple stop-and-wait (send one, wait for ACK) wastes bandwidth. A sliding window sends multiple packets before waiting:

```
Window = 8 packets
Send seq 1-8 → ACK 1-4 → slide window → send 9-12 → ACK 5-8 → ...
```

### Pattern 3: Redundancy (FEC — Forward Error Correction)

Send extra parity packets so the receiver can reconstruct lost data without retransmission:

```
Data:    [D1] [D2] [D3]
Parity:  [D1 XOR D2 XOR D3]

If D2 is lost: D1 XOR D3 XOR Parity = D2 recovered
```

Cost: extra bandwidth. Benefit: zero latency on recovery — no waiting for retransmission.

---

## Real-World Game Protocols

| Protocol | Games | Features |
|----------|-------|----------|
| **UE Replication** | Fortnite, Gears | Built-in: reliable RPCs, unreliable properties, relevancy, prioritization |
| **ENet** | Minecraft Bedrock | Channels (ordered/unordered), reliable/unreliable, fragmentation |
| **RakNet** | Unity (deprecated) | NAT punchthrough, patched reliability layers |
| **Steam Datagram Relay** | Dota 2, CS2 | Valve's relay network, SDR encryption, routing around congestion |
| **QUIC** | Future games? | HTTP/3's transport: streams (no HOL blocking), 0-RTT, built-in TLS |

---

## The Latency vs Reliability Tradeoff

```
Reliability ───────────────────────────────────────►
              │                    │            │
              │   TCP              │  Custom    │  UDP raw
              │   (web, files)     │  RUDP      │  (position
              │                    │  (RPCs)    │   updates)
              │                    │            │
              ▼                    ▼            ▼
Latency:    high (retransmits)  medium       zero overhead
```

The key insight: **different data needs different guarantees.** A single UDP socket can carry both reliable RPCs and unreliable position updates by demultiplexing on a per-packet flag.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

UDP is like throwing paper airplanes across the room. Some get there, some don't, they might arrive out of order, and nobody acknowledges receipt. TCP is like handing a sealed, numbered envelope to a courier who waits for a signature before delivering the next one. For position updates in a game, you WANT paper airplanes — if one goes missing, the next one arrives half a second later with the new position, and the old one is irrelevant. For important messages like "player cast a spell," you DO want an acknowledgment — so you build a mini-TCP on top of UDP just for those messages. Forward Error Correction is like sending each message three times — wasteful, but if one copy is lost, you still get the message instantly instead of waiting for a resend.

### Technical

UDP (RFC 768) is a minimal datagram protocol: 8-byte header (source port, dest port, length, checksum), no connection state, no retransmission, no ordering guarantee. Datagrams may be lost, duplicated, or reordered. The kernel delivers them as-is to the application's receive buffer. Custom reliability layers add: sequence numbers (monotonically increasing per-channel), acknowledgment packets (bitmap or cumulative ACK), retransmission timers (per-packet or per-window), and delivery ordering (sequenced delivery with explicit sequence numbers). The bandwidth-delay product governs throughput: a 50 ms RTT with 100 Mbps requires a 625 KB window to fill the pipe. FEC uses Reed-Solomon or XOR-based erasure codes: for every K data packets, generate N-K parity packets; any K of the N packets can reconstruct the original data. UE5's replication system (`UNetDriver`, `UActorChannel`) classifies properties as `Reliable` or `Unreliable`, with per-actor relevancy and priority-based bandwidth allocation. The Iris replication system (UE5.2+) introduces per-property conditions, delta compression, and network emulation profiles. NAT punchthrough (STUN + hole punching) enables P2P UDP connections between players behind NATs. ENet provides: unreliable sequenced (position), reliable sequenced (RPCs), and unreliable unsequenced (debug stats) channels, plus fragmentation for messages exceeding MTU.

---

## Exercises

1. **UDP loss simulator:** Write a UDP client-server pair. The client sends 1000 packets numbered 0-999. The server simulates 10% random packet loss. The client implements sequence numbers and retransmission for reliable packets. Measure: (a) time to deliver all packets, (b) duplicate packets received, (c) effective throughput.

2. **Sliding window:** Extend the above to use a sliding window of 16 packets instead of stop-and-wait. How does throughput change on a simulated 50 ms RTT link? Use the bandwidth-delay product formula to verify your measurements.

3. **FEC encoding:** Implement XOR-based FEC: for every 4 data packets, generate 1 parity packet (5 total). Simulate 25% random loss. What percentage of data is recoverable without retransmission? How much bandwidth overhead does this add?

4. **UE replication design:** In UE, a `AActor` has 10 replicated properties. Three are position/rotation (unreliable, every tick). Two are health/shield (reliable, on change). Five are cosmetic (unreliable, low priority). Design the `GetLifetimeReplicatedProps` and explain how the engine's bandwidth limiter prioritizes them.

5. **Design:** Design a netcode for a fighting game (like Street Fighter). Both players need sub-frame input synchronization. Would you use UDP? P2P with GGPO-style rollback? Or a dedicated server model? Compare latency, fairness, and cheat resistance.

## See Also

- [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — Original overview
- [[02 — TCP Deep Dive]] — What you're avoiding by using UDP
- [[05 — Game Networking Architecture]] — How these protocols are deployed

04 \u2014 DNS, TLS & HTTPS.content:
# Networking 04 — DNS, TLS & HTTPS

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[00 — The Internet Stack]] — the OSI model.
> [[02 — TCP Deep Dive]] — TCP, which TLS rides on top of.

## Why This Matters

When you type `google.com` in your browser, two things must happen before you see the page: (1) the name `google.com` must be translated to an IP address (DNS), and (2) an encrypted tunnel must be established between your browser and Google's server (TLS). Together, DNS + TLS add 1-3 round-trips before any application data flows. Understanding this overhead is critical for latency-sensitive applications — including game matchmaking and authentication.

---

## DNS — The Internet's Phonebook

DNS is a hierarchical, distributed database that maps names to IP addresses.

### The Hierarchy

```
                              . (root)
                    ┌─────────┼─────────┐
                   com       org       net
              ┌─────┼─────┐
           google  amazon  cloudflare
```

### Resolution Walk-Through

When you query `www.google.com`:

```
1. Your resolver asks root server: "who handles .com?"
   → Response: com nameservers at a.gtld-servers.net (198.41.0.41)

2. Resolver asks .com TLD server: "who handles google.com?"
   → Response: ns1.google.com, ns2.google.com

3. Resolver asks ns1.google.com: "what's www.google.com?"
   → Response: A record: 142.250.80.46, TTL: 300 seconds
```

Each step is a round-trip (RTT). A cold DNS lookup can take 100-500 ms. Caching at every level (browser, OS stub resolver, recursive resolver, authoritative) reduces this to ~0 ms on cache hits.

### DNS Record Types

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| A | IPv4 address | `google.com → 142.250.80.46` |
| AAAA | IPv6 address | `google.com → 2607:f8b0:4005:808::200e` |
| CNAME | Alias | `www.google.com → google.com` |
| MX | Mail server | `google.com → smtp.google.com` |
| TXT | Arbitrary text | SPF, DKIM, domain verification |
| SRV | Service location | `_minecraft._tcp.example.com → server:25565` |

SRV records are critical for games — they tell clients which host and port the game server runs on, enabling non-standard port configuration.

---

## TLS — How Encryption Works

TLS (Transport Layer Security) provides: **confidentiality** (encryption), **integrity** (tamper detection), and **authentication** (server identity verified by certificate).

### The TLS 1.3 Handshake (1-RTT)

```
Client                                              Server
  │── ClientHello ──────────────────────────────────→│
  │   (supported ciphers, key_share for ECDHE)        │
  │                                                    │
  │←── ServerHello ──────────────────────────────────│
  │   (chosen cipher, key_share, certificate,           │
  │    signature over handshake, [Finished])           │
  │                                                    │
  │── [Finished] ─────────────────────────────────→│
  │                                                    │
  │◄═══════ ENCRYPTED APPLICATION DATA ═══════════►│
```

TLS 1.3 uses Diffie-Hellman key exchange (ECDHE) so the shared secret is never transmitted. The server's certificate proves its identity via a chain of trust:

```
Root CA (built into OS/browser)
  └─ Intermediate CA
       └─ Server Certificate (google.com)
```

The client verifies the chain: each certificate is signed by the one above it, and the root is trusted implicitly.

### Certificate Transparency

Since 2018, CAs must publicly log every certificate they issue. Browsers check these logs (via Signed Certificate Timestamps — SCTs) to detect misissued certificates. This prevents a rogue CA from issuing a certificate for `google.com` to an attacker.

---

## HTTPS — HTTP Over TLS

HTTPS = HTTP + TLS. The URL `https://google.com` triggers:

1. DNS: resolve `google.com` → IP (may be cached)
2. TCP: 3-way handshake (1 RTT)
3. TLS: 1.3 handshake (1 RTT) — or 2 RTT for TLS 1.2
4. HTTP: GET request → response (1 RTT)

**Total before first byte of content: 3-4 RTTs.** On a 100 ms RTT link, that's 300-400 ms before the page starts loading. **QUIC/HTTP3** merges the TLS and transport handshakes into 0-1 RTT for returning visitors.

---

## Game Applications

### Matchmaking

The matchmaking flow uses HTTPS:

```
Client → POST /matchmaking/queue (HTTPS, JSON body with player info)
Server → 200 OK (auth token + server connection info)
Client → UDP connect to game server (using info from response)
```

The initial HTTPS round-trips add 200-500 ms to matchmaking. Keep the payload small (single JSON object, <1 KB) to minimize serialization and transfer time.

### Secure Game Connections

Modern games use DTLS (Datagram TLS) for UDP encryption — it's TLS adapted for unreliable transport, handling packet loss and reordering in the handshake. Steam Datagram Relay uses its own encrypted protocol based on the Noise Protocol Framework.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

DNS is the phonebook: you know "Mom" but your phone needs a number. You ask the phonebook (DNS resolver), which asks the city directory (.com), which asks the family directory (google.com), which gives you the number. Caching means you only do this once every few minutes. TLS is a secure envelope: before you send anything sensitive, you and the server agree on a secret code (key exchange), the server shows its ID badge (certificate), and then all communication is in code that only you two can read. HTTPS is just HTTP inside a TLS envelope — same content, secure transport.

### Technical

DNS uses a hierarchical namespace with delegation at zone boundaries. Resolution can be recursive (your resolver walks the tree) or iterative (each server refers you to the next). The wire protocol (RFC 1035) uses UDP port 53 with a 512-byte limit, extended by EDNS0 to 4096 bytes; TCP fallback for oversized responses. DNS over HTTPS (DoH, RFC 8484) and DNS over TLS (DoT, RFC 7858) encrypt DNS queries to prevent eavesdropping. TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) mandates forward secrecy via ECDHE key exchange, removes obsolete algorithms (RSA key transport, RC4, 3DES, CBC mode), and encrypts the server certificate to hide the server identity from passive observers. The handshake uses the HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) to derive session keys from the ECDHE shared secret. Certificate validation: the client checks the certificate chain against its trust store, verifies the signature chain, checks expiration, and validates the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) against the requested hostname. OCSP stapling allows the server to include a signed proof of non-revocation, saving the client an additional OCSP query.

---

## Exercises

1. **DNS resolution trace:** Use `dig +trace google.com` to follow the full DNS resolution from root to authoritative. Identify each nameserver, the query type, and the response. How many queries total? Which steps are cached on subsequent lookups?

2. **TLS handshake capture:** Use `openssl s_client -connect google.com:443 -tls1_3` to examine the TLS handshake. Identify the cipher suite, the certificate chain, and the key exchange group. What's the certificate's expiration date?

3. **Certificate chain verification:** Download a website's certificate chain. Verify each certificate's signature manually using `openssl verify`. What happens if an intermediate certificate is missing? (Hint: this is a common server misconfiguration.)

4. **HTTPS overhead:** Write a program that makes 100 HTTPS GET requests to a server on localhost. Measure total time. Repeat with HTTP (no TLS). What's the per-request overhead of TLS? At what request rate does TLS overhead become the bottleneck?

5. **Design:** Design a matchmaking service for a game. Players authenticate, queue for a match, and receive a game server address. What's the shortest possible flow in terms of RTT? How do you use session resumption (TLS session tickets) to reduce latency for returning players?

## See Also

- [[02 — TCP Deep Dive]] — The transport that carries TLS
- [[03 — UDP & Game Protocols]] — DTLS for encrypted UDP
- [[05 — Game Networking Architecture]] — How these protocols are deployed in games

05 \u2014 Game Networking Architecture.content:
# Networking 05 — Game Networking Architecture

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — Transport layer basics.
> [[03 — UDP & Game Protocols]] — Why games use UDP and custom protocols.
> [[04 — DNS, TLS & HTTPS]] — How matchmaking and authentication work.

## Why This Matters

Netcode — the networking code that synchronizes game state across players — makes or breaks a multiplayer game. A beautifully rendered game with 200 ms of latency feels broken. A simple game with 20 ms of predicted latency feels responsive. Understanding the fundamental architectures — client-server, P2P, dedicated server, rollback — is how you choose the right one for your game.

---

## The Three Architectures

### 1. Listen Server (Peer Hosting)

One player is both client AND server.

```
Player 1 (Host + Client)  ◄──►  Player 2 (Client)
                                  Player 3 (Client)
                                  Player 4 (Client)
```

**Pros:** No server costs, zero-latency for host.
**Cons:** Host advantage (0 ms ping), game ends when host leaves, host can cheat trivially, host's machine runs game AND server.

### 2. Dedicated Server

A separate machine (cloud VM or physical server) runs only the server simulation.

```
Dedicated Server (no rendering, no client)
    │       │       │
Player 1  Player 2  Player 3
```

**Pros:** Fair (all players have network latency), no host advantage, persistent world, harder to cheat.
**Cons:** Server costs, all players have latency.

### 3. Peer-to-Peer (P2P)

Every client communicates with every other client. No central server.

```
Player 1 ◄─────────► Player 2
    │                    │
    └────────────────────┤
                         ▼
                    Player 3
```

**Pros:** No server costs, low latency for nearby peers.
**Cons:** N×(N-1) connections, all peers must agree on state, trivial to cheat, NAT traversal required.

---

## The Authoritative Server Model

In a client-server game, the **server is authoritative** — it decides what really happened. Clients send inputs; the server simulates and sends back results.

```
Client                          Server
  │── Input (move forward) ────→│
  │                              │── Simulate physics
  │                              │── Check bounds
  │                              │── Apply damage
  │←── State (position, HP) ────│
  │                              │
  │── Render result              │
```

The client does NOT decide "I hit the enemy" — the server decides, and the client trusts the server.

---

## Client-Side Prediction

Waiting for server confirmation before showing results feels terrible at high latency. With 100 ms RTT, a movement input takes 100 ms to show feedback — the player feels like they're dragging through mud.

**Client-side prediction:** the client immediately applies the input locally, then corrects when the server response arrives.

```
Client (with prediction):
  Press forward → move character forward immediately (predicted position)
  50 ms later: receive server position → if different, snap to server position (correction)

Without prediction:
  Press forward → wait 100 ms → move character (lag!)
```

UE's `CharacterMovementComponent` does this automatically. The server does the real movement; the client predicts and corrects.

---

## Interpolation and Extrapolation

### Interpolation (for other players)

You receive position updates from the server at ~20-60 Hz. Between updates, you **interpolate** — smoothly move the other player from their last known position toward the new position over the inter-update interval.

```
Server sends: [t=0: pos=(0,0)]  [t=50ms: pos=(5,0)]  [t=100ms: pos=(10,0)]
Client renders: smoothly move from (0,0) to (5,0) over 50 ms
```

Without interpolation: other players teleport between updates (choppy).

### Extrapolation (dead reckoning)

If a packet is delayed/dropped, extrapolate from velocity:

```
Last known: pos=(10,0), vel=(1,0)
Next update delayed → extrapolate: pos = (10,0) + vel × dt
When update arrives → correct (snap or smooth)
```

---

## Lag Compensation

In a first-person shooter, Player A shoots at Player B on A's screen. But due to latency, B has already moved on the server. Without compensation, A would need to lead their shots based on ping — unfair and unintuitive.

**Lag compensation:** the server rewinds time to see what Player A saw when they fired:

```
At server time T:
  Player A fires → server rewinds to T - A_ping
  Check: was B at the crosshair position A saw? Yes → hit
  Even though B has moved since then on the server
```

UE implements this via `bUseServerSideRewind` and the hit registration system. This is what makes "favor the shooter" work — if you hit on your screen, it counts.

---

## Tick Rate and Update Frequency

| Parameter | Typical Value | What It Controls |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Server tick rate | 30-60 Hz | How often the server simulates |
| Network update rate | 20-60 Hz | How often server sends state to clients |
| Client send rate | 60 Hz | How often client sends input to server |
| Interpolation delay | 50-100 ms | Buffer for smooth interpolation |

Higher rates = smoother gameplay, more bandwidth, more CPU. Fortnite uses 30 Hz server tick with dynamic update rates.

---

## Cheat Prevention

| Cheat Type | Mitigation |
|-----------|-----------|
| Aimbot | Server-side hit validation (no client-trusted hits) |
| Wallhack | Server doesn't send enemy positions until they're visible (relevancy + visibility culling) |
| Speed hack | Server validates movement delta against max speed per tick |
| Packet manipulation | Encrypt + sign packets; server validates sequence numbers |
| Lag switch | Server detects excessive latency → disconnect or flag |

Fundamental principle: **never trust the client.** The client is running on the enemy's hardware.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

A multiplayer game is a distributed simulation. The server is the "real" world — it runs the physics, tracks the scores, and decides who shot whom. Clients are slightly-behind mirrors of the server. When you press "forward," your client predicts what will happen and moves your character immediately (prediction) so it feels responsive. When the server confirms your position, if it disagrees, your client smoothly corrects (reconciliation). When you see other players, their movement is interpolated between their last two known positions so it looks smooth. When you shoot someone, the server rewinds time to check if you were aiming at them from your perspective (lag compensation). The server tick rate determines how often this simulation runs — 30 times per second means each tick is 33 ms. The golden rule: the server is always right, and the client never decides anything important.

### Technical

The authoritative server model: the server runs the full game simulation (physics, gameplay logic, state machine transitions) and replicates relevant state to clients via per-actor properties flagged as `Replicated` (UE). Clients send input via RPCs (typically `Server_` prefixed UFUNCTIONs with `Reliable` specifier). Client-side prediction: the client's `APlayerController` simulates pending inputs immediately, caching the predicted state per server frame. When the server acknowledgment arrives (with the authoritative position/timestamp), the `ClientAdjustment` RPC triggers reconciliation: compare predicted vs authoritative, re-simulate any unacknowledged inputs from the correction point, and smooth the visual correction. UE's `CharacterMovementComponent` handles this internally via `ClientUpdatePositionAfterServerUpdate`. Interpolation: the client buffers incoming replicated state updates (`FRepLayout` properties) and renders an interpolated position between the two most recent snapshots at an interpolation time offset (`InterpLocation`) to absorb jitter. Lag compensation (`bUseServerSideRewind`): on receiving a fire input, the server backs up the character movement history to the client's reported timestamp (`GetServerWorldTimeSeconds - ping`), performs the hit check against the historical positions, and registers the hit. Tick rate: controlled by `NetServerMaxTickRate` and `NetUpdateFrequency` for server simulation and replication; `ClientNetSendMoveDeltaTime` controls client input send frequency. Bandwidth management: UE's `MaxRPCPerNetUpdate` and `NetPriority` system prioritize and throttle replication based on actor relevance, distance, and time since last update. Cheat mitigation: the engine validates server-side RPC parameters (`WithValidation`), enforces movement speed limits, and uses `bReplicateMovement` with server-authoritative correction. Dedicated servers run headless (no rendering) via `-server` flag and communicate with clients through the `UNetDriver`'s `UIpNetDriver`.

---

## Exercises

1. **Lag compensation calculation:** Player A has 60 ms ping, Player B has 100 ms ping. A shoots at B at server time T=1000 ms. The server rewinds to T - A_ping = 940 ms. B was at position (100, 0) at T=940 and position (120, 0) at T=1000. If A's crosshair was at position (100, 0), did A hit? Now if A's crosshair was at (110, 0), did A hit? Explain the tradeoff.

2. **Interpolation jitter buffer:** You receive position updates at times: [0 ms: (0,0)], [30 ms: (5,0)], [55 ms: (10,0)], [80 ms: (15,0)]. The updates arrive with 5 ms jitter. With an interpolation delay of 50 ms, at what times do you render each position? What happens if the interpolation delay is only 10 ms?

3. **Bandwidth budgeting:** Your game has 64 players. Each player update is 30 bytes (position, rotation, animation state). Server tick rate is 60 Hz. What's the total downstream bandwidth from the server? If a player has a 256 Kbps upload link, how many players can they host in a listen-server setup?

4. **Client prediction error:** A player presses "jump" at t=0. The client predicts position = (0, 10) at t=16 ms. The server response arrives at t=50 ms saying position = (2, 8) — the player slid on a moving platform the client didn't know about. How does the client reconcile? What happens visually if the correction is (a) a snap, or (b) a smooth interpolation?

5. **Design:** Design the netcode for a racing game with 12 players. Each car's position, velocity, and steering are critical. Collisions must be fair. Would you use client-side prediction with server reconciliation? Or an input-buffer rollback model (GGPO)? Compare the two approaches for a racing game.

## See Also

- [[03 — UDP & Game Protocols]] — The transport and protocol layer underneath
- [[04 — DNS, TLS & HTTPS]] — Matchmaking and authentication
- [[../10 — Networking — TCP, UDP & Sockets]] — Original networking overview

01 \u2014 DRAM Cells & Memory Physics.content:
# DRAM Cells & Memory Physics
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|RAM & Virtual Memory]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview]] — the memory wall and why DRAM latency matters.

## Part 1: What RAM Actually Is — The Capacitor Grid

### The DRAM Cell — One Transistor, One Capacitor

Open your desktop. Pull out a DDR5 DIMM. That green stick with black rectangles contains billions of **DRAM cells** — the fundamental unit of computer memory. Each cell stores exactly **one bit** using precisely two components:

1.  **A capacitor** — a microscopic bucket that either holds electric charge (representing a logical `1`) or doesn't (representing a `0`).
2.  **A transistor** — a microscopic switch that connects the capacitor to the outside world so the CPU can read what's in the bucket or fill/drain it.

These two components are etched into silicon at nanoscale. A single 16 GB DDR5 DIMM contains approximately **128 billion** of these cell pairs, fabricated on a die roughly the size of your fingernail.

```
    Word Line (switch control — "open row #473")
         │
    ─────┼─────  Transistor (the gate — when word line is HIGH, bucket connects to bit line)
         │
        ═══ Capacitor (stores charge — 1 if charged, 0 if drained)
        ═══
         │
       Bit Line (data in/out — read the voltage on this line to sense the bit)
```

When the CPU wants to read a bit, it tells the memory controller: "I need the 47th bit of row 12, column 308." The memory controller activates **Word Line 12** — this opens the transistor gates for every cell in that row. All 8,192 cells in that row (for a typical DDR5 chip) dump their charge onto their respective bit lines simultaneously. Sense amplifiers at the end of each bit line measure the tiny voltage and determine: is this a `1` or a `0`?

This is called the **row buffer hit**. All the bits from that row are now sitting in the row buffer (also called the sense amplifier array), and the CPU can read any column from it at very low latency — roughly **10 ns** per subsequent access in the same row. This is why sequential memory access is fast: you open a row once, then read many columns.

> [!info] Analogy — A Stadium Full of Tiny Cups
> Imagine a football stadium with 8 billion small paper cups on every seat, arranged in a grid of rows and columns. Each cup either has a drop of water (representing `1`) or is dry (`0`). To read a cup, an attendant walks to that row, opens a gate that lets them see EVERY cup in that row at once, and notes which ones are wet. If you want multiple cups in the same row, great — the gate is already open. If you want a cup in a different row, the attendant must close the current gate, walk to the new row, and open that gate. Walking between rows is the dominant cost. The cups themselves leak — within about a minute, the water evaporates. So an army of janitors must constantly patrol every row, checking cups and refilling the wet ones. That's the refresh cycle.

### The Leak Problem — Why It's Called "Dynamic" RAM

Those capacitors are microscopic — think a few tens of nanometers across. At that scale, the insulating material between the capacitor plates isn't perfect. Charge **leaks out** continuously. At room temperature, a DRAM cell loses readable charge within about **64 milliseconds**.

This is the "Dynamic" in DRAM. Unlike SRAM (Static RAM, used for CPU caches), which uses a 6-transistor flip-flop that holds state indefinitely as long as power is applied, DRAM data decays. It must be **refreshed** periodically.

### Refresh Cycles — The Silent Thief of Bandwidth

Every 64 milliseconds, the memory controller must visit **every single row** in the DRAM array and re-write its data. It does this by:

1. Opening the row (activating the word line)
2. Letting the sense amplifiers read and amplify the charge
3. Writing the amplified charge back to the capacitors

The standard DRAM chip has **8,192 rows** (for a typical DDR5 8 Gb die). The memory controller issues refresh commands at a fixed interval called **tREFI** (Refresh Interval). The math:

- 64 ms ÷ 8,192 rows = **7.8125 µs** between refreshes
- Each refresh command takes roughly **350 nanoseconds** (tRFC — Refresh Cycle Time) in DDR5
- That's 350 ns lost every 7.8 µs, or roughly **4.5% of total DRAM time** spent on refresh

At higher temperatures, capacitors leak faster. Above 85°C, the JEDEC standard requires a **32 ms** refresh interval (double the refresh rate → ~9% time lost). This is one reason servers have aggressive cooling — it's not just about the CPU; hot RAM refreshes more often and becomes slower.

> [!summary] Key DRAM Specs (DDR5-6000, typical 2024-2025)
> - **Cell:** 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit
> - **Refresh interval (tREFW):** 64 ms at ≤85°C, 32 ms at >85°C
> - **Refresh command time (tRFC):** ~350 ns (DDR5)
> - **First access latency (tRCD + CL):** ~80-100 ns (row open + column read)
> - **Subsequent access in open row:** ~10-15 ns
> - **Row size:** 8,192 columns (8 Kb) per row in typical DDR5 8 Gb die
> - **Bandwidth per DIMM:** ~38-51 GB/s (DDR5-4800 to DDR5-6400)
> - **DIMM capacity:** up to 64 GB per stick (128 GB with 3D-stacked DRAM on server)

---


## Part 2: Why Is RAM So Slow? — The Physics of Distance

We said a DDR5 access takes 80-100 ns. Inside the CPU, an L1 cache access takes ~1 ns (4-5 cycles). Why is RAM **80-100× slower**? Four reasons, each compounding on the last:

### 1. Physical Distance — The Speed of Light Is a Hard Limit

Electrical signals in copper traces on a PCB travel at roughly **50-70% the speed of light** (the dielectric constant of the PCB material slows propagation). At 60% of c, a signal travels **18 cm per nanosecond**.

The round-trip distance from CPU socket → RAM slot → back is typically **15-25 cm**. That's a minimum of **~0.8-1.4 ns** just for the signal to propagate — 5-7 CPU cycles at 5 GHz. And that's the absolute physical lower bound before any electronics do anything.

Inside the CPU die, signals travel mere millimeters. An L1 cache is physically adjacent to the execution units — the round-trip is under a millimeter, or about 5 picoseconds. That's why L1 access is 200× faster: the signal doesn't have to leave the chip.

### 2. The DRAM Array Itself Is Slow

Even after the signal arrives at the DRAM chip, the internal operation is inherently slow. To read a bit:

- **Row activation (tRCD):** The word line must be driven high, charge from thousands of capacitors flows onto the bit lines, and the tiny voltage changes (millivolts) must be sensed and amplified. This is an analog process — it takes ~14-20 ns (70-100 cycles).
- **Column read (CL):** Once the row is open, selecting the right column and reading the data out through the I/O circuitry takes another ~10-15 ns.
- **Precharge (tRP):** Before opening a new row, the current row's bit lines must be reset to a reference voltage. Another ~14-20 ns.

The total: **40-55 ns** of DRAM-internal latency. Add the bus transfer time and controller overhead, and you get 80-100 ns.

### 3. The Pin Bandwidth Bottleneck

A CPU has ~1,500-2,000 pins on its socket. Only ~200-300 of those are dedicated to the memory bus (for a dual-channel configuration). Each pin can only toggle so fast — DDR5 runs the memory bus at ~3,000-3,600 MHz (DDR = Double Data Rate, so 6,000-7,200 MT/s). A 64-bit wide memory channel at 6,000 MT/s gives **48 GB/s** peak — but real sustained throughput is ~70-80% of that due to refresh, bank conflicts, and bus turnaround.

Meanwhile, the CPU's internal data paths are thousands of bits wide and run at the full core clock. The L1 data cache can deliver **~2 TB/s** of bandwidth internally. The off-chip bottleneck is enormous.

### 4. DRAM Banks and the Precharge Penalty

A DRAM chip is divided into **banks** (typically 16 banks in DDR5). Each bank is an independent array that can have one row open at a time. If you need data from a different row in the same bank, you must precharge (close) the current row first — that's the full tRP + tRCD penalty (~30-40 ns).

Smart memory controllers interleave requests across banks so that while one bank is precharging, another bank's open row can service reads. But random access patterns defeat this — you're constantly opening and closing rows, paying the full latency every time.

> [!important] The Memory Wall in One Sentence
> The CPU can execute 4 instructions per nanosecond. RAM takes 100 ns to respond. That's 400 instructions that could have been executed — wasted, waiting. Every level of cache, every prefetch algorithm, every data-oriented design pattern exists solely to keep the CPU from ever having to actually wait for RAM.

---


## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
DRAM is a grid of tiny capacitors — each stores one bit as a charge (1 = charged, 0 = drained). Think of a spreadsheet where every cell leaks charge over time. The memory controller races through rows refreshing them before they drain (refresh cycle). Ranks, banks, and channels are parallel pipelines — multiple spreadsheets being read simultaneously. DDR generations are faster spreadsheet refreshers.

### Technical
1T1C cell: one transistor, one capacitor. Read is destructive — sense amplifier reads voltage across bitline, then writes back. Refresh: every row every 64ms (tREF). Banks enable parallelism (8-16 per chip). Ranks group chips sharing a CS signal. Channels are independent memory buses. DDR5: 32 banks, 16n prefetch, on-die ECC. Row hammer: repeated access to one row causes bit flips in adjacent rows due to capacitive coupling.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** A DRAM read is destructive — the sense amplifier discharges the capacitor to detect the bit. How does the memory controller ensure the data isn't lost after reading? Walk through the full read-restore cycle for a single row, including tRAS.

2. **Code:** Write a program that allocates two large arrays (say 256 MB each) and repeatedly reads from one in a linear pattern and the other in a random pattern. Use `rdtsc` or `clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC)` to measure average latency per access. Explain your results using the row buffer hit rate: what % of linear accesses hit an open row vs random?

3. **Analysis:** The note says row hammer flips bits in adjacent rows due to capacitive coupling. If a DDR5 chip has rows spaced ~30 nm apart and the wordline toggles at 3.2 billion times per second (DDR5-6400), how many activations to an aggressor row does it take to cause a bit flip in a victim row? Why does increasing DRAM density make row hammer worse?

4. **Real-world:** Your application uses a hash table that's 1 GB and accessed randomly. It's scattered across all 16 banks of a DDR5 DIMM. Calculate the expected row buffer hit rate. If each bank has one open row and rows are 8 KB, what's the probability that two consecutive random lookups hit the same row? Why does this make hash tables DRAM-unfriendly?

5. **Design:** The memory wall says 400 instructions could execute during one DRAM access. Design a software prefetch scheme that issues prefetch hints 100+ instructions before a memory access is needed. Where do you place the `__builtin_prefetch()` calls, and how do you know the access will happen exactly 100 instructions later?

## See Also

- [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — How the CPU finds data in all this DRAM
- [[../Storage/01 — HDD Mechanics|HDD Mechanics]] — Compare RAM latency (~100 ns) to storage (~10,000,000 ns)

02 \u2014 Page Tables & TLB.content:
# Page Tables & TLB
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|RAM & Virtual Memory]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]] — you should understand where the physical RAM is before learning how the CPU finds it.

## Part 4: Virtual Memory — The Greatest Lie in Computing

### The Problem: Every Program Thinks It's Alone

Open Task Manager. You'll see Chrome using 4 GB, Visual Studio using 3 GB, Discord using 1 GB, and a dozen background processes each claiming hundreds of megabytes. Your machine has 16 GB of physical RAM. Simple addition says Chrome + VS + Discord alone would exceed 8 GB — and yet everything works.

The trick: **every process thinks it owns ALL the RAM, starting from address 0**. This is the **virtual address space** — a private, isolated, imaginary memory universe given to each process. The operating system silently translates every "virtual address" the program uses into a "physical address" in the real DRAM chips. The program never sees the physical address. It can't — that's the whole point.

```cpp
int* p = new int(42);
// p = 0x7ffd4a3c1000  ← This is a VIRTUAL address
// The physical address might be 0x1a3f8000 in a DIMM chip somewhere
// Your program will never know. The translation happens in hardware.
```

### Why Bother? Three Reasons This Lie Exists

**1. Isolation.** Process A cannot read or write Process B's memory — their virtual address spaces are completely separate. A pointer in Process A that happens to have the same bit pattern as a pointer in Process B points to completely different physical memory. This is the foundation of OS security. Without it, one crashing program could corrupt the entire system.

**2. Simplification.** Every program can be written as if it owns memory starting at address 0. Compilers don't need to know what else is running. The linker can place code, data, and stack at fixed virtual addresses that work for every process. The executable file format (ELF, PE) describes a virtual memory layout, and the OS just maps it in.

**3. Overcommit and Swapping.** The OS can promise more memory than physically exists. If a program allocates 10 GB but only touches 2 GB, the OS only needs 2 GB of physical RAM. The rest exists as "reservations" in the page table — promises that will be fulfilled only if actually used. And pages not recently touched can be evicted to disk (swap), freeing RAM for active processes.

### The MMU — The Hardware That Makes the Lie Work

Between the CPU core and the L1 cache (or sometimes between L1 and L2, depending on architecture) sits the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. Every memory access — every instruction fetch, every data load, every stack push — must pass through the MMU:

```
CPU executes:  mov rax, [0x7ffd4a3c1000]   ← Virtual address
    ↓
MMU intercepts: "0x7ffd4a3c1000... let me look that up..."
    ↓
MMU translates: "That maps to physical address 0x1a3f8000"
    ↓
Cache/RAM receives: "Read physical address 0x1a3f8000"
    ↓
Data returned to CPU
```

If the MMU can't find the translation? That's a **page fault** — the CPU traps into the OS kernel, which must either set up the mapping (minor fault), load data from disk (major fault), or kill the process (invalid access). We'll walk through all three scenarios in detail.

---


## Part 5: Pages and Page Tables — The OS's Phonebook

### Pages: Memory in Fixed-Size Chunks

The OS doesn't track individual bytes. That would be insane — tracking 16 GB as 16 billion individual mappings would require more memory than the RAM itself. Instead, memory is divided into fixed-size blocks called **pages**.

On x86-64, the default page size is **4 KB (4,096 bytes)**. Every virtual address belongs to exactly one virtual page. Every physical RAM location belongs to exactly one physical **frame** (synonymous with "physical page").

```
Virtual address:  0x7ffd4a3c1ABC
                  │            │
                  └─ Page      └─ Offset within page
                     Number       (12 bits = 4,096 possibilities)
                     (upper bits)

Page Number: 0x7ffd4a3c1  →  "Which page?"
Offset:      0xABC         →  "Which byte within that page?"
```

With 48-bit virtual addresses (the standard on x86-64; the top 16 bits are sign-extended for canonical addresses) and 12-bit offsets, you get 36 bits of page number — that's **68.7 billion** possible virtual pages per process, addressing a 256 TB virtual address space.

### Page Tables — The Hierarchical Phonebook

For every process, the OS maintains a **page table** — a data structure that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers (plus permissions, bookkeeping flags). Each entry is called a **PTE (Page Table Entry)** and looks conceptually like:

```
Virtual Page 42    → Physical Frame 891    [Present: Yes] [Read/Write: Yes] [User: Yes]
Virtual Page 43    → NOT IN RAM            [Present: No]  [Swapped to disk sector 12800]
Virtual Page 44    → Physical Frame 1203   [Present: Yes] [Read-Only] (shared library .text)
Virtual Page 45    → NOT ALLOCATED         [Present: No]  [No backing store — SEGFAULT]
```

**Why is the page table hierarchical?** Because a flat array of 68.7 billion entries (× 8 bytes per entry = 550 GB) obviously doesn't fit. Instead, x86-64 uses a **4-level radix tree** (5-level with Intel's 57-bit "5-level paging" extension on newer server CPUs):

```
Virtual Address (48 bits):

│ 9 bits │ 9 bits │ 9 bits │ 9 bits │ 12 bits │
│  PML4  │  PDPT  │   PD   │   PT   │ Offset  │
│ index  │ index  │ index  │ index  │         │

Walk:
1. CR3 register → PML4 table (512 entries)
2. PML4[0..511] → points to a PDPT table (512 entries)
3. PDPT[0..511] → points to a PD table (512 entries)
4. PD[0..511]   → points to a PT table (512 entries)
5. PT[0..511]   → the PTE: "Physical Frame X, flags..."
6. Physical Frame + Offset → the actual byte in RAM
```

Every level has 512 entries (9 bits of index). To translate one virtual address, the MMU must make **4 sequential memory reads** — each one dependent on the result of the previous. At ~80 ns per read, that's **320 ns** for a full page walk. For every single memory access. This would make the computer unusable.

Enter the TLB.

> [!info] Analogy — The City Directory
> You're a courier in a massive city (the virtual address space). Every building has a "virtual address" that civilians use. But the post office has its own internal "physical address" system for sorting. To deliver a package, you need to translate the civilian address to the postal address. The official directory (the page table) is a 4-volume, cross-referenced encyclopedia — looking up one address requires checking 4 different books. So you keep a sticky note on your dashboard (the TLB) with the last 64 translations you did. The sticky note is tiny but takes 1 second to check vs 4 minutes for the encyclopedias. If the address isn't on the sticky note, you sigh, open all 4 volumes, work through the cross-references, and add the result to your note (discarding the oldest entry). That's a TLB miss.

---


## Part 6: The TLB — Translation Lookaside Buffer

### What the TLB Actually Is

The TLB is a **hardware cache inside the CPU** dedicated to one thing: storing recent virtual→physical page translations. It's built from SRAM (fast, no refresh) and sits directly on the MMU's data path. It is separate from the L1/L2/L3 data caches — it's a specialized content-addressable memory (CAM) that searches all entries in parallel.

### TLB Sizes on Modern CPUs

| TLB Level | What It Caches | Typical Size | Latency | Where |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|---------|-------|
| **L1 D-TLB** | Data page translations | 64 entries (Intel), 72 entries (AMD Zen 4+) | ~1 cycle | Per physical core |
| **L1 I-TLB** | Instruction page translations | 64 entries (Intel), 64 entries (AMD) | ~1 cycle | Per physical core |
| **L2 STLB** | Combined I+D translations | 1,536 entries (Intel Skylake+), 3,072 entries (AMD Zen 4) | ~8-12 cycles | Per physical core |

These numbers seem tiny. But remember: each entry covers a full page. The L1 D-TLB with 64 entries × 4 KB = **256 KB** of address coverage. If your program's working set is larger than 256 KB of data scattered across different pages, you will TLB-miss repeatedly.

> [!warning] The TLB Miss Penalty — This Is What Kills Performance
> A TLB miss means the MMU must hardware-walk the 4-level page table — 4 dependent memory accesses. In the best case (all page table levels in L1/L2 cache), this takes ~50-80 ns. If page table entries have been evicted from cache (common under memory pressure), each of those 4 reads might hit DRAM — taking ~320-400 ns. And during this entire time, the CPU pipeline is **stalled**. The instruction that needs that memory address cannot retire.

For a hot inner loop accessing a scattered 10 MB array, you can easily spend 20-30% of execution time on TLB misses.

### The Fix: Huge Pages (Preview)

If you use **2 MB pages** instead of 4 KB, a single TLB entry covers 512× more address space. The same 64-entry L1 D-TLB covers **128 MB** instead of 256 KB. For memory-hungry workloads, this is transformative. We'll cover huge pages in detail in Part 9.

---


## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Virtual memory is a postal forwarding service. Your program writes letters to "0x7FFF1234" (virtual address). The MMU (post office) looks up the real physical address in a book (page table). If the page isn't in the book (page fault), the post office calls the kernel to fetch it from disk. The TLB is a sticky note on the postman's desk — most recent translations are right there, no need to open the book every time.

### Technical
4-level paging (x86-64): PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT → page (4096 bytes). Each level is a 512-entry table. Virtual address split: [16 unused | 9 PML4 | 9 PDPT | 9 PD | 9 PT | 12 offset]. CR3 register points to PML4. TLB: fully associative cache, ~64-1536 entries, 0.5-1 cycle hit. Page walk: up to 4 memory accesses per translation if TLB misses. Huge pages (2MB/1GB) skip lower PTE levels, reduce TLB pressure.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** A 48-bit virtual address is split into 9+9+9+9+12 bits (4 levels + offset). Why 9 bits per level and not 8 or 10? Walk through: a page table entry is 8 bytes. How many PTEs fit in one 4 KB page? How does that determine the 9-bit field width?

2. **Code:** Write a program that allocates a 256 MB array and accesses it with stride=1 (sequential), stride=64 (one access per cache line), stride=4096 (one access per page), and stride=524288 (one access per 128 pages). Measure TLB miss rate using `perf stat -e dtlb_load_misses.miss_causes_a_walk` on Linux. Plot stride vs TLB miss rate. Why does stride=4096 cause maximum TLB misses?

3. **Analysis:** The L1 D-TLB has 64 entries covering 256 KB with 4 KB pages. Your program's working set is 2 MB of memory in a random access pattern. Calculate the probability that a random access hits the TLB. Now with 2 MB huge pages (64 entries × 2 MB = 128 MB coverage), what's the probability? At what working set size does even 2 MB huge page TLB miss rate exceed 50%?

4. **Real-world:** The note says a full 4-level page walk that misses all cache levels costs ~320-400 ns (~4 × 100 ns DRAM accesses). If your game engine's inner loop accesses one new page per iteration and runs at 60 FPS with 10⁶ iterations per frame, how many cycles per frame are spent on page walks? If each frame has a 16.67 ms budget, what percentage is lost to TLB misses?

5. **Design:** The note says ASLR randomizes the virtual address space layout. If ASLR randomizes the stack base within a 32-bit range on 64-bit Linux, how many bits of entropy does that provide? An attacker needs to guess the stack address to exploit a buffer overflow — what's the probability of guessing correctly in one try? How does this compare to the probability if ASLR were disabled?

## See Also

- [[01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]] — The physical RAM these page tables map to
- [[03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]] — What happens when the TLB misses and the PTE says "not present"
- [[04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]] — Making the TLB cover more memory with huge pages

03 \u2014 Page Faults, mmap & Swap.content:
# Page Faults, mmap & Swap
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|RAM & Virtual Memory]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — you must understand PTEs and the "Present" bit before learning what happens when it's 0.

## Part 7: Page Faults — When Memory Isn't There (Yet)

A **page fault** occurs when the CPU's MMU tries to translate a virtual address and discovers the page table entry marked **"not present"** — the page isn't currently mapped to a physical frame. This is not necessarily a crash. It's the mechanism that makes demand paging, memory-mapped files, and swap all work.

### The Three Kinds of Page Fault

| Type | PTE State | What Happened | OS Response | Latency |
|------|-----------|---------------|-------------|---------|
| **Minor (soft)** | Page is in RAM but not in THIS process's page table | Shared library page already loaded by another process; zero-fill-on-demand page; copy-on-write page | Update PTE to point to existing frame. No I/O needed. | ~1-3 µs |
| **Major (hard)** | Page is NOT in RAM — on disk | Swapped-out anonymous page; memory-mapped file page not yet read | Read from disk into a free frame, update PTE, resume | ~50-100 µs (NVMe), ~5-10 ms (HDD) |
| **Invalid** | No mapping exists, no backing store | NULL pointer dereference; use-after-free; stack overflow hitting guard page | OS sends SIGSEGV (Linux) or ACCESS_VIOLATION (Windows). Program crashes. | Fatal |

### Step-by-Step: What Happens During a Major Page Fault

```
1. CPU executes: mov rax, [0x7ffd4a3c1000]
2. MMU checks TLB → MISS (translation not cached)
3. MMU walks 4-level page table:
   - PML4 entry: Present, points to PDPT
   - PDPT entry: Present, points to PD
   - PD entry: Present, points to PT
   - PT entry: NOT PRESENT (bit 0 = 0) → PAGE FAULT

4. CPU raises exception #14 (Page Fault) → traps to kernel, ring 0
5. Kernel's page fault handler (do_page_fault on Linux) runs:
   a. Read CR2 register: "Which address caused the fault?" → 0x7ffd4a3c1000
   b. Walk the process's VMA (Virtual Memory Area) tree:
      - Is this address in a valid VMA? Yes → continue
      - No → SEGFAULT (address not in any mapped region)
   c. Check VMA flags: does this VMA have a file backing it?
      - Yes (file-backed, e.g., mmap'd region) → read the file
      - No (anonymous, e.g., heap/stack) → allocate a zero page or find in swap
   d. Allocate a free physical frame (or evict a victim page if RAM is full)
   e. If page is on disk: schedule disk I/O
   f. Process sleeps (TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE) while I/O is in flight
   g. Disk I/O completes → DMA transfers data into the allocated frame
   h. Update PTE: set Present=1, point to the new physical frame

6. Kernel returns to user mode (ring 3)
7. CPU retries the faulting instruction:
   - MMU checks TLB → MISS (new mapping, not cached yet)
   - MMU walks page table → HIT (PTE now Present)
   - TLB is loaded with the translation
   - Data is read from RAM → loaded into register
8. CPU continues executing the next instruction as if nothing happened
```

> [!important] This Is Why First Access Is Slow (Demand Paging)
> When you `new` a 100 MB array, the OS does NOT immediately allocate 100 MB of physical RAM. It marks 25,600 virtual pages (100 MB ÷ 4 KB) as "reserved but not present" in the page table. Physical pages are allocated **on first write** to each page — one page fault per 4 KB. The allocation itself takes microseconds. The real cost is amortized: each 4 KB chunk you actually touch costs one minor page fault (~1-3 µs). If you only use 10 MB of your 100 MB array, you only pay for 10 MB. This is **demand paging** — the OS is lazy, and laziness is performance.

### The Zero-Page Trick (Minor Fault)

When you `new int[1000]` but haven't written to it yet, the OS doesn't give you 4,000 bytes of fresh physical RAM. Instead, ALL those pages point to a single, system-wide read-only **zero page** — a 4 KB frame filled with zeros. When you first write to any of those pages:

1. Write triggers a **copy-on-write** (COW) fault
2. OS allocates a new physical frame
3. Copies the zero page into it (or just gives you a pre-zeroed frame from the background zeroing thread)
4. Updates PTE to point to the new frame with Read+Write permissions
5. Your write proceeds

This is why `calloc()` (which zeros memory) and `new` (which doesn't) have different performance characteristics: `calloc` forces the zero-page trick to happen eagerly, while `new` leaves it lazy.

---


## Part 8: mmap — When Files Become Memory

### The Physical Reality of mmap

`mmap` is a system call that makes a region of a file appear as if it's part of your process's virtual address space. Instead of using `read()` and `write()`, you just dereference pointers — and the OS handles loading file data behind the scenes.

```cpp
#include <sys/mman.h>  // POSIX: mmap(), munmap() — map files or anonymous memory into address space
#include <fcntl.h>  // POSIX: open() flags like O_RDONLY, O_CREAT
#include <unistd.h>  // POSIX: read(), write(), close(), ftruncate()

int fd = open("levels.dat", O_RDONLY);
off_t file_size = lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_END);

// Map the entire file into virtual memory
void* data = mmap(
    NULL,           // Let the OS choose the virtual address
    file_size,      // How many bytes to map
    PROT_READ,      // We only want to read
    MAP_PRIVATE,    // Our changes are private (copy-on-write), not written back
    fd,              // The file descriptor
    0                // Offset within the file (start at beginning)
);

// Now access it like an array — no read() calls needed
LevelHeader* header = static_cast<LevelHeader*>(data);
printf("Level: %s, Version: %d
", header->name, header->version);
// ↑ This first access triggers a MAJOR page fault
// The OS reads the first 4 KB of levels.dat into a physical frame,
// maps it into the process's page table, and then the CPU can read it.
```

### What mmap Actually Does to the Kernel

When you call `mmap`, the kernel:

1. Creates a new **VMA (Virtual Memory Area)** entry in the process's `mm_struct`
2. Records the mapping: virtual address range, file pointer, offset, permissions, flags
3. Does NOT allocate physical memory. Does NOT read the file. Does NOT even check if the file exists beyond the file descriptor.
4. Returns the virtual address.

That's it. The first time you touch a mapped page, the page fault handler:

1. Finds the VMA for that address
2. Sees it's file-backed with `MAP_PRIVATE`
3. Allocates a physical frame
4. Queues a disk read for that file offset
5. Puts the process to sleep
6. Wakes it when the DMA transfer completes
7. The page is now in RAM

### MAP_PRIVATE vs MAP_SHARED — The Hardware Truth

```cpp
// MAP_PRIVATE (Copy-on-Write):
void* priv = mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE, fd, 0);
// Writing to priv[0] = 'X' creates a PRIVATE COPY of that page in RAM.
// The original file is never modified. Other processes never see your changes.

// MAP_SHARED:
void* shared = mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0);
// Writing to shared[0] = 'X' modifies the page cache page directly.
// Other processes mapping the same file WILL see your changes.
// Eventually (or on msync/fsync), changes are written back to disk.
```

`MAP_PRIVATE` uses copy-on-write: the PTE is initially marked read-only, pointing to the shared page-cache page. On first write, the MMU triggers a fault, the kernel copies the page to a new private frame, updates the PTE to Read+Write pointing to the private copy, and the write proceeds. Subsequent writes to that page hit the private copy directly with zero overhead.

### Why Games Use mmap

Loading a 6 GB level file with `mmap` means:
- You don't need 6 GB of RAM up front
- Only the parts of the level the player actually sees (textures, geometry near the camera) get loaded
- The OS's page cache and read-ahead automatically pre-load nearby data
- Unused pages can be evicted under memory pressure without you writing eviction logic
- The code is simpler: pointer dereference instead of manual `seek` + `read` loops

Unreal Engine uses memory-mapped I/O extensively for `.pak` file access. The engine maps the archive, then accesses assets inside it as if they were in memory — the OS handles the rest.

---


## Part 9: Swap — When RAM Runs Out

### What Swap Is Physically

Swap is a region of your storage device (a dedicated partition on Linux, or `pagefile.sys` on Windows) that acts as **overflow parking for RAM pages**. When physical RAM is full and the OS needs to satisfy a new page allocation, it must **evict** an existing page from RAM to make room.

### The Eviction Decision — Page Replacement

The OS maintains a list of all physical pages and tracks when each was last accessed. When RAM is exhausted, the kernel must choose a **victim page** to evict. The simplest algorithm: **LRU (Least Recently Used)** approximation.

```
1. Kernel needs a free physical frame
2. No free frames available? Scan the LRU list:
   a. Is the page "clean" (not modified since last read from disk)?
      → Just drop it. The page cache still has the disk copy.
   b. Is the page "dirty" (modified in RAM, not yet written to disk)?
      → Must write to swap or file before reusing the frame.

3. For anonymous pages (heap, stack) marked dirty:
   → Write to swap area on disk
   → Mark PTE as "not present, swapped to sector N"
   → Free the frame

4. For file-backed pages (mmap'd files, page cache) marked dirty:
   → Write back to the original file (writeback)
   → Free the frame (the file IS the backing store)
```

### The Catastrophic Cost

| Operation | Latency | Relative to RAM |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| RAM access | ~100 ns | 1× |
| L1 cache miss → L2 hit | ~4-7 ns extra | — |
| Major page fault (NVMe) | ~50-100 µs | **500-1,000×** |
| Major page fault (SATA SSD) | ~200-500 µs | **2,000-5,000×** |
| Major page fault (HDD) | ~5-10 ms | **50,000-100,000×** |
| Swap out + swap in (full round trip) | 2× the above | 2× worse |

When your system starts swapping heavily, the CPU doesn't run your program at 1/1000th speed — it runs **even slower** because the constant page faults cause context switches, and the disk I/O queue becomes a bottleneck. This is **thrashing**: the system spends more time moving pages between RAM and disk than doing useful work. The disk light stays on solid. Mouse movements lag by seconds. This is why "add more RAM" is always the first performance advice.

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Context switch is explained in detail in [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]]. This note only covers what is necessary for the current topic.


> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Context switch is explained in detail in [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]]. This note only touches what is necessary for the current topic.


```cpp
// The WRONG way: allocate more than physical RAM, touch it all, cause thrashing
char* huge = new char[20ULL * 1024 * 1024 * 1024];  // 20 GB on a 16 GB machine
memset(huge, 0, 20ULL * 1024 * 1024 * 1024);         // Touches every page → swap storm

// The RIGHT way: know your working set, stay within physical RAM bounds
// Or: mmap the file instead of loading everything into anonymous memory
// Or: lock critical memory so it can NEVER be swapped: mlock() / VirtualLock()
```

### Locking Memory — "This Page Stays in RAM"

For latency-critical data (audio buffers, game frame data, network packet rings), you can tell the OS: "Never swap these pages." 

```cpp
// Linux: mlock
#include <sys/mman.h>  // POSIX: mmap(), munmap() — map files or anonymous memory into address space
mlock(critical_buffer, buffer_size);  // These pages are now unswappable

// Windows: VirtualLock
VirtualLock(critical_buffer, buffer_size);  // Same guarantee
```

**The trap:** `mlock`/`VirtualLock` reduce the effective pool of swappable RAM. If you lock too much, the remaining swappable pool gets squeezed, and swap pressure on the unlocked pages becomes even worse. Lock sparingly — only what truly cannot tolerate a 10 ms stall.

---


## Exercises

1. **Page fault classification:** For each scenario, classify the page fault as minor, major, or invalid: (a) accessing a newly `malloc`'d page for the first time, (b) reading from an `mmap`'d file region that hasn't been loaded yet, (c) dereferencing a NULL pointer, (d) accessing a stack page just beyond the current stack limit before the OS auto-grows it, (e) writing to a `MAP_PRIVATE` page for the first time after the parent process `fork()`'d. Explain the kernel's response in each case.

2. **Demand paging cost:** You `new` a 1 GB array but only write to the first 100 MB. How many physical frames does the OS actually allocate? How many minor page faults occur? If each minor fault costs 2 µs, what is the total overhead? Now compare to eagerly allocating and zeroing all 1 GB up front — how much slower is the eager approach?

3. **mmap vs read — throughput comparison:** Read a 2 GB file sequentially using (a) `mmap` with `MAP_PRIVATE` and (b) `read()` in 4 KB chunks into a pre-allocated buffer. Which involves fewer context switches? Which involves fewer memory copies (DMA → page cache → user buffer vs DMA → page cache only)? If the file is randomly accessed (thousands of 4 KB reads scattered throughout), which approach wins and why?

4. **Swap thrashing diagnosis:** Your game server suddenly drops from 60 FPS to 2 FPS. `top` shows 100% disk I/O wait. RAM is 32 GB, the server process's RSS is 28 GB, swap usage is 8 GB. What is happening? Which pages are being swapped in and out? If you can't add RAM, what three things could you change to reduce swap pressure?

5. **mlock tradeoff:** You `mlock` 2 GB of audio buffer data to prevent swap latency spikes on a machine with 16 GB RAM. Your game process uses 10 GB total. What is the maximum swappable memory remaining? If the OS needs to evict pages under pressure, which pages can it target? What is the danger of locking too much memory — can `mlock` cause OOM?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A page fault is not a crash — it's the OS saying "hold on, I need to fetch that page before you can use it." It's like ordering a book at a library: you point to a call number (virtual address), and if the book is already on the reserve shelf (minor fault — page is in RAM but not mapped to YOUR library card), the librarian just stamps your card in 2 µs. If the book is in offsite storage (major fault — on disk), the librarian sends a truck to fetch it, and you wait 100 µs (NVMe) or 10,000 µs (HDD). If you point to a call number that doesn't exist (invalid fault), security escorts you out — SIGSEGV. `mmap` is the clever trick of saying "those books over there? They're now part of your private collection" without actually moving anything. You only fetch a book when you try to read it. Swap is the library being over capacity: the librarian puts rarely-read books in offsite storage to make room for popular ones. Thrashing is when the librarian spends ALL their time shipping books back and forth — no actual reading gets done.

### Technical
A page fault occurs when the MMU encounters a PTE with Present=0. Three types: **minor** (soft) — the physical page exists in RAM (e.g., shared library page, zero-fill-on-demand, copy-on-write) and the kernel only needs to update the PTE, costing ~1-3 µs. **Major** (hard) — the page is on disk (anonymous page in swap, or file-backed page not yet read); the kernel allocates a frame, schedules DMA I/O, puts the process in TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE sleep, and wakes it when the transfer completes — ~50-100 µs on NVMe, ~5-10 ms on HDD. **Invalid** — no mapping exists and no backing store can satisfy it (NULL dereference, use-after-free, stack overflow into guard page); the kernel delivers SIGSEGV. Demand paging exploits laziness: `new`/`malloc` creates VMA entries but no physical pages; pages are allocated on first write via the zero-page COW trick. `mmap` similarly defers I/O — the VMA records file/offset/permissions, but physical pages are faulted in on first access. `MAP_PRIVATE` uses COW: initial PTEs are read-only pointing to the shared page-cache page; first write triggers a fault, kernel copies the page to a private frame, remaps as Read+Write. Swap eviction: when RAM is exhausted, the kernel's LRU approximation selects victim pages; clean file-backed pages are simply dropped (the file is the backing store), dirty anonymous pages are written to the swap area, and their PTEs are updated to "not present, swapped to sector N." Thrashing is the catastrophic regime where the working set exceeds physical RAM and every memory access triggers a major fault — throughput collapses from CPU-speed to disk-speed.

## See Also

- [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — The page table structure that makes page faults possible
- [[../Storage/04 — Filesystems & Page Cache|Filesystems & Page Cache]] — The page cache that mmap reads from
- [[04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]] — Locking pages to prevent swap (mlock)

04 \u2014 Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Proces.content:
# Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|RAM & Virtual Memory]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — understand the TLB before learning how huge pages reduce TLB misses.
> [[03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]] — understand swap before learning how overcommit interacts with OOM.

## Part 10: Huge Pages — The TLB's Best Friend

### The Trap: 4 KB Pages Are Too Small for Modern Workloads

Recall: L1 D-TLB has 64 entries. Each entry covers one 4 KB page → total TLB coverage = **256 KB**. If your game's working set (textures, meshes, physics state) is 2 GB, you're thrashing the TLB on virtually every access. TLB misses cost ~50-400 ns and stall the pipeline.

### The Hardware Solution: Larger Pages

x86-64 CPUs support two additional page sizes:

| Page Size | Offset Bits | TLB Entries to Cover 1 GB | L1 D-TLB (64 entries) Coverage |
|-----------|------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **4 KB** (standard) | 12 bits | 262,144 | 256 KB |
| **2 MB** (huge page) | 21 bits | 512 | **128 MB** |
| **1 GB** (gigantic page) | 30 bits | 1 | **64 GB** |

With 2 MB pages, a single TLB entry covers **512× more memory**. The same 64-entry L1 D-TLB now covers 128 MB instead of 256 KB. For a game engine streaming through a 2 GB texture array, this can mean the difference between 99% TLB hit rate and 90% TLB hit rate — which translates to 5-15% overall performance improvement.

### How to Use Huge Pages

**Linux Transparent Huge Pages (THP):**
The kernel automatically attempts to promote contiguous 4 KB pages to 2 MB huge pages without any application changes. If you allocate 2 MB of memory that happens to be physically contiguous, the kernel may collapse those 512 pages into a single 2 MB huge page.

> [!warning] The THP Tradeoff — It's Not Free
> **Memory bloat:** A 2 MB page is all-or-nothing. If your process uses only 10 KB of a 2 MB huge page, the other 2,038 KB is wasted. With 4 KB pages, you'd only waste 4 KB per unused page in the mapped range.
> 
> **Compaction latency:** The kernel periodically runs **memory compaction** — a process that physically moves pages around to create contiguous 2 MB blocks. This can cause unpredictable latency spikes (50-500 ms pauses). Many game server operators and latency-sensitive applications disable THP entirely (`echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`) because a random 200 ms pause is worse than a consistent 5% TLB miss overhead.

**Explicit Huge Pages (hugetlbfs):**
Pre-allocate huge pages at boot time and use them explicitly:

```bash
# Reserve 1024 huge pages of 2 MB each (2 GB total) at boot
echo 1024 > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages
```cpp
// Allocate from the reserved pool
void* buf = mmap(NULL, 2*1024*1024, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE,
                 MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANONYMOUS|MAP_HUGETLB, -1, 0);
// buf is now backed by a dedicated 2 MB huge page
```

This avoids the compaction latency problem but requires you to pre-plan your memory budget. Game engines with fixed-size texture pools or physics buffers are ideal candidates.

### 1 GB Pages

Available on CPUs with the `pdpe1gb` flag (most server CPUs since ~2012, all modern desktop CPUs). A single TLB entry covers 1 GB. But: you must reserve them at boot (the kernel can't allocate 1 GB of contiguous physical memory on the fly), and internal fragmentation is enormous. Used almost exclusively by databases and hypervisors for massive, long-lived allocations.

---


## Part 11: NUMA — Location Matters

### The Physical Reality of Multi-Socket Systems
