On multi-socket servers (two or more physical CPU packages on one motherboard), each CPU socket has its **own memory controller** and its **own bank of RAM** directly attached. This is **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)**.

```
┌──────────────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────┐
│    Socket 0 (CPU 0)      │     │    Socket 1 (CPU 1)      │
│    Cores 0-15            │     │    Cores 16-31           │
│                          │     │                          │
│  ┌────────────────────┐  │     │  ┌────────────────────┐  │
│  │   LOCAL RAM        │  │     │  │   LOCAL RAM        │  │
│  │   64 GB DDR5       │  │     │  │   64 GB DDR5       │  │
│  └────────┬───────────┘  │     │  └────────┬───────────┘  │
│           │              │     │           │              │
│     FAST (~100 ns)       │     │     FAST (~100 ns)      │
│           │              │     │           │              │
│           └──────────────┼─────┼───────────┘              │
│                          │ UPI │  (Intel) or Infinity     │
│                          │     │  Fabric (AMD)            │
│               SLOW (~150-250 ns, lower bandwidth)         │
└──────────────────────────┘     └──────────────────────────┘
```

- **Local access:** Core 0 reading RAM on Socket 0 → ~100 ns, full bandwidth (~38-51 GB/s per channel)
- **Remote access:** Core 0 reading RAM on Socket 1 → ~150-250 ns (1.5-2.5× slower), reduced bandwidth (contended inter-socket link)

### The Trap: First-Touch Policy

Linux uses a **first-touch** allocation policy: when a page is first written to, it's allocated from the NUMA node that the writing thread is running on. This means:

```cpp
// THE TRAP — data lands on the wrong NUMA node
// Thread runs on Socket 0 cores
char* data = new char[1_GB];

// Spawn worker threads bound to Socket 1...
// Worker threads now read data from Socket 1 → ALL accesses are REMOTE (slow)
```

Even if threads are pinned to sockets, the memory allocation happened on whatever socket the allocating thread was on. The fix: either allocate memory from the correct node explicitly (`numa_alloc_onnode()`), or use the **first-touch pattern deliberately** — touch (write to) each page from the thread that will primarily use it.

```cpp
// RIGHT: first-touch initialization on the target NUMA node
#pragma omp parallel for
for (size_t i = 0; i < num_elements; i++) {
    data[i] = 0;  // Each thread touches its portion → pages allocated locally
}
```

---


## Part 12: Memory Overcommit — The OS That Cries Wolf

### The Problem: Linux Says "Yes" to Everything

Linux has a feature (or bug, depending on who you ask) called **memory overcommit**. When you call `malloc(1_GB)` on a machine with 512 MB free, Linux says "Sure, here's your pointer!" — and `malloc` returns a non-NULL address. No physical memory is reserved. No swap space is reserved. The kernel simply marks the virtual address range as valid in the VMA tree and returns.

```cpp
// This "succeeds" with 512 MB free on a Linux machine
char* huge = (char*)malloc(10'000'000'000ULL);  // 10 GB
printf("malloc returned: %p
", huge);           // Not NULL!

// As long as you never write to most of it, nothing bad happens
huge[0] = 'X';  // Kernel gives you one 4 KB page — fine

// But if you touch ALL of it and there's no RAM and no swap left...
memset(huge, 0, 10'000'000'000ULL);  
// → KABOOM: OOM Killer activates, kills some process (maybe yours!)
```

### The OOM Killer — Arbitrator of Last Resort

When the kernel truly cannot satisfy a memory allocation (no free RAM, no clean pages to evict, swap is full), the **Out-Of-Memory Killer** activates. It assigns every process an **oom_score** based on heuristics:

- Higher score = more likely to be killed
- Large memory usage → higher score
- Recently started → higher score (less "investment" lost)
- Low priority (nice value) → higher score
- Parent of many child processes → higher score (kill one, free their combined memory)
- Running as root? Score reduced (but can still be killed)

The killer selects the highest-scoring process, sends SIGKILL, and hopes that frees enough memory. There is no negotiation. The kernel is panicking and must free memory NOW.

```bash
# Check your process's OOM score
cat /proc/self/oom_score

# Protect a critical process (like your game server) from OOM killer
echo -1000 > /proc/$(pidof myserver)/oom_score_adj  # Highly protected
echo 1000 > /proc/$(pidof mytool)/oom_score_adj     # First to die
```

**vm.overcommit_memory sysctl:**
- `0` (default): Heuristic overcommit — kernel estimates if RAM+swap can satisfy the request. Allows "reasonable" overcommit.
- `1`: "Yes" to everything, always. OOM Killer handles consequences. Use only if you love chaos.
- `2`: Never overcommit. `malloc` returns NULL if RAM+swap can't cover the request. Safer, but programs that don't check `malloc` return values will SEGFAULT.

> [!tip] How Game Engines Handle This
> Unreal Engine uses its own memory allocators (`FMallocBinned`, `FMallocAnsi`) that pre-allocate large blocks from the OS at startup and sub-allocate internally. This means:
> - The engine knows exactly how much physical memory it has committed
> - Individual actor allocations never cause OS-level page faults (they're sub-allocated from pre-committed pools)
> - Memory tracking (`stat memory`, `LLM`) gives precise visibility into where RAM is going
> - The engine can gracefully degrade (lower texture quality, unload distant levels) before hitting OS-level OOM

---


## The Boot Process — How the OS Gets Loaded

Before virtual memory exists, a chain of firmware loads the kernel:
1. **UEFI/BIOS** — Firmware on the motherboard. Initializes hardware, finds a boot device (SSD/HDD).
2. **Bootloader** (GRUB, Windows Boot Manager) — Reads the kernel image from disk into RAM. Sets up an initial, identity-mapped page table (virtual address = physical address).
3. **Kernel** — Takes over. Creates the real page tables. Enables paging (sets CR3 register). Spawns the first user process (`init`/`systemd`/`smss.exe`).
4. **init** — Launches user-space: login screen, desktop, services.

After step 3, virtual memory is active — every address your program sees is virtual.



## Exercises

1. **TLB coverage with huge pages:** Your game's render thread touches a 2 GB scene data structure scattered across many pages. Calculate the TLB miss rate with 4 KB pages (L1 D-TLB: 64 entries, L2 STLB: 1,536 entries). Now calculate with 2 MB huge pages. If each TLB miss costs 80 ns and you execute 5×10⁸ memory accesses per frame at 30 FPS, what is the total TLB miss overhead per frame with each page size?

2. **First-touch NUMA trap:** A 2-socket server has 64 GB local RAM per socket. The main thread on Socket 0 allocates a 32 GB array with `new`, then spawns 16 worker threads pinned to Socket 1. All worker threads read from the array. Where does the physical memory get allocated? What latency does each worker experience? How would you fix this — show the initialization pattern that ensures local access.

3. **OOM Killer simulation:** A Linux machine has 8 GB RAM and 2 GB swap. Process A uses 6 GB, Process B uses 3 GB, Process C uses 1 GB. A memory leak in Process B causes it to try to touch an additional 3 GB. Walk through what the kernel does: can it satisfy the allocation? When does the OOM Killer activate? Which process is most likely to be killed and why — consider memory usage, runtime, and `oom_score_adj`.

4. **THP compaction latency tradeoff:** Transparent Huge Pages can improve TLB coverage by 512× but the kernel's memory compaction daemon causes unpredictable 200 ms latency spikes. A game server operator tells you "I disabled THP because a 200 ms spike kills gameplay more than a steady 5% TLB miss overhead." Do you agree? Calculate: at 60 FPS, a single 200 ms pause = how many dropped frames? TLB misses at 5% overhead reduce FPS from 60 to what? Which is worse for a fighting game? For a turn-based strategy game?

5. **Overcommit design:** You're designing a game engine memory allocator. Linux defaults to `vm.overcommit_memory=0` (heuristic overcommit). Your engine pre-allocates 8 GB at startup using `mmap(MAP_NORESERVE)`. Explain what `MAP_NORESERVE` does differently from regular `mmap`. If your engine touches all 8 GB and the system has 4 GB free + 0 swap, what happens? How does Unreal Engine's approach (pre-allocating large blocks, sub-allocating internally) avoid this problem entirely?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Huge pages are like replacing your 64-slot sticky note (TLB) with a 64-slot whiteboard — each slot now describes a continent instead of a city block. Your 2 GB game world fits in 1,000 entries instead of 500,000 — suddenly everything fits on the board and you stop running to the filing cabinet (page table walk). NUMA: imagine a two-wing office building. Your desk is in the West wing. Files in the West wing filing room take 1 minute to retrieve. Files in the East wing filing room take 2.5 minutes — same company, but crossing the skybridge costs time. The first-touch trap: you hire an assistant who orders all your files delivered to whichever wing they're standing in when they place the order — even if all the workers who'll use them sit in the other wing. Memory overcommit: Linux is a bank that approves every loan application without checking your credit. "You want $10 million with $500K in your account? Approved!" You only get in trouble when you actually try to spend it — then the repo man (OOM Killer) shows up and takes someone's car.

### Technical
Huge pages (2 MB and 1 GB on x86-64) reduce the page table depth and multiply TLB coverage. A 2 MB page uses 21 offset bits (vs 12 for 4 KB), so a single PTE covers 512× more memory; the same 64-entry L1 D-TLB covers 128 MB instead of 256 KB. Transparent Huge Pages (THP) in Linux automatically promotes contiguous 4 KB pages to 2 MB, but memory compaction (physically relocating pages to create 2 MB contiguous blocks) can cause latency spikes of 50-500 ms — many latency-sensitive applications disable THP. Explicit huge pages (`hugetlbfs`) avoid compaction by pre-reserving pages at boot, at the cost of internal fragmentation (a 2 MB page used for 10 KB wastes 2,038 KB). NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) arises on multi-socket systems where each CPU socket has its own memory controller and locally-attached RAM — local access is ~100 ns, remote access 150-250 ns via inter-socket links (UPI/Infinity Fabric). Linux's first-touch policy allocates physical pages from the NUMA node of the thread that first writes to them, requiring deliberate initialization on target cores. Memory overcommit (`vm.overcommit_memory=0` heuristic default on Linux) allows `malloc` to succeed even when RAM+swap can't cover the allocation; physical pages are only committed on first write. The OOM Killer activates when the kernel cannot satisfy a page fault after exhausting all RAM, clean cache pages, and swap — it selects a victim based on `oom_score` heuristics (memory usage, runtime, priority) and sends SIGKILL. Game engines avoid this by pre-allocating fixed pools at startup and sub-allocating internally, ensuring they never request OS pages they can't back.

## See Also

- [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — Why the TLB needs huge pages
- [[01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]] — The physical DRAM that NUMA organizes
- [[03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]] — Swap is what the OOM Killer is trying to avoid

RAM & VM \u2014 Chapter Summary.content:
# RAM & Virtual Memory — Chapter Summary

> [!info] Prerequisites
> Read [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview]], [[01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]], [[02 — Page Tables & TLB]], [[03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]], and [[04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]] before this summary.

## Why This Chapter Matters

RAM is the workspace where all computation happens. Virtual memory is the illusion that every process has infinite workspace. Together, they're the most performance-critical subsystem in your computer — a single DRAM access costs 300 CPU cycles, and a major page fault costs 30,000,000.

This chapter answers: why does `std::vector` outperform `std::list` by 10×? Why does a pointer maze destroy your frame rate? Why do cloud servers charge more for "dedicated" instances? The answer is always the same: DRAM physics and virtual memory overhead.

---

## The Memory Illusion

```
Your Program Sees:
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│  0x0000000000000000                 │
│  ... (48 bits of virtual space)     │
│  0x00007FFFFFFFFFFF                 │
└────────────────────────────────────┘

What Actually Exists:
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Physical RAM: 16 GB                │
│  Swap on disk: 8 GB                 │
│  Each process: its own page table   │
│  Kernel maps and unmaps on demand   │
└────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## The Five Pillars

| Pillar | Note | Core Concept |
|--------|------|-------------|
| **Physics** | 01 — DRAM Cells | 1T1C cells, destructive reads, refresh, row hammer |
| **Translation** | 02 — Page Tables & TLB | 4-level paging, TLB caching, page walks |
| **Demand** | 03 — Page Faults & mmap | Lazy allocation, COW, swap, thrashing |
| **Scale** | 04 — Huge Pages & NUMA | 2MB/1GB pages, multi-socket topology, overcommit |
| **Big Picture** | 00 — Overview | The memory wall, pointer maze trap, mmap trap |

---

## The Memory Wall in One Diagram

```
CPU Core @ 5 GHz
  │  1 cycle = 0.2 ns
  │  L1 hit: 4-5 cycles (1 ns)
  │  L2 hit: 12-14 cycles (3 ns)
  │  L3 hit: 40-50 cycles (10 ns)
  │
  ▼  ======== THE MEMORY WALL ========
  │
  │  DRAM access: ~350 cycles (100 ns)
  │  TLB miss + page walk: ~500-1500 cycles
  │  Page fault (minor): ~3,000 cycles (1 µs)
  │  Page fault (major, NVMe): ~150,000 cycles (50 µs)
  │  Page fault (major, HDD): ~30,000,000 cycles (10 ms)
```

Every performance optimization in a game engine — pool allocators, data-oriented design, texture streaming, LOD systems — is ultimately about avoiding these penalties.

---

## The Three Deadly Patterns

### Pattern 1: The Pointer Maze

```cpp
// DEATH: each object in a different 4 KB page
std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Enemy>> enemies;  
// 10,000 enemies = 10,000 TLB entries needed
// L1 D-TLB has 64 entries → 99.4% miss rate

// LIFE: contiguous allocation
std::vector<Enemy> enemies;  // 100 enemies per 4 KB page
// 10,000 enemies = 100 TLB entries needed → much better
```

### Pattern 2: First-Touch NUMA Trap

```cpp
// Main thread on Socket 0 allocates and initializes
float* data = new float[1_GB];  // Allocated on Socket 0's RAM
// Workers on Socket 1 access data → 150-250 ns per access (remote)
// Fix: have Socket 1 workers touch data first
```

### Pattern 3: Swap Thrashing

When working set > physical RAM, every frame triggers major page faults. The game doesn't slow down — it stops. `mlock` can prevent this for critical buffers, but over-locking starves the rest of the system.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

RAM is a grid of billions of tiny buckets (capacitors) that each hold one bit. The buckets leak, so they need constant refreshing. Virtual memory is the OS's magic trick: each program thinks it has its own private RAM starting at address 0, but really the OS is constantly shuffling pages between physical RAM and disk. The TLB is a cheat sheet — "page 42 of program A is at physical location 0x1A3F000" — so the CPU doesn't have to look up the full mapping every time. A page fault isn't a crash; it's the OS saying "give me a moment to fetch that page." Huge pages make the cheat sheet cover more territory at once. NUMA means "not all RAM is equally close" on multi-CPU servers — accessing the other CPU's RAM costs extra.

### Technical

DRAM cells are 1T1C (one transistor, one trench capacitor). Reads are destructive: the sense amplifier detects voltage, then writes back. Refresh cycles (tREFI ≈ 7.8 µs per row) prevent charge leakage from corrupting data. The MMU translates 48-bit virtual addresses through a 4-level page table (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT, 9 bits per level) to 52-bit physical addresses. Each PTE is 8 bytes; 512 PTEs fit in a 4 KB page, hence the 9-bit field width. The TLB is a fully-associative CAM caching VA→PA translations: L1 D-TLB (64 entries, 1 cycle) and L2 STLB (1536 entries, 8-12 cycles). Page faults are classified by the kernel's fault handler: minor (page in RAM but PTE not present — COW, demand paging), major (page on disk — file I/O or swap), invalid (SIGSEGV). `mmap` creates VMA entries but defers physical allocation to first access. Swap uses a two-level LRU eviction policy on anonymous pages, writing them to swap slots and encoding the location in the PTE. Huge pages (2 MB/1 GB) reduce page table depth and multiply TLB coverage: the same 64-entry L1 D-TLB covers 128 MB instead of 256 KB. NUMA domains are defined by ACPI SRAT/SLIT tables; the kernel's first-touch policy allocates pages from the node of the first-writer thread. Memory overcommit (`vm.overcommit_memory`) allows `malloc` to succeed beyond RAM+swap capacity; the OOM Killer selects victims when page faults can't be satisfied.

---

## Exercises

1. **Memory wall calculation:** At 5 GHz, the CPU executes 20 instructions per nanosecond (IPC=4). A single DRAM access takes 100 ns. How many instructions could execute during one DRAM access? If a game loop makes 10⁸ memory accesses per frame and 1% miss L3, how many total cycles are lost to DRAM per frame? At 60 FPS, what percentage of frame time is DRAM stalls?

2. **TLB pressure:** Your rendering system stores 50,000 draw calls, each in a separate allocation (fragmented). Each draw call struct is 64 bytes. With 4 KB pages, how many pages does this span? Can the 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover it? How about the 1536-entry L2 STLB? If each TLB miss costs 80 ns and you iterate all draw calls, what is the total TLB overhead?

3. **Swap design:** Your game server has 64 GB RAM and runs 8 game instances of 10 GB each (80 GB total). The machine has 16 GB swap on NVMe. Calculate the page fault rate under round-robin scheduling if each instance's working set is (a) 8 GB, (b) 6 GB. At what working set size does thrashing begin?

4. **NUMA topology:** A dual-socket server has 128 GB per socket. Your physics system allocates 40 GB of rigid body data on Socket 0 but runs physics workers on Socket 1. Remote access costs 200 ns vs 100 ns local. If the physics loop makes 5×10⁷ memory accesses at 30 FPS, how much time is lost to remote access? Rewrite the initialization to fix this.

5. **Design:** Design a memory allocator for a game engine that (a) pre-allocates large blocks from the OS, (b) sub-allocates within those blocks for game objects, (c) uses huge pages for the block backing, and (d) pins critical blocks with `mlock` to prevent swap. What are the tradeoffs — internal fragmentation, startup time, flexibility?

## See Also

- [[../Hardware — Chapter Summary]] — The DRAM hardware context
- [[../Storage/Storage — Chapter Summary]] — Where swapped pages go
- [[../../Concurrency/Concurrency — Chapter Summary]] — Multi-threaded memory access
- [[../../Learning Path]] — Where to go next

01 \u2014 HDD Mechanics.content:
# HDD Mechanics
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|Storage]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[00 — Storage Overview]] — context on why storage latency matters.

## Part 1: The Hard Disk Drive — Magnets, Motors, and Milliseconds

### What's Inside an HDD — The Physical Machine

Open a hard drive (don't — a speck of dust will destroy it, but imagine it). Inside a sealed, dust-free chamber filled with inert gas (helium in modern high-capacity drives), you'll find:

**The Platters.**
One or more circular discs of aluminum or glass, coated on both sides with a thin film of magnetic material — typically a cobalt-platinum-chromium alloy just a few nanometers thick. These platters spin continuously at **5,400, 7,200, 10,000, or 15,000 RPM** (revolutions per minute). A 7,200 RPM drive completes one full rotation every **8.33 milliseconds**.

**The Read/Write Heads.**
For each platter surface, there's a tiny electromagnetic head mounted on the tip of an actuator arm. This head doesn't touch the platter — it flies on a cushion of air at a height of roughly **3 nanometers**. To put that in perspective: a human hair is ~80,000 nanometers thick. The head is flying closer to the platter than the height of the magnetic grains it's reading. If it touches — a **head crash** — the platter is gouged, data destroyed, and the drive is dead.

**The Actuator Arm.**
All heads are mounted on a common arm assembly that pivots like a record player tonearm, swinging the heads across the platter surfaces in an arc. A voice coil motor (the same electromagnetic principle as a speaker driver) positions the arm with nanometer precision.

**How Data Is Stored.**
The magnetic coating on the platters is divided into billions of microscopic regions. Each region can be magnetized in one of two directions — representing a `0` or a `1`. To write, the head generates a strong localized magnetic field that flips the orientation of the grains in that region. To read, the head senses the magnetic field transitions as the platter spins underneath — a changing magnetic field induces a tiny voltage in the head's coil. No physical contact. No moving electrons (beyond the current in the coil). Pure magnetism.

### The Three Operations That Kill HDD Performance

Every HDD access requires three sequential mechanical operations:

**1. Seek — Finding the Right Track**

The actuator arm must swing the head to the correct **track** (one of the concentric circles on the platter surface). This is a physical movement — a precision motor accelerating an arm, coasting, then decelerating to a precise stop. 

- Average seek time (consumer 7,200 RPM drive): **8-9 milliseconds**
- That's 40-45 million CPU cycles at 5 GHz
- The arm literally moves. You can hear it — that clicking/chattering sound is the actuator seeking.

**2. Rotational Latency — Waiting for the Right Sector**

Once the head is on the right track, it must wait for the correct **sector** (a small arc of the track) to spin underneath. On average, you wait half a rotation.

- At 7,200 RPM: half rotation = **4.17 milliseconds**
- At 15,000 RPM (enterprise): half rotation = **2 milliseconds**

**3. Transfer — Actually Reading Data**

Once the correct sector is under the head, the drive reads the magnetic transitions as they fly past. The data rate depends on how fast the platter is spinning and how densely the bits are packed:

- Sequential read speed: **150-280 MB/s** (modern consumer HDD)
- A single 4 KB sector at 250 MB/s: **~16 microseconds** of transfer time

**Total for one random 4 KB read:** 8 ms (seek) + 4 ms (rotational) + 0.016 ms (transfer) ≈ **12 milliseconds**. The transfer time is negligible — 99.9% of the latency is mechanical. The CPU could have executed **60 million instructions** waiting for those 4 KB.

> [!info] Analogy — A Warehouse Filing System
> An HDD is a massive warehouse with spinning carousels of file folders (platters). A mechanical arm (actuator) moves to the correct carousel row (seek), then waits for the correct folder to rotate into position (rotational latency), then reads the papers as they pass by (transfer). Finding one random sheet takes 12 seconds (12 ms scaled up). Reading 100 sheets from the same folder location takes barely any extra time — because the arm is already there and the carousel keeps spinning. This is why sequential access is 100× faster than random access on an HDD: you only pay the seek + rotation penalty ONCE for an entire track's worth of data.

### The Geometry That Makes Sequential Access Fast, Random Access Slow

Data on an HDD is physically laid out in concentric circles. Reading sequentially means the head stays on the same track, and sectors flow under the head one after another as the platter spins — you read 500+ sectors per rotation at zero additional seek cost. This is where HDDs achieve their rated 250 MB/s throughput.

Random access means the head must physically move to a different track for every 4 KB — each movement costs 12 ms. At 12 ms per I/O, you get approximately **83 random reads per second** (IOPS). At 4 KB each, that's **~330 KB/s** — less than 0.2% of the sequential throughput. This 600:1 ratio is the defining characteristic of HDDs.

```
Sequential 4K reads (same track):  250 MB/s  (1,500,000+ sectors/second)
Random 4K reads (different tracks):  ~0.33 MB/s  (83 sectors/second)

Ratio: ~750:1
```

---


## Part 4: Block Devices — The OS Reads Sectors, Not Bytes

### Why Sectors Exist

A storage device presents itself to the OS as a **block device** — a linear array of numbered sectors, each typically **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4,096 bytes** (modern "Advanced Format" / 4K native). You cannot read or write less than one sector. Even if you only want 1 byte, the OS reads the entire sector containing that byte.

```cpp
// You ask for 1 byte
char c;
pread(fd, &c, 1, 1000000);  // Read 1 byte at file offset 1,000,000

// What actually happens:
// 1. OS calculates which disk sector contains byte 1,000,000
// 2. Issues a read for that entire sector (4,096 bytes)
// 3. Extracts byte 1,000,000 from the sector
// 4. Returns it to you
// 5. Caches the other 4,095 bytes in the page cache
//    → Your next read of byte 1,000,001 is free (page cache hit)
```

### Logical vs Physical Sector Size — The Alignment Trap

**Logical sector size:** What the OS thinks the sector size is. Usually 512 bytes for backward compatibility.

**Physical sector size:** What the drive actually uses internally. 4,096 bytes on all modern drives.

When logical = 512 and physical = 4,096, the drive does **read-modify-write** internally:

```
OS writes 512 bytes to "sector 7" (logical 512-byte sector)
  ↓
Drive must:
  1. Read the physical 4,096-byte sector containing logical sectors 0-7
  2. Modify bytes 3584-4095 (logical sector 7's position within the physical sector)
  3. For HDD: write the modified 4,096 bytes back (just overwrite magnetic pattern)
     For SSD: need to read entire block, modify, erase block, write block → write amplification nightmare
```

This is why **partition alignment** matters. If your partitions start on a boundary that's not a multiple of the physical sector size, every read-modify-write operation spans TWO physical sectors instead of one, doubling the overhead. Modern partitioning tools align to 1 MB by default, which is safe for any reasonable sector size.

---


## Part 8: Random vs Sequential I/O — The Gap That Defines Storage Design

### The Numbers (2024-2025 Hardware)

| | HDD Sequential | HDD Random 4K | NVMe Gen4 Sequential | NVMe Gen4 Random 4K |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | 250 MB/s | ~0.33 MB/s | 7,000 MB/s | ~3,000-4,000 MB/s |
| **IOPS** | ~200 | ~83 | 1,500,000+ | ~800,000-1,000,000 |
| **Why slow?** | Seek + rotation (~12 ms per random I/O) | Same | NAND dies can work in parallel | Command overhead, NAND page granularity, queue depth saturation |
| **Ratio sequential:random** | ~750:1 | — | ~2:1 | — |

> [!important] Where the Gaps Come From
> **HDD (750:1):** Every random 4 KB read forces the actuator arm to physically move (8 ms seek) + wait for rotation (4 ms) = 12 ms per I/O. If you're reading 4 KB chunks scattered across the disk, you get 1 ÷ 0.012 = ~83 reads per second = 332 KB/s. Sequential reads eliminate the mechanical overhead entirely — the head stays on one track, sectors stream by continuously at 250 MB/s. The gap is entirely mechanical.
>
> **SSD (2:1):** There's no mechanical seek, but random reads still can't use the full parallelism of the SSD. With sequential reads, the controller can pipeline reads across all NAND dies simultaneously — die 0 reads page 0, die 1 reads page 1, etc. With random reads, multiple concurrent reads compete for the same NAND dies, and the FTL must translate each LBA individually. The overhead is in command management and die contention, not physics. You can improve random I/O throughput by increasing queue depth (more concurrent commands → more dies utilized), but latency per command goes up.

### What This Means for Game Loading

**The trap:** loading 500,000 small files individually.

```
// THE TRAP: Loading textures as individual files
for (const auto& texturePath : texturePaths) {
    FILE* f = fopen(texturePath, "rb");
    fread(buffer, 1, textureSize, f);  // ~500,000 fopen + fread calls
    fclose(f);
}
// HDD: 500,000 × 84 ms (file open + 1 read) = 42,000 seconds = 11.7 HOURS
// NVMe: 500,000 × 0.35 ms = 175 seconds = ~3 minutes (for what should take 5 seconds)

// THE RIGHT WAY: Pack everything into one large archive file
// fopen("textures.pak") → 1 file open cost
// Sequential read of the entire archive → full disk bandwidth
// HDD: 10 GB ÷ 250 MB/s = 40 seconds
// NVMe: 10 GB ÷ 7,000 MB/s = 1.4 seconds
```

This is why virtually every game engine packs assets into archives (`.pak` in Unreal, `.resources` in Unity, `.vpk` in Source). One big sequential read beats thousands of random reads by orders of magnitude.

---


## Part 9: Append-Only Patterns — Why Writing at the End Is Fast

### The Physics That Makes Append-Only Fast

**HDD:** The write head is already positioned at the end of the file (assuming no intervening seeks). Writing 4 KB takes ~16 µs (transfer time only) — no seek, no rotational latency. Compare to an in-place update that requires seeking to the middle of the file: 12 ms seek + 16 µs transfer = 750× slower.

**SSD:** Data is always written to new, pre-erased pages (out-of-place writes, thanks to the FTL). An append just extends into the next free page — no garbage collection needed, no read-modify-write. An in-place update triggers the whole FTL nightmare: mark old page stale, write to new page, eventually trigger GC on the block containing the stale page.

```
// APPEND-ONLY LOG (fast):
// Current file size = 1 GB. Writing 4 KB.
// HDD: head is at end of file (or near it). 4 KB write ≈ 0.04 ms (negligible)
// SSD: controller writes to next free page in the current open block. ~200 µs.

// IN-PLACE UPDATE (slow):
// Seeking to byte offset 524,288,000 (500 MB in). Writing 4 KB.
// HDD: 8 ms seek + 4 ms rotational + 0.04 ms write = 12 ms
// SSD: old page marked stale, new page written, eventually GC. Write amp >1×.
```

### Where This Shows Up in Games

**Replay systems:** Every frame's player inputs are appended to a binary log file. The file grows sequentially — writes are essentially free. Replaying means reading the log linearly from the beginning — also sequential, also fast.

**Save systems:** Rather than patching specific fields in an existing save file, serialize the entire game state to a memory buffer, compress it, and write it as one atomic blob (write to a temp file, fsync, then rename over the old save file — atomic on most filesystems). This is a single sequential write instead of hundreds of random seeks.

**Unreal Engine Cooking:** The cooker packs thousands of individual asset files into a handful of `.pak` archives. At runtime, the engine memory-maps the `.pak` and accesses assets through the archive's internal index. Loading a level = one big sequential read of the relevant chunks from the `.pak` file.

---


## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A hard drive is a record player with magnetic platters instead of vinyl. The read/write head floats nanometers above the spinning platter on a cushion of air (like a jet ski). Data lives in concentric circles (tracks) divided into pie slices (sectors). To read a file, the arm swings to the right track (seek), waits for the right sector to spin under the head (rotational latency), then reads the bits as magnetic polarity changes.

### Technical
Platters: 5400-15000 RPM. Seek time: arm movement = 2-15ms (full stroke). Rotational latency: 0.5/RPM = 2-5.5ms average. Transfer rate: 100-250 MB/s. Sectors: 512B (legacy) or 4KB (AF). Zone Bit Recording: outer tracks have more sectors (constant angular density). SMART: Self-Monitoring Analysis Reporting — predicts failures. Free-fall sensor: parks heads on acceleration.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** A 7,200 RPM drive rotates once every 8.33 ms. Why is the average rotational latency ~4.17 ms and not 8.33 ms? What's the worst case, and why does the average converge to half the rotation time?

2. **Code:** Write a benchmark that reads 4 KB from 10,000 random offsets vs 10,000 sequential offsets on the same file. On HDD, the random run measures seek + rotational latency; the sequential run measures transfer rate. Calculate the actual seek time from the difference. Warning: the page cache will interfere — be sure to drop caches between runs.

3. **Analysis:** Zone Bit Recording places more sectors on outer tracks. A 4 TB drive has 6 platters (12 surfaces) at 7,200 RPM. If the outermost track holds 2× the sectors of the innermost, by what percentage does sequential read throughput drop from the outermost track to the innermost? Why do benchmark tools report lower speeds on a full drive?

4. **Real-world:** The note says a free-fall sensor parks the heads on acceleration. If a laptop falls from a desk (0.8 m), how much time does the sensor have to park the heads before impact? Does the voice coil motor have enough time? (Calculate using d = 0.5gt².)

5. **Design:** The append-only log is ~3,000× faster than in-place updates on HDD. Design a database storage engine that never does in-place updates on HDD — only appends. How do you handle deletions? How do you reclaim space from deleted records without random writes?

## See Also

- [[02 — NAND Flash & SSDs]] — Why SSDs don't have these mechanical problems
- [[04 — Filesystems & Page Cache]] — How filesystems exploit sequential access

02 \u2014 NAND Flash & SSDs.content:
# NAND Flash & SSDs
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|Storage]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[01 — HDD Mechanics]] — understand the mechanical alternative to appreciate SSD physics.

## Part 2: The Solid State Drive — No Moving Parts, New Problems

### NAND Flash — Trapping Electrons in Silicon

An SSD stores data in **NAND flash** chips — pure semiconductor devices with no moving parts. Each bit is stored as the presence or absence of electrons trapped in a **floating gate** — a conductive layer completely surrounded by insulating oxide, buried inside a MOSFET transistor.

```
                    Control Gate (top)
                         │
                    ─────┼───── Oxide insulator
                         │
                    ┌────┴────┐
                    │ Floating │ ← Electrons trapped here? Bit = 0.
                    │  Gate    │    No electrons? Bit = 1.
                    └────┬────┘
                         │
                    ─────┼───── Tunnel oxide (thin insulator)
                         │
                    Source ─┴─ Drain (silicon substrate)
```

To **program (write) a 0**, a high voltage is applied to the control gate. Through quantum tunneling (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling), electrons punch through the thin tunnel oxide and get stuck in the floating gate. Once there, they can't escape — the oxide completely surrounds the gate. The trapped electrons change the transistor's threshold voltage, and this voltage difference is what's sensed during a read.

To **erase (set back to 1)**, a high voltage is applied to the substrate while the control gate is grounded. The electrons tunnel back out of the floating gate. This is a bulk operation — it must be done to an entire **block** of cells at once (more on this in a moment).

### The Hierarchy: Pages, Blocks, and the Erase-Before-Write Problem

NAND flash is organized in a strict hierarchy:

```
NAND Chip (or "target")
 └── Die (independent semiconductor piece; an SSD has multiple dies)
      └── Plane (independent set of blocks with its own page buffer)
           └── Block (the ERASE unit — typically 512 to 2,048 pages)
                ├── Page (the READ/WRITE unit — 4 KB to 16 KB of data)
                │    └── Spare area (ECC bytes, bad block markers, metadata)
                ├── Page
                ├── ...
                └── Page
```

**The crucial asymmetry — this is the defining fact of NAND flash:**

| Operation | Granularity | Latency (TLC, typical) |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Read** | Per page | ~25-100 µs |
| **Program (Write)** | Per page, ONLY to erased pages | ~200-1,500 µs |
| **Erase** | Per BLOCK (hundreds of pages) | ~1,500-10,000 µs (1.5-10 ms) |

> [!warning] You Cannot Overwrite a NAND Page
> You read that correctly. Once data is written to a NAND page, you cannot change even a single byte. The page must be erased first — and erasing destroys the entire block containing that page. If you want to modify 4 KB inside a 2 MB block (512 pages × 4 KB), the SSD controller must:
> 1. Read all 511 valid pages from the block into an internal SRAM buffer
> 2. Update the 4 KB you changed in the buffer
> 3. Find a newly-erased block
> 4. Write all 512 pages (2 MB) to the new block
> 5. Update the FTL mapping: "that logical address now lives at this new physical block"
> 6. Mark the old block for erasure (garbage collection, later)
> 
> Your 4 KB write triggered a **2 MB write**. This is **write amplification** — the single most important concept for understanding SSD performance. A 4 KB user write can become 500× that internally.

> [!info] Analogy — A Whiteboard That Can't Be Erased Per Word
> Imagine a whiteboard divided into 512 rows. You can read any row instantly. You can write on a blank row. But you cannot erase a single row — when you want to erase, you must erase the ENTIRE whiteboard at once. If you want to change one word on row 47, you must: copy every other row to a new whiteboard, write your changed row 47 onto the new board, then throw away the old whiteboard. The SSD controller is a very clever assistant who hides all this complexity from you, juggling multiple whiteboards behind the scenes.

### SLC, MLC, TLC, QLC — The Density/Cost/Durability Tradeoff

NAND flash cells can store more than one bit per cell by distinguishing multiple levels of trapped charge:

| Type | Bits per Cell | Voltage Levels to Distinguish | P/E Cycles (Endurance) | Typical Use |
|------|--------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **SLC** | 1 | 2 (charged/not charged) | 50,000-100,000 | Enterprise, industrial, cache buffers |
| **MLC** | 2 | 4 | 3,000-10,000 | Prosumer, older enterprise |
| **TLC** | 3 | 8 | 1,000-3,000 | Mainstream consumer (most common in 2024-2025) |
| **QLC** | 4 | 16 | 500-1,000 | Budget consumer, read-intensive |
| **PLC** (emerging) | 5 | 32 | <500 | Early experimental |

**Why more bits per cell means lower endurance:**
To store 4 bits (16 distinct levels), the cell must distinguish between 16 different amounts of trapped charge. The voltage windows between levels are tiny — measured in millivolts. Each program/erase cycle damages the tunnel oxide slightly, making it harder to hold a precise charge level. After ~1,000 cycles for TLC, the oxide is too degraded to reliably distinguish all 8 levels. SLC, with only 2 levels (just "charged enough" vs "not charged enough"), tolerates 50-100× more wear because the threshold is much fuzzier.

### The Flash Translation Layer (FTL) — The SSD's Secret Brain

The FTL is firmware running on the SSD's controller chip. It's the abstraction layer that makes a block-erasure device look like a simple array of logical blocks to the OS. Its three jobs:

**1. Address Mapping.**
The OS issues reads and writes using Logical Block Addresses (LBAs) — "read block 42." The FTL maintains a massive indirection table mapping LBA 42 → "physical NAND die 2, plane 0, block 15, page 23." When you overwrite LBA 42, the FTL doesn't modify the old physical page — it writes the new data to a fresh page, updates the mapping table, and marks the old page as stale.

**2. Garbage Collection (GC).**
Stale pages accumulate. When the pool of free, erased blocks runs low, the garbage collector:
- Selects a "victim block" with many stale pages
- Reads all still-valid pages from that block
- Writes them to a new block (more write amplification!)
- Erases the victim block (now all stale → all free)
- Returns the erased block to the free pool

GC is the main source of **write amplification**. If a block has 20% valid pages and 80% stale pages, the GC must copy those 20% valid pages to keep them alive before erasing the block. Those valid page copies are "extra" writes the user didn't request.

**3. Wear Leveling.**
The FTL ensures all NAND blocks wear evenly. If you repeatedly write to the same logical address (e.g., a frequently-updated log file), the FTL maps each write to a *different* physical block, spreading the P/E cycles across the entire drive. Without wear leveling, the first few blocks would die after weeks of heavy use while the rest of the drive remained pristine. With wear leveling, a consumer TLC drive can survive **5-10 years** of typical desktop use despite individual cells being rated for only 1,000-3,000 P/E cycles.

### Why SSDs Slow Down When Full

When the drive is 90% full, there are very few free, pre-erased blocks. Every write operation now triggers garbage collection:

```cpp
// Writing 1 GB to a 90% full SSD (reality, not the marketed number):
// 1. Controller needs ~250 free blocks (1 GB ÷ 4 MB per block, typical)
// 2. Only ~50 blocks are actually free
// 3. GC must reclaim 200 more blocks
// 4. For each reclaimed block with 30% valid data: must copy ~1.2 MB of valid data
// 5. 200 blocks × 1.2 MB = 240 MB of extra writes (internal)
// 6. Plus the original 1 GB write = 1.24 GB total written to NAND
// 7. Write amplification: 1.24× (not terrible in this example, but can spike to 5-10×)
//
// Real-world result: throughput drops from 7,000 MB/s to ~500-1,500 MB/s
```

> [!tip] The Over-Provisioning Solution
> A "1 TB" consumer SSD typically contains ~1,024 GB of raw NAND but only exposes 1,000 GB to the OS. The hidden 24 GB (~2.4%) is **over-provisioning**: a dedicated pool of empty blocks the controller can use for garbage collection without touching user-visible space. Enterprise SSDs often over-provision by 20-30%. You can achieve the same effect by simply leaving 10-20% of your consumer SSD unpartitioned or as unallocated space — the controller will use those NAND blocks as additional spare area.

---


## DMA — Direct Memory Access

DMA is a hardware feature that allows devices (SSD, GPU, network card) to read/write RAM **without the CPU copying every byte.**

**Without DMA:** CPU runs a loop: `while (bytes_left) { *ram++ = *disk++; }` — wasted cycles.
**With DMA:** CPU tells the DMA controller "copy N bytes from disk to RAM address X." The DMA controller does the transfer while the CPU runs other threads. When done, the device sends an interrupt.

This is why `read()` can block without burning CPU — the scheduler switches to another thread while DMA transfers data in the background. The CPU is only involved at the start (issuing the read command) and end (handling the completion interrupt).




## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
NAND flash is a grid of trapped electrons — no moving parts. A cell stores charge in a floating gate (like a tiny bucket). Reading is checking the bucket's water level. Writing is filling or emptying the bucket — but you can only empty the ENTIRE row at once (erase block). SSDs hide this with a translator (FTL) that pretends you can write anywhere, while behind the scenes it shuffles data and erases in bulk (garbage collection).

### Technical
Floating gate transistor: charge trapped in isolated conductor changes threshold voltage. SLC (1 bit): 2 voltage levels. MLC (2): 4 levels. TLC (3): 8 levels. QLC (4): 16 levels — lower endurance per cell. FTL: logical block → physical block mapping. Write amplification: actual writes / requested writes (1-3x typical). Garbage collection: compact valid pages, erase stale blocks. TRIM: OS tells SSD which blocks are free before GC runs. Wear leveling: distribute writes evenly across cells.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** The note says "you cannot overwrite a NAND page." Why, at the physics level? What specific property of the floating-gate transistor makes erasing require a bulk block operation instead of a per-page operation?

2. **Code:** Write a program that writes 4 KB to the same file offset 100,000 times in a loop. Run it on an SSD. Use a SMART monitoring tool (`smartctl`) before and after to measure total bytes written (TBW). Compare the SMART-reported NAND writes to the user-facing writes. Calculate the write amplification factor. Why is it > 1?

3. **Analysis:** A TLC cell stores 3 bits = 8 voltage levels within a ~3V range. What's the voltage gap between adjacent levels? If the tunnel oxide degrades by ~1 mV per P/E cycle, after how many cycles does the gap shrink enough that two adjacent levels overlap? How does the SSD controller detect this before it causes data loss?

4. **Real-world:** You're designing a write-heavy application (a video recording server writing 500 MB/s continuously). The SSD has 1,200 TBW endurance and a 5-year warranty. Is the drive sufficient? Calculate: 500 MB/s × 86,400 seconds/day × 365 days/year × 5 years vs 1,200 TBW. What over-provisioning strategy would extend its life?

5. **Design:** The FTL uses an LBA→PBA indirection table. With 4 KB pages and a 1 TB drive, how many entries are in this table? If each entry is 4 bytes, what's the total table size? How does the FTL map a 64-bit LBA to a table entry efficiently (hint: you can't do a linear scan of 250 million entries)?

## See Also

- [[01 — HDD Mechanics]] — The mechanical predecessor
- [[03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]] — The interfaces that connect SSDs to the CPU
- [[04 — Filesystems & Page Cache]] — How the OS caches SSD data

03 \u2014 NVMe, SATA & PCIe.content:
# NVMe, SATA & PCIe
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|Storage]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[02 — NAND Flash & SSDs]] — understand what NVMe and SATA are connecting to.

## Part 3: NVMe vs SATA — The Interface Matters

### SATA: A Protocol Built for Spinning Rust

SATA (Serial ATA) was designed in the early 2000s to replace parallel ATA ribbon cables. It was built for **hard drives**. The SATA specification assumes a device with one queue (because an HDD can only do one thing at a time — the head is in one place) and a command queue depth of **32 entries** max. The AHCI (Advanced Host Controller Interface) protocol that manages SATA was designed around these mechanical constraints.

**SATA III maximum theoretical bandwidth:** 6 Gbps = 750 MB/s. After 8b/10b encoding overhead: **600 MB/s** practical maximum. This was plenty for HDDs that topped out at 150-250 MB/s. For SSDs, it became a straitjacket.

### NVMe: Built for Silicon from the Ground Up

NVMe (Non-Volatile Memory Express) was designed in the late 2000s specifically for SSDs. It assumes:

- **Massive parallelism:** SSDs have multiple NAND dies that can operate independently. NVMe exposes **65,536 queues**, each with up to **65,536 commands** — enabling the OS to send I/O requests to different dies simultaneously without queue contention.
- **Low overhead:** The NVMe command set requires roughly half the CPU cycles of AHCI to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
- **Direct PCIe connection:** NVMe SSDs connect directly to the CPU's PCIe root complex — no intermediate SATA controller chip.

| | SATA SSD (AHCI) | NVMe PCIe 3.0 ×4 | NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4 | NVMe PCIe 5.0 ×4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max sequential read** | 560 MB/s | 3,500 MB/s | 7,000 MB/s | 14,000 MB/s |
| **Max random 4K IOPS** | ~100K | ~500K-750K | ~1M-1.5M | ~2M+ |
| **Command queues** | 1 × 32 | 65,536 × 65,536 | Same | Same |
| **Latency (4K random read)** | ~120 µs | ~80 µs | ~60 µs | ~40-50 µs |
| **PCIe lanes used** | None (SATA controller) | 4 (PCIe 3.0) | 4 (PCIe 4.0) | 4 (PCIe 5.0) |

**PCIe lane math:**
- PCIe 3.0: 8 GT/s (gigatransfers) with 128b/130b encoding → ~985 MB/s per lane → **~3.94 GB/s** for 4 lanes
- PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s → ~1,969 MB/s per lane → **~7.88 GB/s** for 4 lanes  
- PCIe 5.0: 32 GT/s → ~3,938 MB/s per lane → **~15.75 GB/s** for 4 lanes

Real-world throughput is ~85-90% of these theoretical maxima due to NVMe protocol overhead and NAND latency.

> [!warning] M.2 Is Just the Connector, Not the Protocol
> The M.2 form factor (that gumstick-shaped card) can carry either SATA or NVMe — the slot looks identical. A SATA M.2 drive is limited to 600 MB/s even though it's in the same physical slot as an NVMe drive that does 7,000 MB/s. Always check: "NVMe M.2" means PCIe lanes. "SATA M.2" means the old 600 MB/s bottleneck. The notch (key) on the connector tells you: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes), B-key = SATA, B+M-key = either.

---


## PCIe — The Bus That Connects Everything

PCIe (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) is the high-speed serial bus connecting the CPU to devices:

| Device | Typical Lanes | Bandwidth (PCIe 4.0) |
|---|---|---|
| GPU | 16 lanes | ~32 GB/s |
| NVMe SSD | 4 lanes | ~8 GB/s |
| Network card | 4-8 lanes | ~8-16 GB/s |
| USB controller | 1 lane | ~2 GB/s |

Each lane is a pair of differential signal wires (transmit + receive). PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s per lane (~2 GB/s). PCIe 5.0: 32 GT/s (~4 GB/s per lane).

The CPU has a fixed number of PCIe lanes — typically 20-28 on consumer CPUs. A GPU consumes 16, leaving 4-12 for SSDs and other peripherals. This is why populating multiple M.2 slots can disable SATA ports or reduce GPU lanes to x8.



## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Storage interfaces are roads between the CPU and drives. SATA is a two-lane country road — 6 Gbps, one car at a time, simple. NVMe is a 16-lane highway with express lanes — 32 GB/s, 65,000 cars queued simultaneously, each with its own lane. PCIe is the highway system itself — it carries NVMe traffic, GPU traffic, network traffic, all multiplexed onto the same lanes. DMA is the self-driving car that moves data without the CPU driver.

### Technical
SATA 3.0: 6 Gbps, AHCI protocol, 1 command queue × 32 entries. NVMe 1.4: PCIe 4.0 ×4 = 8 GB/s, NVMe protocol, 65535 queues × 65536 entries each, MSI-X interrupts, no register reads (command submission via memory-mapped doorbells). PCIe: lanes of 2 differential pairs each, 16 GT/s (4.0), 128b/130b encoding. NVMe command: submission queue entry (SQE) → controller → completion queue entry (CQE). Multi-queue enables per-core I/O without lock contention.

---

## Exercises

1. **Conceptual:** SATA III's 6 Gbps raw bandwidth becomes 600 MB/s after 8b/10b encoding. NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4 is 8 GT/s × 4 lanes with 128b/130b encoding. Calculate the actual data bandwidth (remove encoding overhead). Why did SATA use 8b/10b (20% overhead) while PCIe 3.0+ uses 128b/130b (1.5% overhead)?

2. **Code:** Write a benchmark that spawns N threads each issuing 4 KB random reads from a file. Run with N=1, 2, 4, 8, 16 on both SATA SSD and NVMe SSD. Plot IOPS vs thread count. Why does SATA SSD IOPS level off at N=1-2 while NVMe continues scaling? Quote the queue depth numbers from this note.

3. **Analysis:** A consumer CPU has 24 PCIe 5.0 lanes. A GPU uses 16, an NVMe SSD uses 4, leaving 4 lanes. If you add a second NVMe SSD, what happens? Where do the remaining 4 lanes come from, and what components share them? What's the bandwidth impact?

4. **Real-world:** You buy an M.2 drive that says "M.2 2280 SATA III" and one that says "M.2 2280 NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4." They look identical. What physical key notch difference distinguishes them? What happens if you plug the SATA M.2 into an NVMe-only slot?

5. **Design:** The NVMe spec allows 65,536 queues. Design an I/O scheduler that assigns one queue per CPU core. When a game thread on core 3 issues a read, how does the submission to the NVMe device work without a syscall (hint: memory-mapped doorbells)? Why does this eliminate the lock contention that AHCI's single queue suffers from?

## See Also

- [[02 — NAND Flash & SSDs]] — The NAND chips these interfaces connect to
- [[04 — Filesystems & Page Cache]] — What happens after the data arrives

04 \u2014 Filesystems & Page Cache.content:
# Filesystems & Page Cache
> [!info] Part of the [[Learning Path|Storage]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]] — understand how data gets to/from the device before learning how the OS organizes it.

## Part 5: Filesystems — What Happens When You fopen()

### The Filesystem as a Database

A filesystem is a specialized database that maps human-readable paths (`/Game/Maps/Level1.umap`) to physical disk sectors. It must:

1. **Track which sectors belong to which files** (allocation)
2. **Store file metadata** (size, permissions, timestamps)
3. **Organize the directory hierarchy** (which files are in which folders)
4. **Recover from crashes** without data corruption (journaling)

### inodes — The Unix Way

On Linux (ext4, XFS, btrfs), every file and directory has exactly one **inode** — a fixed-size data structure identified by a unique number (the inode number). The inode stores:

- File type (regular file, directory, symlink, device, etc.)
- File size in bytes
- Owner UID, group GID
- Permission bits (rwx for user/group/other)
- Timestamps: ctime (inode change), mtime (data modify), atime (access)
- Link count (how many directory entries point to this inode — hard links)
- **Pointers to data blocks** — this is how the filesystem finds your actual data

**Inode data block pointers — how ext4 handles files of any size:**

```
Inode
├── Direct pointers (12): Each points to a 4 KB data block → max 48 KB without indirection
├── Indirect pointer (1): Points to a block of pointers (1,024 pointers × 4 KB = 4 MB)
├── Double-indirect (1): Block of pointers to blocks of pointers (1,024² × 4 KB = 4 GB)
├── Triple-indirect (1): Block of pointers³ (1,024³ × 4 KB = 4 TB)
```

Small files are fast — the data blocks are directly listed in the inode. Large files require walking multiple levels of indirection, requiring extra disk reads to find the data. But most files are small (source code, config files, logs during early writes), so the direct pointers cover the common case.

**The directory separates names from metadata:**
Directories are just special files containing a list of `(name, inode_number)` pairs. The filename is NOT stored in the inode — it lives only in the directory entry. This is why you can have multiple names (hard links) pointing to the same inode: each is just another directory entry with the same inode number. Deleting a file removes the directory entry and decrements the inode's link count; only when the link count reaches 0 AND no process has the file open is the inode and its data blocks actually freed.

### NTFS — The Windows Way: Everything Is a File

Windows uses NTFS, which takes a different approach. The entire filesystem is organized around the **Master File Table (MFT)** — a file containing one record for every file and directory on the volume (including the MFT itself, which is record 0). Each MFT record is usually 1 KB.

**The clever trick: resident vs non-resident data.**
If a file's data is small enough to fit inside the MFT record itself (< ~700 bytes), NTFS stores it **resident** — right there in the MFT, consuming no separate data blocks. This makes accessing very small files nearly instantaneous: reading the file = reading the MFT record you already had to read for the metadata.

For larger files, NTFS stores the data as **non-resident** — the MFT record contains "data runs" (extents) listing `(starting_cluster, cluster_count)` pairs that describe where the file's data lives on disk.

### Journaling — How Your Files Survive Power Loss

Without journaling, a crash during a multi-step filesystem update (e.g., creating a file that requires updating a directory, allocating an inode, marking blocks used, updating free block bitmaps) could leave the filesystem in an inconsistent state:

- A file's data blocks are allocated, but the directory entry was never written → **lost space** (blocks marked used, nothing points to them)
- An inode's size was updated but the data blocks weren't written → **file contains garbage from previous data**
- The free block bitmap says a block is free but a file is actively using it → **double allocation**, data corruption

Fixing this with `fsck` (filesystem check) on a multi-TB drive could take hours.

**Journaling solves this with a write-ahead log:**

```
1. BEFORE making any filesystem change, write a journal entry:
   "I am about to: allocate inode #48291, add 'Level1.umap' to directory X, 
    mark blocks 10034-10036 as used. Transaction ID: 1173."

2. Write the journal entry to a dedicated journal area on disk.
   If this write fails (power loss mid-write), it's fine — we just haven't started.

3. Mark the journal entry as COMMITTED (atomically — one block write).

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Atomic is explained in detail in [[04 — Data Races & Mutexes]]. This note only touches what is necessary for the current topic.


4. Now do the actual work:
   - Allocate the inode
   - Add the directory entry
   - Mark blocks used
   - Update superblock free counts

5. Mark the journal entry as CHECKPOINTED (done, can be discarded).
```

**On reboot after a crash:**
- Entries with COMMITTED but not CHECKPOINTED → **replay**: re-do the described operations (they might have been partially completed before the crash). All journaled operations are idempotent.
- Entries without COMMITTED → **discard**: they never actually started.
- Entries fully CHECKPOINTED → **ignore**: already safe on disk.

The filesystem is consistent in seconds, not hours. This is why you almost never need to run `chkdsk` or `fsck` anymore — the journal recovery runs automatically at mount time.

| Filesystem | Journaling Modes | Default |
|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| ext4 | `data=writeback` (metadata only), `data=ordered` (metadata + enforce data-before-metadata ordering), `data=journal` (everything) | `data=ordered` on most distros |
| NTFS | Journal only metadata ($LogFile); optional data journaling via TxF (deprecated) | Metadata only |
| XFS | Metadata only (always) | Metadata only |
| btrfs, ZFS | Copy-on-Write: never overwrites in-place, always writes to new location, atomically updates root pointer | COW always |

### What Actually Happens When You fopen() — Step by Step

```cpp
FILE* f = fopen("/Game/Maps/Level1.umap", "rb");
```

Here's every I/O operation (in the worst case, with a cold cache):

```
1. Parse path: "/Game/Maps/Level1.umap"
2. Walk from root inode (inode #2 on ext4):
   a. Read root directory inode → find entry "Game" → inode number X
   b. Read inode X (it's a directory) → find entry "Maps" → inode number Y
   c. Read inode Y (it's a directory) → find entry "Level1.umap" → inode number Z
3. Read inode Z → check permissions: does the calling process's UID/GID
   have read permission? Yes.
4. Allocate a file descriptor (small integer) in the process's fd table
5. Create a kernel "struct file" object tracking: current file position = 0,
   access mode = O_RDONLY, the inode pointer
6. Link the fd to the struct file
7. Return FILE* (which wraps the fd)

Total minimum I/O: 3 directory inode reads + 3 directory data reads + 1 target inode read
= 7 random reads in the worst case.
On HDD: 7 × 12 ms = 84 ms. On NVMe SSD: 7 × 0.05 ms = 0.35 ms.
```

This is why SSDs make desktop computing feel snappy — opening a file goes from "perceptible pause" to "instant."

---


## Part 6: The Page Cache — Why Files Are Served from RAM

### What the Page Cache Is

The **page cache** is a region of kernel-managed RAM that caches disk data. When you `read()` from a file, the data doesn't go directly from disk to your buffer. It passes through the page cache — and stays there after your `read()` returns:

```
First read:  read(fd, buf, 4096)
    → Kernel checks page cache: is there a page for this file at this offset? MISS
    → Kernel allocates a page frame, issues disk I/O
    → Disk DMA transfers 4 KB into the page frame (~50 µs NVMe, ~10 ms HDD)
    → Kernel copies 4 KB from page frame to your `buf` (userspace)
    → Page stays in the page cache

Second read (same file, same offset):  read(fd, buf, 4096)
    → Kernel checks page cache: HIT!
    → Kernel copies 4 KB from page frame to your `buf`
    → ZERO disk I/O. Latency: ~100 ns (RAM) instead of 10 ms (HDD)
    → 100,000× faster
```

> [!important] The Page Cache IS Why "Free RAM" Looks Low
> When you check Task Manager and see "16 GB RAM, 14.5 GB in use," don't panic. Much of that "in use" memory is the page cache — disk data cached in RAM. The kernel will instantly evict clean page cache pages if an application needs the memory. **Unused RAM is wasted RAM**; the page cache puts it to productive use accelerating every repeated file access. In fact, if your "free RAM" is high, you have less RAM than you need for the page cache to do its job — you'd benefit from more RAM.

### Dirty Pages and Writeback — Why "Write" Returns Instantly

When you `write()` to a file, the data goes to the page cache first — NOT to disk:

```cpp
write(fd, data, 4096);
// Data is copied from userspace `data` to a page cache page frame
// The page is marked "dirty" (modified, not yet on disk)
// write() returns IMMEDIATELY — sometimes in under a microsecond
// The actual disk I/O happens LATER
```

The kernel has background threads (`pdflush` on older Linux, per-backing-device writeback threads on modern kernels) that periodically flush dirty pages to disk:

- **Default interval:** Every ~30 seconds (`dirty_writeback_centisecs = 3000` on Linux)
- **Pressure triggers:** When dirty pages exceed `dirty_background_ratio` (% of total RAM, default 10%), background writeback starts. When dirty pages exceed `dirty_ratio` (default 20%), writes become **synchronous** — `write()` blocks until enough pages are flushed.

> [!warning] The fsync Trap — "Saved" Isn't Saved Until You fsync
> ```cpp
> write(fd, critical_data, size);
> printf("Saved successfully!
");
> // POWER FAILURE RIGHT HERE → critical_data is LOST
> // It was only in the page cache. Never made it to disk.
> // User sees "Saved successfully!" but the data is gone.
> 
> // THE RIGHT WAY:
> write(fd, critical_data, size);
> fsync(fd);  // FORCES dirty pages to disk. Returns ONLY when disk confirms write.
> printf("Saved successfully!
");  // NOW this statement is actually true
> ``
> 
> Game save files, database commits, transaction logs — ALWAYS `fsync()` (or `FlushFileBuffers()` on Windows) before acknowledging the save to the user. The ~10 ms of latency is worth the certainty.

### The Read-Ahead Optimization

The kernel detects sequential access patterns and **pre-loads** upcoming pages before you ask for them:

```
You read: page 0, page 1, page 2...
Kernel detects: "This is sequential → probably wants page 3, 4, 5, 6..."
Kernel issues read-ahead: asynchronously loads pages 3-6 into the page cache
You request page 3: it's already in RAM! (page cache hit)
```

Read-ahead turns sequential file access into a pipeline: the disk transfers data continuously while your application processes previously-read pages. This is why reading a file sequentially achieves near-full disk bandwidth even though each individual `read()` call has latency: the kernel overlaps the I/O with your processing.

---


## Part 7: Buffered vs Unbuffered I/O — Why printf() Lies to You

### The Two Levels of Buffering

There are **two separate buffers** between your code and the disk:

```
Your program
    │
    ▼
stdio buffer (userspace, per-FILE*): 4-8 KB default
    │  └─ fflush(), fclose(), or buffer-full triggers...
    ▼
write() syscall → kernel
    │
    ▼
Page cache (kernelspace): dirty pages, not yet on disk
    │  └─ writeback or fsync() triggers...
    ▼
Disk (physical storage)
```

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Syscall is explained in detail in [[04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]. This note only covers what is necessary for the current topic.


### stdio Buffering — Why printf("Loading...") Doesn't Show Up

```cpp
// THE TRAP:
printf("Loading...");
// Nothing appears! Text is stuck in the stdio stdout buffer.
do_slow_work();  // Takes 10 seconds
printf(" Done!
");
// BOTH lines appear at the same time, 10 seconds late.
// User stared at a blank screen for 10 seconds wondering what happened.

// THE FIX:
printf("Loading...");
fflush(stdout);  // Force the stdio buffer to flush → write() syscall
do_slow_work();
printf(" Done!
");  // Flushes automatically on newline (line-buffered mode)
```

**The three buffering modes (per `setvbuf()`):**

| Mode | When It Flushes | Used For |
|------|----------------|----------|
| **_IONBF (unbuffered)** | Every call → immediate `write()` | stderr (must appear NOW) |
| **_IOLBF (line-buffered)** | On newline (`
`) or buffer full | stdout (when connected to terminal) |
| **_IOFBF (fully-buffered)** | Only when buffer is full (4-8 KB) | stdout (when redirected to file), regular files |

> [!warning] Redirecting stdout Changes Buffering
> ```bash
> # When stdout is a terminal → line-buffered
> ./myprogram                    # printf("Loading...
") appears immediately
> 
> # When stdout is a pipe/file → fully-buffered
> ./myprogram > output.txt       # printf("Loading...
") won't appear until buffer fills
> ./myprogram | grep "Error"     # Same — might buffer 4 KB before grep sees anything
> ``
> This is why programs sometimes appear to hang when piped — their output is sitting in a 4 KB buffer, waiting to fill.

### Direct I/O — When You Don't Want the Page Cache

Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite in WAL mode) often use **direct I/O** (`O_DIRECT` on Linux, `FILE_FLAG_NO_BUFFERING` on Windows). This bypasses the page cache entirely:

```cpp
int fd = open("database.db", O_RDWR | O_DIRECT);
// All reads go directly to disk. All writes go directly to disk.
// No page cache involvement.
// Buffer MUST be aligned to the disk's logical block size (usually 512 bytes).
```

**Why databases do this:** They have their own buffer pool — often a multi-GB allocation of application-managed memory with a custom caching policy (LRU with database-specific optimizations). The OS's page cache would duplicate this, wasting RAM and potentially caching stale data. By using direct I/O, the database is the sole cache — more efficient and predictable.

---


## Exercises

1. **Trace an `fopen` — cold vs warm:** Walk through all I/O operations for `fopen("/home/user/documents/report.txt", "r")` on ext4 with a cold page cache. Count each inode read and directory data read. Now trace the same call with a warm cache (all inodes and directory blocks cached). What is the I/O count difference? What is the latency difference on HDD vs NVMe?

2. **Journaling replay:** A power failure occurs while ext4 is creating a new file. The journal contains: `[COMMITTED] Allocate inode #58291, add "savegame.dat" to dir inode #1200, mark blocks 4000-4003 used. [COMMITTED] Update inode #58291 size to 16384.` After reboot, which operations does journal replay redo? Are they idempotent — what if they were already partially applied before the crash? Why does the "COMMITTED" marker need to be atomic?

3. **fsync trap experiment:** Write a program that writes 1 MB of data with `write()`, prints "Saved!", then exits without `fsync()`. Pull the power cord (metaphorically — use a VM snapshot). Reboot and check the file. Is the data there? Now add `fsync()` after `write()`. What guarantee does `fsync()` provide at the hardware level that `write()` alone does not?

4. **Page cache detective work:** Your machine has 32 GB RAM. Task Manager shows 30 GB "in use" but your applications have only allocated ~12 GB. Where is the other 18 GB? How can you verify this hypothesis on Linux (`/proc/meminfo`) or Windows (Performance Monitor)? At what point would this cached memory actually be freed for application use?

5. **Direct I/O design decision:** A database uses `O_DIRECT` to bypass the page cache, managing its own buffer pool in application memory. Under what conditions would this be FASTER than relying on the OS page cache? Under what conditions would it be SLOWER? Consider: file size vs available RAM, access pattern (random vs sequential), and whether the database buffer pool uses application-specific knowledge the kernel's LRU does not.

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A filesystem is a librarian who tracks every book in a massive warehouse. Each book has an index card (inode on Linux, MFT record on Windows) listing its location, size, and who can read it. When you ask for a book, the librarian walks through the card catalog (directory tree) — "Fiction → Sci-Fi → Dune" — pulling each index card until finding the right one. This takes several trips to the card drawer. But once you've asked for Dune, the librarian keeps it on a cart by the front desk (page cache) — your second request is instant, no warehouse trip. The `fsync` trap: the librarian nods and says "got it" when you HAND her a revision sheet, but she puts it on a processing pile. If the building loses power before she walks to the warehouse, your revision is gone — even though she said "got it!" `fsync` means she walks it to the warehouse shelf before telling you "done." Direct I/O means you bypass the front-desk cart entirely and walk to the warehouse yourself — databases do this because they have their own, smarter cart system.

### Technical
A filesystem maps hierarchical paths to physical disk blocks via metadata structures: inodes on Unix (fixed-size records with direct/indirect/double-indirect/triple-indirect block pointers) and the Master File Table on NTFS (1 KB records with resident data for small files, extent runs for large files). Directory lookups walk the path component by component, each requiring an inode read + directory data read — ~7 random I/O operations for a cold-cache `fopen`. The page cache (kernel-managed DRAM) intercepts all `read()`/`write()` calls: reads hit cache when possible (~100 ns vs ~10 ms HDD), writes complete to cache immediately (dirty pages) and flush to disk asynchronously via background writeback threads (~30-second Linux default, triggered earlier at `dirty_background_ratio`). Journaling prevents filesystem corruption across crashes by writing a write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying filesystem structures, and on reboot, committed-but-not-checkpointed entries are replayed (idempotent operations). `fsync()` forces dirty pages for a specific file to durable storage before returning — the only guarantee that data survived. Direct I/O (`O_DIRECT`) bypasses the page cache, used by databases with their own buffer pools to avoid double-caching and stale-data risks. stdio buffering adds a SECOND userspace buffer (4-8 KB, line-buffered for terminals, fully-buffered for files/pipes) — explaining why `printf("Loading...")` without `\n` or `fflush()` doesn't appear until the buffer fills or the program exits.

## See Also

- [[01 — HDD Mechanics]] — Random vs sequential, why it matters for filesystem layout
- [[../RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap|Page Faults, mmap & Swap]] — How mmap interacts with the page cache

Storage \u2014 Chapter Summary.content:
# Storage — Chapter Summary

> [!info] Prerequisites
> Read [[00 — Storage Overview]], [[01 — HDD Mechanics]], [[02 — NAND Flash & SSDs]], [[03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]], and [[04 — Filesystems & Page Cache]] before this summary.

## Why This Chapter Matters

Storage is where data lives when the power goes off. It's also the slowest part of the memory hierarchy by a factor of 100,000× or more. Understanding the I/O gap — and the layers of software designed to hide it — is the difference between a game that loads in 2 seconds and one that takes 2 minutes.

This chapter explains why `fopen("data.bin")` can cost anywhere from 50 µs to 10 ms depending on what's cached, why SSD random reads are ~2× slower than sequential, and why `fsync()` is the most expensive function call most developers never think about.

---

## The Storage Stack

| Layer | Note | Key Insight |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **Physics** | 01 — HDD Mechanics | Seek + rotational latency make random I/O ~750× slower than sequential |
| **Flash** | 02 — NAND Flash & SSDs | Erase-before-write: pages are 4-16 KB, blocks are 256+ pages, erase takes milliseconds |
| **Protocol** | 03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe | NVMe's 65K queues × 65K depth vs SATA's single queue × 32 entries |
| **OS Cache** | 04 — Filesystems & Page Cache | The page cache is RAM that acts as storage — dirty pages must be flushed |

---

## The I/O Gap in Numbers

| Medium | Sequential Read | Random Read (4 KB) | Latency |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|---------|
| L1 Cache | ~2 TB/s | ~2 TB/s | ~1 ns |
| RAM (DDR5) | ~50 GB/s | ~10 GB/s | ~100 ns |
| NVMe SSD (Gen5) | ~14 GB/s | ~1.5M IOPS (6 GB/s) | ~50 µs |
| SATA SSD | ~550 MB/s | ~100K IOPS | ~100 µs |
| HDD (7200 RPM) | ~250 MB/s | ~1.5 MB/s | ~5-10 ms |

The sequential-to-random ratio is the key number: **250:1 for HDDs**, **~2:1 for SSDs**. This is why SSDs revolutionized computing — random access became practical.

---

## The Three Traps

### Trap 1: `write()` Is Not Persisted

```c
write(fd, data, 4096);  // Data is in page cache — NOT on disk
// Power loss here → data GONE
fsync(fd);               // NOW it's on disk (and you paid 50 µs-10 ms)
```

### Trap 2: Small Random Writes Kill SSDs

Writing one 4 KB page to an SSD can trigger: read 256-page block → erase block (2-10 ms) → reprogram block with new data + unchanged pages. Write amplification = 256×. This is why SSDs have over-provisioning and why the FTL (Flash Translation Layer) exists.

### Trap 3: `mmap` Is Not Always Faster

`mmap` avoids the read() syscall overhead, but page faults are still I/O. Random access through an `mmap`'d file triggers one major page fault per new page — and at 10 ms per HDD seek, that's 100 pages/second. Sequential access lets the kernel's readahead hide the latency.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Storage is your computer's long-term memory. Your desk (RAM) is fast but small and forgets everything when you leave. Your filing cabinet (SSD/HDD) is huge but slow to access. The OS is a clever assistant who pre-fetches files you'll need soon (readahead), keeps recently-used papers on the corner of your desk (page cache), and only files things away when the desk is full (writeback). When you `write()` to a file, you're handing a paper to the assistant — it goes on the desk, not in the cabinet. `fsync()` is you saying "no, really, file this NOW." A page fault when reading an `mmap`'d file is you reaching for a paper and discovering the assistant hasn't fetched it yet — so you wait while they walk to the cabinet.

### Technical

The Linux storage stack: VFS → filesystem (ext4/XFS/NTFS) → block layer (I/O scheduler, bio structures) → device driver → hardware. The page cache (`struct address_space`) maps file offsets to physical pages via the radix tree. `write()` copies user data into a page cache page and marks it dirty; the actual disk I/O happens later via `flush_work` (writeback threads) or explicit `fsync()/fdatasync()`. `read()` checks the page cache first; on miss, allocates a page, submits a bio to the block layer, and blocks until DMA completion. The I/O scheduler (mq-deadline, kyber, BFQ) merges and reorders bios for optimal throughput. `mmap` creates a VMA mapping file pages into the process address space; first access triggers a major page fault that reads the page into the page cache and wires up the PTE. Writeback is controlled by `dirty_ratio`, `dirty_background_ratio`, and `dirty_expire_centisecs` — when dirty pages exceed thresholds, the kernel forces writeback, potentially blocking `write()` calls.

---

## Exercises

1. **Buffer cache sizing:** Your game streams 500 MB of audio data during a level. The machine has 16 GB RAM and `dirty_ratio = 20`. How much dirty page cache can accumulate before `write()` blocks? If the SSD writes at 3 GB/s, how long does the kernel spend flushing when the threshold is hit?

2. **Write amplification:** Your game's save system writes one 4 KB file every 30 seconds (autosave). The SSD has 256-page blocks, 16 KB pages, and 10% over-provisioning. After 1 hour of gameplay, how many bytes were actually written to NAND (not the file)? What's the write amplification factor?

3. **mmap vs read for random access:** You need to randomly sample 10,000 4 KB chunks from a 2 GB data file. Compare `mmap` + fault-driven access vs `pread` in a loop. Which has fewer context switches? Which has fewer memory copies? If your SSD delivers 100K random IOPS, how long does each approach take?

4. **Page cache pollution:** Your game streams a 50 GB texture pack via `read()` in a linear scan. The machine has 32 GB RAM. After the scan completes, what's in the page cache? How does this affect other applications' memory? What `posix_fadvise()` flag would prevent this?

5. **Design:** Design a save-game system that must never lose more than 5 seconds of progress. The save file is 10 MB. The storage is a consumer SATA SSD (550 MB/s write, ~100 µs latency). Should you `fsync` after every write? Use a write-ahead log? Explain your design and calculate worst-case data loss.

## See Also

- [[../Hardware — Chapter Summary]] — How storage fits into the broader hardware picture
- [[../RAM & Virtual Memory/RAM & VM — Chapter Summary]] — The layer above storage
- [[../../System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]] — How `read()`/`write()`/`mmap` work
- [[../../Learning Path]] — Where to go next

CPU Simulator \u2014 Project.content:
# Project — Build a CPU Simulator

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits & Bytes]], [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness|Negative Numbers & Endianness]], [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language|Assembly Language]], [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture|CPU Architecture]], [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy|Memory Hierarchy]], [[Hardware/13 — Interrupts & Exceptions|Interrupts & Exceptions]].
> **Estimated effort:** 20-40 hours. **Language:** C++ (or Rust).

## Why This Project

You've read 16 notes about how a CPU works. Now you're going to build one. Not a real one in Verilog — a software simulator that models a simplified x86-64-like CPU at the cycle level. Every instruction will go through your simulated pipeline. Your cache will have configurable associativity and replacement policy. Your branch predictor will track pattern history.

When you finish, you'll understand exactly what happens between `call` and `ret` at the hardware level — not because someone told you, but because you implemented it.

---

## Project Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CPU SIMULATOR                    │
│                                               │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐ │
│  │  FETCH   │──►│  DECODE  │──►│ EXECUTE  │ │
│  │  unit    │   │  unit    │   │  unit    │ │
│  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘ │
│       │                              │       │
│       ▼                              ▼       │
│  ┌──────────┐                    ┌──────────┐│
│  │ I-CACHE  │                    │   ALU    ││
│  │ L1 32KB  │                    │          ││
│  └──────────┘                    └──────────┘│
│       │                              │       │
│       ▼                              ▼       │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │         MEMORY SUBSYSTEM              │   │
│  │  L1 D-Cache → L2 Cache → Main Memory │   │
