│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │     BRANCH PREDICTOR                  │   │
│  │  GShare / Tournament                  │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Your simulator takes a binary program (compiled from a simple assembly language), runs it through the pipeline, and produces: register state, memory state, cycle count, CPI (cycles per instruction), cache hit/miss stats, and branch prediction accuracy.

---

## Design Methodology: From Sequential to Pipelined

This section teaches the CS:APP Ch 4 methodology — how to evolve a processor design from a simple one-instruction-at-a-time machine to a fully pipelined superscalar core. This is the intellectual foundation for Phase 2's implementation. Read this before writing pipeline code.

### Stage 0: What Are We Building?

At every clock cycle, a CPU does exactly one thing per pipeline stage. Our target is a 5-stage pipeline:

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │         PIPELINED PROCESSOR           │
                    │                                      │
                    │  ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐
PC ───────────────► │  │ FETCH│──►│DECODE│──►│ EXE- │──►│MEMORY│──►│WRITE │
                    │  │ (IF) │   │ (ID) │   │CUTE  │   │ (MEM)│   │BACK  │
                    │  │      │   │      │   │ (EX) │   │      │   │ (WB) │
                    │  └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘
                    │      ↑          ↑          ↑          ↑          ↑
                    │      │          │          │          │          │
                    │  IF/ID reg  ID/EX reg  EX/MEM reg  MEM/WB reg     │
                    │  (pipeline registers between every stage)         │
                    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each stage takes one clock cycle. Five instructions can be in flight simultaneously — one in each stage. The pipeline registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) capture the output of each stage at the end of every cycle and feed it to the next stage on the following cycle.

### Stage 1: Sequential Design — One Instruction At a Time

Before adding pipelining, design a processor that executes instructions sequentially: finish one completely, then start the next. This is your correctness baseline.

**Sequential execution trace (3 instructions):**

```
Clock:  [── Cycle 1 ──][── Cycle 2 ──][── Cycle 3 ──][── Cycle 4 ──]
Inst 1:   FETCH  DECODE  EXECUTE  MEMORY  WRITEBACK
Inst 2:                                          FETCH  DECODE  EXECUTE  MEMORY  WRITEBACK
Inst 3:                                                                          FETCH  DECODE  EXECUTE ...
```

The sequential design is simple — each stage reads from and writes to the same register file and memory directly. There are no pipeline registers and no hazards. Implement this first and validate correctness against your trivial interpreter.

**The key insight:** In a sequential design, the register file is written in the first half of the cycle (falling edge) and read in the second half (rising edge). This means a value written by one instruction can be read by the NEXT instruction in the SAME clock cycle — no data hazards exist because only one instruction is in flight at a time.

### Stage 2: Add Pipeline Registers — Let It Flow

Now insert pipeline registers between each stage. Each pipeline register is a struct containing all the data the next stage needs:

```
  ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐
  │  FETCH  │───────►│ DECODE  │───────►│ EXECUTE │───────►│ MEMORY  │
  │   (IF)  │ IF/ID  │   (ID)  │ ID/EX  │   (EX)  │ EX/MEM │  (MEM)  │
  └─────────┘        └─────────┘        └─────────┘        └─────────┘
                                                               │
                                                          MEM/WB
                                                               │
                                                               ▼
                                                         ┌─────────┐
                                                         │  WRITE  │
                                                         │  BACK   │
                                                         │  (WB)   │
                                                         └─────────┘
```

At this point, the processor is **pipelined but INCORRECT.** It will produce wrong results because instructions now overlap. An instruction in ID reads the register file in cycle N, but an earlier instruction won't write its result until cycle N+2 (WB stage). The ID stage reads the OLD value — the write hasn't happened yet.

### Stage 3: Data Hazards — Forwarding Paths

The problem:

```asm
ADD R1, R2, R3       ; R1 = R2 + R3
SUB R4, R1, R5       ; R4 = R1 - R5  ← NEEDS R1 from ADD!
```

**Trace through the pipeline:**

```
Cycle:     1        2        3        4        5
ADD:    IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► MEM ──► WB    (R1 written at END of cycle 5)
SUB:          IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► ...      (SUB needs R1 in cycle 3!)
```

SUB's ID stage reads the register file in cycle 3. But ADD won't write R1 until the END of cycle 5 — two cycles too late. SUB would read the OLD value of R1.

**Solution: Forwarding (also called bypassing)** — route the ALU result directly from the pipeline registers back to the ALU input, bypassing the register file write entirely.

```
Forwarding paths:
                                 ┌───────────────────────────┐
                                 │   Forwarding from MEM/WB   │
                                 │   (result available at     │
                                 │    end of cycle 4)         │
                                 │                           │
                    ┌────────────┼───────────┐               │
                    │ Forwarding from EX/MEM  │               │
                    │ (result available at    │               │
                    │  end of cycle 3)        ▼               ▼
                ┌───┴──────────────────────────────────────────┐
                │           ALU INPUT MULTIPLEXORS              │
                │                                               │
                │  ALU input can come from:                     │
                │   1. Register File (normal case — no hazard)  │
                │   2. EX/MEM pipeline register (forwarding)    │
                │   3. MEM/WB pipeline register (forwarding)    │
                └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**The forwarding logic (implemented in the ID stage, sent as control signals to EX):**

```
if (EX/MEM.reg_write AND EX/MEM.dest_reg == ID/EX.src1_reg)
    forward_src1 = FROM_EX_MEM;        // Use the ALU result from EX/MEM
else if (MEM/WB.reg_write AND MEM/WB.dest_reg == ID/EX.src1_reg)
    forward_src1 = FROM_MEM_WB;        // Use the result from MEM/WB
else
    forward_src1 = FROM_REGFILE;       // No hazard — read normally
```

Most data hazards are resolved by forwarding. The result is available in the pipeline register before it's written to the register file — forwarding steals it early.

### Stage 4: The Load-Use Hazard — When Forwarding Isn't Enough

```asm
LD  R1, [R2 + 8]      ; R1 = memory[R2 + 8] — result available after MEM stage (cycle 4)
ADD R3, R1, R4         ; Needs R1 in EX stage (cycle 3) — ONE CYCLE BEFORE it's ready!
```

Trace:

```
Cycle:     1        2        3         4        5
LD:     IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► MEM ──► WB     (R1 available at END of cycle 4)
ADD:           IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► ...     (ADD needs R1 in cycle 3!)
```

LD produces its result at the END of the MEM stage (cycle 4). ADD needs the value at the START of its EX stage (cycle 3). Even with forwarding, the result arrives one cycle too late. This is the **load-use hazard** — the ONLY hazard that requires a stall.

**Solution: Stall (Pipeline Bubble)** — insert one cycle of NOP (a bubble) into the pipeline:

```
Cycle:     1        2        3         4         5        6
LD:     IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► MEM ──► WB
ADD:           IF ──► ID ──► STALL ──► EX ──► ...       (bubble inserted)
                                          ↑
                          Now ADD's EX runs in cycle 5 —
                          LD's result is forwarded from MEM/WB
                          (available at end of cycle 4 → start of cycle 5)
```

**The stall hardware:** The hazard detection unit (in the ID stage) checks:

```
if (ID/EX.mem_read AND                              // Current instruction is a load
    (ID/EX.dest_reg == IF/ID.src1_reg OR            // Next instruction uses loaded value
     ID/EX.dest_reg == IF/ID.src2_reg))
{
    // STALL: insert bubble in ID, keep IF from advancing
    ID/EX.control_signals = ALL_ZERO;   // Turn instruction into NOP
    stall_IF = true;                    // Don't fetch new instruction
}
```

### Stage 5: Control Hazards — Branches

```asm
CMP R1, R2
JE  target         ; Which instruction comes next? We won't know until EX!
ADD R3, R4, R5     ; Might be wrong path
```

The branch outcome (taken or not taken) is computed in the EX stage (cycle 3 for the branch). But IF needs to know the next PC in cycle 2 (when fetching the instruction after the branch). We've already fetched two instructions from the wrong path.

**The full control hazard:**

```
Cycle:     1        2        3        4
CMP/JE: IF ──► ID ──► EX  ← branch resolved here (taken!)
                           But we've already fetched:
ADD:           IF ──► ID ──► ???  ← WRONG PATH — must flush!
SUB:                 IF ──► ???   ← WRONG PATH — must flush!
target_inst:                    IF ← correct path, finally
```

**Solution 1: Flush on misprediction** — when the branch is resolved and the prediction was wrong, invalidate all instructions in IF and ID stages:

```
if (EX/MEM.branch_taken != EX/MEM.branch_predicted) {
    // Flush: invalidate pipeline registers
    IF/ID.valid = false;    // Instruction in ID stage → NOP
    // Next IF will fetch from correct PC
    flush_count++;
    cycles_wasted += 2;      // Flush penalty = 2 cycles (instructions in IF and ID)
}
```

**Solution 2: Branch prediction** — guess before the branch is resolved. If your predictor is 95% accurate, you only flush 5% of the time. This is covered in Phase 4 of the project.

### The Full Pipeline Datapath

After implementing forwarding, load-use stalls, and branch flushing, your pipeline looks like this:

```
                          ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                          │              FORWARDING PATHS                        │
                          │                                                     │
               ┌──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┐              │
               │    ┌─────┴──────────────────────────┐           │              │
               │    │                                │           │              │
    ┌─────┐    │    │     ┌─────┐    ┌─────┐    ┌────┴───┐  ┌────┴───┐    ┌─────┐
PC ─┤ FETCH├────┼────┼────►DECODE├────┤ EXE- ├────┤ MEMORY ├──┤ WRITE  │    │     │
    │ (IF) │    │    │     │ (ID) │    │ CUTE │    │ (MEM)  │  │ BACK   │    │     │
    └──┬───┘    │    │     └──┬───┘    │ (EX) │    └───┬────┘  │ (WB)   │    │     │
       │        │    │        │        └──┬───┘        │       └───┬────┘    │     │
       │   IF/ID│reg │   ID/EX│reg   EX/MEM│reg   MEM/WB│reg       │        │     │
       │        │    │        │            │            │           │        │     │
       │   ┌────┴────┴────────┼────────────┼────────────┼───────────┘        │     │
       │   │                  │            │            │                     │     │
       │   │          Forwarding MUX (ALU inputs):                            │     │
       │   │          Src = RegFile OR EX/MEM.output OR MEM/WB.output         │     │
       │   │                                                                  │     │
       ▼   ▼                                                                  │     │
  ┌──────────────┐                                                            │     │
  │ REGISTER     │◄─────────────────────── WRITE DATA ────────────────────────┘     │
  │ FILE         │                                                              
  │ (read in ID, │◄─────────────────────── WRITE ADDR ────────────────────────────
  │  write in WB)│                                                            
  └──────┬───────┘                                                            
         │                                                                    
         │ (register values read in ID, saved in ID/EX)                       
         │                                                                    
         ▼                                                                    
  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐                                 
  │ HAZARD       │──────►│ STALL IF         │                                 
  │ DETECTION    │       │ INSERT BUBBLE    │                                 
  │ (in ID)      │       │ (load-use only)  │                                 
  └──────────────┘       └──────────────────┘                                 
         │                                                                    
         │ (forwarding control signals to EX stage)                           
         ▼                                                                    
  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐                                 
  │ FORWARDING   │──────►│ FORWARDING MUX   │                                 
  │ UNIT         │       │ (select source)  │                                 
  └──────────────┘       └──────────────────┘                                 

                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │ BRANCH PREDICTOR │──► Next PC for IF stage
                    │ (Phase 4)        │
                    └──────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │ FLUSH IF MISPRED │──► Invalidate IF/ID register
                    │ (in EX stage)    │
                    └──────────────────┘
```

**Data flow through one instruction:**
1. **IF:** PC → I-cache → raw instruction bytes → IF/ID register (captured at cycle end)
2. **ID:** IF/ID.instruction → decoder → register reads + control signals → ID/EX register
3. **EX:** ID/EX data + forwarding MUX selects actual operands → ALU → result + flags → EX/MEM register
4. **MEM:** EX/MEM.result → D-cache read/write → loaded data or pass-through → MEM/WB register
5. **WB:** MEM/WB.result → register file write (with forwarding to earlier stages)

> [!note] Design Checklist — Before You Write Code
> After reading through Stages 1-5, verify your understanding:
> 1. Why does the sequential design have zero data hazards but the pipelined design has them?
> 2. Which hazard CANNOT be fixed by forwarding alone and requires a stall? Why?
> 3. When a branch misprediction is detected in EX, how many instructions must be flushed? (Count stages between IF and EX.)
> 4. If we moved branch resolution from EX to ID (by adding a comparator in the decode stage), what would happen to the misprediction penalty?
> 5. Why can't we forward from MEM/WB to a LOAD instruction's destination in the NEXT instruction's EX stage? Draw the pipeline diagram to explain.
>
> Answers are in the Two-Way Explanation section — but try to work them out first.

---

## Phase 1: The ISA — Define Your Instruction Set (4-6 hours)

Design a RISC-like ISA with ~30 instructions. Keep it simple — you're building the CPU, not a compiler.

### Required Instructions

| Category | Instructions | Encoding |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Data movement | `MOV`, `LD` (load), `ST` (store), `PUSH`, `POP` | Register/memory addressing |
| Arithmetic | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`, `CMP` | reg, reg/immediate |
| Logic | `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHL`, `SHR` | reg, reg/immediate |
| Control flow | `JMP`, `JE`, `JNE`, `JG`, `JL`, `CALL`, `RET` | Conditional and unconditional |
| System | `HLT` (halt), `NOP` | — |

### Instruction Encoding (32-bit fixed)

```
┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────────┐
│  opcode  │   dst    │   src1   │   src2   │  immediate   │
│  6 bits  │  5 bits  │  5 bits  │  5 bits  │   11 bits    │
└──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────────┘
```

### Registers (16 general-purpose + special)

```
R0-R13:  General purpose (64-bit)
R14:     Stack Pointer (SP)
R15:     Instruction Pointer (IP)
FLAGS:   ZF (zero), SF (sign), CF (carry), OF (overflow)
```

### Memory Model

Flat 64-bit address space. Memory is a byte array. Load/store use little-endian. Stack grows downward from high address.

> [!note] Milestone 1
> Write an assembler that converts human-readable assembly to binary `.cpu` files. Write a disassembler for debugging. Implement a trivial interpreter (no pipeline yet — just execute one instruction at a time) to verify correctness.

---

## Phase 2: The Pipeline (6-8 hours)

Implement a classic 5-stage pipeline:

```
IF ──► ID ──► EX ──► MEM ──► WB
 │       │      │       │      │
Fetch  Decode  Execute Memory Write
instr  regs    ALU     load/  Back
from   and     and     store  to
I$     control branch  data   regs
```

### Pipeline Registers

Between each stage, a pipeline register holds the instruction's state:

```cpp
struct IF_ID {
    uint32_t instruction;  // Raw 32-bit instruction
    uint64_t pc;           // Program counter of this instruction
    uint64_t predicted_pc; // Branch predictor's guess
};

struct ID_EX {
    uint8_t opcode;
    uint64_t reg_val1, reg_val2;  // Values read from register file
    uint8_t dest_reg;             // Destination register index
    uint64_t immediate;           // Sign-extended immediate
    bool is_branch;
    uint64_t predicted_target;
    // Control signals (computed by decoder)
    bool reg_write, mem_read, mem_write, alu_src, branch;
    uint8_t alu_op;
};
// ... MEM_WB, EX_MEM
```

### Hazards You Must Handle

**Data hazard (RAW — Read After Write):**

```asm
ADD R1, R2, R3    // R1 = R2 + R3 (result in WB stage)
SUB R4, R1, R5    // Needs R1 — but ADD hasn't written back yet!
```

Solutions: (a) **Stalling** — insert NOPs (bubbles) until result is ready. (b) **Forwarding** — route ALU result directly from EX/MEM or MEM/WB back to EX stage. Implement forwarding.

**Control hazard (branch):**

```asm
CMP R1, R2
JE  target        // Which instruction to fetch next? We don't know yet!
ADD R3, R4, R5    // Might be wrong path
```

Solutions: (a) **Flush** — if branch was mispredicted, flush ID+IF and restart. (b) **Delay slot** — the instruction after branch always executes (MIPS approach). Implement flushing with flush counter.

### Stalling Logic

When an instruction in ID stage needs a result not yet computed:

```
ID stage detects hazard → insert bubble in ID (NOP), stall IF
Next cycle: check again → still hazard? → another bubble
...
Result available → forward or read from regfile → proceed
```

Count stall cycles. They're the #1 contributor to CPI > 1.

> [!note] Milestone 2
> Your pipeline should correctly execute programs, handling all hazards via forwarding + stalling. Compare cycle count with the trivial interpreter — the pipeline should be ~5× faster (CPI ≈ 1 when no hazards). Verify: write a program with intentional read-after-write hazards and confirm forwarding eliminates stalls.

---

## Phase 3: Caches (4-6 hours)

Implement a configurable cache hierarchy.

### L1 Instruction Cache

```
Size: 32 KB
Associativity: 4-way set associative
Line size: 64 bytes
Replacement: LRU (Least Recently Used)
Write policy: N/A (instruction cache is read-only)
```

### L1 Data Cache

```
Size: 32 KB
Associativity: 8-way set associative
Line size: 64 bytes
Replacement: LRU
Write policy: Write-back + Write-allocate
```

### L2 Unified Cache

```
Size: 256 KB
Associativity: 16-way set associative
Line size: 64 bytes
Replacement: LRU
Write policy: Write-back + Write-allocate
Inclusive of L1
```

### Cache Implementation

```cpp
struct CacheLine {
    bool valid;
    bool dirty;
    uint64_t tag;
    std::array<uint8_t, 64> data;
    uint64_t last_access;  // For LRU
};

struct Cache {
    int sets;              // Number of sets
    int ways;              // Associativity
    int line_size;         // 64 bytes
    std::vector<std::vector<CacheLine>> lines;  // [set][way]

    // Access: returns {hit, latency_cycles}
    std::pair<bool, int> access(uint64_t address, bool is_write);
};
```

### Cache Statistics

Track: accesses, hits, misses, compulsory misses, conflict misses, capacity misses, writebacks, cycles spent on misses. Compute hit rate, miss rate, and AMAT (Average Memory Access Time):

```
AMAT = hit_time + miss_rate × miss_penalty
```

> [!note] Milestone 3
> Run a matrix multiplication program. Plot cache misses as a function of matrix size and stride. Show that row-major access has fewer misses than column-major. Compute AMAT for different cache configurations.

---

## Phase 4: Branch Predictor (4-6 hours)

### Bimodal Predictor (baseline)

A table of 2-bit saturating counters indexed by PC bits:

```
States: 00 (Strongly Not Taken) → 01 (Weakly Not Taken) 
        10 (Weakly Taken) → 11 (Strongly Taken)
```

On branch outcome: increment toward 11 if taken, decrement toward 00 if not taken.

### GShare Predictor (global history)

```
Index = (PC_lower_bits XOR global_history) % table_size
Global history: shift register of last N branch outcomes (1 = taken, 0 = not taken)
```

### Tournament Predictor (hybrid)

Two predictors + a chooser table that selects which to trust per branch:

```
Chooser[PC] selects between Bimodal and GShare
Chooser increment if Bimodal was correct, decrement if GShare was correct
```

### Simulate Speculative Execution

When the branch predictor says "taken," fetch from the predicted target. If mispredicted: flush pipeline (increment flush counter), incur misprediction penalty (configure: 5-20 cycles).

> [!note] Milestone 4
> Run a program with a loop that branches N times. Measure misprediction rate for bimodal, GShare, and tournament predictors. Show that tournament predictor achieves >95% accuracy on mixed workloads.

---

## Phase 5: Integration & Benchmarking (4-6 hours)

### Benchmark Programs

Write (or compile) test programs that stress specific subsystems:

| Benchmark | What It Tests |
|-----------|--------------|
| `fibonacci` | Recursive calls — branch prediction + stack |
| `matmul` | Nested loops — data cache behavior |
| `quicksort` | Random memory access — cache + branch prediction |
| `bubblesort` | Sequential access — cache-friendly |
| `linkedlist` | Pointer chasing — cache-unfriendly |

### Metrics Dashboard

```
═══════════════════════════════════════════
 CPU SIMULATOR — BENCHMARK RESULTS
═══════════════════════════════════════════
 Program:      matmul 256x256
 Instructions: 34,000,000
 Total cycles: 49,300,000
 CPI:          1.45
 IPC:          0.69
 L1 I$ hits:   33,950,000  (99.85%)
 L1 D$ hits:   31,200,000  (91.76%)
 L2 hits:      2,750,000   (98.21%)
 Miss penalty: 500,000 cycles
 Branch pred:  97.3% correct
 Pipeline flushes: 12,400
 Bubbles (stalls): 1,850,000
═══════════════════════════════════════════
```

### Optimization Experiments

1. **Cache size sweep:** Vary L1 D$ from 4 KB to 128 KB. Plot miss rate.
2. **Associativity sweep:** Fix size at 32 KB, vary ways from 1 to 16.
3. **Branch predictor comparison:** Run all benchmarks with each predictor. Which wins?
4. **Forwarding on/off:** Compare CPI with and without forwarding.
5. **Speculative execution depth:** How deep should speculation go before misprediction cost outweighs benefit?

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

You're building a model of a CPU. Your model takes instructions (like a program written in simple assembly), runs them through a pipeline (assembly line where each instruction is processed in stages), manages caches (fast memory close to the CPU), and predicts branches (guesses which way an "if" statement will go before it's decided). Like a real CPU, your model tracks how many cycles everything takes. At the end, you can run programs and see: "this program took 49 million cycles, 1.45 cycles per instruction, L1 cache hit rate 91.76%, branch prediction 97.3% correct." You can tweak cache sizes, predictor types, and pipeline depth to see how they affect performance.

### Technical

A cycle-accurate simulator models the microarchitectural state on every simulated clock cycle. Each pipeline stage produces output state that becomes the next stage's input on the subsequent cycle (pipeline registers). Hazards: RAW (read-after-write) hazards are resolved via forwarding/bypassing — the ALU result from EX/MEM or MEM/WB is multiplexed into the ALU inputs in the EX stage, bypassing the register file write. Control hazards from branches: the branch target is computed in EX (or ID for simple branches); the pipeline speculatively fetches from the predicted target; on misprediction, the pipeline register between IF/ID is flushed (invalidated) and the correct PC is loaded. Caches: a set-associative cache with LRU replacement uses an access counter per line; on miss, the least-recently-used line in the set is evicted, triggering a writeback if dirty. The cache implements the MOESI or MSI coherence protocol for multi-core (extra credit). AMAT = hit_latency + miss_rate × miss_penalty. Branch predictors: bimodal uses a PHT (Pattern History Table) of 2-bit saturating counters indexed by low PC bits; GShare XORs PC with a global history register (GHR) to index the PHT, correlating branches; tournament predictor (Alpha 21264 style) uses a chooser table (also 2-bit counters) to select between bimodal and GShare per branch.

---

## Exercises

1. **Hazard count:** Write a program with 5 consecutive RAW hazards (each instruction depends on the previous). Run with forwarding on and off. How many stall cycles does forwarding save? What's the CPI difference?

2. **Cache sweep:** Fix L1 D$ at 8 KB. Vary line size: 16, 32, 64, 128 bytes. Run `matmul` (row-major). Plot miss rate vs line size. Explain the U-shaped curve — why does larger line size eventually HURT?

3. **Branch predictor warm-up:** Run `fibonacci(20)`. Plot branch prediction accuracy vs instructions executed for bimodal, GShare, and tournament. How many iterations before the predictor "learns" the pattern?

4. **AMAT budget:** Your target CPI is 1.2. Base CPI (no misses) is 1.0. L1 hit = 1 cycle, L2 hit = 10 cycles, main memory = 100 cycles. What's the maximum L1 miss rate that still meets the target?

5. **Design extension:** Add a TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) to your simulator. Model 4 KB pages, 64-entry fully-associative L1 D-TLB, 1536-entry L2 STLB. Run a program that touches N pages randomly. Plot TLB miss rate vs working set size.

## See Also

- [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]] — The theory behind your pipeline implementation
- [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — Cache design theory
- [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language]] — Your simulator's instruction set
- [[Memory Allocator — Project]] — The companion project
- [[Learning Path]] — Where to go next

Memory Allocator \u2014 Project.content:
# Project — Build a Memory Allocator

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits & Bytes]], [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy|Memory Hierarchy]], [[Hardware/06 — Memory Allocation|Memory Allocation]], [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture|Virtual Memory]], [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB|Page Tables & TLB]], [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap|Page Faults & mmap]], [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process|Huge Pages & NUMA]], [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel|Syscalls & Kernel]].
> **Estimated effort:** 20-30 hours. **Language:** C.

## Why This Project

Every `malloc` call in your game goes through a memory allocator. The default allocator (glibc's ptmalloc2 / jemalloc / mimalloc) is generic — it must handle every allocation pattern. But game engines have specific patterns: many fixed-size allocations (game objects), short-lived scratch buffers, large streaming allocations, and lock-free per-thread pools.

You're going to build one. Starting from raw `mmap` / `VirtualAlloc`, you'll build: free lists, segregated size classes, slab allocation, thread-local caches, huge page backing, and `mlock` pinning. By the end, you'll have an allocator that outperforms `malloc` for game workloads by 2-10×.

---

## Project Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            GAME MEMORY ALLOCATOR                  │
│                                                    │
│   User calls: my_alloc(SIZE), my_free(PTR)        │
│                                                    │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│   │         THREAD-LOCAL CACHE (per thread)     │  │
│   │  Size class 0: 8B   → freelist             │  │
│   │  Size class 1: 16B  → freelist             │  │
│   │  ...                                        │  │
│   │  Size class N: 256B → freelist              │  │
│   └───────────────────┬───────────────────────┘  │
│                       │ (refill from global)     │
│   ┌───────────────────▼───────────────────────┐  │
│   │           GLOBAL HEAP                       │  │
│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐   │  │
│   │  │  Slab   │  │  Slab   │  │  Large  │   │  │
│   │  │ 8B obj  │  │ 16B obj │  │ objects │   │  │
│   │  │ 4 KB    │  │ 64 KB   │  │ >128 KB │   │  │
│   │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘   │  │
│   │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐     │  │
│   │  │     Huge Page Segments (2 MB)     │     │  │
│   │  └──────────────────────────────────┘     │  │
│   └───────────────────┬───────────────────────┘  │
│                       │                          │
│   ┌───────────────────▼───────────────────────┐  │
│   │            OS INTERFACE                     │  │
│   │  mmap / VirtualAlloc                       │  │
│   │  madvise / mlock                           │  │
│   │  huge pages (MAP_HUGETLB)                  │  │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Phase 1: The Free List — Your First Allocator (3-4 hours)

Start simple: a singly-linked free list managing a pre-allocated memory block.

### Block Header

```c
typedef struct BlockHeader {
    size_t size;              // Size of this block (including header)
    int free;                 // 1 if free, 0 if allocated
    struct BlockHeader *next; // Next block in free list
} BlockHeader;

#define BLOCK_SIZE  (1 << 20)  // 1 MB heap
static BlockHeader *free_list = NULL;
static void *heap_start = NULL;
```

### malloc: First-Fit Search

```c
void *my_malloc(size_t size) {
    // Align to 8 bytes
    size = (size + 7) & ~7;
    
    BlockHeader *curr = free_list;
    while (curr) {
        if (curr->free && curr->size >= size + sizeof(BlockHeader)) {
            // Found a free block! Split if large enough.
            split_block(curr, size);
            curr->free = 0;
            return (void*)(curr + 1);  // User data starts after header
        }
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    return NULL; // Out of memory (no sbrk yet)
}
```

### free: Coalesce Adjacent Blocks

```c
void my_free(void *ptr) {
    BlockHeader *block = ((BlockHeader*)ptr) - 1;
    block->free = 1;
    
    // Coalesce with next block if free
    BlockHeader *next = (BlockHeader*)((char*)block + block->size);
    if (next->free) {
        block->size += next->size;
        block->next = next->next;
    }
    
    // Coalesce with previous block if free
    // (Need doubly-linked list or footer for this — implement!)
}
```

> [!note] Milestone 1
> Implement first-fit with splitting and coalescing. Write a test that allocates and frees many blocks, verifying no fragmentation leaks. Measure fragmentation: `(total_heap - max_allocatable) / total_heap`.

---

## Phase 2: Segregated Free Lists (3-4 hours)

A single free list for all sizes causes O(N) search and fragmentation. Use **segregated size classes**.

### Size Classes

```
Class 0:  8 bytes
Class 1:  16 bytes
Class 2:  32 bytes
Class 3:  64 bytes
Class 4:  128 bytes
Class 5:  256 bytes
Class 6:  512 bytes
Class 7:  1024 bytes
Class 8+:  >1024 bytes → fall back to general free list
```

Each class has its own free list. Allocation: round up to nearest class, pop from that list. Free: push back.

### Slab Allocation

For size classes 0-7, allocate **slabs** — large pages subdivided into fixed-size slots:

```c
#define SLAB_SIZE   (64 * 1024)  // 64 KB per slab

typedef struct Slab {
    struct Slab *next;
    int free_count;
    void *free_list;     // Embedded free list in unused slots
    char data[SLAB_SIZE - sizeof(struct Slab)];
} Slab;
```

Each slab contains `SLAB_SIZE / slot_size` slots. Unused slots store a pointer to the next free slot (embedded free list, zero overhead — the pointer lives in the free slot's user-data space).

> [!note] Milestone 2
> Implement slab allocation for size classes 0-7. Benchmark: allocate + free 1 million objects of each size class. Compare speed with naive first-fit allocator.

---

## Phase 3: Thread-Local Caches (4-5 hours)

Lock contention on global free lists kills multi-threaded performance. Give each thread its own cache.

### Per-Thread Cache

```c
typedef struct ThreadCache {
    void *freelists[NUM_CLASSES];  // One free list per size class
    int counts[NUM_CLASSES];       // Items in each list
    int batch_size[NUM_CLASSES];   // How many to refill at once
} ThreadCache;

// Thread-local storage
__thread ThreadCache *tc = NULL;
```

### Allocation Flow

```
my_malloc(size):
  → Calculate size class
  → If thread cache has free item → pop and return (FAST PATH)
  → Else → refill thread cache from global heap
       → Lock global heap
       → Transfer batch_size items from global freelist to thread cache
       → Unlock global heap
       → Pop from thread cache and return
```

### Free Flow

```
my_free(ptr, size):
  → If thread cache has room → push to thread cache (FAST PATH)
  → Else → flush thread cache to global heap
       → Lock global heap
       → Transfer excess items back to global freelist
       → Unlock global heap
       → Now there's room → push to thread cache
```

### Refill/Flush Batch Size

Too small: frequent global heap access (contention). Too large: memory hoarding (thread X has objects thread Y needs). Typical batch size: 32-128 items.

> [!note] Milestone 3
> Run a multi-threaded test: 16 threads each allocating/freeing 1M objects. Measure throughput (ops/sec) with and without thread caches. The speedup should be 3-10×.

---

## Phase 4: Huge Pages & mlock (4-5 hours)

### Huge Page Allocation

```c
// Linux: request 2 MB huge pages
void *arena = mmap(NULL, ARENA_SIZE,
                   PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
                   MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_ANONYMOUS | MAP_HUGETLB,
                   -1, 0);
```

Benefits: 2 MB page = single TLB entry covers 512 standard pages. For a 1 GB heap, that's 512 TLB entries instead of 262,144.

### Transparent Huge Pages Fallback

If `MAP_HUGETLB` fails, use `madvise` to request THP:

```c
mmap(..., MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_ANONYMOUS, ...);
madvise(arena, ARENA_SIZE, MADV_HUGEPAGE);
```

### mlock — Pin Critical Memory

Prevent the OS from swapping your audio ring buffer, render command buffer, or physics state:

```c
mlock(critical_buffer, critical_size);
```

Track total locked pages: if `mlock` would exceed `RLIMIT_MEMLOCK`, fall back gracefully.

### NUMA-Aware Allocation

On multi-socket machines, allocate from the local NUMA node:

```c
// Linux: mbind or move_pages after allocation
// Simpler: allocate from the local node first
unsigned long node_mask = 1 << numa_node_of_cpu(sched_getcpu());
mbind(arena, ARENA_SIZE, MPOL_BIND, &node_mask, sizeof(node_mask)*8, 0);
```

> [!note] Milestone 4
> Profile TLB misses with and without huge pages using `perf stat -e dTLB-load-misses`. Benchmark: allocate 1 GB, random-access 4-byte reads. Huge pages should reduce TLB misses by ~99%.

---

## Phase 5: Advanced Features & Compare (4-6 hours)

### Memory Tagging

Embed a canary or type tag for debugging:

```c
typedef struct AllocHeader {
    uint32_t canary;        // 0xDEADBEEF
    uint32_t size_class;
    uint64_t alloc_size;
    const char *file;
    int line;
} AllocHeader;
```

On free: verify canary is intact. If corrupted → "double free or buffer overflow" with file/line info.

### Realloc

Handle `realloc` efficiently: if the block can be extended in place (next block is free), do it. Otherwise, `malloc` new + `memcpy` old + `free` old.

### Alignment Support

```c
void *my_aligned_alloc(size_t alignment, size_t size);
```

For SIMD: 16/32/64-byte alignment. For cache lines: `alignas(64)`. Over-allocate, then adjust pointer.

### Benchmark vs glibc/jemalloc/mimalloc

| Benchmark | glibc malloc | jemalloc | mimalloc | Your Allocator |
|-----------|-------------|----------|----------|---------------|
| 1M × 8B alloc/free | | | | |
| 1M × 64B alloc/free | | | | |
| Producer-consumer (16 threads) | | | | |
| Random free order | | | | |
| Realloc-heavy | | | | |
| Max RSS (memory usage) | | | | |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Your allocator is a librarian for memory. When `my_malloc(64)` is called, you look in the "64 bytes" shelf (size class). If there's a book-shaped hole (free slot), you hand it out. If the shelf is empty, you go to the warehouse (global heap) and grab a whole box of 64-byte slots (slab). To avoid crowding at the warehouse counter, each librarian (thread) keeps a personal cart (thread cache) with frequently-requested sizes. Huge pages are like pre-assembled bookshelves — instead of assembling shelves one plank at a time (4 KB pages), you get an entire pre-built unit (2 MB), reducing the number of shelf-assembly instructions (TLB entries). `mlock` is a "DO NOT REMOVE" sticker — the OS is not allowed to temporarily move this shelf to the slow basement storage (swap). Memory tagging writes "Property of Library" inside each book — if someone tries to return a book with a torn page (buffer overflow), the tag is corrupted and you catch the vandal.

### Technical

A modern allocator balances three concerns: speed (allocation/deallocation latency), fragmentation (internal — wasted space within allocated blocks; external — inability to satisfy large requests despite free space), and scalability (lock contention and NUMA effects). Segregated free lists with exact-size classes eliminate search (O(1) alloc/free) and internal fragmentation (slot size = class size). Slab allocation pre-allocates large virtual memory regions (`mmap` with `MAP_HUGETLB`) and carves them into fixed-size slots, using an embedded free list (LIFO for cache warmth, or FIFO for use-after-free detection). Thread-local caches use `__thread` or `tls_model("initial-exec")` variables protected by a per-thread mutex (or lock-free with atomic LIFO stacks using CAS on the head pointer). Refill amortizes global heap access: a batch of B items is transferred under a global lock (typically a per-size-class spinlock). Huge pages (2 MB / 1 GB) increase TLB reach: the x86-64 L1 D-TLB has 64 entries — with 4 KB pages that covers 256 KB; with 2 MB pages, 128 MB. The kernel's Transparent Huge Pages (THP) can promote 4 KB pages to 2 MB automatically, but with 2 ms khugepaged scan latency. Explicit `MAP_HUGETLB` bypasses this latency. `mlock` pins pages in physical RAM, preventing the page reclamation daemon (kswapd) from swapping them. The locked page set must fit within `RLIMIT_MEMLOCK` (default: 64 KB — must be raised via `ulimit -l`). NUMA awareness: the `get_mempolicy`/`mbind` syscalls and `move_pages` optimize the physical placement of pages relative to the accessing core. `mimalloc` uses a novel approach: extremely aggressive thread-local caching with "free lists as message queues" between threads, enabling lock-free cross-thread deallocation.

---

## Exercises

1. **Fragmentation measurement:** Allocate blocks of random sizes (8-1024 bytes) in random order, free half of them randomly. Measure external fragmentation. Now sort allocations by size before freeing. Is there a difference? Why?

2. **Thread cache sizing:** Run a benchmark with 32 threads. Vary the thread cache batch size from 8 to 1024. Plot throughput (ops/sec) and peak memory usage (RSS). Find the optimal batch size. What tradeoff does it balance?

3. **Huge page TLB impact:** Allocate a 512 MB array with both 4 KB pages and 2 MB huge pages. Write random 4-byte reads across the entire array. Use `perf stat -e dTLB-load-misses` to count TLB misses. Compute the miss ratio and explain the difference.

4. **NUMA locality:** On a dual-socket machine (or simulate by pinning), allocate a 1 GB array on node 0, then read it from node 1. Measure bandwidth. Now copy the array to node 1 and read locally. What's the bandwidth ratio? How does `numactl --membind` help?

5. **Design:** Your game uses 3 allocation patterns: (a) 100K small objects (64-256 bytes) allocated per frame and freed in bulk, (b) 500 large streaming buffers (1-4 MB) with 2-second lifetimes, (c) 50 persistent allocations (10-100 MB) for the entire session. Design the allocation strategy for each pattern. Which goes to a slab? Which to mmap directly? Which gets mlock'd?

## See Also

- [[Hardware/06 — Memory Allocation]] — The theory behind malloc/free
- [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB]] — TLB mechanics
- [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]] — mmap internals
- [[CPU Simulator — Project]] — The companion project
- [[Learning Path]] — Where to go next

02 \u2014 Compiler Pipeline.content:
# Compiler Pipeline

> **Why This Matters.** The CPU doesn't speak C++ — it fetches raw machine-code bytes from memory and executes them, nothing else. The compiler pipeline is the sequence of transformations that bridges your human-readable source and the binary stream the CPU actually runs. Knowing what each stage does tells you *where* bugs originate, *why* build times are what they are, and *how* optimization flags actually change your program.

## Prerequisites

- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — The fetch-decode-execute cycle; what the final machine code runs on.
- [[01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] — Binary and hex encoding used in object files and machine code.
- [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]] — How code and data are laid out in address space (relevant for the assembler's section model).

---

## What the CPU Actually Executes

A modern x86-64 CPU has no concept of C++, functions, classes, or variable names. It sees a linear stream of bytes in memory, fetched by `RIP`, decoded into µops, and executed. A "program" is a sequence of these bytes, packaged in a format the OS loader understands.

Given that the CPU only understands machine code, how does this:

```cpp
#include <cstdio>  // C standard I/O: printf() — used here to show compiler optimizations
int main() {
    printf("Hello, world!
");
    return 0;
}
```

...become bytes like `0x48 0x8D 0x3D ...` that the CPU can actually run?

The answer is the **compilation pipeline** — four sequential transformations (five if you count the linker, covered separately in [[03 — Linking & Executables]]). We'll trace each from the bottom up: what hardware ultimately receives.

---

## The Pipeline at a Glance

```
.cpp/.h  ──►  Preprocessor  ──►  Compiler  ──►  Assembler  ──►  Linker  ──►  OS Loader  ──►  CPU
 (text)        (cpp)            (cc1/cc1plus)    (as)           (ld/lld)      (execve)     (fetch-execute)

Input:        Modified         Assembly        Object file    Resolved       Memory-       Instructions
C++ source    source           (.s) text       (.o/.obj)      executable     mapped        streaming
                                                                             binary        through pipeline
```bash
g++ -E main.cpp -o main.i      # Stop after preprocessor → see expanded source
g++ -S main.cpp -o main.s      # Stop after compiler → see assembly text
g++ -c main.cpp -o main.o      # Stop after assembler → see object file
g++ main.o -o main             # Link → executable
```

Each stage is a separate tool (though `g++` drives them transparently). The CPU never sees anything except the final linked executable — but understanding each stage tells you exactly where and why things go wrong.

---

## Phase 1: Preprocessor — Text → Text

The preprocessor operates on **text**, not C++. It has no understanding of syntax, types, or semantics. It's a text transformation engine that runs *before* the compiler ever sees your code.

### `#include` — Literal File Insertion

```cpp
#include `<iostream>`
```

The preprocessor finds `iostream` in the system include path (e.g., `/usr/include/c++/13/iostream`) and **copies the entire file** into your source at the point of the `#include`. After preprocessing, a single `#include <iostream>` can expand your translation unit to **30,000+ lines**. The compiler then parses all of it.

```bash
echo '`#include <iostream>`' | g++ -E -x c++ - | wc -l
# GCC 13: ~30,000 lines of preprocessed output
```

### `#define` — Token Substitution

```cpp
#define MAX_HEALTH 100
#define SQUARE(x) ((x) * (x))
```

Macros are pure text replacement. The preprocessor substitutes tokens before the compiler parses anything:

```cpp
int hp = MAX_HEALTH;        // → int hp = 100;
int area = SQUARE(5 + 1);   // → int area = ((5 + 1) * (5 + 1));  // = 36 ✓
```

> [!warning] The Trap — Missing Defensive Parentheses
> Without the defensive parentheses: `#define BAD_SQ(x) x * x` → `BAD_SQ(5+1)` → `5 + 1 * 5 + 1` → `5 + 5 + 1 = 11`. Not 36. The preprocessor has no understanding of operator precedence — it blindly substitutes text, and the compiler's parser later applies precedence rules to the expanded tokens.

### Conditional Compilation

```cpp
#ifdef _WIN32
    `#include <windows.h>`
#elif defined(__linux__)
    `#include <unistd.h>`
#else
    #error "Unsupported platform"
#endif
```

The preprocessor evaluates `#ifdef`/`#if` conditions and includes or excludes entire blocks of text. Excluded code **never reaches the compiler**. This is how platform-specific code compiles on one target and vanishes on another.

### Preprocessor Directive Reference

| Directive | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `#include` | Insert file contents |
| `#define` | Text macro |
| `#ifdef / #ifndef / #if / #elif / #else / #endif` | Conditional text inclusion |
| `#error` | Force compile error |
| `#pragma` | Compiler-specific instructions (`once`, `pack`, `comment`) |
| `##` | Token pasting in macro expansion |
| `#` | Stringizing: `#x` → `"x"` |
| `#line` | Change reported line number (used by code generators) |

> [!tip]
> **Prefer `constexpr` and `inline` over macros.** `constexpr int MAX_HEALTH = 100;` gives you type checking, namespace scoping, and debugger visibility. Macros give you none. The remaining valid macro uses: include guards, platform detection, and code generation impossible with templates.

---

## Phase 2: The Compiler — C++ → Assembly

The compiler is a three-stage pipeline: **front-end** (understand the code), **middle-end** (optimize it), and **back-end** (translate to machine instructions). The output is assembly text — human-readable mnemonics that map 1:1 to machine code.

### Front-End: Lexing → Parsing → Semantic Analysis

**Lexing:** Splits source text into tokens — keywords, identifiers, literals, operators. Discards whitespace and comments.

```
Source:  int x = a + b * 2;

Tokens: [Keyword:int] [Identifier:x] [Operator:=] [Identifier:a]
        [Operator:+] [Identifier:b] [Operator:*] [Literal:2] [Semicolon:;]
```

**Parsing:** Verifies the token sequence follows C++ grammar and builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a tree capturing the program's grammatical structure:

```
Declaration
├── Type: int
├── Name: x
└── Value: BinaryOp(+)
    ├── Left:  Variable(a)
    └── Right: BinaryOp(*)
        ├── Left:  Variable(b)
        └── Right: Literal(2)
```

**Semantic analysis:** Walks the AST and validates meaning — are types compatible? Is `x` declared? Does the function overload resolve? This is where template instantiation fires: the compiler generates concrete code from template definitions.

> [!note]
> **C++ parsing is notoriously hard.** The grammar is context-sensitive. `A * B` could be a multiplication or a pointer declaration — the parser must consult the symbol table to decide. C++ compilers use recursive descent with backtracking and semantic feedback.

### Middle-End: IR and Optimization

The middle-end converts the AST into an **Intermediate Representation (IR)** — a simplified, language-independent, platform-independent form. All optimizations run on the IR.

**LLVM IR** (Clang) is a typed Static Single Assignment (SSA) form:

```llvm
; C++:    int result = (a + b) * (a - b);

; LLVM IR:
  %add = add i32 %a, %b
  %sub = sub i32 %a, %b
  %result = mul i32 %add, %sub
```

**GCC** uses GIMPLE (a three-address code IR) then RTL (Register Transfer Language).

#### Key Optimizations at the IR Level

| Optimization | What It Does | Example |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)** | Remove computations whose results are never used | Unused variables, unreachable branches |
| **Constant Folding** | Evaluate constant expressions at compile time | `60 * 60 * 24` → `86400` |
| **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** | Compute identical subexpressions once | `a*b` appears twice → compute once, reuse |
| **Function Inlining** | Copy function body to call site, eliminating call overhead | `add(3,4)` → inlined; with constant folding → `7` |
| **Loop-Invariant Code Motion** | Move calculations that don't change out of loops | `for(...) { x = sqrt(2.0); }` → hoist `sqrt(2.0)` above loop |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Replicate loop body to reduce branch overhead | 1000-iteration loop → 250 iterations × factor 4 |
| **Strength Reduction** | Replace expensive operations with cheaper equivalents | `i*8` → `i << 3`; array index multiply → pointer add |
| **Auto-Vectorization** | Convert scalar loop operations to SIMD | Process 4 `float`s at once with SSE |

### Back-End: Code Generation

The back-end converts optimized IR into target-specific machine code:

1. **Instruction selection:** Map each IR operation to the best CPU instruction(s). `add i32` → `add eax, ebx` on x86; `ADD w0, w1, w2` on ARM64.
2. **Register allocation:** Map unlimited IR virtual registers to the 16 physical x86-64 GPRs. This is NP-complete — compilers use graph-coloring heuristics. Spills (variables that don't fit) go to stack slots.
3. **Instruction scheduling:** Reorder instructions to keep the CPU's execution pipeline full, accounting for latencies (e.g., `imul` has 3-cycle latency — don't use the result in the very next instruction).
4. **Code emission:** Output assembly text (or directly to binary).

> [!tip]
> **Why GCC, Clang, and MSVC produce different code for the same C++:** The front-ends parse identically, but the IR design, optimization pass ordering, register allocator heuristics, and instruction selection models differ. Performance deltas of 5–20% between compilers on the same `-O2` code are common.

---

## Phase 3: Assembler — Assembly → Object File

The assembler (`as` / `ml64.exe`) translates assembly mnemonics into actual machine code bytes and packages them into an **object file**. The object file is a structured binary containing:

| Section | Contents |
|---------|----------|
| `.text` | Machine code — the actual CPU instructions |
| `.data` | Initialized global/static variables with values |
| `.bss` | Zero-initialized data — stored as just a size (no bytes in file) |
| `.symtab` | Symbol table — functions/variables defined or referenced |
| `.rela.text` / `.rela.data` | Relocation entries — "patch this address when you find the real symbol" |
| `.debug_info` | Debug mappings (DWARF/CodeView) — source line → instruction address |

Object file formats by platform:

| Platform | Format | Extension |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Linux | ELF (Executable and Linkable Format) | `.o` |
| macOS/iOS | Mach-O | `.o` |
| Windows | COFF inside PE (Portable Executable) | `.obj` |

Object files are **not executable** — they contain unresolved symbol references. The linker resolves these ([[03 — Linking & Executables]] covers that process).

---

> [!note] The trace below shows what happens with `-O2` optimization. See the **Optimization Levels** section below to understand what each optimization level does.

## Concrete Trace — `int x = 5; x = x + 1;` Through All Stages

Let's follow one simple line of C++ through the entire compiler pipeline:

```cpp
// Input source (main.cpp):
int main() {
    int x = 5;
    x = x + 1;
    return x;
}
```

**After Preprocessor:** Unchanged — no `#include`, no `#define`, no macros.

**After Compiler Front-End (AST):**
```
FunctionDecl: main
  CompoundStmt
    DeclStmt: int x = Literal(5)
    BinaryOp: Assign(x, Add(x, Literal(1)))
    ReturnStmt: x
```

**After Compiler Middle-End (optimized IR — -O2):**
```
define i32 @main() {
  ret i32 6       // The compiler constant-folded 5+1=6. No variable needed!
}
```

**After Compiler Back-End (x86-64 assembly):**
```asm
main:
    mov     eax, 6      ; Return value = 6
    ret
```

**After Assembler (object file — `main.o`):**
```
Bytes: B8 06 00 00 00 C3
Text:  mov eax, 6    ; B8 = mov eax, imm32; 06 00 00 00 = 6 (little-endian)
       ret            ; C3 = ret (near return)
```
The assembler also generates a symbol table entry: `main` at offset 0 in the `.text` section.

**After Linker (executable — `a.out`):**
The linker sees that `main` is the entry point. If `main` called `printf`, the linker would resolve that symbol against `libc.so`. Since this program has no external dependencies, the linker just sets the ELF entry point to the address of `main` and writes the final executable.



## Optimization Levels — What the Flags Actually Control

| Flag | Compile Time | Binary Size | Speed vs `-O0` | What's Enabled |
|------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| `-O0` | 1× | 1× | 1× | Nothing. Every variable on stack. Source-order emission. |
| `-O1` | ~1.5× | ~1.1× | ~3–5× | Dead code elimination, basic constant propagation, jump threading |
| `-O2` | ~2× | ~1.3× | ~10–30× | Inlining, CSE, loop optimizations, auto-vectorization, instruction scheduling, strict aliasing |
| `-O3` | ~3× | ~2× | ~10–35× | All of `-O2` + aggressive inlining, loop unrolling, predictive commoning, function cloning |
| `-Os` | ~2× | ~0.85× | ~8–25× | `-O2` minus size-increasing passes; prefers smaller instruction encodings |

### What `-O2` Actually Does to Code — Concrete Before/After

```cpp
// Before optimization:
int sum_array(int* arr, int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        sum += arr[i];
    }
    return sum;
}
```

After `-O2`:
- `sum` lives entirely in a register (never spilled to stack).
- The loop uses pointer arithmetic instead of index math (`add rax, [rdi]; add rdi, 4`).
- If the CPU has SSE/AVX, the loop is auto-vectorized — 4 or 8 ints per iteration.
- The loop counter is eliminated; the loop ends when pointer reaches `arr + n`.

> [!warning] The Trap — `-O3` Can Be Slower Than `-O2`
> Aggressive loop unrolling and inlining bloats code size. If the unrolled loop no longer fits in the L1 instruction cache (32 KB), every iteration triggers an I-cache miss — potentially wiping out the performance gain entirely. Always benchmark, never assume.

---

## Compiler Architecture Comparison

| Aspect | GCC | Clang/LLVM | MSVC |
|--------|-----|------------|------|
| **IR** | GIMPLE → RTL | LLVM IR (SSA) | CIL (internal) |
| **Back-end** | RTL → assembly per target | LLVM target backends (shared) | `c2.dll` |
| **Linker used** | GNU `ld` (BFD/Gold) | `lld` (fast, multi-threaded) | `link.exe` |
| **LTO** | `-flto` (GIMPLE in `.o`) | `-flto=thin` (LLVM bitcode) | `/GL` + `/LTCG` |
| **Debug info** | DWARF | DWARF / CodeView | PDB |
| **EH model** | DWARF EH / SjLj | DWARF (Linux), SEH (Windows) | SEH + VEH |
| **Standard library** | libstdc++ | libc++ (or libstdc++) | Microsoft STL |

> [!tip]
> **For game development:** Unreal Engine uses MSVC on Windows and Clang on Linux/Mac. Performance differences between MSVC and Clang at equivalent optimization levels are typically within 5–10% — Clang often edges ahead in auto-vectorization. The bigger factor is compile times: `clang + lld` can be significantly faster than MSVC + `link.exe` for large codebases.

---

## Unreal Engine Connection — Full UE Compilation Pipeline

```
Your .cpp/.h
  → UHT (Unreal Header Tool) — generates .generated.h and .gen.cpp (reflection)
  → Preprocessor — #include, #define, platform #ifdefs
  → Compiler (MSVC/Clang) — produces .obj/.o per .cpp
  → Assembler — machine code in object files
  → Linker — per-module .dll (Development) or monolithic .exe (Shipping)
  → OS Loader — maps executable and DLLs into memory, resolves dynamic imports
  → Module System — reads .modules, loads DLLs via FModuleManager
  → Running Engine
```

**UBT (UnrealBuildTool)** — UE's build orchestrator. UBT reads `.Build.cs` files (which define a module's dependencies and compiler flags) and `.Target.cs` files (which define build configurations like Development, Shipping, DebugGame). UBT invokes UHT, then the platform compiler, then the linker — managing the entire pipeline across thousands of source files. reads `.Build.cs` and `.Target.cs` files, constructs a module dependency graph, and invokes the platform compiler with correct flags:

```
UBT reads: MyModule.Build.cs
  → Depends on: Core, Engine, Renderer
  → Defines: MYMODULE_EXPORTS
  → Include paths: Engine/Source/Runtime/Core/Public, ...

UBT invokes:
  MSVC: cl.exe /O2 /std:c++20 /D"UE_BUILD_DEVELOPMENT" ...
  Linux: clang++ -O2 -std=c++20 -DUE_BUILD_DEVELOPMENT ...
```

---

## Summary

| Stage | Input → Output | What Matters at the Hardware Level |
|-------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Preprocessor** | `.cpp` text → expanded text | Pure text. No hardware impact. |
| **Compiler Front-End** | Text → AST | Parses grammar; no hardware yet. |
| **Compiler Middle-End** | AST → Optimized IR | Where all speed decisions are made — the IR determines the instruction stream the CPU will execute. |
| **Compiler Back-End** | IR → Assembly | Selects exact CPU instructions, allocates physical registers, schedules for pipeline. |
| **Assembler** | Assembly → Object file | Assembly mnemonics become the exact bytes the CPU's instruction decoder reads. |

---


## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A compiler translates your C++ into machine code the CPU can execute. The preprocessor handles `#include` and `#define` as text substitution first. The compiler itself parses your code into a tree (the AST), optimizes that tree by removing redundant work and reordering operations, then generates assembly — the human-readable text that maps directly to CPU instructions. The assembler converts that assembly text into the actual binary bytes. Each stage transforms the program one step closer to what the hardware understands.

### Technical
The compilation pipeline proceeds through phases: preprocessing (translation phase 4 — pure text transformation), compilation front-end (lexing → parsing → semantic analysis including template instantiation), middle-end (AST → IR translation in SSA form → optimization passes: DCE, CSE, inlining, loop transforms, vectorization), back-end (instruction selection via pattern matching on IR DAGs, register allocation via graph coloring, instruction scheduling via list scheduling respecting µarch latencies), and assembly (mnemonic → opcode encoding with relocation entries for unresolved symbols). GCC uses GIMPLE/RTL; Clang uses LLVM IR; MSVC uses CIL. Optimization levels control which passes run and with what aggressiveness.

---

## Exercises

1. **Trace compilation:** Write `int Square(int x) { return x * x; }`. Compile with `g++ -S -O2`. Read the .s file. Find `Square` in the assembly. What instructions does the optimizer generate? Compare to `-O0`.

2. **Inlining:** Write two small functions, one called 1000 times in a loop. Compile with `-O2` and check if it was inlined (no `call` instruction). Now mark it `__attribute__((noinline))`. What's the performance difference?

3. **Link-Time Optimization:** Compile a multi-file program with `-flto`. Compare binary size and performance to non-LTO. Why does LTO enable cross-module inlining that regular -O2 can't?

4. **UE's UHT:** UE's UnrealHeaderTool runs BEFORE the standard compiler pipeline. It parses `UCLASS()`, `UPROPERTY()`, `UFUNCTION()` macros and generates `.generated.h` files. Trace: what does UHT generate for a `UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable) void TakeDamage(int Damage)`?

5. **Precompiled headers:** UE uses `SharedPCH.h` as a precompiled header. What's in it? Why does including it first speed up compilation by 10x? Measure compile time with and without PCH on a new UE module.
```

03 \u2014 Linking & Executables.content:
# Linking & Executables

> **Why This Matters.** After the compiler and assembler produce object files, the CPU still can't run them — every cross-file function call and global variable reference is a dangling placeholder. The linker is what resolves those placeholders into real addresses, producing the actual binary the OS can load and the CPU can execute. Understanding linking tells you why link errors happen, how dynamic libraries actually work at runtime, and what happens every time your code calls a function in another module.

## Prerequisites

- [[02 — Compiler Pipeline]] — The stages that produce `.o`/`.obj` files with unresolved symbols and relocation entries.
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — The instruction set; `call`/`jmp` instructions need absolute or relative addresses.
- [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]] — How executables are mapped into virtual address space by the OS loader.

---

## What the CPU Needs

A `call` instruction needs a target address — an absolute or RIP-relative offset pointing to the first instruction of the called function. When your code calls `printf()`:

```asm
call    printf        ; But printf lives in a different .o, in libc!
```

The assembler doesn't know where `printf` will be at runtime. So it emits a placeholder:

```
call    0x00000000    ; Placeholder — patch this later
```

And records a **relocation entry**: \"At offset 0x15 in this object file, patch the address with the final address of symbol `printf`.\" The linker's job is to find `printf` and fill in that blank.

---

## What Is a Symbol?

A **symbol** is a named entity that can be referenced across object file boundaries. Think of it as a key-value pair: the name maps to an address (unknown until link time).

```bash
nm main.o                          # List all symbols
objdump -t main.o                  # Detailed symbol table
readelf -s main.o                  # All symbol table fields (Linux)
```

Sample `nm` output:

```
0000000000000000 T main            # T = code (.text), defined here
0000000000000000 D global_counter  # D = initialized data, defined here
                 U printf          # U = undefined — needs resolution
                 U _Z3addii        # Mangled C++ name: add(int, int)
0000000000000008 b static_var      # b = uninitialized data (BSS), LOCAL
```

| Symbol Property | Meaning |
|----------------|---------|
| **Name** | Identifier (mangled for C++: `_Z3addii` = `add(int, int)`) |
| **Value** | Offset within object file (later: absolute virtual address) |
| **Size** | Bytes occupied |
| **Type** | `T` = code (`.text`), `D` = data (`.data`), `B` = BSS, `U` = undefined |
| **Binding** | `LOCAL` (static), `GLOBAL`, `WEAK` (can be overridden) |

---

## C++ Name Mangling

C++ supports overloading — `void foo(int)` and `void foo(double)` are different functions with the *same human-readable name*. The linker only understands flat strings, so the compiler **mangles** the name to encode the full signature:

```cpp
void foo(int x);           // → _Z3fooi       (Itanium ABI — GCC/Clang)
void foo(double x);        // → _Z3food
void foo(int x, int y);    // → _Z3fooii

// MSVC scheme is different:
void foo(int x);           // → ?foo@@YAXH@Z
```

You can see the demangled names with:

```bash
nm main.o | c++filt        # Demangle all symbols
nm -C main.o               # Some nm versions support -C for demangling
```

> [!warning] The Trap — Cross-Compiler Incompatibility
> GCC and MSVC object files are **not compatible**. Different name mangling, different C++ standard libraries, different exception handling ABIs. GCC and Clang use the same Itanium C++ ABI on Linux and are generally compatible. MSVC uses its own. Even on Windows, you can't link a Clang-built `.obj` with MSVC's standard library unless Clang targets the MSVC ABI explicitly (`--target=x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`).

---

## Linker Operation — Step by Step

```
main.o:                    math.o:                  libc.a:
  defines: main              defines: add, sub       defines: printf
  references: add, printf    references: (none)      references: (syscalls)

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Syscall is explained in detail in [[04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]. This note only covers what is necessary for the current topic.


                    ↓ all fed to LINKER ↓

1. Collect all object files and extract referenced symbols from static archives
2. Build global symbol table:
   { main → 0x1000, add → 0x1200, sub → 0x1250, printf → 0x2000 }
3. Process relocation entries:
   - main.o offset 0x15: \"call add\"    → patch with 0x1200
   - main.o offset 0x2A: \"call printf\" → patch with 0x2000
4. Merge sections: all .text together, all .data together, all .bss together
5. Emit executable with resolved addresses
```

The linker can also discard unreferenced sections (garbage collection: `--gc-sections`), reorder sections for cache efficiency, and apply Link-Time Optimization if enabled.

### Common Linker Errors

| Error | Meaning | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| `undefined reference to 'foo'` | Symbol used but not defined anywhere | Link the missing library or add the missing `.cpp` |
| `multiple definition of 'foo'` | Symbol defined in two object files | Use `inline`, move definition to `.cpp`, or add include guards |
| `undefined symbol: __gxx_personality_v0` | Linking C++ with C linker (no C++ runtime) | Use `g++` as the linker driver, not `gcc` or `ld` directly |

> [!tip]
> **Link order matters.** Traditional linkers process files left-to-right and only pull from static libraries (`lib*.a`) to resolve *currently pending* undefined symbols. Always put libraries AFTER your object files: `g++ main.o -lmymath -lpthread`, not `g++ -lmymath main.o`.

---

## Static vs Dynamic Linking

### Static Linking

Library object files are **copied into** the executable at build time. The executable is self-contained — all code lives in one binary.

```
Pros: No runtime dependencies, faster startup (no library loading), LTO possible across library boundaries.
Cons: Larger binary, duplicate library code across executables, must re-link to update any library.
```

### Dynamic Linking — The PLT/GOT Mechanism

Dynamic libraries (`.so` on Linux, `.dll` on Windows) stay as separate files. The executable contains **stubs** that resolve to the real library functions at runtime.

When your code calls `printf()`, it doesn't jump directly to `printf` in libc. It goes through a two-level indirection:

```
Your code:   call printf@plt

printf@plt (in your executable's .plt section):
    jmp    [printf@got]          ; jump via GOT entry

printf@got (in .got.plt section):
    Initially: points back to PLT stub → calls dynamic linker resolver
    After first call: patched to the REAL printf address in libc.so
```

This is **lazy binding** — symbols are resolved on first use, not at startup. The first call to `printf` invokes the dynamic linker's `_dl_runtime_resolve()`, which looks up `printf` in libc's symbol table, writes the real address into `printf@got`, and jumps to it. Every subsequent call goes straight through the GOT — a single extra memory indirection.

```
First call:   call printf@plt → jmp [printf@got] → resolver → REAL printf
Later calls:  call printf@plt → jmp [printf@got] → REAL printf (fast, 1 extra load)
```

Set `LD_BIND_NOW=1` (or link with `-z now`) to resolve everything at startup — catches missing symbols early, at the cost of startup time.

### Walking the PLT/GOT Indirection — Hardware Perspective

From the CPU's point of view, each dynamic function call becomes:

```
1. CALL <plt_entry>           ; 1 µop (predicted taken)
2. JMP  [rip + offset]        ; 1 µop, loads target from GOT (L1 data cache hit, ~4-5 cycles)
3. <real function body>       ; now executing in libc.so
```

That `JMP [rip + offset]` is the key cost: an indirect branch through memory. If the GOT entry is in L1d cache (which it will be after the first call), the overhead is ~3–5 cycles per dynamic call. If it misses L1d, add ~12 cycles (L2) or ~40+ cycles (L3).

### Static vs Dynamic — Tradeoff Summary

| Property | Static | Dynamic |
|----------|--------|---------|
| **Call overhead** | Direct `call` instruction (~1 cycle to decode) | `call` → PLT → GOT load → indirect jmp (~3–5 extra cycles) |
| **Code sharing in RAM** | Each process has its own copy of library code | Library pages are shared across all processes (OS page cache deduplication) |
| **L1 I-cache pressure** | Larger code footprint → more I-cache misses | Smaller per-process footprint, but PLT stubs add indirection |
| **LTO** | Yes — compiler can inline across library boundaries | No — library is opaque at build time |
| **Updates** | Must re-link and redistribute entire binary | Replace the `.so`/`.dll` file; all processes get the update on next launch |

> [!tip]
> **In game engines:** Engine core is typically statically linked (performance, no DLL dependency issues). Plugins are dynamically loaded (`dlopen` / `LoadLibrary`) so they can be updated independently. The engine = static; plugins = dynamic.

---

## PE vs ELF — Windows and Linux Executable Formats

| Feature | Windows PE | Linux ELF |
|---------|------------|-----------|
| **Symbol visibility** | Must explicitly `__declspec(dllexport)` / `__declspec(dllimport)` | All symbols exported by default (`-fvisibility=hidden` to hide) |
| **Import library** | Separate `.lib` pairs with `.dll` | Linker reads `.so` directly |
| **Search order** | CWD → System32 → PATH | `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` → `ld.so.cache` → `/lib`, `/usr/lib` |
| **Versioning** | None built-in (DLL Hell historically) | `libfoo.so.1.2.3` — soname/soversion system prevents conflicts |
| **Delay-load** | `/DELAYLOAD` flag — load on first use | `dlopen()` + `dlsym()` manual loading |
| **Exception handling** | SEH (Structured Exception Handling) | DWARF `.eh_frame` unwind tables |

### DLL Search Order (Windows)

When `LoadLibrary(\"MyPlugin.dll\")` is called:

1. The directory from which the application loaded.
2. The system directory (`C:\\Windows\\System32`).
3. The Windows directory (`C:\\Windows`).
4. The current working directory.
5. Directories listed in the `PATH` environment variable.

> [!warning]
> **DLL search order was a major security vulnerability.** Attackers could place a malicious `kernel32.dll` in the application directory, and it would be loaded instead of the system one. Windows now has \"Known DLLs\" and Safe DLL Search Mode to mitigate this.

---

## Unreal Engine Connection — Modules and Live Coding

### UE Modules

UE organizes code into modules (self-contained units). In Development builds, each module compiles to a separate DLL — changing one module only requires recompiling and re-linking that one DLL. In Shipping builds, all modules are linked into a single executable for faster startup and cross-module LTO.

The `.modules` JSON manifest describes which modules exist, their dependencies, and loading rules:

```json
{
    \"Name\": \"MyGame\",
    \"Modules\": {
        \"MyGameModule\": {
            \"Type\": \"Runtime\",
            \"LoadingPhase\": \"Default\",
            \"Binaries\": [\"MyGameModule.dll\"]
        }
    }
}
```

At engine startup: `FModuleManager::LoadModule()` → `FPlatformProcess::GetDllHandle()` → `dlopen()` / `LoadLibrary()`.

### Live Coding — What Can and Cannot Be Patched

**Live Coding** applies binary patches to already-loaded DLLs:

| Works (function body changes) | Doesn't Work (requires restart) |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Changing `.cpp` implementations | Changing header files that alter class layout |
| Adding new functions to existing classes | Adding/removing member variables → changes `sizeof(MyClass)` |
| Changing non-UObject C++ logic | New `UCLASS`/`USTRUCT`/`UENUM` declarations → requires UHT re-run |
| Console command implementations | Changes to `.Build.cs` / `.Target.cs` → requires UBT regeneration |
| | Adding new module dependencies → requires re-link |

**Why header changes can't be hot-patched:** Objects already allocated in memory have the old `sizeof()`. Changing class layout would require walking the entire heap to find and resize every instance — impossible to do safely at runtime without a full garbage collection and relinking of every vtable reference.

---

## Summary

| Concept | What Happens | Hardware Reality |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Symbol** | Named code/data entity with an unknown final address | Placeholder → the linker fills in a real address the CPU can `call`/`load` |
| **Relocation** | Linker patches placeholder addresses in machine code | Bytes in `.text` are overwritten with the correct RIP-relative or absolute offset |
| **Static linking** | Library code copied into the binary | Direct `call` instructions; no runtime resolution overhead |
| **Dynamic linking (PLT/GOT)** | Stub → GOT indirection → real function | Extra 3–5 cycles per dynamic call (L1d-cached GOT load) |
| **Lazy binding** | Symbol resolved on first use, not startup | First call: ~hundreds of cycles (dynamic linker). Subsequent: ~3–5 cycles |
| **Name mangling** | Encodes C++ signature into linker-compatible string | The linker sees `_Z3fooi`, not `foo(int)` |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
The linker is like a postal service for function calls. Each object file says \"I need to call `printf` but I don't know where it lives.\" The linker looks through all object files and libraries, finds `printf`, and writes its address into every place that needs it. Dynamic linking is like giving someone a forwarding address instead of a final destination — when you first call, you get redirected to finder service; after that, you go directly to the real address. Name mangling is how the linker tells `foo(int)` from `foo(double)` since the linker only sees strings, not types.

### Technical
The linker performs symbol resolution (matching undefined symbols to definitions across object files and static archives) and relocation (patching machine code with computed addresses according to relocation types: `R_X86_64_PC32`, `R_X86_64_PLT32`, etc.). Dynamic linking uses the PLT (Procedure Linkage Table) for lazy binding: each dynamic call targets a PLT stub that does an indirect jump through the GOT (Global Offset Table). On first invocation, the GOT entry points back to the dynamic linker's `_dl_runtime_resolve`, which resolves the symbol and patches the GOT. PE and ELF differ in symbol visibility defaults, import mechanisms, and metadata structure, but the PLT/GOT principle applies to both.

---

## Exercises

1. **Symbol resolution:** Create two .cpp files — one defines `int x = 42`, the other declares `extern int x` and prints it. Compile to .o, then link. Use `nm` to find `x` in the object files. What type of symbol is it? Now mark it `static`. What changes?

2. **Static vs dynamic:** Compile a program linking `libm` statically (`-static`) and dynamically (default). Compare binary sizes. Use `ldd` (Linux) or `dumpbin /dependents` (Windows) to see dynamic dependencies.

3. **PLT/GOT:** Write a program calling `printf` in a loop. Use `objdump -d` to find the PLT stub. Trace: `call printf@plt` → PLT entry → GOT entry → actual printf. Why is there an extra jump? (Hint: lazy binding.)

4. **UE modules:** UE uses its own module system on top of DLLs. A `.Build.cs` file declares module dependencies. How does UBT translate module dependencies into linker flags? What happens if module A depends on B which depends on A?

5. **Symbol visibility:** Compile a shared library with `-fvisibility=hidden` and explicitly export one function with `__attribute__((visibility("default")))`. Compare the exported symbol table to default visibility. Why does UE use `DLLEXPORT` macros?


## See Also

- [[02 — Compiler Pipeline]] — Compilation produces the .o files the linker consumes
- [[04 — Syscalls & Kernel]] — The ELF loader invokes the kernel to start your process
- [[../Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]] — Where code and data land after loading
- [[Function Calls/00 — Function Calls Overview]] — How call instructions resolve through PLT/GOT

04 \u2014 Syscalls & Kernel.content:
# Syscalls & Kernel

> **Why This Matters.** Your program runs in a sandbox — it can add numbers and access its own memory, but it cannot touch the filesystem, talk to the network, allocate physical memory, or spawn threads on its own. Every interaction with the outside world requires crossing into the kernel through a system call. Understanding this boundary explains where latency comes from, why batching operations matters, and what actually happens when your program "asks the OS for help."

## Prerequisites

- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — The `syscall` instruction, privilege levels, and the MSR registers it reads.
- [[00 — RAM & Virtual Memory Overview|RAM & Virtual Memory]] — Page tables, TLB, and why KPTI flushes are expensive.
- [[01 — Processes & Threads]] — How `fork`/`clone`/`execve` create processes; futex as the foundation of thread synchronization.
- [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]] — How futex enables mutexes, semaphores, and condition variables in userspace.

> [!note] Learning Path Note
> Mutex is explained in detail in [[00 — Concurrency Overview|Concurrency]]. This note only touches what is necessary for the current topic.


---

## The Hardware Protection Mechanism — Protection Rings

x86-64 CPUs implement **protection rings** — four privilege levels (Ring 0 through Ring 3). In practice, only two are used:

| Ring | Privilege | Runs |
|------|-----------|------|
| **Ring 0** | Full hardware access | OS kernel, device drivers |
| **Ring 3** | Restricted | Userspace applications (your code) |

From Ring 3, the CPU **physically cannot**:
- Access memory mapped as kernel-only (page table U/S bit blocks it).
- Execute privileged instructions (`lgdt`, `lidt`, `mov cr3`, `hlt`, `in`, `out`, etc.).
- Modify page tables directly.
- Mask interrupts.
