| **ASLR** | Randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution. Attacker can't hardcode return addresses. | OS/kernel randomizes `mmap` bases | Yes — leak a code pointer to calculate the slide |
| **NX Bit (DEP)** | Page table entry bit marks stack pages as non-executable. CPU faults on `RIP` pointing to stack. | Hardware: NX bit in page tables, enforced by MMU on instruction fetch | Yes — ROP (Return-Oriented Programming): chain existing code snippets instead of injecting new code |
| **Shadow Stack (Intel CET)** | CPU maintains a hardware-protected copy of return addresses. `ret` compares stack-ret against shadow-ret. Mismatch → #CP fault. | Hardware: dedicated shadow stack pointer, protected memory | Much harder — hardware-enforced, requires CET bypass |
| **KPTI** | Kernel page tables unmapped in userspace. Even if you hijack RIP, you can't read kernel memory. | Hardware: separate CR3 for kernel/userspace | Mitigates info leaks, not control-flow hijack itself |

---

### The Mechanism

```c
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
```

Each recursive call executes `call factorial`, which pushes a new return address (8 bytes) and the callee's prologue pushes RBP (8 more bytes) and allocates locals (~16–48 bytes). For depth N, you have N frames simultaneously alive on the stack.

### Stack Size Limits

The stack is a **fixed-size contiguous virtual memory allocation** created at thread start:

| Platform | Default Stack Size |
|----------|-------------------|
| Linux (main thread) | 8 MB (`ulimit -s`) |
| Linux (pthread) | 2–8 MB (configurable via `pthread_attr_setstacksize`) |
| Windows (main thread) | 1 MB (set in PE header) |
| Windows (threads) | 1 MB (configurable via `CreateThread` dwStackSize) |

At ~48 bytes per frame (unoptimized), a 1 MB stack allows ~22,000 recursive calls; an 8 MB stack allows ~175,000.

### Guard Pages — The Hardware Limit

The OS places **guard pages** (inaccessible memory with no page table mapping) just beyond the stack allocation:

```
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Stack (8 MB)         │  ← R/W virtual memory
│  ↓ RSP moves down     │
│  ...                  │
├──────────────────────┤  ← Stack limit
│  Guard Pages (64 KB)  │  ← NO page table entries. Access → page fault → SIGSEGV
├──────────────────────┤
│  Other mappings       │
└──────────────────────┘
```

When `RSP` crosses into the guard pages, the CPU's MMU raises a **page fault**. The kernel detects \"stack overflow\" and delivers `SIGSEGV` (Linux) or raises `STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW` (Windows). The guard pages exist to make this a clean fault rather than silent corruption of adjacent memory.

> [!tip]
> **Tail Call Optimization (TCO)** converts recursion into iteration when the recursive call is the last thing the function does (tail position). The compiler transforms `call factorial` into `jmp factorial` — reusing the same stack frame. Constant stack usage regardless of depth:
> ```c
> int factorial_tail(int n, int acc) {
>     if (n <= 1) return acc;
>     return factorial_tail(n - 1, n * acc);  // Tail position → TCO possible
> }
> ``
> At `-O2`, this becomes a loop — zero stack growth.

---

When the compiler inlines a function, it eliminates **all** the hardware mechanisms described above:

```
Without inlining (~15 instructions):
    mov    edi, 3
    mov    esi, 4
    call   add              ; push RIP, jmp, prologue, body, epilogue, ret
    mov    [rbp-4], eax

With inlining + constant folding (1 instruction):
    mov    DWORD PTR [rbp-4], 7
```

Eliminated hardware operations:
- `call` instruction (push return address + jump)
- Prologue (`push rbp; mov rbp, rsp`)
- Epilogue (`pop rbp; ret`)
- Register save/restore for callee-saved registers
- Pipeline flush from the control-flow discontinuity (branch mispredict at `ret`)

### Tradeoffs at the Hardware Level

| Benefit | Cost |
|---------|------|
| No call/ret overhead (~5–20 cycles saved) | Code bloat — function body duplicated at every call site |
| Enables constant folding, dead code elimination at call site | I-cache pressure — larger code footprint → more L1I misses |
| No indirect branch misprediction (~15–20 cycle penalty) | Compile time increase |
| Better instruction scheduling across call boundary | Debug builds inline without optimization benefit |

> [!note]
> **Unreal's `FORCEINLINE`:** UE uses `FORCEINLINE` (`__forceinline` on MSVC) for `FVector::Size()`, `FMath::Min()`, and math operators called millions of times per frame. The call overhead alone (~5 cycles per call × 10^6 calls = 5 ms) would be catastrophic at 60 FPS (16.7 ms budget).

---

### The Problem

```c
int printf(const char* format, ...);
```

The compiler knows nothing about the variadic arguments — their count, types, or positions. Yet at runtime, `printf` must locate every one. The calling convention is the key.

### System V Solution: AL as XMM Count

Arguments 1–6 go in registers (RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9 for integers; XMM0–XMM7 for floats). The caller sets **AL** (low byte of RAX) to the number of XMM registers used for float arguments:

```asm
; printf(\"%d %f %s\", 42, 3.14, \"hello\")
mov    rdi, format_string    ; 1st int arg → RDI
mov    esi, 42               ; 2nd int arg → RSI (%d)
movsd  xmm0, 3.14            ; 1st float arg → XMM0 (%f)
mov    rdx, hello_string     ; 3rd int arg → RDX (%s)
mov    eax, 1                ; AL = 1 (one XMM register used)
call   printf
```

Inside `printf`, the `va_list` machinery saves register arguments to a structure, then walks the format string to pop values from the save area.

### Microsoft x64 Solution: Shadow Space Spill

Windows `printf` is simpler because the caller always spills RCX, RDX, R8, R9 to the 32-byte shadow space. `printf`'s `va_list` reads sequentially from the shadow space upward:

```
For printf(\"%d %d %d %d %d\", 1, 2, 3, 4, 5):

        ┌──────────────────┐
        │  5                │  ← [RSP+40] — 5th arg (above shadow space + ret addr)
        ├──────────────────┤
        │  return address   │  ← [RSP+32]
        ├──────────────────┤
        │  R9 slot = 4      │  ← [RSP+24]
        │  R8 slot = 3      │  ← [RSP+16]
        │  RDX slot = 2     │  ← [RSP+8]
        │  RCX slot = 1     │  ← [RSP+0]  — 32-byte shadow space
        └──────────────────┘

va_list walks: [RSP+0]=1, [RSP+8]=2, [RSP+16]=3, [RSP+24]=4, [RSP+40]=5
```

---

When Unreal hits a failed `check()` or `ensure()`, it captures the callstack — which is exactly the chain of stack frames on the hardware call stack:

```
UObject::ProcessEvent()
UFunction::Invoke()
AActor::BeginPlay()
UWorld::SpawnActor()
```

The crash reporter performs **stack unwinding**: starting from the current RBP, it reads `[RBP+8]` to get the return address, looks up which function contains that address using the `.pdb` symbol file, then follows `[RBP]` to the caller's saved RBP, and repeats:

```
Current RBP = 0x1000
    [0x1008] = 0x7FF6AABB1234  → symbol lookup → \"AActor::BeginPlay\"
    [0x1000] = 0x0FF0           → caller's RBP

    [0x0FF8] = 0x7FF6AABB5678  → \"UWorld::SpawnActor\"
    [0x0FF0] = 0x0FE0           → caller's RBP

    [0x0FE8] = 0x7FF6AABB9ABC  → \"UFunction::Invoke\"
    [0x0FE0] = 0x0FD0           → caller's RBP

    ... until RBP = 0 (end of chain = thread entry point)
```

The frame pointer chain (`RBP → saved RBP → saved RBP → ...`) is a hardware-level linked list. UE builds Development and Debug configurations with `/Oy-` (no frame pointer omission) to preserve this chain at a ~2–3% performance cost. Shipping builds omit frame pointers — crash reconstruction there depends on `.pdb` symbol files and stack-scanning heuristics, which is far less reliable.

---


## Exercises

1. **Buffer overflow by hand:** The function `vulnerable` has a 16-byte buffer at `[RBP-16]` through `[RBP-1]`. The saved RBP is at `[RBP+0]` and the return address is at `[RBP+8]`. An attacker provides a 40-byte input. At what byte offsets does the attacker overwrite the saved RBP? At what offsets the return address? If the attacker wants to redirect execution to `0x7FF6_AABB_1234` (a gadget in the program's own code), what exact byte sequence must appear at which offset? Write it in little-endian.

2. **Stack canary effectiveness:** A compiler inserts a stack canary (random 8-byte value) between the buffer and saved RBP. The attacker's overflow now corrupts the canary before reaching the return address. Under what conditions can the attacker still hijack control flow? Name two techniques: (a) leaking the canary via an information disclosure bug, (b) overwriting a function pointer stored BEFORE the canary in the stack frame. Why doesn't the canary protect against (b)?

3. **Recursion depth calculation:** A recursive function uses 64 bytes per stack frame (return address + saved RBP + 3 local variables + alignment padding). On Linux (8 MB default stack), what is the maximum recursion depth? On Windows (1 MB default stack), what is it? If each recursive call also calls `printf` (which uses an additional 512 bytes of stack for its own frame), what are the new depth limits? What happens if the recursion is deep enough to hit the guard pages?

4. **Tail call optimization proof:** Convert this function to tail-recursive form and explain why TCO transforms the `call` into a `jmp`:
   ```c
   int sum_array(int* arr, int len) {
       if (len == 0) return 0;
       return arr[0] + sum_array(arr + 1, len - 1);
   }
   ```
   Show the stack frames BEFORE and AFTER TCO at depth 1,000. After TCO, how many frames exist simultaneously? What register holds the accumulator across iterations?

5. **Mitigation layered defense:** A binary has all five mitigations enabled: stack canary, ASLR, NX bit (DEP), Intel CET shadow stack, and RELRO. An attacker finds a `strcpy` buffer overflow. For each mitigation, explain what the attacker must additionally accomplish to achieve code execution. Which single mitigation, if perfectly implemented in hardware with no bypass, would make stack buffer overflows impossible for code execution (not just data corruption)?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A buffer overflow is a mailbox with no bottom. You put in a letter with your return address on it (the return address at `[RBP+8]`). But the mailbox slot is 16 inches deep and someone sends you a 40-inch scroll — the scroll doesn't stop at the bottom; it keeps going and overwrites the "return to sender" address with the attacker's address. Now the post office delivers the reply to the attacker's house. The stack canary is a tiny, fragile flag planted between the mailbox and the return address — if the scroll tears the flag, the post office notices and refuses delivery. NX (DEP) makes the mailbox area a "no parking" zone for execution — even if the return address points there, the CPU refuses to run code. ASLR randomizes house numbers so the attacker can't hardcode which house to redirect to. Shadow stack (CET) is a separate, locked mailbox that only the post office can write to — the return address written there must match the one on the scroll, or delivery is refused. Each mitigation alone has a bypass; together they make exploitation extremely difficult — but a single unprotected `strcpy` is still a loaded gun.

### Technical
A stack buffer overflow occurs when an unbounded write (e.g., `strcpy`, `gets`) writes past the end of a stack-allocated buffer, overwriting adjacent stack data. In the standard frame layout, the saved RBP and return address sit at higher addresses than local variables — overflowing upward corrupts them. When the function executes `ret`, the CPU pops the overwritten value into RIP, redirecting execution to an attacker-controlled address. Modern mitigations: **Stack canary** — the compiler inserts a random value between locals and saved RBP; the function epilogue checks it before `ret`, aborting on mismatch. Bypass: leak the canary via a format string or out-of-bounds read, or overwrite function pointers stored below the canary. **ASLR** — the OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries, and PIE executables, so the attacker can't hardcode gadget addresses. Bypass: leak a code pointer to calculate the randomization slide. **NX/DEP** — the NX bit in page tables marks stack/heap pages non-executable; the MMU faults on instruction fetch from NX pages. Bypass: ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) chains existing code gadgets ending in `ret`, without injecting new code. **Shadow stack (Intel CET)** — the CPU maintains a hardware-protected copy of every return address in a separate memory region; `ret` compares the stack's return address against the shadow copy and raises #CP on mismatch. Much harder to bypass — requires a CET-specific vulnerability. **Guard pages** — inaccessible virtual pages beyond the stack allocation; RSP entering them triggers a page fault → SIGSEGV, preventing silent corruption of adjacent memory. Tail call optimization is a compiler transform: when a function call is in tail position (the calling function's frame is no longer needed), the compiler replaces `call` with `jmp`, reusing the current frame — constant stack depth regardless of logical recursion depth.

## See Also

- [[04 — Syscalls & Kernel]] — How the kernel detects and handles stack overflows
- [[03 — Linking & Executables]] — ASLR, NX bit, and RELRO at the executable level
- [[04 — CPU Architecture]] — SMEP/SMAP: hardware protection against ROP

Function Calls \u2014 Chapter Summary.content:
# Function Calls — Chapter Summary

> [!info] Prerequisites
> Read [[00 — Function Calls Overview]], [[01 — Hardware Mechanics]], [[02 — Calling Conventions]], and [[03 — Edge Cases & Security]] before this summary.

## Why This Chapter Matters

Function calls are the most fundamental abstraction in programming — and they're not free. Every `call` instruction pushes a return address, every parameter passing has a cost, and every calling convention mismatch is a security vulnerability. Understanding what happens between `call MyFunction` and `ret` is how you reason about stack overflows, tail-call optimization, and the real cost of virtual function dispatch.

---

## What Actually Happens During a Function Call

```
Caller:                         Callee:
  push arg3         ──┐           push rbp          // Save frame pointer
  push arg2           │           mov rbp, rsp      // Set new frame
  push arg1           │ args      sub rsp, 48       // Allocate locals
  call callee   ──────┘           ...               // Function body
  add rsp, 24    ◄── cleanup      mov rax, result   // Return value
                                  leave             // Restore rbp/rsp
                                  ret               // Pop return address
```

Every step has a purpose — and a cost:

| Step | What Happens | Cost |
|------|-------------|------|
| Parameter passing | Registers (fast) or stack (moderate) | 0-5 cycles each |
| `call` | Push RIP, jump to callee | 2-3 cycles |
| Prologue | Save rbp, allocate stack frame | 2-3 cycles |
| Body | The actual work | Varies |
| Epilogue | Restore rbp/rsp, `ret` | 2-3 cycles |
| Return value | In rax (integer) or xmm0 (float) | 0 cycles |
| Cleanup | Adjust rsp if caller-cleanup | 1 cycle |

A trivially-inlined getter costs 0 function-call overhead. A `virtual` function call through a vtable costs: load vtable pointer → load function pointer → indirect call → branch mispredict (if unpredictable). That's ~15-20 cycles before the function body even starts.

---

## Calling Conventions at a Glance

| Convention | Where | Arg Registers | Cleanup |
|-----------|-------|--------------|---------|
| **System V AMD64** | Linux, macOS | rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 | Caller |
| **Microsoft x64** | Windows | rcx, rdx, r8, r9 | Caller |
| **cdecl (x86)** | Legacy 32-bit | Stack (all) | Caller |
| **stdcall (x86)** | Win32 API | Stack (all) | Callee |
| **fastcall (x86)** | Optimized | ecx, edx + stack | Callee |

The calling convention mismatch trap: if a Windows DLL expects `stdcall` but you declare it as `cdecl`, the stack pointer is wrong after the call returns — corruption or crash.

---

## The Security Angle

Function calls are the primary attack surface. A stack buffer overflow overwrites the return address; when `ret` executes, it jumps to attacker-controlled code. Mitigations:

| Defense | What It Does |
|---------|-------------|
| Stack canaries | Random value between locals and return address — check before `ret` |
| NX/DEP | Stack and heap are non-executable — injected code won't run |
| ASLR | Randomizes code/data/stack addresses — attacker can't hardcode addresses |
| CET (Intel) | Hardware shadow stack — `ret` must match the corresponding `call` |
| CFG/CFI | Control Flow Guard — indirect calls must target valid function entries |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

A function call is like delegating a task. You write instructions on a sticky note (parameters), hand them to your colleague (the callee), and say "come back here when you're done" (return address on the stack). Your colleague does the work, writes the answer on another note (return value), and comes back to where they left off. Calling conventions are the agreed-upon handwriting format — if you write parameters in English but the callee reads French, chaos ensues. Virtual functions add a layer of indirection: instead of calling "Bob the Accountant" directly, you look up "whoever currently holds the Accountant role" in a directory (vtable), then call them. Stack overflow attacks exploit the return address note — an attacker overwrites it with their own address so the colleague "comes back" to the attacker's code instead.

### Technical

On x86-64 System V, `call` pushes the return address (RIP of next instruction) onto the stack and jumps to the target. The callee prologue (`push rbp; mov rbp, rsp`) establishes a frame pointer chain for stack unwinding. Parameters 1-6 go in rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9; parameters 7+ go on the stack (right-to-left push order, caller cleanup). Integer return values ≤64 bits go in rax; 128-bit values use rax:rdx; floating-point in xmm0. The red zone (128 bytes below rsp on System V) is reserved for leaf functions — they can use it without adjusting rsp. Virtual calls compile to `mov rax, [rdi]; call [rax + vtable_offset]` — two dependent loads before the branch target is known, causing a pipeline stall if the branch predictor mispredicts. Security mitigations: `-fstack-protector` inserts canary checks; `-fcf-protection` enables CET shadow stack; `-fsanitize=cfi` enforces control-flow integrity. UE4's `UFUNCTION` macro generates reflection data but does not affect the raw calling convention — it uses standard C++ ABI.

---

## Exercises

1. **Stack frame trace:** Write a function `int foo(int a, int b, int c) { int d = a + b; return d + c; }`. Compile with `-O0 -S`. Draw the stack frame showing: return address, saved rbp, local `d`, and parameters. Label every offset from rbp.

2. **Virtual call cost:** Write a benchmark: 10 million calls to (a) a non-virtual member function, (b) a virtual function with a predictable target (always the same derived class), and (c) a virtual function with random targets (many derived classes). Measure cycles per call. Explain the branch predictor's role.

3. **System V vs Microsoft x64:** Write a function with 8 integer parameters. Compile on both Linux (System V) and Windows (MS x64). Compare the generated assembly — where are parameters 7 and 8 placed? Why does this difference prevent cross-OS binary compatibility?

4. **Stack smash:** Write a program with a 16-byte buffer and `gets(buf)` (deliberately unsafe). Compile with and without `-fno-stack-protector`. Trigger the overflow. With the canary enabled, what happens? Read the disassembly to find where the canary check is inserted.

5. **Design:** You're designing an RPC (Remote Procedure Call) system for a game engine. Client A calls `Server::DamagePlayer(playerId, damage)` — but the server is a different process, possibly on a different machine. How do you serialize the function identifier and parameters? How do you handle the return value? Compare to UE4's RPC system (`Server_`, `Client_`, `NetMulticast_` UFUNCTION specifiers).

## See Also

- [[../System Software — Chapter Summary]] — Compilation and linking (how function symbols resolve)
- [[../../Hardware/03 — Assembly Language]] — The `call` and `ret` instructions
- [[../../Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]] — Branch prediction and indirect calls
- [[../../Learning Path]] — Where to go next

01 \u2014 Variables, Types & Memory.content:
# Variables, Types & Memory

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Every frame, the CPU reads and writes billions of variables. A variable isn't magic — it's specific transistors holding charge at a specific physical location in DRAM. Understanding what a variable IS at the hardware level — not just `int x = 5` — is the difference between code that works by accident and code you can reason about from the silicon up.

---

## What a Variable Is — Hardware Reality

RAM is a grid of billions of microscopic capacitors. One capacitor holds charge (`1`) or doesn't (`0`) = one **bit**. Eight capacitors = one **byte** (256 patterns). A **variable** is a name for a location in this grid.

```cpp
int score = 0;
```

Three things happen: (1) the compiler reserves 4 bytes (32 capacitors) in RAM, (2) records that `score` means those specific bytes, (3) at runtime, those capacitors are set to all-zero.

Every variable has three properties: a **name**, a **type** (how to interpret the bits), and an **address** (where in the capacitor grid).

### The Stack — RSP Allocation

Local variables live on **the stack** — a contiguous memory region. The `RSP` register points to the stack top. Allocation = subtract from RSP. Deallocation = add to RSP. One CPU instruction.

```cpp
void Foo() {
    int a = 10;     // RSP -= 4; 4 bytes at [RSP] = bit pattern for 10
    int b = 20;     // RSP -= 4; 4 bytes at [RSP] = bit pattern for 20
}                   // RSP += 8; both "deleted" in one instruction
```

Stack allocation is effectively free — a single register subtraction. No heap, no `malloc`, no GC.

---

## Types — Same Bits, Different Meaning

The bit pattern `01000010 11001000 00000000 00000000` (`0x42C80000`) is:

| Interpreted as | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| `int` | 1,120,632,832 |
| `unsigned int` | 1,120,632,832 |
| `float` | 100.0 |
| 4 × `char` | `'B'`, `'\xC8'`, `'\0'`, `'\0'` |

Same 32 capacitors. Same charge. Four meanings. The type doesn't change the bits — it changes which CPU circuit interprets them.

### Fundamental Types

```cpp
int score = 0;          // 4 bytes, signed. ±2.1 billion.
float health = 100.0f;  // 4 bytes, IEEE 754 single-precision.
char letter = 'A';      // 1 byte, ASCII value 65. Technically 8-bit integer.
bool alive = true;      // 1 byte, NOT a single bit. CPU can't address bits.
double pi = 3.14159;    // 8 bytes, IEEE 754 double-precision.
```

> [!warning] `bool` wastes 7 bits — 8 `bool`s = 8 bytes, not 1.

### `sizeof` — Compile-Time

```cpp
sizeof(int);       // 4 on modern platforms
sizeof(char);      // 1 (guaranteed by the standard)
sizeof(float);     // 4
sizeof(double);    // 8
sizeof(void*);     // 8 (64-bit), 4 (32-bit)
```

NOT a function call — evaluated at compile time. Zero runtime cost.

---

## Deep Dive: Number Encoding

### Unsigned — Straight Binary

`unsigned int x = 42;` → `00000000 00000000 00000000 00101010` = 32+8+2 = 42. Range: 0 to 4,294,967,295.

### Signed — Two's Complement

Negate: flip all bits, add 1.

```cpp
int a = 5;   // 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000101
int b = -5;  // 11111111 11111111 11111111 11111011 (flip + 1)
// 5 + (-5) = 0 with carry-out discarded. Same adder circuit works for both.
```

| Type | Bytes | Range |
|------|-------|-------|
| `unsigned int` | 4 | 0 to 4,294,967,295 |
| `int` | 4 | −2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 |

### IEEE 754 Float — 32 Bits, Three Fields

```
Sign (1)  Exponent (8)   Mantissa (23)
  0       10000101       10010010000000000000000

Value = (-1)^sign × (1 + mantissa/2²³) × 2^(exponent - 127)
```

For 100.0f: sign=0, 100=1.5625×2⁶ → exponent=133, mantissa=0.5625×2²³. The implicit leading `1.` gives ~7 decimal digits of precision. `0.1f + 0.2f ≠ 0.3f` — floats are approximate. Never use `==`.

### Char & ASCII

`char c = 'A';` stores integer 65. ASCII maps 0–127: `'0'`=48, `'A'`=65, `'a'`=97, `'\n'`=10, `'\0'`=0.

```cpp
char c = 'A';       // c = 65
char d = 65;        // Same — d prints as 'A'
int x = c + 1;      // 66 — char promotes to int in arithmetic
```

---

## `auto` — Compile-Time Deduction

```cpp
auto score = 0;         // int (0 is int literal)
auto health = 100.0f;   // float ('f' suffix)
auto result = health;   // float
```

No runtime cost. No dynamic typing. The compiler figures out the type, then it's fixed forever. Use when the type is obvious; don't use when explicitness aids readability.

---

## Exercises

1. **Bit interpreter:** The 32-bit pattern `0xC0A00000` in memory. Calculate: (a) `unsigned int`, (b) signed `int` (two's complement), (c) `float` (sign=1, exponent=129, mantissa=0.25). Same bits, three different numbers.

2. **Two's complement by hand:** Compute 8-bit two's complement of −42. Show flip + add-1. Add your −42 to +42 in binary — verify zero with carry-out.

3. **Float precision limit:** `16777216.0f` is exact. Next float: `16777218.0f`. No float equals `16777217.0f`. Why? (24-bit mantissa.) At what integer magnitude does the gap exceed 1?

4. **Address comparison:** Print addresses of: local `int` in `main()`, local `int` in another function, global `int`, `static` local `int`. Which are close? Which are far? What does this reveal about memory regions?

5. **sizeof exploration:** Print `sizeof` for `bool`, `int`, `long`, `long long`, `float`, `double`, `void*`, `int*`, `char[10]`. Why is `sizeof(long)` platform-dependent?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A variable is a labeled box in a warehouse of tiny light switches. Each switch is ON (1) or OFF (0). Eight switches = one byte — enough for a letter or small number. The label is the variable name (`score`). The box size is the type (`int` = 4 bytes = 32 switches). The shelf location is the address. The type also tells HOW to read the switches — same 32-switch pattern could mean "one billion" (integer) or "one hundred point zero" (float). The computer doesn't know which — it flips switches exactly as told. Local variables live on a special shelf (stack) that auto-clears when you leave the room.

### Technical
A variable is a named, typed storage location at a virtual address mapped through the MMU to physical DRAM capacitors. The type determines storage size (`sizeof`), bit-pattern interpretation (integer/two's complement vs IEEE 754 vs ASCII), and valid operations. Two's complement enables a single adder circuit for signed and unsigned. IEEE 754 partitions 32 bits into sign/exponent/mantissa with implicit leading 1 — ~7 decimal digits. Stack allocation = RSP decrement (~1 cycle); deallocation = RSP increment. `auto` performs compile-time deduction with zero runtime cost.

## See Also

- [[../Primer/02 — Control Flow]] — How the CPU decides which code to run based on variables
- [[../02 — Syntax & Types/07 — const Correctness|const Correctness]] — `const` variables and why immutability matters

02 \u2014 Control Flow.content:
# Control Flow

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Your CPU executes billions of instructions per second but isn't smart — it fetches the instruction at RIP (instruction pointer), executes it, advances RIP. Without branches, every program would be a straight line — no decisions, no loops. Control flow changes RIP, making the CPU jump based on conditions. Understanding how `if` becomes `cmp`+`jmp` in assembly is foundational to understanding performance.

---

## RIP — The Instruction Pointer

RIP holds the address of the next instruction. Normally, it marches forward. Control flow is anything that makes RIP jump:

```asm
; Sequential
mov    eax, 10        ; RIP += 5
add    eax, 5         ; RIP += 3

; Branching
cmp    eax, 100       ; Compare eax with 100 (sets CPU flags)
jg     .label_enough  ; If eax > 100, RIP = address of .label_enough
```

The hardware doesn't know `if`. It knows `cmp` (subtract, set flags) and `jmp` variants (jump if flags match). Every C++ control flow construct compiles to these.

---

## `if` / `else` — Compare, Conditional Jump

```cpp
if (health > 50) { printf("Healthy\n"); }
else             { printf("Wounded\n"); }
```

Compiles to (conceptual):

```asm
    cmp    dword ptr [health], 50    ; health - 50 → sets ZF, SF, OF, CF
    jle    .L_else                   ; Jump if ≤ (signed)
    lea    rcx, [str_healthy]
    call   printf
    jmp    .L_end
.L_else:
    lea    rcx, [str_wounded]
    call   printf
.L_end:
```

The `cmp` instruction sets **CPU flags** — single-bit registers:
- **ZF** (Zero Flag): result = 0 (values equal)
- **SF** (Sign Flag): result negative
- **OF** (Overflow Flag): signed overflow
- **CF** (Carry Flag): unsigned overflow

Conditional jumps test these: `je` (ZF=1), `jg` (ZF=0, SF=OF), `jl` (SF≠OF), `ja` (CF=0, ZF=0, unsigned). Signed vs unsigned comparisons use DIFFERENT jump instructions.

Chained `if-else`: sequential `cmp`+jump pairs — O(N) worst-case. Fine for 4 options. Terrible for 256.

---

## `switch` — Jump Table, O(1) Dispatch

```cpp
switch (key) {
    case 'w': MoveForward(); break;
    case 's': MoveBack();    break;
    case 'a': StrafeLeft();  break;
    case 'd': StrafeRight(); break;
}
```

When cases are dense, the compiler builds a **jump table** — an array of code addresses:

```asm
    movzx  eax, byte ptr [key]     ; Load key
    sub    eax, 'a'                ; Normalize: 'a'=0, 'b'=1...
    cmp    eax, 3                  ; Out of range?
    ja     .L_default
    lea    rcx, [jumptable]
    jmp    [rcx + rax*8]           ; ONE indexed jump — O(1)!
```

One subtraction, one range check, one indexed jump. Whether 4 cases or 4,000 — same instruction count. Sparse cases (1, 1000, 50000) fall back to binary search or if-else chain.

Without `break`, execution falls through — deliberate C heritage for shared handlers.

---

## Loops — Conditional Jump Backward

All three loop constructs compile to the same pattern: comparison, conditional forward jump (exit), backward jump (repeat).

### `while`

```asm
    mov    [i], 0
.L_loop:
    cmp    [i], 10        ; Test at TOP
    jge    .L_exit
    ; ... body ...
    inc    [i]
    jmp    .L_loop        ; Jump BACK
.L_exit:
```

### `for` — Identical Assembly

`for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)` produces EXACTLY the same machine code as the equivalent `while`. `for` is syntactic sugar — no performance difference.

### `do-while` — Saves One Jump

```asm
.L_body:                   ; No initial forward jump!
    ; ... body ...
    inc    [i]
    cmp    [i], 10
    jl     .L_body        ; Test at BOTTOM
```

One less `jmp` per loop. When the body always executes at least once, `do-while` is marginally faster.

---

## `break` and `continue`

Both compile to single unconditional `jmp` instructions — not function calls, zero overhead. `break` jumps past the loop. `continue` jumps to the increment-and-test section.

---

## Branch Prediction — The CPU Guesses

Modern CPUs don't wait for `cmp` to finish. The **branch predictor** guesses which path, speculatively executes, and if wrong flushes the pipeline — ~15-20 cycle penalty.

```cpp
// Predictable — alternating T/F learned in a few iterations
for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; i++)
    if (i % 2 == 0) doEven(); else doOdd();

// Unpredictable — random → ~50% mispredict → pipeline flushes constantly
for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; i++)
    if (randomArray[i] > 0.5f) doA(); else doB();
```

> [!tip] Sorting data before processing can make a loop faster than the sort cost — because the branch predictor thrives on patterns.

---

## Exercises

1. **Assembly trace:** `if (x > 0) A(); else if (x == 0) B(); else C();` — how many `cmp` instructions execute when x=-5, x=0, x=5? Why does condition order matter for performance?

2. **Jump table design:** 26 cases `case 'A'` through `case 'Z'`. Why can a jump table work? What is the subtraction normalization? If cases were `case 1`, `case 100`, `case 10000`, how much memory would a jump table waste?

3. **while vs do-while instructions:** Count total instructions for a 10-iteration loop using `while` vs `do-while`. Which uses fewer? When would `while` execute zero body iterations?

4. **Branch prediction benchmark:** Sum array elements where `array[i] >= 128`. Time unsorted vs sorted. Why is sorted faster? Implement a branchless version using arithmetic masking — compare all three.

5. **switch fallthrough bug:** `case 10: case 20: ApplyEffect(); case 30: PlaySound(); break;` — trace execution for damage=10, 20, 30. Where does fallthrough occur and why?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Control flow is the CPU's GPS. Normally it drives straight. `if` is a fork — look at the signpost, turn left or right. `switch` with dense cases is a multi-exit roundabout — you know your exit number and go there instantly. A loop is a roundabout you keep circling until the exit condition is met. Branch prediction is the CPU's traffic forecaster — it guesses your exit before you signal. Guess right = smooth. Guess wrong = slam brakes, back up, try again. Predictable patterns keep the CPU fed.

### Technical
Control flow on x86-64: `cmp` sets flags (ZF/SF/OF/CF) by computing dest−src. Conditional jumps test flag combinations. `if-else` chains = sequential cmp+jcc pairs — O(N). `switch` on dense values = jump table (array of code addresses indexed by `value−min`) — O(1). Loops compile to pre-test cmp+jcc (exit) + unconditional jmp (repeat); `do-while` saves the forward test. Branch predictor uses pattern history; mispredict flushes pipeline (~15-20 cycles). `break`/`continue` are single jmp instructions — not function calls.

## See Also

- [[03 — Functions & The Call Stack]] — How `call`/`ret` change RIP for function calls
- [[../../Computer Systems/Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture|CPU Architecture]] — Pipelining, branch prediction in depth

03 \u2014 Functions & The Call Stack.content:
# Functions & The Call Stack

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Every line of gameplay code lives inside a function. Every crash manifests as a call stack. Every performance mystery traces to function call overhead or its absence (inlining). Functions are not just C++ syntax — they are the hardware mechanism by which the CPU organizes work, saves its place, and returns.

---

## Declaration vs Definition

```cpp
int Add(int a, int b);          // Declaration — "this function exists somewhere"

int Add(int a, int b) {         // Definition — here are the instructions
    return a + b;
}
```

**Parameters** are the variables in the declaration (`a`, `b`). **Arguments** are the values at the call site (`Add(3, 4)`). At the hardware level, arguments are placed into registers before `call`.

---

## `call` and `ret` — The Hardware

```
call Add:
  1. RSP = RSP - 8           ; Make room on stack
  2. [RSP] = RIP_next         ; Save return address
  3. RIP = &Add               ; Jump to function

ret:
  1. RIP = [RSP]              ; Pop return address
  2. RSP = RSP + 8            ; Reclaim stack space
```

Two registers (`RIP`, `RSP`) and memory. No heap. No OS. Pure hardware.

### Stack Frame — Prologue and Epilogue

```c
int Add(int a, int b) {
    int sum = a + b;
    return sum;
}
```

Compiles to (conceptual x86-64 System V):

```asm
Add:
    push   rbp              ; Save caller's frame pointer
    mov    rbp, rsp         ; New frame base = current stack top
    sub    rsp, 16          ; Allocate 16 bytes for locals

    mov    [rbp-4], edi     ; Spill 'a' (arrived in EDI)
    mov    [rbp-8], esi     ; Spill 'b' (arrived in ESI)
    mov    eax, [rbp-4]     ; Load a
    add    eax, [rbp-8]     ; a + b → EAX (return register)

    mov    rsp, rbp         ; Deallocate locals
    pop    rbp              ; Restore caller's frame pointer
    ret                     ; Return
```

Stack frame layout during execution:

```
    HIGH ADDRESSES
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │  Return Address       │  ← [RBP+8]  (pushed by `call`)
RBP ├──────────────────────┤
    │  Saved RBP (caller's) │  ← [RBP+0]
    │  'a' (spilled)        │  ← [RBP-4]
    │  'b' (spilled)        │  ← [RBP-8]
RSP ├──────────────────────┤
    │  (free)               │
    └──────────────────────┘
    LOW ADDRESSES
```

`RBP` stays fixed — locals at constant offsets. `RSP` moves with push/pop/call/ret.

### Return Values — Always RAX

Integer/pointer returns go in `RAX`. Float returns in `XMM0`. Large structs: caller allocates space, passes hidden pointer as implicit first argument. `void` functions still use `ret` — just nothing meaningful in `RAX`.

---

## Pass-by-Value — Copying Bits

```cpp
void SetHealth(int hp) { hp = 100; }  // hp is a COPY

int health = 50;
SetHealth(health);  // health STILL 50 — function modified the copy
```

At the hardware level: `health`'s 4 bytes are copied into `EDI` register. The function only sees `EDI`. The original is untouched.

### Pass-by-Reference — Passing the Address

```cpp
void SetHealth(int& hp) { hp = 100; }  // hp is a REFERENCE

int health = 50;
SetHealth(health);  // health NOW 100
```

At the hardware level, a reference is a pointer with mandatory syntax sugar. `int& hp` compiles identically to `int* hp` — the variable's address is passed in a register. No null references, no reassignment, no `*` needed. Full depth in Pointers & References note.

---

## Recursion — A Function Calling Itself

```cpp
int Factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * Factorial(n - 1);
}
```

Every recursive call creates a **new stack frame**. For `Factorial(5)`, 5 copies of `n` (5,4,3,2,1) exist simultaneously, each in its own frame. Each frame costs ~48-64 bytes (return address + saved RBP + locals + alignment). With an 8 MB stack (Linux), ~130,000-170,000 recursive calls max.

### Stack Overflow — Guard Page

The stack is a fixed-size region. When `RSP` crosses into unmapped **guard pages** beyond the stack allocation, the MMU raises a page fault → OS delivers `SIGSEGV` (Linux) or `STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW` (Windows). Your program crashes.

```cpp
void Crash() { Crash(); }  // Infinite recursion → stack overflow guaranteed
```

> [!tip] Tail Call Optimization
> If the recursive call is the LAST thing (tail position), the compiler transforms `call` into `jmp` — reusing the same frame. Constant stack depth:
> ```cpp
> int FactorialTail(int n, int acc = 1) {
>     if (n <= 1) return acc;
>     return FactorialTail(n - 1, n * acc);  // Tail → `jmp`, not `call`
> }
> ``

---

## Exercises

1. **Stack frame trace:** For `int MultiplyAdd(int x, int y, int z) { int temp = x*y; return temp+z; }`, draw the complete stack frame after prologue. Label: return address, saved RBP, each spilled parameter, local `temp`.

2. **Call-by-value proof:** Write `void Swap(int a, int b)` — attempt to swap. Explain why it fails. Write `void SwapByPointer(int* a, int* b)` — show how passing addresses fixes it. What's the assembly difference?

3. **Recursion depth measurement:** Write a recursive function counting calls until stack overflow. Calculate bytes-per-frame from your result. Try `-O0` vs `-O2` — why does unoptimized code produce shallower recursion?

4. **Return value mechanics:** A function returns `struct { int x; float y; }` — too large for RAX alone. Compile with `-O0 -S`. How does the caller pass a hidden pointer? Where does the callee write the result?

5. **Callstack tracer:** Using the RBP chain, walk up the call stack: start at RBP, read `[RBP+8]` (return address), follow `[RBP]` to caller's RBP. Stop at 0. Why does this fail on `-O2` builds (frame pointer omission)?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A function is a detour with a map back home. The CPU writes a sticky note with the return address and drops it on the stack, then jumps to the function. The function builds a workstation (stack frame) for its local variables on top. When done, it tears down the workstation, reads the sticky note, and jumps back. Recursion: each call adds a new workstation on top. Too many = pile topples over (stack overflow). Pass-by-value photocopies the data; pass-by-reference gives the function a map to the original.

### Technical
`call` atomically pushes RIP and jumps. The callee's prologue (`push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N`) saves the frame pointer, establishes a base, allocates locals. The epilogue (`mov rsp, rbp; pop rbp; ret` or `leave; ret`) reverses this. Parameters arrive in registers (RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 for System V; RCX,RDX,R8,R9 for MS). Return values in RAX/XMM0. Recursion creates per-call frames (~48-64 bytes); the fixed-size stack (8 MB Linux, 1 MB Windows) bounds depth. Stack overflow = RSP crossing guard pages → page fault → SIGSEGV. Tail call optimization changes `call` to `jmp`, reusing the frame. Pass-by-value copies arguments to registers; pass-by-reference passes the address.

## See Also

- [[../../Computer Systems/System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics|Function Call Hardware]] — Complete CPU-level trace of call/ret
- [[../../Computer Systems/System Software/Function Calls/02 — Calling Conventions|Calling Conventions]] — System V vs Microsoft ABI

04 \u2014 Arrays, Vectors & Loops.content:
# Arrays, Vectors & Loops

> [!info] Part of the [[../Learning Path|C++ Learning Path]].

---

## Why This Matters

A single variable holds one value. Programs deal with collections — the 100 highest scores, every pixel on screen, every NPC in a world. Arrays and vectors store multiple values in contiguous memory — the CPU's cache loves contiguous memory. Loops process these collections element by element. Together, arrays and loops are the most common pattern in programming.

After this note, you'll understand how arrays live in memory, why vectors grow, and how to iterate over both efficiently.

---

## The Hardware Reality — Memory Is a Long Tape

RAM is a sequence of numbered slots (addresses), each holding one byte. An array is a contiguous block:

```
Address:  1000  1004  1008  1012  1016
Content:  [ 5 ] [ 3 ] [ 8 ] [ 1 ] [ 9 ]
           arr[0] arr[1] arr[2] arr[3] arr[4]
```

Accessing `arr[0]` pulls a 64-byte cache line — `arr[0]` through `arr[15]` (for 4-byte ints) arrive in L1 cache in one go. Accessing `arr[1]` is nearly free — spatial locality.

---

## C-Style Arrays

```cpp
int scores[5];                    // 5 uninitialized ints (garbage!)
int scores[5] = {10,20,30,40,50}; // initialized
int scores[] = {10,20,30};        // size 3 deduced

scores[0] = 100;                  // first element
```

**Trap:** Nothing stops `scores[5]` or `scores[-1]`. Undefined behavior — might crash, might silently corrupt. C arrays **decay to pointers** when passed to functions — `sizeof(arr)` becomes pointer size, not array size.

---

## std::array — Fixed-Size Safety

```cpp
std::array<int, 5> scores = {10,20,30,40,50};

scores[0] = 100;       // fast, no bounds check
scores.at(5);          // throws std::out_of_range — SAFE
scores.size();         // always 5
scores.data();         // raw pointer for C interop
```

Zero-overhead: same memory layout as C array, no heap allocation, adds safety through `.at()` and size through `.size()`. Never decays to pointer.

---

## std::vector — Dynamic Growth

```cpp
std::vector<int> scores;
scores.push_back(10);        // [10]
scores.push_back(20);        // [10, 20]
scores.push_back(30);        // [10, 20, 30]

scores.size();               // 3
scores.capacity();           // probably 4 (growth factor)
```

**Growth mechanism:** When `size == capacity`, allocate new buffer at `capacity * 2`, move elements, free old. `reserve(n)` pre-allocates to avoid repeated moves:

```cpp
scores.reserve(1000);        // one allocation
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
    scores.push_back(i);     // no reallocation
```

---

## Loops

**Index-based:**
```cpp
for (size_t i = 0; i < scores.size(); i++)
    std::cout << scores[i];
```

**Range-based (C++11):**
```cpp
for (int score : scores)          // copy
    std::cout << score;
for (int& score : scores)         // reference — can modify
    score *= 2;
for (const int& score : scores)   // const ref — read-only, no copy
    std::cout << score;
```

**Iterator-based:**
```cpp
for (auto it = scores.begin(); it != scores.end(); ++it)
    std::cout << *it;
```

---

## Performance Reality

| Operation | C Array | std::array | std::vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access `[i]` | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Iterate (warm cache) | Fastest | Same | Same |
| Add element | Fixed | Fixed | Amortized O(1) |

All three are contiguous — the CPU prefetcher streams them identically.

---

## Exercises

1. **Array trace:** Draw memory at addresses 2000-2019 for `int arr[5] = {10,20,30,40,50}`. Label `&arr[0]`, `&arr[0]+1`. What's the difference in bytes?

2. **Vector growth:** push_back 100 elements. Print capacity() after each. Count reallocations. Growth factor? Why not grow by 1?

3. **Bounds error:** Read `arr[5]` and `arr[10]` on a C array. What values? Write `arr[100]` — does it crash? Use `std::array::at(5)` — what happens?

4. **Range-for vs index:** Double every element using index loop, then range-for with `int&`. Which is clearer? Which for vector?

5. **Reserve benchmark:** Push 1M ints without reserve, then with. Time both. How many reallocations without? Memory copying volume?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
An array is a row of numbered mailboxes. A vector is a magical row that can grow — when full, buys a longer row, moves all mail, tears down old. A loop is the mail carrier walking down the row, visiting each box.

### Technical
Contiguous memory: `arr[i]` at `base + i*sizeof(T)`. L1 cache loads 64-byte lines — neighbor access is free. vector stores `{ptr, size, capacity}`. Reallocation: `capacity *= 2`, move elements. Range-for desugars to iterator-based. `std::array` has identical layout to `T[N]` — zero overhead.

## See Also

- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Where arrays live
- [[03 — Memory/06 — std vector & Container Internals|std::vector]] — Deep vector internals
- [[03 — Memory/05 — std array & C Arrays|std::array]] — Full comparison

05 \u2014 Pointers & References.content:
# Pointers & References

> [!info] Part of the [[../Learning Path|C++ Learning Path]].

---

## Why This Matters

Every variable lives at a memory address. A pointer stores that address. Without pointers: no heap allocation, no linked data structures, no passing large objects to functions without copying. Pointers are the most feared and most essential concept in C++.

After this note, you'll understand what `&x` and `*p` do at the hardware level, and why references are safer than pointers.

---

## Hardware: Every Variable Has an Address

```cpp
int  x = 42;         // Compiler: put 42 at some address, call it x
int* p = &x;         // p stores the ADDRESS of x
```

```
Address:  0x7FFF1000    0x7FFF1008
Value:    [    42    ]  [0x7FFF1000]
           x              p
```

`*p = 100` at assembly level:
```asm
mov  rax, [p]       ; Load address from p
mov  [rax], 100     ; Write 100 to that address — two memory accesses
```

---

## Pointer Syntax

```cpp
int* p = &x;     // & = "address of"
*p = 100;        // * = "dereference" — access value AT the address

int* p = nullptr; // null pointer — points to nothing
if (p) { /* safe */ }
```

Dereferencing nullptr → undefined behavior (typically crash).

---

## Pointer Arithmetic = Array Indexing

```cpp
int arr[] = {10,20,30,40,50};
int* p = arr;         // p → arr[0]

arr[2]   ≡  *(p + 2)  ≡  *(arr + 2)  ≡  2[arr]    // all identical
p++;                   // p → arr[1]
```

`p + n` advances by `n * sizeof(int)` bytes.

---

## References — Safer Aliases

```cpp
int x = 42;
int& r = x;     // r IS x — another name

r = 100;        // x = 100 (no dereference)
```

| | Pointer | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Can be null? | Yes | No |
| Reassignable? | Yes | No |
| Dereference? | `*p` | Implicit |
| Implementation | Stores address | Stores address (compiler hides it) |

**Pass-by-reference** avoids copying:
```cpp
void Process(const std::vector<int>& data) { /* no 4MB copy */ }
```

**Trap — dangling reference:**
```cpp
int& Bad() { int local = 42; return local; } // local destroyed!
```

---

## Exercises

1. **Address print:** Print addresses of `int x`, `double y`, `char z`. Adjacent? Distance? Why might compiler not place contiguously?

2. **Pointer swap:** `void Swap(int* a, int* b)`. Test with `Swap(&x, &y)`. Handle null safely.

3. **Reference param:** `void DoubleVec(vector<int>& vec)`. Double every element. Change to `const&` — compiles? Why not?

4. **Array as pointer:** `void PrintArr(int* arr, size_t n)`. Call with C array, `std::array::data()`, `std::vector::data()`. Why does one function accept all three?

5. **Dangling trap:** Return `const string&` to local. Print result. Compile with -Wall — what warning?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Pointer = slip with home address. Write new address (reassign), leave blank (null). To visit, read slip first, then go there (*p). Reference = sticky note saying "this IS that person." Never blank, never moved, no address lookup needed.

### Technical
Pointers: 8 bytes on 64-bit. `&x` → `LEA RAX, [x]`. `*p = v` → `MOV [RAX], v`. References: compiler stores address, enforces non-null/reassignable at compile time. `const T&` extends temporary lifetime. Pass-by-ref: always 8 bytes regardless of object size.

## See Also

- [[02 — Syntax & Types/02 — References|References]] — Full depth
- [[03 — Memory/03 — Smart Pointers & Ownership|Smart Pointers]] — unique_ptr, shared_ptr
- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Where pointers point

06 \u2014 Compiling & Running Programs.content:
# Compiling & Running Programs

> [!info] Part of the [[../Learning Path|C++ Learning Path]].

---

## Why This Matters

Between writing `hello.cpp` and running the program, four stages transform text into machine code. Understanding these means understanding where errors come from and why builds take time.

---

## The Pipeline

```
hello.cpp            Source code
    ↓ Preprocessor   (#include expanded)
hello.i              Single giant text file
    ↓ Compiler       (C++ → assembly)
hello.s              Assembly language
    ↓ Assembler      (assembly → machine code)
hello.o              Object file + symbol table
    ↓ Linker         (resolve symbols)
hello.exe            Executable
```

---

## Stage 1: Preprocessor — Text Replacement

Handles `#` lines. Doesn't understand C++.

```cpp
// hello.cpp:
#include <iostream>
#define PI 3.14159
int main() { cout << PI * 2; }
```

Preprocessed: ~25,000 lines (entire iostream pasted), PI replaced with `3.14159`.

- `#include <file>` — copy-paste entire file
- `#define NAME value` — find-and-replace
- `#ifdef` — conditional compilation

View: `g++ -E hello.cpp -o hello.i`

---

## Stage 2: Compiler — C++ → Assembly

Translates C++ to CPU instructions:

```cpp
int Add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
```
→
```asm
Add:
    lea  eax, [rdi + rsi]   ; single instruction!
    ret
```

View: `g++ -S -O2 hello.cpp -o hello.s`

Compiler errors occur here — syntax, type errors.

---

## Stage 3: Assembler — Assembly → Machine Code

Converts `.s` to `.o` — machine code + symbol table:

```
hello.o:
  PROVIDES: main at 0x0000
  REQUIRES: std::cout, operator<< (from libstdc++)
```

View symbols: `nm hello.o`

---

## Stage 4: Linker — Stitch Together

Combines object files + libraries → executable. Resolves all "required" symbols to their definitions. Linker errors: `undefined reference to 'Foo'` (called but never defined).

---

## What Happens on Run

1. OS creates process — private virtual address space
2. Loads executable segments: `.text` (code), `.data` (globals), stack, heap
3. Loads required `.dll`/`.so` libraries
4. Sets instruction pointer to `_start` → calls `main()`

---

## Common Compiler Flags

| Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-std=c++20` | Use C++20 |
| `-O0` | No optimization (debug) |
| `-O2` | Standard optimization (release) |
| `-g` | Debug symbols |
| `-Wall -Wextra` | All warnings |
| `-Werror` | Warnings = errors |

---

## Exercises

1. **Trace pipeline:** Write `int Add(int,int)`. Run all 4 stages manually. Inspect each file. How many lines in preprocessed output?

2. **Compiler error dissection:** Deliberate syntax error (missing `;`). Read error line by line. Add linker error (undeclared function). How do messages differ?

3. **Optimization comparison:** Sum 1M element array. Compile -O0 and -O2. Compare assembly. SIMD? Loop unrolling?

4. **Symbol table:** Multi-file program. `nm` on each .o. Find `Add` in math.o (defined, `T` type) vs main.o (referenced, `U`).

5. **Preprocessor abuse:** `#define MAX(a,b) ((a)>(b)?(a):(b))`. Call `MAX(i++, j++)`. Print i,j after. Run preprocessor only — what expanded?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Compiling is translating a recipe to robot language. Preprocessor gathers ingredients. Compiler translates to robot-speak. Assembler converts to binary firmware. Linker staples all pieces + libraries together into one executable. Double-click = robot starts cooking.

### Technical
Preprocessor → translation unit. Compiler: AST → IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR) → target assembly. Assembler: `.s` → ELF/COFF `.o` with relocation entries. Linker: symbol resolution + relocation application. OS loader: creates virtual address space, maps segments, jumps to `_start`.

## See Also

- [[../06 — Bridge/02 — From Source to Running|From Source to Running]] — Deeper trace
- [[02 — Syntax & Types/08 — Preprocessor & Macros|Preprocessor]] — Full macro system

01 \u2014 Enums & enum class.content:
# Enums & enum class

## What Is an Enum?

An enum (enumeration) is a set of named integer constants. Instead of passing magic numbers, you pass names with meaning.

```cpp
// ❌ Magic numbers — unreadable, error-prone
void SetColor(int color);  // What does 3 mean?
SetColor(3);               // Is 3 red? blue? Who knows?

// ✅ Enum — self-documenting, compiler-checked
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue };
void SetColor(Color color);
SetColor(Blue);  // Clear, type-safe
```

---

## Plain `enum` — The Old Style

```cpp
enum Direction { North, East, South, West };

Direction d = North;  // No scope prefix needed

// Values start at 0 and increment:
int n = North;  // 0 — IMPLICIT conversion to int!
int e = East;   // 1
int s = South;  // 2
int w = West;   // 3

// You can set specific values:
enum ErrorCode { OK = 0, NotFound = 404, ServerError = 500 };
```

**The problem with plain enums:** Values leak into the surrounding scope. Two enums can't share member names:

```cpp
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue };
enum TrafficLight { Red, Yellow, Green };  // ❌ ERROR — Red and Green already defined!
```

And implicit conversion to `int` means you can accidentally pass any integer where an enum is expected — bypassing type safety.

---

## `enum class` — The Modern Standard (C++11)

```cpp
enum class Color { Red, Green, Blue };
enum class TrafficLight { Red, Yellow, Green };  // ✅ OK — different scopes

Color c = Color::Red;                              // Must use scope prefix
TrafficLight t = TrafficLight::Red;                // Names don't conflict

// int x = Color::Red;  // ❌ ERROR — no implicit conversion to int!
int x = static_cast<int>(Color::Red);  // ✅ OK — explicit conversion
```

`enum class` is **strongly typed and scoped.** Values don't leak, don't convert to int implicitly, and don't conflict with other enums. Always prefer `enum class` unless you're interfacing with C code.

---

## Underlying Type

By default, the underlying type is `int` for both enum styles. You can specify a smaller type to save memory:

```cpp
enum class Permission : uint8_t {  // 1 byte instead of 4
    None = 0,
    Read = 1 << 0,   // 1
    Write = 1 << 1,  // 2
    Execute = 1 << 2 // 4
};

// Bitwise operations on enums:
Permission p = Permission::Read | Permission::Write;  // Combined flags
bool canRead = (p & Permission::Read) == Permission::Read;
```

> [!tip] Use `uint8_t` for small enums
> UE uses `uint8` extensively for enum class types — `ENetRole`, `EClassCastFlags` subtypes, etc. This saves memory when enums are stored in arrays or as struct members.

---

## C++23 Additions

### `std::to_underlying` — Safe Conversion

```cpp
#include <utility>  // C++23: std::to_underlying() — gets the integer backing an enum class value

enum class Status : uint8_t { Idle = 0, Running = 1, Error = 255 };
auto value = std::to_underlying(Status::Running);  // value is uint8_t = 1
// Cleaner than static_cast<uint8_t>(Status::Running)
```

### `using enum` — Import All Values (C++20)

```cpp
enum class Axis { X, Y, Z };

void Process() {
    using enum Axis;  // Import all Axis values into this scope
    Axis a = X;       // Now you can use X directly (no Axis:: prefix)
    if (a == Y) { }
}
```

Use sparingly — avoiding scope prefixes defeats the purpose of `enum class`. Good for tight local scopes where the enum name would be repetitive.

---

## Iterating Over Enums

C++ doesn't have built-in enum iteration. The common pattern:

```cpp
enum class Direction { North, East, South, West, Count };  // Add Count as sentinel

for (int i = 0; i < static_cast<int>(Direction::Count); ++i) {
    Direction d = static_cast<Direction>(i);
    // Process d
}
```

> [!warning] Adding `Count` as last value breaks if you assign non-sequential values
> This pattern only works when enum values are 0, 1, 2, 3... sequentially. For non-sequential values, use a separate array of all values.

---

## Enums vs Constants

```cpp
// ❌ Old C-style constants
const int Red = 0;
const int Green = 1;
const int Blue = 2;
// No type grouping, no compiler validation that values are distinct

// ✅ enum class
enum class Color { Red = 0, Green = 1, Blue = 2 };
// Values belong to Color type — compiler ensures type safety
```

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

An enum is a list of named options. Instead of remembering that "3 means blue," you write `Color::Blue` and the compiler knows what you mean. Plain enums are like notes scribbled on a shared whiteboard — anyone can read them and they might conflict with someone else's notes. `enum class` puts each list in its own locked drawer — the names don't leak out and you can't accidentally mix drawers.

### Technical

`enum` declares an unscoped enumeration with implicit conversion to its underlying integral type (default `int`). `enum class` (C++11) declares a scoped enumeration with no implicit conversions — values must be accessed with scope qualification and explicitly cast to the underlying type. `std::to_underlying` (C++23) provides a cleaner alternative to `static_cast`. The underlying type can be explicitly specified (`enum class E : uint8_t`), enabling memory optimization for storage in arrays and bitfields. `using enum` (C++20) imports all enumerators into the current scope.

---

## Exercises

1. **Unscoped vs scoped:** Write `enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }` and `enum class Fruit { Apple, Banana, Orange }`. Try assigning `Red` to a `Fruit`. What does the compiler say?

2. **Underlying type:** `enum class Permission : uint8_t { Read=1, Write=2, Execute=4 }`. Combine with `|`. Check with `&`. Write `bool HasPermission(uint8_t, Permission)`.

3. **to_underlying:** Template function converting enum class to underlying type. Implement with `static_cast` and `std::to_underlying`. What value does to_underlying add?

4. **UE UENUM:** Research `UENUM(BlueprintType)`. How does UE's enum differ from standard? What does `GetValueAsString()` provide?

5. **Enum reflection:** Write a macro generating `ToString(MyEnum)` via switch. Compare to UE's generated reflection. Why no built-in enum-to-string after 40 years?


## See Also

- [[../02 — References|References]] — How enum references work vs values
- [[../../04 — OOP & Generics/01 — Inheritance & Virtual Functions|Inheritance & Virtual Functions]] — Polymorphism vs enum-based dispatch
- [[../../../UE Core/UE Enums & Flags|UE Enums & Flags]] — Unreal's UENUM system
- [[../05 — Type Deduction|Type Deduction]] — How `auto` interacts with enum types

02 \u2014 References.content:
# References

## What Is a Reference?
> [!note] Why This Matters
> References are the backbone of C++ — they enable zero-copy parameter passing, operator overloading without syntax clutter, and function chaining. Without them, every function call would copy its arguments.
> [!info] Systems Connection
> References are typically implemented as pointers under the hood — the compiler stores an address. See [[../../Computer Systems/System Software/Function Calls/00 — Function Calls Overview|Function Calls]] (Computer Systems) for how the CPU passes those addresses through registers during function calls.



A reference is an **alias** — another name for an existing variable. It's not a copy and not a pointer. It IS the original variable, just accessed through a different name.

```cpp
int original = 42;
int& ref = original;  // ref IS original — not a copy, not a pointer

ref = 100;            // Changes original to 100
original = 200;       // Changes ref to 200 — they're the SAME variable
```

**Analogy:** Your legal name vs your nickname. Both refer to the same person. Change one, and the person changes — because there's only one person.

---

## References vs Pointers — The Key Differences

```cpp
int x = 5;

// Pointer
int* ptr = &x;    // ptr holds the ADDRESS of x
*ptr = 10;         // Must dereference to access x
ptr = nullptr;     // Can be null — dangerous!
ptr = &y;          // Can point to something else

// Reference
int& ref = x;      // ref IS x
ref = 10;          // No dereferencing needed — direct access
// ref = nullptr;  // ❌ CANNOT be null
// ref = y;        // ❌ DOES NOT reseat — this assigns y's value to x!
```

| | Pointer (`T*`) | Reference (`T&`) |
|---|---|---|
| Can be null? | ✅ Yes — `nullptr` | ❌ No — must bind to valid object |
| Can be reseated? | ✅ Yes — `ptr = &other` | ❌ No — bound for life |
| Dereference needed? | ✅ Yes — `*ptr` or `ptr->` | ❌ No — direct access |
| Takes address? | `&ptr` gives pointer's own address | `&ref` gives the ORIGINAL's address |
| Syntax | `int* p = &x;` | `int& r = x;` |

---

## Why References Exist

### Reason 1: Function Parameters Without Copying

```cpp
// ❌ Pass by value — copies the entire vector (expensive!)
void ProcessBad(std::vector<int> data) {
    data.push_back(42);  // Only modifies the COPY
}

// ✅ Pass by reference — no copy, modifies the original
void ProcessGood(std::vector<int>& data) {
    data.push_back(42);  // Modifies the ORIGINAL
}
```

### Reason 2: Read-Only Access Without Copying

```cpp
// Const reference — cannot modify, no copy cost
void Print(const std::string& str) {
    // str = "changed";  // ❌ ERROR — const reference
    std::cout << str;    // ✅ OK — reading is fine
}

Print("hello");  // No copy — the temporary string is passed by reference
```

### Reason 3: Operator Overloading

```cpp
FVector operator+(const FVector& A, const FVector& B) {
    return FVector(A.X + B.X, A.Y + B.Y, A.Z + B.Z);
}
// Without references, every operator call would copy both operands — catastrophic.
```

---

## Const References — The Backbone of C++

```cpp
void ProcessData(const LargeStruct& data);  // Read-only, zero copy
```

**The golden rule:** Pass by `const&` unless you specifically need to modify or move the data. This is the default for function parameters in modern C++.

**Lifetime extension:** A const reference extends the lifetime of a temporary:
```cpp
const std::string& ref = GetTemporaryString();  // Temporary lives as long as ref
// Without const&, the temporary would be destroyed at the semicolon
```

---

## References as Return Values

```cpp
class Container {
    std::vector<int> data;
public:
    const std::vector<int>& GetData() const { return data; }  // No copy!
    std::vector<int>& GetDataMutable() { return data; }       // Allows modification
};
```

**Danger alert — returning a reference to a local:**
```cpp
int& GetDanglingReference() {
    int local = 42;
    return local;  // ❌ DANGER! local is destroyed when function returns
}
// The reference now points to garbage — undefined behavior on use
```

---

## When to Use Pointers vs References

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Parameter will always be valid (never null) | `T&` |
| Parameter might be null (optional) | `T*` |
| Need to reseat (point to different object later) | `T*` |
| Return value — always valid | `T&` |
| Return value — might be null | `T*` |
| Function parameter — read-only | `const T&` |
| Stored as member variable | `T*` or smart pointer (reference members are rare and awkward) |

---

## Reference Members (Rare)

```cpp
class Widget {
    std::string& nameRef;  // Reference member — must be initialized in constructor
public:
    Widget(std::string& name) : nameRef(name) {}  // Must use initializer list
    // Cannot default-construct, cannot copy-assign — references are locked for life
};
```

Reference members exist but are uncommon. They prevent default construction and copy assignment. Prefer pointers or smart pointers for member variables unless you have a strong reason.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

A reference is a nickname for a variable. Change the nickname, and the original changes — because they're the same person. Pointers are like a home address written on a sticky note — you can have the address without the house, you can lose the note, and you can change the address on it. References are the house itself — you can't have a reference without a house, you can't lose it, and you can't make it point to a different house.

### Technical

A reference is typically implemented as a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores the address of the referenced object. The difference is semantic: references cannot be null, cannot be reseated, and use value syntax rather than pointer syntax. `const T&` can bind to temporaries, extending their lifetime to the reference's scope. Returning a reference to a local variable is undefined behavior. References enable zero-copy parameter passing and are the fundamental building block of operator overloading and move semantics (rvalue references extend the concept for temporaries).

---

## Exercises

1. **Dangling reference:** Return reference to local variable. Compile with -Wall. Return `const string&` to temporary — does the temporary live?

2. **const& lifetime extension:** Bind temporary to `const string&`. Bind to `string&` (non-const). Compiles? Why asymmetry?

3. **Reference collapsing:** `template<typename T> void Foo(T&&)`. Pass lvalue, rvalue, lvalue ref. Deduce T.

4. **UE pass-by-reference:** Benchmark `void Foo(FVector v)` vs `void Foo(const FVector& v)`. At what size does const& win?

5. **Reference vs pointer:** `int*` vs `int&`. Which is safer? Why does Google prefer pointers for out-params?


## See Also

- [[01 — Enums & enum class|Enums]] — Value types that references can bind to
- [[03 — static in C++|static in C++]] — Static vs reference lifetime
- [[../../03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Where references actually live
- [[../../../UE Core/Pointer Ecosystem|UE Pointer Ecosystem]] — UE's reference-like types

05 \u2014 Type Deduction.content:
# Type Deduction — `auto`, `decltype`, `decltype(auto)`

## `auto` — Let the Compiler Figure Out the Type

```cpp
auto x = 42;                        // int
auto y = 3.14;                      // double
auto z = std::vector<int>{1,2,3};  // std::vector<int>

// Most useful for complex types:
auto it = myMap.find(key);  // Instead of: std::unordered_map<std::string, Widget>::iterator
```

**The rules:** `auto` strips references and const (top-level). This causes surprises:

```cpp
const int& GetValue();
auto v = GetValue();   // v is int — reference AND const both stripped!
auto& r = GetValue();  // r is const int& — reference preserved, const preserved
```

> [!warning] auto strips references — use auto& to keep them
> `auto` never deduces to a reference unless you write `auto&`. This is why `for (auto x : vec)` copies every element — use `for (auto& x : vec)` instead.

---

## `decltype` — What Type Is This Expression?

```cpp
int x = 5;
decltype(x) y = 10;  // y is int — decltype deduces x's declared type

const int& GetRef();
decltype(GetRef()) z = GetRef();  // z is const int& — preserves const and reference
```

Unlike `auto`, `decltype` preserves references and const exactly as declared.

---

## `decltype(auto)` — Best of Both (C++14)

```cpp
const int& GetValue();

auto v1 = GetValue();         // int — strips const and ref
decltype(auto) v2 = GetValue();  // const int& — preserves everything
```

Use `decltype(auto)` for perfect return type forwarding — return exactly what the expression gives you.

---

## When NOT to Use auto

```cpp
// ❌ Obfuscates the type — reader has to deduce what initialize() returns
auto result = initialize();

// ✅ Make the type explicit when the return type isn't obvious
Widget result = initialize();

// ❌ auto in function signatures is confusing
auto Process(/* ... */) { }  // What does this return?

// ✅ Use trailing return type or explicit type
Widget Process(/* ... */) { }
```

---



## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
`auto` is a shortcut — you write `auto x = 5` and the compiler knows you mean `int`. It's like telling someone "pick up that thing" instead of "pick up the blue pen on the left" — the context is enough. `decltype` is the reverse: "what type would that expression have?" Useful when the compiler deduces types you don't want to type out. Just remember: `auto` strips references and const, `auto&` keeps them, and `decltype(auto)` keeps everything.

### Technical
`auto` uses template argument deduction rules — top-level const and references are dropped. `decltype` preserves the exact declared type including references and cv-qualifiers. `decltype(auto)` (C++14) combines the deduction mechanism of `auto` with the type-preserving semantics of `decltype`, commonly used for perfect return type forwarding. Structured bindings with `auto` follow the same rules: `auto [x, y]` copies, `auto& [x, y]` refers.

## Exercises

1. **auto strips references:** `const int& x = GetValue()`. `auto y = x`. What's y's type? Try `auto&` and `decltype(auto)`.

2. **auto with initializer_list:** `auto x = {1,2,3}`. What type? `auto x{1}` vs `auto x = {1}`. Why controversial?

3. **decltype rules:** `int x=0; int& r=x;`. `decltype(x)`, `decltype(r)`, `decltype((x))`. Why extra parens change type?

4. **Forwarding reference:** `template<typename T> void Foo(T&&)`. Call with lvalue and rvalue. Deduce T.

5. **auto return:** Return int one path, double another. `auto` vs `decltype(auto)`?


## See Also

- [[../09 — String Types & string_view|String Types & string_view]] — `auto` with string literals (const char* vs std::string)
- [[../../04 — OOP & Generics/05 — Templates & Concepts|Templates & Concepts]] — Template type deduction rules
- [[../../05 — Modern C++/01 — Move Semantics & Perfect Forwarding|Move Semantics]] — `auto&&` as forwarding reference
- [[../06 — Explicit & Implicit Conversion|Explicit & Implicit Conversion]] — When `auto` hides conversions

06 \u2014 Explicit & Implicit Conversion.content:
# Explicit & Implicit Conversion

## The Core Idea

In C++, a type conversion is **turning an X into a Y.** 

| | **Implicit** | **Explicit** |
|---|---|---|
| Who decides | Compiler | You |
| What you write | Nothing — it just happens | A cast or direct-init |
| Risk | Silent bugs | Visible in code review |
| UE's stance | Allowed sparingly (TObjectPtr → T*) | Preferred; most single-arg ctors are `explicit` |

---

## The Two Mechanisms

A conversion from `Src` to `Dest` can work through **either gate**:

### Gate 1: Conversion operator — written inside the **source** type

```cpp
class Src {
public:
    operator Dest() const { return Dest(/*...*/); }  // "I know how to become a Dest"
};
```

### Gate 2: Converting constructor — written inside the **destination** type

```cpp
class Dest {
public:
    Dest(const Src& s); // NOT explicit — acts as implicit conversion
};
```

The compiler checks **both paths**. If either is non-explicit, the conversion goes through. Both non-explicit → ambiguity error.

---

## What `explicit` Does

`explicit` tells the compiler: **disable this conversion from happening silently.**

| Context | Implicit allowed? |
|---|---|
| Function argument matching (`f(x)`) | ❌ Blocked |
| Return statement conversion (`return x;`) | ❌ Blocked |
| Copy-initialization (`Dest d = src;`) | ❌ Blocked |
| **Direct-initialization** (`Dest d(src);`) | ✅ Allowed |
| **Explicit cast** (`static_cast<Dest>(src)`) | ✅ Allowed |
| **Conditional context** (`if`, `while`, `!`) | ✅ Allowed — special rule for `explicit operator bool` |

```cpp
struct Container { explicit Container(const Tag& t); };
void TakeContainer(Container c);

TakeContainer(myTag);                // ❌ Blocked by explicit
Container c = myTag;                 // ❌ Blocked
Container c(myTag);                  // ✅ Direct-init
TakeContainer(static_cast<Container>(myTag)); // ✅ Explicit cast
```

---

## The `explicit operator bool` Special Case — Contextual Conversion

The standard grants `explicit operator bool` special permission to work implicitly in boolean contexts:

```cpp
explicit operator bool() const { return !IsNull(); }

// ✅ These all work (boolean contexts):
if (ptr)           { }
while (ptr)        { }
ptr && other       { }
!ptr               { }
ptr ? a : b        { }

// ❌ These do NOT:
bool b = ptr;      // ERROR — not a boolean context
int x = ptr + 1;   // ERROR — explicit bool blocks arithmetic
```

This is why smart pointers (`std::unique_ptr`, `TObjectPtr`) can be used in `if (ptr)` without being convertible to `int`. The explicit keyword blocks the dangerous implicit conversion to integer that plagued old `std::auto_ptr`.

---

## User-Defined Conversion Chains — How Many Steps?

The compiler allows at most **ONE user-defined conversion** in an implicit conversion sequence:

```cpp
struct A {};
struct B { B(const A&); };          // A → B (user-defined)
struct C { C(const B&); };          // B → C (user-defined)

void Foo(C c);

Foo(A{});  // ❌ ERROR: requires TWO user-defined conversions: A→B→C
           // The compiler will NOT chain user-defined conversions

// But it WILL chain ONE user-defined + built-in conversions:
struct D { D(int); };               // int → D (user-defined)
void Bar(D d);
Bar(3.14);  // ✅ OK: double→int (built-in) + int→D (user-defined) = one user step
```

This is the **single user-defined conversion rule**. It prevents surprising multi-step conversions and limits the search space during overload resolution.

### The Converting Constructor vs Conversion Operator Ambiguity

```cpp
struct Dest;
struct Src {
    operator Dest() const;  // Src knows how to become Dest
};
struct Dest {
    Dest(const Src&);       // Dest knows how to be built from Src
};

Dest d = Src{};  // ❌ AMBIGUITY: two equally-good paths
                 // Path 1: Src::operator Dest()
                 // Path 2: Dest::Dest(const Src&)
                 // Compiler refuses to choose — you must resolve it
```

Fix: make one of them `explicit`, or refactor to have only one conversion path.

---

## C++20: `explicit(bool)` — Conditional Explicitness

```cpp
template<typename T>
struct Wrapper {
    T value;

    // Explicit ONLY if T is not convertible to int
    explicit(!std::is_convertible_v<T, int>)
    operator int() const { return static_cast<int>(value); }
};

Wrapper<float> wf{3.14f};
int x = wf;         // ✅ OK — float is convertible to int, so operator int() is implicit

Wrapper<std::string> ws{"hello"};
int y = ws;         // ❌ ERROR — string not convertible to int, so operator int() is explicit
```

`explicit(bool)` lets template authors make conversions conditional: safe for `float`, guarded for `std::string`.

---

## Real UE Examples

### `TObjectPtr<T> → `T*` — Implicit

`TObjectPtr` is a transparent wrapper — the implicit conversion to raw pointer is by design, enabling drop-in migration from raw pointers.

### `FGameplayTag` → `FGameplayTagContainer` — Explicit

A single tag and a container of tags are semantically different. `explicit` prevents `RemoveTags(singleTag)` from silently creating a one-element container.

### `FNativeGameplayTag` → `FGameplayTag` — Implicit

Safe — a native tag IS a gameplay tag with a different declaration syntax. Zero information loss.

---

## Practical Rule

```
Q: Can this conversion cause a bug if it happens silently?
   ├─ YES → explicit
   └─ NO  → implicit might be fine

Q: Does this conversion allocate memory or have side effects?
   ├─ YES → explicit (make the cost visible)
   └─ NO  → implicit might be fine

Q: Is this type designed as a transparent wrapper?
   ├─ YES → implicit (like TObjectPtr)
   └─ NO  → explicit by default
```

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
A conversion is turning one type into another. Implicit conversions happen automatically — the compiler sees you passing a `float` where an `int` is expected and silently truncates. Explicit conversions require you to write it out — `static_cast<int>(x)` or direct-init `Container(x)`. `explicit` is the "do not enter" sign on a constructor or conversion operator: the compiler knows how to convert, but refuses to do it silently. The `explicit operator bool` exception lets smart pointers work in `if (ptr)` without being accidentally used in arithmetic. The compiler chains at most ONE user-defined conversion — it won't do A→B→C automatically, preventing surprising multi-hop type changes that are hard to spot in code review.

### Technical
Implicit conversion sequences consist of at most one user-defined conversion (constructor or conversion operator) plus standard conversions (integral promotion, array decay, qualification adjustment). Both conversion operators in the source type and non-explicit converting constructors in the destination type are considered — if both are non-explicit, the call is ambiguous. `explicit` removes the candidate from implicit overload resolution but preserves it for direct-initialization and explicit casts. `explicit operator bool` has special contextual conversion rules allowing boolean contexts (`if`, `while`, `&&`, `!`, `?:`). C++20 `explicit(bool)` makes explicitness conditional on a compile-time predicate, useful in templates. The single-user-defined-conversion rule limits implicit chains and reduces overload resolution complexity.

## See Also

- [[../05 — Modern C++/01 — Move Semantics & Perfect Forwarding|Move Semantics]] — Implicit conversions interact with rvalue reference binding
- [[../05 — Modern C++/02 — constexpr, consteval & constinit|constexpr]] — `explicit(bool)` uses compile-time type traits
- [[04 — OOP & Generics/03 — C++ Casting & RTTI|Casting & RTTI]] — The explicit cast operators (`static_cast`, `dynamic_cast`, `const_cast`, `reinterpret_cast`)

## Exercises

1. **Implicit catastrophe:** Write a class `Meters` with a single-argument constructor `Meters(int)` and a function `void Drive(Kilometers km)`. Call `Drive(100)` — what happens? The int 100 is implicitly converted to `Meters`... but the function wants `Kilometers`! Add `explicit` to the constructor. What changes?
