2. **explicit operator bool:** Create a `SmartPointer<T>` class with `explicit operator bool()`. Test: does `if (ptr)` compile? Does `bool b = ptr;` compile? Does `int x = ptr;` compile? What if you remove `explicit` — what dangerous conversions become possible?

3. **Conversion chain:** Define `struct A { operator int(); }`, `struct B { B(int); }`, `void f(B)`. Does `f(A{})` compile? How many implicit conversions happen? Now add `explicit` to `B(int)` — does it still compile? The standard limits implicit conversion chains to ONE user-defined conversion.

4. **Contextual bool:** Define a class with `explicit operator bool()`. Test it in: `if (obj)`, `while (obj)`, `!obj`, `obj && other`, `obj || other`. Which compile? Which don't? These are "contextual conversions" — the standard carves out exceptions for boolean contexts.

5. **Design:** Your game's `AssetHandle` wraps an integer ID. Should it have `operator int()` for easy comparison with raw IDs? Or should it be `explicit` to prevent `void LoadTexture(AssetHandle)` from accepting a raw `int`? What are the safety vs convenience tradeoffs in a 100K-line codebase?

09 \u2014 String Types & string_view.content:
# String Types & string_view

## The Four String Types of Modern C++

| Type | Owns data? | Mutable? | Null-terminated? | Typical size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `std::string` | ✅ Yes (heap or inline) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (C++11+) | 24-32 bytes |
| `std::string_view` | ❌ No (borrows) | ❌ No | ❌ Not guaranteed | 16 bytes (ptr + size) |
| `const char*` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (by convention) | 8 bytes (pointer) |
| `std::wstring` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 24-32 bytes |

**The rule of thumb:** `string` when you own the data. `string_view` when you're just looking at it. `const char*` when you're calling a C API. Never `wstring` unless the platform demands it (Windows API).

> [!info] C++20 also introduces `std::span<T>` which wraps any contiguous range (including strings) with bounds checking. Useful for passing arrays to functions without pointer decay.

---

## std::string — Owned, Mutable, Null-Terminated

### The Small String Optimization (SSO)

`std::string` doesn't always allocate on the heap. If the string is short enough (typically 15 characters on GCC/MSVC, ~22 on Clang), the characters live inside the string object itself. This is the **Small String Optimization.**

```cpp
std::string short_str = "hello";       // 5 chars — lives IN the string object (no allocation)
std::string long_str(1000, 'x');       // 1000 chars — allocated on the heap
```

**Why SSO matters:** Creating and destroying short strings is nearly free. If you're concatenating names, tags, or identifiers (all typically <15 characters), no heap allocation occurs. This is why `std::string` can be used for small strings without guilt — the compiler handles the optimization transparently.

**SSO capacity by implementation (typical, subject to version changes):**
- **GCC (libstdc++):** ~15 chars (sizeof typically 32 bytes)
- **Clang (libc++):** ~22 chars (sizeof typically 24 bytes)
- **MSVC:** ~15 chars (sizeof typically 32 bytes)

Exact values vary between standard library versions. The key point: most strings under 15 characters never allocate.

### Construction and Allocation

```cpp
std::string a = "hello";                    // From C-string literal
std::string b(10, 'x');                     // "xxxxxxxxxx"
std::string c = a + " world";               // Concatenation (creates new string)
std::string d = std::to_string(42);         // From integer
std::string e = std::format("{} + {}", 1, 2); // C++20 — safer than printf
```

### When It Allocates

```cpp
std::string s;
s = "hello";         // SSO — no allocation (5 chars < 15)
s += " world";       // SSO — no allocation (11 chars < 15)
s += "!!!";          // 14 chars — still SSO, no allocation
s += "?";            // 15 chars — STILL SSO on most implementations
s += " more text";   // 25 chars — ALLOCATION! Exceeds SSO capacity
```

> [!tip] C++20: std::format
> Replace `sprintf`, `stringstream`, and manual concatenation with `std::format`:
> ```cpp
> std::string msg = std::format("Player {} has {} health", name, hp);
> ``
> It's type-safe, readable, and faster than `stringstream` in most cases.

---

## std::string_view — Non-Owning, Read-Only Window

### What It Is

`string_view` is a **borrowed reference** to a string. It doesn't own the data. It doesn't allocate. It's just a pointer and a length. Think of it as `const std::string&` without the lifetime coupling.

```cpp
#include <string_view>

// string_view can point to any contiguous char data
std::string_view sv1 = "hello";              // From C-string literal
std::string s = "world";
std::string_view sv2 = s;                    // From std::string
std::string_view sv3 = s.data() + 2;         // From middle of string: "rld"
std::string_view sv4 = sv3.substr(0, 1);     // "r" — no allocation, just pointer math
```

### Why string_view Is Fast

`substr()` on `std::string` allocates a new string and copies the data. `substr()` on `string_view` just adjusts the pointer and length — zero allocation, zero copy.

```cpp
std::string s = "hello world";

// std::string::substr — ALLOCATES new string, copies 5 chars
std::string sub1 = s.substr(0, 5);  // "hello" — heap allocation!

// std::string_view::substr — just pointer math
std::string_view sv = s;
std::string_view sub2 = sv.substr(0, 5);  // "hello" — zero cost
```

### The Danger: Dangling string_view

Because `string_view` doesn't own data, it can become a dangling reference:

```cpp
std::string_view GetGreeting()
{
    std::string s = "hello";  // Local variable
    return std::string_view(s);  // ❌ DANGER — s is destroyed, sv dangles!
}

// ❌ Undefined behavior — reading freed memory
auto sv = GetGreeting();
std::cout << sv;  // BOOM
```

**The rule:** Use `string_view` for function parameters (where the caller's data outlives the call). Don't store `string_view` as a member variable unless you control the lifetime of the source string.

### When To Use Each

| Scenario | Use |
|---|---|
| Function parameter — read-only string access | `std::string_view` (accepts string, char*, string_view) |
| Return value — need to own the string | `std::string` |
| Parsing — splitting a string into tokens | `std::string_view` (no allocations) |
| Storing in a class member | `std::string` (owns data, safe lifetime) |
| Calling a C API | `s.c_str()` or `sv.data()` (need null-terminated) |

> [!warning] string_view is NOT null-terminated
> `data()` returns a pointer to the start, but there's no guarantee the byte after the end is `\0`. If you need to pass to a C function, use `std::string` which guarantees null termination (since C++11), or create a temporary string with `std::string(sv)`.

---

## const char* — The Legacy Interface

C-string literals and C APIs still use `const char*`. In modern C++, treat it as the "interop type" — convert to `string_view` or `string` at the boundary:

```cpp
void ProcessName(const char* name)  // C API
{
    std::string_view sv(name);       // Convert to modern type immediately
    // Work with sv, not name
}
```

**Why not use `const char*` everywhere?** No size information (every operation needs `strlen`), no bounds checking, manual memory management. `string_view` gives you the same zero-cost borrowing with size tracking.

---

## std::wstring — For Windows (Only)

`wstring` stores wide characters (`wchar_t`). On Windows, this is UTF-16 and required for the Windows API. On Linux/macOS, `wchar_t` is 32-bit and rarely used.

```cpp
#ifdef _WIN32
std::wstring wide = L"Hello";  // Windows API interop
#endif
```

**The rule:** Don't use `wstring` for general-purpose string handling. Use `std::string` (UTF-8) everywhere, and convert to `wstring` only at the Windows API boundary.

---

## C++20/23 String Features

| Feature | What It Does |
|---|---|
| `std::format` (C++20) | Type-safe string formatting, replaces `sprintf` |
| `std::string::starts_with` / `ends_with` (C++20) | Check prefix/suffix without allocating |
| `std::string::contains` (C++23) | Check if substring exists |
| `std::string_view::contains` (C++23) | Same for string_view |
| `std::ranges::to<std::string>` (C++23) | Convert any range to a string |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

A `string` is a suitcase — it carries its contents with it. A `string_view` is a window — you can look through it at someone else's suitcase, but you don't carry anything yourself. A `const char*` is a post-it note with an address — it tells you where to look, but you have to walk there and count the letters yourself.

### Technical

`std::string` is `std::basic_string<char>. Under SSO, the string object contains a union of either inline storage (typically 16 bytes for chars, plus size) or heap pointer + size + capacity. The implementation switches from inline to heap when the string exceeds the SSO threshold. `std::string_view` stores `const CharT* data_` and `size_t size_` — 16 bytes total on 64-bit. It can be constructed from any contiguous char range without allocation. C++20's `std::format` uses `std::format_string` (a `string_view` wrapper) for compile-time format string checking. C++23 adds `contains()`, `resize_and_overwrite()`, and ranges support.

---

## Exercises

1. **SSO benchmark:** 1M small strings (8 chars) vs 1M large (80 chars). Allocation count difference? SSO threshold?

2. **string_view dangling:** Return `string_view` to temporary string. Print. What happens? Why compile silently?

3. **string_view param:** `void Process(string_view sv)`. Call with `const char*`, `string`, literal. Copy count?

4. **UE string types:** `FString` vs `FName` vs `FText`. Scenario where `FString` causes 100x regression vs `FName`.

5. **String conversion:** Accept `string` and `const char*` with single overload. Handle `FString` via `TCHAR_TO_UTF8`.


## See Also

- [[../../01 — Primer/04 — Arrays, Vectors & Loops|Arrays & Vectors]] — Contiguous memory and strings
- [[../../03 — Memory/06 — std vector & Container Internals|std::vector]] — Dynamic array backing for std::string
- [[../../03 — Memory/05 — std array & C Arrays|C Arrays]] — C-string null termination vs std::string
- [[../../05 — Modern C++/01 — Move Semantics & Perfect Forwarding|Move Semantics]] — Move-constructing strings

11 \u2014 Initialization Rules.content:
# Initialization Rules

## Why This Matters

```cpp
Widget w();     // Declares a FUNCTION called w that returns Widget — not a default-constructed Widget
Widget w{};     // Default-constructs a Widget
Widget w;       // Also default-constructs... unless Widget is a built-in type, then it's uninitialized garbage
Widget w(10);   // Direct-initializes with 10
Widget w{10};   // List-initializes with 10 — but if Widget has an initializer_list ctor, that wins
Widget w = 10;  // Copy-initializes... sort of. Actually calls the converting constructor, then elides the copy
auto x{1};      // C++11: initializer_list<int>. C++17: int. Same syntax, different meaning depending on standard.
```

No other C++ feature has this many ways to say the same thing, with subtly different — and sometimes dangerously wrong — meanings. This note untangles every form of initialization and gives you a mental model that survives the standard's complexity.

---

## The Five Families of Initialization

C++ has five distinct initialization syntaxes. They look similar. They are not.

```cpp
// 1. DEFAULT initialization
int x;           // Uninitialized (garbage) for built-ins. Default-ctor for classes.
Widget w;        // Calls Widget()

// 2. VALUE initialization
int x{};         // Zero for built-ins. Default-ctor for classes.
int x = int{};   // Same — zero
Widget w{};      // Calls Widget(). If Widget() is deleted, does aggregate init (C++11+)

// 3. DIRECT initialization
Widget w(1, 2);  // Calls Widget(int, int). Explicit ctors OK.
Widget w{1, 2};  // Same, but prefers initializer_list ctor if it exists

// 4. COPY initialization
Widget w = other; // Calls copy ctor... or move ctor if other is rvalue... or converting ctor
                  // Explicit ctors NOT allowed here

// 5. LIST initialization
Widget w = {1, 2}; // Copy-list-init. Same rules as direct-list-init.
```

The trap: these categories overlap. `Widget w{1, 2}` is BOTH direct-init AND list-init. If `Widget` has `Widget(std::initializer_list<int>)`, that constructor wins — even if a perfect-match `Widget(int, int)` exists.

---

## The Most Vexing Parse

```cpp
struct Timer { Timer(int ms); };
struct Widget { Widget(const Timer& t); };

Widget w(Timer(100));  // Declares: Widget w(Timer (*)(int)) — a FUNCTION!
// w is a function taking a pointer-to-function-taking-int-returning-Timer, returning Widget
```

The compiler prefers to interpret ambiguous syntax as a function declaration. Fix with brace-init or double-parens:

```cpp
Widget w{Timer{100}};  // Object construction
Widget w((Timer(100))); // Extra parens force expression context
```

Anywhere a declaration could be either a function or an object, the function wins. This is the single most confusing compiler error in C++.

---

## Aggregate Initialization

A type is an aggregate if it has no user-declared constructors, no private/protected non-static members, no virtual functions, and no virtual/private/protected base classes (simplified — the rules changed in C++14/17/20).

```cpp
struct Point { float x, y, z; };
Point p{1.0f, 2.0f, 3.0f};  // Aggregate init — members initialized in order
Point p = {1, 2, 3};         // Copy-aggregate-init — same thing
Point p{1.0f};               // p.z is VALUE-initialized → 0.0f

// C++20: designated initializers
Point p{.x = 1.0f, .z = 3.0f};  // y = 0.0f (value-initialized)
```

When do you get aggregate init? The answer changed across C++11 → 14 → 17 → 20. In C++17, a class with a user-declared constructor (even `= default`) is NOT an aggregate. In C++20, it IS again (if the ctor is defaulted or the class has no user-provided ctors). This means `std::array<int, 3> arr = {1, 2, 3}` works because `std::array` IS an aggregate.

---

## Value Init vs Default Init

This is the trap that turns uninitialized variables into CVEs:

```cpp
void Foo() {
    int a;       // DEFAULT-init: uninitialized GARBAGE. UB if read.
    int b{};     // VALUE-init: zeroed.
    int c = int();  // VALUE-init: zeroed (but looks weird)
    
    std::vector<int> v(100);  // All 100 elements VALUE-initialized → zeros
    std::vector<int> v2(100, 42);  // All 100 set to 42 (copy-init, not value-init)
}
```

The rule: for built-in types, default init = garbage, value init = zero. For class types, both call the default constructor — but value init zero-initializes the storage FIRST, then calls the constructor (relevant for types with padding or union-like behavior).

---

## The std::initializer_list Ambush

```cpp
std::vector<int> v{10, 20};  // vector of TWO elements: {10, 20}
std::vector<int> v(10, 20);  // vector of TEN elements, all 20

// The TRAP:
std::vector<int> v{10};      // vector of ONE element: {10}
std::vector<int> v(10);      // vector of TEN zeroed elements
```

When a constructor taking `std::initializer_list` exists, brace-init PREFERS it — even when a more logical constructor exists. This is why `std::vector{10}` and `std::vector(10)` do radically different things. The `initializer_list` constructor wins so aggressively that it's often called the "initializer_list ambush."

---

## auto and Brace Init — The C++11 vs C++17 Schism

```cpp
auto x{1};      // C++11: std::initializer_list<int>.  C++17: int
auto x = {1};   // Always std::initializer_list<int>
auto x{1, 2};   // C++11: std::initializer_list<int>.  C++17: ERROR (N3922)
```

Never use `auto x{...}` for anything except single-element braced init. Always prefer `auto x = ...` for clarity. This was so broken that C++17 changed the rules — and it's still confusing.

---

## Quick Reference

| Syntax | Kind | Explicit ctors OK? | Prefers init_list? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `T x;` | Default | N/A | N/A |
| `T x{};` | Value | N/A | N/A |
| `T x(args);` | Direct | Yes | No |
| `T x{args};` | Direct-list | Yes | **Yes** |
| `T x = other;` | Copy | No | N/A |
| `T x = {args};` | Copy-list | No | **Yes** |
| `T x = T(args);` | Copy from temporary | Yes (via direct) | No |
| `auto x = expr;` | Copy (deduced) | N/A | N/A |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Initialization is how you give a variable its first value. C++ has too many ways: `int x = 5`, `int x(5)`, `int x{5}`, `int x = {5}`, `auto x = 5`. They look different, do slightly different things, and the compiler sometimes interprets them as function declarations instead of variable definitions (the "most vexing parse"). The braces version `{}` is the safest default — it prevents narrowing conversions and avoids the vexing parse. But it has its own trap: if the type has a `std::initializer_list` constructor, that constructor hijacks the braces and may do something unexpected.

### Technical

C++ initialization is governed by [dcl.init] in the standard — one of the densest sections. Default-initialization ([dcl.init.general]/7) performs no initialization for non-class, non-array types (leaving indeterminate values) and calls the default constructor for class types. Value-initialization ([dcl.init.general]/9) zero-initializes the object first, then default-initializes — meaning built-ins become zero and class types get their default ctor called on zeroed storage. List-initialization ([dcl.init.list]) disallows narrowing conversions at compile time and has special overload resolution rules: if an `initializer_list` constructor exists, it's preferred over non-list constructors ([over.match.list]/1.1). This preference is so strong that `std::vector<int>{10}` calls the `initializer_list` ctor (one element: 10) rather than the size ctor (ten zeroes). The most vexing parse ([dcl.ambig.res]/1) is a syntactic ambiguity resolution rule: anything that can be parsed as a function declaration IS parsed as a function declaration. Brace-init sidesteps this because `T x{args}` cannot be a function declaration.

---

## Exercises

1. **Vexing parse in the wild:** Write `std::string s(std::string("hello"))` — what does this declare? Now write it with braces. What's the difference? Try it with `std::vector<int> v(std::vector<int>(10))` — same pattern, same trap.

2. **Init list ambush:** Create a class with BOTH `Widget(int, int)` and `Widget(std::initializer_list<int>)`. Call `Widget{1, 2}` — which constructor runs? Now call `Widget(1, 2)` — which runs? Prove with a print statement in each constructor.

3. **C++11 vs C++17 auto:** Write `auto x{1}`, `auto y = {1}`, `auto z{1, 2}`. Compile with `-std=c++11` and `-std=c++17`. For each, what is the type of x, y, z? Which line fails in C++17 that compiled in C++11?

4. **Value vs default init:** Define `struct S { int a; std::string b; }`. Create `S s1;` (default) and `S s2{};` (value). Print `s1.a` (garbage), `s2.a` (zero), `s1.b` (empty string), `s2.b` (empty string). Which member differs? Why only the int?

5. **Design:** Your game engine has a `Vector3` class with `Vector3(float, float, float)` and you add `Vector3(std::initializer_list<float>)` for convenience. A team member writes `Vector3 up{0, 1, 0}` — which constructor runs? Is this the intended behavior? Should you remove the initializer_list constructor or live with the ambush?

## See Also

- [[01 — Variables, Types & Memory|Variables, Types & Memory]] — What a variable IS at the hardware level
- [[02 — References|References]] — How reference initialization works
- [[../05 — Modern C++/01 — Move Semantics & Perfect Forwarding|Move Semantics]] — Forwarding references and auto&&
- [[../05 — Modern C++/05 — Type Deduction|Type Deduction]] — auto, decltype, template deduction
- [[../../Computer Systems/Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits & Bytes]] — What "zeroed memory" means at the hardware level

02 \u2014 Object Memory Layout.content:
# Object Memory Layout

## Why Layout Matters

> [!info] Systems Connection
> Padding and alignment aren't compiler quirks — they're hardware requirements. CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks. See [[Memory Hierarchy]] for why cache lines (64 bytes) make AoS vs SoA a 3-5x performance difference.

The puzzle:

```cpp
struct Bad {
    char a;    // 1 byte
    int  b;    // 4 bytes
    char c;    // 1 byte
};

std::cout << sizeof(Bad);  // 12 — NOT 6!
```

Six bytes of data, twelve bytes of object. Where did the extra 6 come from? **Padding.** The CPU reads memory in aligned chunks — a 4-byte `int` must live at an address divisible by 4. The compiler inserts invisible padding bytes to satisfy these alignment rules. If you don't understand this, your structs silently waste memory and your cache performance suffers.

---

## Alignment — The Hardware Reason

CPUs don't read memory one byte at a time — they read in **words** (4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit). A 4-byte `int` stored at an address that crosses a word boundary requires TWO reads — the CPU must fetch both words, extract the pieces, and stitch them together. Aligned data — where the `int` sits entirely within one word — needs only one read.

This is why alignment rules exist: a type of size N must live at an address divisible by N (for fundamental types up to pointer size). The compiler enforces this by inserting invisible padding bytes.

**Analogy:** Imagine you're filling a bookshelf where every shelf must be exactly 4 books wide. A thin book (1-wide) can go anywhere. A medium book (2-wide) must start at the left or middle of a shelf. A thick book (4-wide) must start at the left edge of a shelf. If you put a thin book, then a thick book, you can't start the thick book at position 1 — it must start at position 4. Positions 2 and 3 are empty padding.

```cpp
alignof(char);   // 1 — anywhere
alignof(short);  // 2 — even addresses
alignof(int);    // 4 — addresses divisible by 4
alignof(double); // 8 — addresses divisible by 8
alignof(void*);  // 8 on 64-bit
```

The struct itself inherits the alignment of its most-aligned member. A struct containing an `int` has `alignof(struct) == 4`. This means the struct's total size must be divisible by its alignment — hence trailing padding.

---

## How Padding Actually Works

Let's trace `struct Bad`:

```
Offset 0:  char a    (1 byte)   [a]
Offset 1:  PADDING   (3 bytes)  [--]  ← int b must be at offset 4 (aligned)
Offset 4:  int b     (4 bytes)  [b b b b]
Offset 8:  char c    (1 byte)   [c]
Offset 9:  PADDING   (3 bytes)  [--]  ← struct total must be multiple of 4 (int alignment)

Total: 12 bytes. Wasted: 6.
```

**Reorder the fields and the waste disappears:**

```cpp
struct Good {
    int  b;    // 4 bytes at offset 0
    char a;    // 1 byte at offset 4
    char c;    // 1 byte at offset 5
    // PADDING: 2 bytes at offset 6-7 (to align total to 4)
};

std::cout << sizeof(Good);  // 8 — saved 33%!
```

Offset trace for `Good`:

```
Offset 0:  int b     [b b b b]
Offset 4:  char a    [a]
Offset 5:  char c    [c]
Offset 6:  PADDING   [-- --]  ← total must be multiple of 4
```

**Rule: sort members by descending size.** `double`s first, then `int`s, then `short`s, then `char`s. Most compilers don't reorder (they must preserve declaration order), so YOU must.

---

## `sizeof` Surprises

### Empty Base Optimization

```cpp
struct Empty {};           // sizeof(Empty) = 1 (can't be zero — must have an address)

struct Derived : Empty {
    int x;
};                         // sizeof(Derived) = 4 — Empty takes ZERO space! (EBO)

struct Composite {
    Empty e;               // sizeof(e) = 1
    int x;                 // +4 + 3 padding
};                         // sizeof(Composite) = 8 — Empty as MEMBER wastes space!
```

**The rule:** Empty BASE classes can be optimized away (zero size). Empty MEMBER variables cannot (they must have unique addresses). C++20's `[[no_unique_address]]` fixes this:

```cpp
struct Composite {
    [[no_unique_address]] Empty e;  // May overlap with other members
    int x;
};                                   // sizeof(Composite) = 4 — saved 4 bytes!
```

### vtable Pointer

```cpp
struct Plain { int x; int y; };           // sizeof = 8
struct Polymorphic { virtual ~Polymorphic() = default; int x; int y; };  // sizeof = 24!
// 8 bytes for vtable pointer + 8 bytes for ints + 8 bytes padding (vtable ptr is 8-byte aligned)
```

Every polymorphic class carries an invisible 8-byte vtable pointer (vptr) at offset 0. This is the cost of `virtual`. UE's `Cast<T>()` avoids vtable walks, but the vptr itself still costs space.

---

## Struct of Arrays vs Array of Structs

**This is the single most impactful data layout decision for game performance.**

```cpp
// Array of Structs — intuitive, cache-unfriendly
struct HitBox_AoS {
    FVector position;   // 12 bytes
    FVector extent;     // 12 bytes
    int ownerID;        // 4 bytes
    bool active;        // 1 byte
    // ... more rarely-accessed fields
};
std::vector<HitBox_AoS> hitboxes(1000);

// Iterating positions:
for (auto& hb : hitboxes)
    CheckCollision(hb.position);  // Loads 29+ bytes per iteration, uses only 12
                                   // Cache lines fill with cold data (extent, owner, flags)
```

```cpp
// Struct of Arrays — cache-friendly
struct HitBoxes_SoA {
    std::vector<FVector> positions;    // 12,000 bytes contiguous
    std::vector<FVector> extents;      // 12,000 bytes contiguous
    std::vector<int>     ownerIDs;     // 4,000 bytes contiguous
    std::vector<bool>    active;       // Separate
};

// Iterating positions:
for (auto& pos : hitboxes.positions)
    CheckCollision(pos);  // Every cache line is 100% positions — zero waste
```

**The rule:** If you iterate ALL fields of an object, use AoS (everything in cache). If you iterate only SOME fields, use SoA (cache lines are dense with what you actually use). Fighting games iterate positions AND extents for collision → SoA wins.

---

## Cache Lines

### What Cache Is

Your CPU is much faster than your RAM. Reading from RAM takes ~100 CPU cycles. To bridge this gap, CPUs have **cache** — small, ultra-fast memory inside the CPU itself.

| Cache Level | Size | Speed | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | 32-64 KB per core | ~4 cycles | Inside each core |
| L2 | 256 KB – 2 MB per core | ~12 cycles | Inside each core |
| L3 | 4-32 MB shared | ~40 cycles | Shared across all cores |
| RAM | 8-64 GB | ~100 cycles | Separate chips |

When the CPU reads any data from RAM, it copies the data into L3, then L2, then L1. Next time you access that data (or data nearby), it's already in cache — near-instant. This is why iteration order matters: if you access memory sequentially, every read after the first is from L1 cache, not RAM.

### Cache Lines

A cache line is the smallest chunk the CPU reads — **64 bytes** on all modern x86-64 CPUs. When you read even 1 byte, the CPU loads the entire surrounding 64-byte line into L1. If you're going to need the next 63 bytes anyway (because your data is laid out contiguously), this is free performance. If you don't need them, you just evicted useful data for garbage — a **cache miss.**

**Spatial locality:** Structs are good here — all fields of one object sit together. Iterating all fields of a struct loads one cache line and gets everything.

**Temporal locality:** If you access the same data multiple times in a short window, it stays in L1/L2 cache. The stack has excellent temporal locality because you're constantly reusing the top of it.

### False Sharing — The Silent 100x Slowdown

Two threads write to different variables that happen to live on the same cache line. Each write invalidates the other core's copy of the entire 64-byte line, forcing a costly cache coherency protocol ping-pong — even though they never touch each other's data.

```cpp
struct Counters {
    int thread1Counter;  // Written by thread 1
    int thread2Counter;  // Written by thread 2
    // Both on the same cache line → FALSE SHARING!
};
// Fix: pad to cache line boundaries
struct alignas(64) Counters {
    int thread1Counter;
    char padding[60];    // Ensure thread2Counter is on its own cache line
    int thread2Counter;
};
// Or: alignas(64) for each counter individually
```

`alignas(64)` ensures each variable starts at a 64-byte boundary — guaranteeing they live on different cache lines. The struct is now 128 bytes but the threads stop stepping on each other.

---

## Trivially Copyable — When `memcpy` Is Safe

Some types can be safely copied by just copying their bytes. Others (with constructors, destructors, or virtual functions) cannot. The type trait that answers this:

```cpp
std::is_trivially_copyable_v<int>;           // true
std::is_trivially_copyable_v<std::string>;   // false — has destructor, manages heap memory
std::is_trivially_copyable_v<FVector>;       // true — just three floats
std::is_trivially_copyable_v<AActor>;        // false — virtual destructor, UObject hierarchy
```

**Why this matters for performance:** If a type is trivially copyable, you can use `memcpy`/`memmove` instead of per-element copy constructors. This is how `TArray::RemoveAt` achieves O(N) shift-left without calling constructors — it just `memmove`s the trivially-copyable elements:

```cpp
// Simplified RemoveAt for trivially-copyable T
void RemoveAt(int index)
{
    // Shift elements after 'index' left by one — memmove, no constructors called
    std::memmove(data + index, data + index + 1, (size - index - 1) * sizeof(T));
    --size;
}
```

UE's `TArray` checks `TIsTriviallyCopyConstructible<T> to decide whether to use `memmove` (fast) or per-element move constructors (slower but correct for non-trivial types).

---

## Quick Reference

| Problem | Diagnosis | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `sizeof` bigger than expected | Padding between members | Reorder fields by descending size |
| Hot loop slower than expected | Cache misses from scattered data | SoA instead of AoS |
| Multithreaded code inexplicably slow | False sharing | `alignas(64)` on per-thread data |
| Container operations (remove, resize) slow | Non-trivial types forcing per-element construction | Use trivially-copyable types where safe |
| Struct with many booleans wastes space | 1-byte bool + 3 bytes padding per bool | Bitfield or `std::bitset` |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

The CPU reads memory in fixed-size chunks (64 bytes). If your data is laid out well, one chunk gives you everything you need. If not, the CPU spends most of its time waiting for memory. Think of it like organizing files on a desk: a pile of papers (scattered data) means you hunt through the pile for every document. A filing cabinet with labeled folders (aligned, dense data) means you grab one folder and get everything at once.

### Technical

Memory alignment requirements derive from the CPU's load/store architecture. The compiler inserts padding to satisfy alignment constraints while preserving declaration order. Struct size is determined by the most-aligned member; trailing padding ensures array elements remain aligned. Cache lines (64 bytes on x86-64) dictate spatial locality — data structures should organize hot fields contiguously. False sharing occurs when independent writes target the same cache line, triggering MESI protocol invalidations. Trivially-copyable types (defined by `std::is_trivially_copyable`) can be bit-copied via `memcpy`/`memmove`, enabling container optimizations that bypass constructors/destructors. SoA transforms multiple parallel arrays into dense per-field streams, trading one hot loop's dense cache usage for another loop's sparse access.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Given `struct A { char a; int b; double c; };` what is `sizeof(A)` on x86-64? Show the padding bytes.
2. Reorder the fields to minimize `sizeof(A)`.
3. Write a benchmark comparing AoS (Array of Structs) vs SoA (Struct of Arrays) for iterating a single field of 10 million objects. Measure the performance difference.
4. What is false sharing? Write a program where two threads increment adjacent `int` fields and observe the slowdown. Fix it with `alignas(64)`.
5. Why is `std::vector` iteration faster than `std::list` iteration even when both have O(n) complexity? (Hint: cache lines and prefetching)

# std::array & C Arrays

## The Problem with C Arrays

> [!note] Why This Matters
> C arrays have been the source of more security vulnerabilities than any other language feature. `std::array` gives you the same zero-overhead stack allocation with bounds safety and a standard container interface.

```cpp
int arr[10];              // Fixed size, no bounds checking
arr[10] = 5;              // ❌ UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR — out of bounds, silent corruption

void Process(int arr[]) { // arr decays to int* — size information LOST
    // sizeof(arr) = 8 (pointer size), NOT 40 (10 ints)
}

int* ptr = arr;           // Decays to pointer automatically — dangerous
```

---

## std::array — The Fix (C++11)

```cpp
#include <array>

std::array<int, 10> arr;        // Fixed-size array, stack-allocated
arr[10] = 5;                    // ❌ UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR (but .at() throws!)
arr.at(10) = 5;                 // ❌ Throws std::out_of_range

size_t s = arr.size();          // 10 — size preserved!
bool empty = arr.empty();       // false

// Iteration
for (const auto& elem : arr) { }  // Works naturally
for (size_t i = 0; i < arr.size(); ++i) { }  // Traditional

// Comparison
std::array<int, 3> a{1,2,3}, b{1,2,3};
if (a == b) { }  // Element-by-element comparison — works!
```

**The rule:** Always prefer `std::array` over C arrays. Same performance (stack-allocated, zero overhead) but with size tracking, bounds-checked access via `.at()`, and standard container interface.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

C arrays are like carrying loose papers — the size gets lost the moment you hand them to a function. `std::array` is those papers in a labeled folder — the size stays with it, you can check bounds, and it works with all the standard library tools. Same speed (both live on the stack), but `std::array` is safe.

### Technical

`std::array<T, N> is an aggregate type wrapping a C-style array — it has zero runtime overhead (no heap allocation, no vtable, sizeof equals N * sizeof(T)). Unlike C arrays, `std::array` preserves size information, supports `.at()` (bounds-checked, throws `std::out_of_range`), `.size()`, iterators, and comparison operators. C arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions — losing the size and enabling buffer overflows. `std::array` passes by reference, preserving all information.

## When to Use Which

| Scenario | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed size, known at compile time, stack | `std::array<T, N>` | Same performance as C array, safer, standard interface |
| Dynamic size, heap | `std::vector<T>` | Resizes automatically, RAII |
| Legacy C API interop | `int arr[10]` or `.data()` from `std::array` | C functions expect raw pointers |
| Multidimensional fixed size | `std::array<std::array<T, C>, R>` | Nested — no heap overhead |

## C++20: `std::to_array`

```cpp
int c_arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
auto arr = std::to_array(c_arr);  // Deduces std::array<int, 5>

// Useful when you have a C array from a legacy API:
auto modern = std::to_array(GetLegacyArray());
```

`std::array` is zero-overhead — `sizeof(std::array<int, 5>) == sizeof(int[5]) == 20 bytes`. It's purely a compile-time wrapper with no runtime cost.

## Exercises

1. **Decay trap:** `void Foo(int arr[10])`. sizeof(arr) inside? Use `std::array<int,10>` — sizeof?

2. **Bounds safety:** C array `arr[5]`. `arr[10]=42`. Crash? AddressSanitizer. `std::array::at(10)`?

3. **to_array (C++20):** `auto arr = std::to_array({1,2,3,4,5})`. Type? Better than specifying size?

4. **Passing to C:** `std::array<int,100> to `void Legacy(int*, size_t)`. Get pointer and size?

5. **UE TArray vs std::array:** When choose std::array over TArray? TArray overhead?

06 \u2014 std vector & Container Interna.content:
# std::vector & Container Internals

## The Two Most Important Numbers

Every `std::vector` has two sizes. Understanding the difference is 90% of using vectors correctly:

```cpp
std::vector<int> v;
v.size();       // Number of elements currently stored — 0
v.capacity();   // Number of elements that can be stored without reallocation — 0

v.push_back(1);
// size() = 1, capacity() >= 1 (implementation-dependent)
```

| Member | Meaning | Cost to change |
|---|---|---|
| `size()` | How many elements you've put in | O(1) — just reads a field |
| `capacity()` | How many elements the allocated memory can hold | O(1) — just reads a field |

**The critical insight:** `push_back` is O(1) **only when `size() < capacity()`**. When `size() == capacity()`, the vector must reallocate — allocate a larger buffer, copy (or move) all existing elements, and free the old buffer. That one `push_back` is O(n), where n is the current size.

---

## The Growth Factor — How Capacity Increases

When a vector reallocates, it doesn't add one slot. It multiplies:

```cpp
// Typical implementations:
// GCC (libstdc++):  capacity = capacity * 2
// Clang (libc++):  capacity = capacity * 2
// MSVC:            capacity = capacity * 1.5
```

**Why geometric growth?** If you add one slot at a time, adding N elements costs O(N²) — you copy 0 + 1 + 2 + ... + (N-1) elements. With 2x growth, you copy N/2 + N/4 + N/8 + ... ≈ N elements across all reallocations. The **amortized** cost per `push_back` is O(1).

**Growth factor 1.5 vs 2.0:** 1.5x is more memory-efficient (the previously freed buffer is never large enough to hold the new allocation, so memory can be reused). 2x is slightly faster (fewer reallocations). The standard requires amortized O(1) — any geometric factor achieves this.

**Concrete trace — adding 17 elements with 2x growth:**

| push_back # | size after | capacity after | Reallocation? | Copies this step |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Yes | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | Yes | 1 |
| 3 | 3 | 4 | Yes | 2 |
| 4 | 4 | 4 | No | 0 |
| 5 | 5 | 8 | Yes | 4 |
| 6-8 | 6-8 | 8 | No | 0 |
| 9 | 9 | 16 | Yes | 8 |
| 10-16 | 10-16 | 16 | No | 0 |
| 17 | 17 | 32 | Yes | 16 |

Total copies: 31 across 6 reallocations. Average: ~1.8 copies per insertion. That's amortized O(1) — the cost per insertion approaches a constant as n grows.

---

## push_back vs emplace_back

```cpp
struct Person
{
    std::string name;
    int age;
    
    Person(const std::string& n, int a) : name(n), age(a) {}
};

std::vector<Person> people;

// push_back — constructs a temporary, then moves it into the vector
people.push_back(Person("Alice", 30));
// Step 1: Construct Person("Alice", 30) — temporary
// Step 2: Move-construct the temporary into the vector
// Step 3: Destroy the temporary

// emplace_back — constructs directly inside the vector
people.emplace_back("Bob", 25);
// Step 1: Construct Person("Bob", 25) inside the vector's memory
// No temporary. No move.
```

**When `emplace_back` matters:** For types that are expensive to move (large structs), or non-movable (like `std::mutex`), or where the temporary construction + move costs more than direct construction.

**When `push_back` is fine:** For cheap-to-move types (ints, pointers, strings — SSO means they're 24 bytes and moving is O(1)). In practice, the difference is negligible for most types. Use whichever reads better.

> [!tip] Implicit Conversions Warning
> `emplace_back` uses perfect forwarding. Arguments are forwarded directly to the element's constructor. This can produce surprise results if you pass the wrong number or type of arguments — for example, `v.emplace_back(10, 'x')` for a `vector<string> constructs a `string(10, 'x')` (10 copies of 'x'), which might not be what the caller intended. Another trap: `v.emplace_back({1, 2, 3})` with brace-init can fail type deduction — use `push_back({1, 2, 3})` instead when passing initializer lists. `push_back` would catch a type mismatch at compile time because it expects a fully constructed object. With `emplace_back`, always verify the argument list matches one of the element type's constructors.

---

## reserve() — Pre-Allocate, No Surprises

If you know how many elements you'll need, call `reserve()` before the insert loop:

```cpp
std::vector<int> v;
v.reserve(1000);  // Allocate space for 1000 elements once

for (int i = 0; i < 1000; ++i)
{
    v.push_back(i);  // No reallocations — capacity was already reserved
}
```

**Without `reserve()`:** 11 reallocations, ~1000 total copies.

**With `reserve()`:** 1 allocation, 0 copies during insert.

`reserve()` changes `capacity()` but not `size()`. `resize()` changes `size()` (default-constructing or copying new elements). Don't confuse them.

---

## Iterator Invalidation — The Silent Killer

Every vector operation falls into one of three categories for iterator safety:

| Operation | Invalidates... | Safe if... |
|---|---|---|
| `push_back`, `emplace_back` | ALL iterators | Only if `size() < capacity()` and you're not inserting past the end |
| `insert`, `emplace` | Iterators at and after insertion point | You reassign iterators after the operation |
| `erase` | Iterators at and after erasure point | Use the returned iterator: `it = v.erase(it)` |
| `clear` | ALL iterators | — |
| `reserve` (with growth) | ALL iterators | — |
| `operator[]`, `at()`, `front()`, `back()` | Nothing | Always safe |
| Reading through iterators without modifying | Nothing | Always safe |

**The classic crash:**

```cpp
std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};

// ❌ Push triggers reallocation — it is now a dangling pointer
for (auto it = v.begin(); it != v.end(); ++it)
{
    if (*it == 3)
        v.push_back(6);  // Reallocation! All iterators invalidated!
    // BOOM — it now points to freed memory
}
```

**The fix:**

```cpp
// ✅ Reserve first
std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
v.reserve(v.size() + expected_additions);

for (auto it = v.begin(); it != v.end(); ++it)
{
    if (*it == 3)
        v.push_back(6);  // Safe — capacity already allocated
}
```

**The erase loop pattern:**

```cpp
// ✅ Use the returned iterator
for (auto it = v.begin(); it != v.end(); )
{
    if (ShouldRemove(*it))
        it = v.erase(it);  // erase returns iterator to next element
    else
        ++it;
}

// ✅ Or use erase-remove idiom (C++20: std::erase_if)
std::erase_if(v, ShouldRemove);
```

---

## Beyond vector — Choosing The Right Container

| Container | Best For | Avoid For |
|---|---|---|
| `std::vector` | Sequential access, random access by index, stack-like push/pop at end | Insert/erase in the middle, frequent front insertion |
| `std::deque` | Push/pop at both ends, no reallocation (chunked storage) | Contiguous memory requirement |
| `std::list` | Frequent insert/erase anywhere, no iterator invalidation on insert | Random access, cache performance (pointer chasing) |
| `std::array` | Fixed-size, compile-time known, stack-allocated | Dynamic resizing |
| `std::set` / `std::map` | Sorted data, O(log n) lookup/insert | Hash-table performance needed |
| `std::unordered_set` / `unordered_map` | O(1) average lookup/insert | Ordered iteration, predictable memory |

> [!important] The Default Is vector
> `std::vector` is the right default for 90% of use cases. Its contiguous memory means excellent cache performance. Only switch when you have a specific need: front insertion (`deque`), stable pointers (`list`), or O(1) lookup (`unordered_map`).

---

## C++20/23 Container Features

| Feature | What It Does |
|---|---|
| `std::erase` / `std::erase_if` (C++20) | Remove elements from any container by value or predicate — no more erase-remove idiom |
| `std::ranges::contains` (C++23) | Check if a value exists in any range (non-member, works with vector) |
| `std::ranges::to<std::vector>` (C++23) | Convert any range to a container |
| `std::flat_map` / `std::flat_set` (C++23) | Sorted vector-backed map/set — better cache performance, worse insert |
| `std::mdspan` (C++23) | Multi-dimensional array view |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

A `vector` is like a parking lot. `size()` is how many cars are parked. `capacity()` is how many parking spaces exist. When the lot is full and another car arrives, you build a bigger lot, move all the cars over, and demolish the old one. That's a reallocation — expensive, but rare. `reserve()` is like building the big lot before cars arrive so you never have to move them.

### Technical

`std::vector<T> stores three pointers (or a pointer + two size fields): begin, end (size), and end of storage (capacity). On 64-bit, sizeof(vector<T>)` = 24 bytes. The growth factor is implementation-defined but must be exponential to satisfy the amortized O(1) requirement. GCC/libc++ use 2x; MSVC uses 1.5x. `emplace_back` uses perfect forwarding and placement new to construct in-place. Reallocation move-constructs elements from the old buffer; if the move constructor is `noexcept`, elements are moved (fast); otherwise they're copied (slow). C++20's `std::erase_if` replaces the erase-remove idiom. C++23's `std::flat_map` stores sorted key-value pairs in a contiguous vector, trading O(n) insert for O(log n) binary search lookup with excellent cache behavior.

---

## Exercises

1. **Growth factor:** push_back 1M ints. Track capacity. Growth factor? Why not 1?

2. **emplace_back vs push_back:** `pair<int,string>`. 1000 of each. Count copy/move ctor calls.

3. **Iterator invalidation:** Push 5, save iterator to [3], push 100 more. Dereference saved?

4. **Shrink to fit:** 1000 elements, resize(10). capacity()? shrink_to_fit() guaranteed?

5. **UE TArray growth:** TArray::Add vs vector::push_back. Growth strategy difference? TArray::Shrink() guarantee?

11 \u2014 std deque, set & Container Ada.content:
# std::deque, std::set & Container Adaptors

> [!info] Prerequisites: [[06 — std vector & Container Internals]], [[07 — std map & unordered_map]]

---

## Why

vector is the right choice 90% of the time. But sometimes you need: fast insertion at BOTH ends (deque), always-sorted unique elements (set), or a LIFO/FIFO wrapper over existing containers (stack/queue). These are the second-tier containers — less common than vector and map, but essential when you need their specific properties.

---

## std::deque — Double-Ended Queue

Like vector, but with O(1) push_front AND push_back. Unlike vector, elements are NOT contiguous in memory — they're stored in fixed-size blocks (typically 512 bytes).

```cpp
#include <deque>

std::deque<int> d;
d.push_back(3);    // [3]
d.push_front(1);   // [1, 3]
d.push_back(5);    // [1, 3, 5]
d.push_front(0);   // [0, 1, 3, 5]

d[2] = 99;         // random access O(1) — but slightly slower than vector (two indirections)
d.front();         // 0
d.back();          // 5
d.pop_front();     // [1, 3, 5]
d.pop_back();      // [1, 3]
```

**Memory layout:**

```
[Block 0]  →  [ptr to block] [ptr to block] [ptr to block]
[Block 1]  →  [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem]
[Block 2]  →  [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem] [elem]
...
```

A map of pointers to fixed-size blocks. This means:

- No reallocation on growth (just allocate another block) → references/pointers to elements are STABLE (except at ends)
- Appending at front just writes to a new block → O(1)
- Random access requires: block_index = pos / block_size, offset = pos % block_size → two indirections

| | vector | deque |
|---|---|---|
| push_back | Amortized O(1), may reallocate | O(1), never reallocates |
| push_front | O(n) — shift everything | O(1) |
| Random access | O(1), single pointer arithmetic | O(1), two indirections |
| Memory | Contiguous | Block-based (cache-friendly but not single buffer) |
| Pointer stability | None on reallocation | Stable (except erased elements) |

**When deque wins:** FIFO queue (need both push_back and pop_front), undo/redo history, work-stealing task queues. When you want vector-like random access but need front insertion.

---

## std::set — Always Sorted, Always Unique

A set is a mathematical set: elements are unique and stored in sorted order. Insertion is O(log n). Iteration produces elements in order.

```cpp
#include <set>

std::set<int> numbers = {5, 1, 3, 1, 4};
// Result: {1, 3, 4, 5} — duplicate 1 removed, sorted

numbers.insert(2);     // {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
numbers.erase(3);      // {1, 2, 4, 5}

if (numbers.count(4)) {} // O(log n) — check existence

// Lower/upper bound:
auto it = numbers.lower_bound(3);  // first >= 3
auto it2 = numbers.upper_bound(3); // first > 3

// Custom ordering:
auto cmp = [](int a, int b) { return a > b; };
std::set<int, decltype(cmp)> descending(cmp);  // {5, 4, 3, 2, 1}
```

**Implementation:** Red-black tree (same as std::map). Same performance characteristics. Uses `operator<` by default for ordering.

**std::multiset** allows duplicates. **std::unordered_set** is the hashed version (O(1) average, no ordering).

---

## Container Adaptors — stack, queue, priority_queue

These don't store data themselves — they wrap an existing container and restrict its interface.

### std::stack — LIFO (Last In, First Out)

```cpp
#include <stack>

std::stack<int> s;  // wraps std::deque by default
s.push(1);
s.push(2);
s.push(3);

s.top();    // 3 — peek at top
s.pop();    // removes 3
s.size();   // 2

// Use vector as underlying container (rare):
std::stack<int, std::vector<int>> sv;
```

### std::queue — FIFO (First In, First Out)

```cpp
#include <queue>

std::queue<int> q;  // wraps std::deque by default
q.push(1);
q.push(2);
q.push(3);

q.front();  // 1 — first inserted
q.back();   // 3 — last inserted
q.pop();    // removes 1
```

### std::priority_queue — Largest Element First

```cpp
std::priority_queue<int> pq;
pq.push(3);
pq.push(1);
pq.push(5);

pq.top();  // 5 — always the largest
pq.pop();  // removes 5
pq.top();  // 3

// Custom comparator — min-heap (smallest first):
auto cmp = [](int a, int b) { return a > b; };
std::priority_queue<int, std::vector<int>, decltype(cmp)> min_pq(cmp);
```

**Implementation of priority_queue:** A binary heap stored in a vector. push = O(log n) (sift up), pop = O(log n) (sift down), top = O(1).

---

## Container Selection Guide

| Need | Use |
|---|---|
| Fast random access, grow at end only | `vector` |
| Fast insert/remove at FRONT and BACK | `deque` |
| Always-sorted unique elements | `set` |
| Always-sorted, allow duplicates | `multiset` |
| O(1) lookup, no ordering | `unordered_set` |
| LIFO (undo stack, expression evaluation) | `stack` |
| FIFO (task queue, BFS) | `queue` |
| Priority ordering (Dijkstra, task scheduler) | `priority_queue` |

---

## UE Equivalents

```cpp
// UE doesn't have exact std::deque equivalent — TArray with RemoveAt(0) is O(n)
// For queue behavior, use TQueue (lock-free, thread-safe):
TQueue<AActor*> ActorQueue;

// UE has TSet for unordered (hash-based):
TSet<FName> UniqueNames;

// For sorted sets, UE has TSortedSet or TSortedMap:
// (No built-in tree-based set like std::set)
```

---

## Exercises

1. **Deque benchmark:** Push 1M ints to front of deque vs vector. Measure. Push to back. Measure. Why is vector push_front O(n)? Draw the memory movement.

2. **Set operations:** Create two sets `{1,2,3,4,5}` and `{3,4,5,6,7}`. Compute union, intersection, difference using `std::set_union`, `std::set_intersection`, `std::set_difference`. Complexity?

3. **Priority queue Dijkstra:** Implement Dijkstra's shortest path using priority_queue. Why min-heap? What happens with max-heap by mistake?

4. **Stack evaluation:** Implement a postfix expression evaluator using stack. Input: `"3 4 + 2 *"` → result 14. Use std::stack.

5. **UE queue:** Compare TQueue (lock-free MPMC) to std::queue. When would you use TQueue over std::queue in a game engine? Thread safety implications?

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Vector = one-ended tube (add at back). Deque = tube open at both ends (add at front OR back). Set = sorted unique collection (no duplicates, always in order). Stack = pile of plates (last on, first off). Queue = line at a store (first in, first out). Priority queue = emergency room (most urgent first).

### Technical

deque: map of pointers to fixed-size blocks. O(1) push_front without reallocation. Random access: two indirections. set: red-black tree, O(log n) operations. Container adaptors (stack/queue/priority_queue) template over underlying container (default deque for stack/queue, vector for priority_queue). priority_queue uses std::make_heap, push_heap, pop_heap on the underlying container.

## See Also

- [[06 — std vector & Container Internals]] — The primary container
- [[07 — std map & unordered_map]] — Associative containers
- [[05 — std array & C Arrays]] — Fixed-size container

04 \u2014 Operator Overloading.content:
# Operator Overloading

## Why Overload Operators?

You can make your custom types work like built-in types — with `+`, `==`, `<<`, and more.

```cpp
FVector pos = FVector(1, 0, 0) + FVector(0, 1, 0);  // pos = (1, 1, 0)
if (pos == FVector(1, 1, 0)) { }  // Natural comparison
std::cout << pos;                 // Printable
```

Without operator overloading, you'd write:
```cpp
FVector pos = FVector::Add(FVector(1,0,0), FVector(0,1,0));  // Ugly and unreadable
```

---

## Which Operators Can You Overload?

Almost all of them — 38 operators are overloadable:

| Category | Operators |
|---|---|
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` |
| Comparison | `==`, `!=`, <`, `>, <=`, `>=`, <=>` (C++20) |
| Assignment | `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=` |
| Increment/Decrement | `++`, `--` (prefix and postfix) |
| Subscript | `[]` |
| Function call | `()` |
| Dereference | `*`, `->` |
| Stream | `<<`, `>>` |
| Logical | `!`, `&&`, `||` |

**Cannot overload:** `.` (member access), `::` (scope), `?:` (ternary), `sizeof`, `typeid`

---

## Member vs Non-Member Operators

```cpp
struct Vector {
    float x, y;

    // Member operator — left operand is *this
    Vector operator+(const Vector& rhs) const {
        return Vector{x + rhs.x, y + rhs.y};
    }
};

// Non-member operator — both operands are parameters
Vector operator-(const Vector& lhs, const Vector& rhs) {
    return Vector{lhs.x - rhs.x, lhs.y - rhs.y};
}
```

**The rule:**
- `=`, `[]`, `()`, `->` MUST be members
- `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` — typically non-member (symmetry — both sides treated equally)
- `<<`, `>>` — MUST be non-member (left operand is `std::ostream&`, not your type)

---

## The Spaceship Operator `<=>` (C++20)

```cpp
struct Person {
    std::string name;
    int age;

    // One operator gives you ALL comparisons automatically
    auto operator<=>(const Person&) const = default;
    // Generates: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >= — all from comparing name then age
};

Person a{"Alice", 30}, b{"Bob", 25};
bool result = a < b;   // Works! Compares name first, then age
bool equal = a == b;   // Works!
```

> [!tip] `= default` on <=>` is magic
> If all your members support `<=>`, the compiler generates a perfect lexicographic comparison for free. No more writing boilerplate comparison functions.

---

## When NOT to Overload

- **Don't change expected behavior:** `operator+` should add, not concatenate. If the meaning isn't obvious, don't overload.
- **Don't overload `&&` and `||`:** They lose short-circuit evaluation when overloaded.
- **Don't overload just because you can:** If a named function reads better, use it.

---



## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Operator overloading lets your custom types work like built-in types. Instead of `a.add(b)`, you write `a + b`. It's syntactic sugar — the compiler translates `a + b` into either a member function `a.operator+(b)` or a non-member function `operator+(a, b)`. The spaceship operator `<=>` (C++20) is the ultimate shortcut: defaulting one operator gives you all six comparisons automatically.

### Technical
Operator overloading is resolved through overload resolution — the compiler searches for `operator+` in the current scope and through argument-dependent lookup (ADL). Member operators receive the left operand as `*this`; non-member operators receive both as explicit parameters. The spaceship operator `<=>` returns a comparison category type (`std::strong_ordering`, `std::partial_ordering`, etc.) that the compiler uses to synthesize `<`, `<=`, `>`, and `>=`. C++20 intentionally separates equality (`==` and `!=`) from `<=>` for efficiency, meaning `<=>` does not synthesize `==`. However, if you explicitly default the spaceship operator (`auto operator<=>(const T&) const = default;`), the compiler automatically generates `operator==` for you alongside it. Finally, operators `&&`, `||`, and `,` (comma) lose their short-circuit evaluation and sequencing guarantees when overloaded, and overloading them should generally be avoided.

## Exercises

1. **Arithmetic:** `Vector2D` with `+`, `-`, `*`, `==`. Member vs free function. Which allows implicit conversion on left?

2. **Spaceship (C++20):** `struct Version { int major, minor; }`. `auto operator<=>(const Version&) const = default`. Which operators free?

3. **Stream operator:** `ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const MyClass&)`. Why can't this be member?

4. **Subscript:** `Matrix` with `operator()(int,int)` vs `operator[]` returning proxy. Which more readable?

5. **UE FVector operators:** Find in Vector.h. Why no operator<=> in UE 5.8 (C++17)?




## User-Defined Literals

C++11 lets you define custom literal suffixes. The standard library uses them for `std::string` and `std::chrono`:

```cpp
using namespace std::literals;
auto s = "hello"s;           // std::string
auto dur = 500ms;            // std::chrono::milliseconds
auto sec = 3.5s;             // std::chrono::duration<double>
```

Define your own for type-safe units:

```cpp
// Literal for meters (double)
constexpr double operator""_m(long double val) { return static_cast<double>(val); }
constexpr double operator""_km(long double val) { return static_cast<double>(val) * 1000.0; }

double distance = 5.0_km + 300.0_m;  // 5300.0 — type-safe, readable

// Literal for bits (unsigned long long)
constexpr std::size_t operator""_bits(unsigned long long n) { return n; }
constexpr std::size_t operator""_bytes(unsigned long long n) { return n * 8; }

std::size_t total = 4_bytes + 8_bits;  // 40 bits — units are explicit
```

Allowed parameter types: `unsigned long long` (integer), `long double` (floating point), `const char*` (string), `char` (character). The literal suffix must start with `_` (underscore) — suffixes without `_` are reserved for the standard library.
## See Also

- [[../02 — Lambdas & Function Objects|Lambdas]] — operator() for function objects
- [[../05 — Templates & Concepts|Templates]] — Template operator overloading with concepts
- [[../../03 — Memory/03 — Smart Pointers & Ownership|Smart Pointers]] — operator* and operator-> overloading
- [[../../02 — Syntax & Types/06 — Explicit & Implicit Conversion|Conversion]] — Conversion operators (operator T())

06 \u2014 Multiple Inheritance.content:
# Multiple Inheritance & Virtual Base Classes

## Why This Matters

```cpp
class Drawable  { virtual void draw() = 0; };
class Collidable { virtual bool hit_test(Vec3 p) = 0; };

class Prop : public Drawable, public Collidable {
    void draw() override { /* ... */ }
    bool hit_test(Vec3 p) override { /* ... */ }
};
```

Most languages ban multiple inheritance. C++ allows it — and game engines use it extensively. UE's `UObject` hierarchy is a tree of MI. Interfaces (pure abstract classes) are the safe pattern. But mix in data members and you hit the diamond problem: when two base classes share a grandparent, do you get one copy or two? C++ gives you both options, and choosing wrong produces bugs that survive code review.

---

## Basic Multiple Inheritance

```cpp
struct A { int a; };
struct B { int b; };
struct C : A, B { int c; };

C obj;
obj.a = 1;  // OK
obj.b = 2;  // OK
obj.c = 3;  // OK
```

Memory layout:

```
C object:
  ┌────────┬────────┬────────┐
  │ A::a   │ B::b   │ C::c   │
  │ 0-3    │ 4-7    │ 8-11   │
  └────────┴────────┴────────┘
```

The subobjects are laid out left-to-right in declaration order. A `C*` can be implicitly converted to `A*` (no offset) or `B*` (offset by `sizeof(A)`). The compiler handles these adjustments silently — they're a common source of bugs when casting.

---

## The Diamond Problem

```cpp
struct Base { int data; };
struct A : Base { int a; };
struct B : Base { int b; };
struct C : A, B { int c; };  // C has TWO copies of Base!
```

Memory layout (non-virtual):

```
C object:
  ┌────────────┬────────────┬────────────┬─────┐
  │ Base (via A)│ A::a      │ Base (via B)│ ... │
  │ data        │           │ data        │     │
  └────────────┴────────────┴────────────┴─────┘
```

`c.data` is ambiguous — the compiler doesn't know which `Base::data` you mean. You must write `c.A::data` or `c.B::data`. Two copies of `Base` inside `C` — almost never what you want.

The fix: **virtual inheritance**.

```cpp
struct A : virtual Base { int a; };
struct B : virtual Base { int b; };
struct C : A, B { int c; };  // C has ONE copy of Base
```

Memory layout (virtual):

```
C object:
  ┌────────────┬─────┬────────────┬─────┬─────┬────────────┐
  │ A subobject│ ... │ B subobject│ ... │ c   │ Base (shared)│
  │ vptr→Base  │ a   │ vptr→Base  │ b   │     │ data        │
  └────────────┴─────┴────────────┴─────┴─────┴────────────┘
```

Each direct child of `Base` gets a **vptr** (virtual pointer) pointing to a table that tells the compiler where `Base` lives relative to the subobject. The compiler uses these vptrs at runtime to find the single shared `Base`. Cost: an extra indirection for every access to `Base` members, plus storage for the vptr. In most cases, the cost is negligible.

---

## Construction Order with Virtual Bases

```cpp
struct Base {
    Base() { std::cout << "Base\n"; }
};
struct A : virtual Base {
    A() { std::cout << "A\n"; }
};
struct B : virtual Base {
    B() { std::cout << "B\n"; }
};
struct C : A, B {
    C() { std::cout << "C\n"; }
};

C obj;  // Output: Base, A, B, C
```

Virtual bases are constructed FIRST, before any non-virtual bases. And the most-derived class (`C`) is responsible for constructing the virtual base — NOT the intermediate classes (`A` and `B`). If `Base` requires constructor arguments, `C` must provide them, not `A` or `B`:

```cpp
struct Base { Base(int x) {} };
struct A : virtual Base { A() : Base(0) {} };  // This Base(0) is IGNORED when A is a subobject of C
struct C : A { C() : Base(42), A() {} };        // C must initialize Base directly
```

---

## Interfaces — The Safe Use of MI

When base classes have no data members (pure interfaces), there's no diamond problem because there's nothing to duplicate:

```cpp
struct IDrawable   { virtual void draw() = 0; virtual ~IDrawable() = default; };
struct ICollidable { virtual bool hit_test(Vec3 p) = 0; virtual ~ICollidable() = default; };
struct ISerializable { virtual void serialize(Archive& a) = 0; virtual ~ISerializable() = default; };

class Prop : public IDrawable, public ICollidable, public ISerializable {
    // One class, three capabilities. No diamond, no ambiguity.
};
```

This is the pattern Unreal Engine uses: `UObject` inherits from multiple `IInterface`-derived classes for capabilities. The interface classes have no data — only pure virtual functions — so there's never a data duplication issue.

---

## When to Use Virtual Inheritance

| Situation | Virtual? |
|---|---|
| All bases are pure interfaces (no data) | Not needed — nothing to duplicate |
| One base has data, multiple paths to it | **Yes** — virtual inheritance |
| Mixin pattern (add functionality, don't share data) | Not needed — each mixin is independent |
| Deep hierarchy with a shared root (e.g., `UObject`) | **Yes** — virtual inheritance |

The rule of thumb: if you're writing a library where classes will multiply inherit from a shared root, use virtual inheritance. If you're writing application code where everything inherits from `UObject` via UE's macro system, UE already handled this for you.

---

## Common Pitfalls

### 1. static_cast vs dynamic_cast across branches

```cpp
Base* b = new C();  // C : A, B, both virtual Base
A* a = static_cast<A*>(b);   // ❌ Wrong — b might point to B subobject
A* a = dynamic_cast<A*>(b);  // ✅ Correct — follows vptr to find correct A subobject
```

Across virtual inheritance, only `dynamic_cast` can navigate the hierarchy correctly. `static_cast` assumes a fixed offset which may be wrong.

### 2. Slicing with virtual bases

```cpp
C c;
A a = c;  // Slices — only the A subobject is copied. Virtual base is NOT duplicated.
```

The compiler cannot meaningfully slice an object with virtual bases into a base class — the virtual base pointer would need to change. Avoid slicing in MI hierarchies.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Multiple inheritance means a class can have more than one parent. It's like a person who's both a musician and an athlete — they can perform on stage AND compete in races. The diamond problem is when both parents share a grandparent — do you get one copy of grandparent's traits or two? C++ gives both options: non-virtual (two copies, must specify which one you're using) and virtual inheritance (one shared copy, with a small runtime cost). Game engines use MI for interfaces — a `Prop` IS a `Drawable` AND a `Collidable` — and since interfaces have no data, the diamond problem never arises.

### Technical

Multiple inheritance ([class.mi]) extends the object model to support multiple base class subobjects laid out in declaration order. Ambiguity resolution ([class.member.lookup]/6) requires qualified names when a name is found in multiple base classes. Virtual inheritance ([class.virtual]) uses a vptr in each direct derived class to locate the shared virtual base subobject. The vptr points to a vtable that stores offsets from the subobject to the virtual base. Construction order ([class.base.init]/13.1): virtual bases first (depth-first, left-to-right), then non-virtual bases (declaration order), then the class's own members. The most-derived class initializes virtual bases — intermediate class initializers for virtual bases are ignored. `dynamic_cast` ([expr.dynamic.cast]) uses RTTI to navigate virtual inheritance hierarchies at runtime; `static_cast` cannot safely cross virtual inheritance branches.

---

## Exercises

1. **Layout investigation:** Create `struct A { int a; }; struct B { int b; }; struct C : A, B { int c; };`. Print `sizeof(A)`, `sizeof(B)`, `sizeof(C)`. Cast a `C*` to `A*` and `B*` — what are the pointer values? Explain the differences.

2. **Diamond with and without virtual:** Create a diamond `Base → A, B → C` with `int data` in Base. Without virtual: access `c.data`. What error? With virtual: does it compile? Print `&c.data` and explain the layout.

3. **Constructor order:** Create the diamond with `Base(int)`, `A()`, `B()`, `C()`. Add print statements to every constructor. Does `A::A()`'s `Base(0)` get called? Why or why not?

4. **dynamic_cast requirement:** Create `C : virtual A, virtual B`. Create a `C` object and store a `Base*` pointing to it. Try `static_cast<A*>` — does it compile? Try `dynamic_cast<A*>` — does it work? When would static_cast produce a wrong pointer?

5. **Design:** Your game has `Actor` (has transform), `Damageable` (has health), `Renderable` (has mesh). Should `Enemy` multiply inherit all three? Which should be virtual? Which should be non-virtual? Draw the class hierarchy and justify each choice.

## See Also

- [[01 — Inheritance & Virtual Functions|Inheritance & Virtual Functions]] — Single inheritance and virtual dispatch
- [[../../03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] — vtable layout, vptr placement
- [[../03 — C++ Casting & RTTI|C++ Casting & RTTI]] — dynamic_cast internals
- [[../../../UE Core/UE Inheritance & UObject System|UE Inheritance]] — Unreal's MI-heavy UObject hierarchy

01 \u2014 Move Semantics & Perfect Forwa.content:
# Move Semantics & Perfect Forwarding

## The Problem Move Semantics Solves
> [!info] Systems Connection
> Move semantics is a type-system trick built on a hardware reality: copying memory is expensive, reassigning a pointer is cheap. See [[Memory Hierarchy]] for the cache-level cost of copying large objects, and [[CPU Architecture]] for why `noexcept` matters for `std::vector` reallocation.



Before C++11, every "copy" was a real copy — allocate new memory, copy all the bytes. For a `std::vector<int>` with 10,000 elements, that's 40KB copied every time you returned it from a function or passed it by value.

Move semantics fixes this by allowing objects to **steal** resources from other objects instead of copying them. A moved-from `vector` simply transfers its internal pointer to the new owner, leaving the old one empty — zero allocations, zero copies of the 10,000 elements.

> [!summary] The One-Liner
> Copy = "make a duplicate." Move = "take ownership of the original's resources, leave the original empty." Move is always at least as fast as copy, and for containers/strings/buffers, it's vastly faster.

---

## Lvalues vs Rvalues — The Foundation

```cpp
int x = 5;           // x is an lvalue — it has a name and an address
int y = x + 1;       // x + 1 is an rvalue — temporary, no persistent address
```

| | Lvalue | Rvalue |
|---|---|---|
| **Has a name?** | Yes | No (temporary) |
| **Can take address?** | `&x` works | `&(x + 1)` doesn't compile |
| **Lifetime** | Until scope ends | Until end of full expression |
| **Can bind to** | `T&` or `const T&` | `T&&` or `const T&` |

**The key rule:** Rvalues are "expiring" values — temporaries, return values, results of `std::move`. They're safe to steal from because nobody else has a reference to them.

---

## std::move — "I'm Done With This"

`std::move` doesn't move anything. It's a **cast** — it casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics.

```cpp
std::vector<int> source(10000, 42);  // 10,000 ints — 40KB
std::vector<int> dest = std::move(source);  // Move! Just transfers pointers.

// source is now empty — its internal pointer was moved to dest
// dest owns the 10,000 ints
// No allocation. No copy. ~3 pointer assignments.
```

**What `std::move` actually does:**

```cpp
template<typename T>
constexpr std::remove_reference_t<T>&& move(T&& t) noexcept
{
    return static_cast<std::remove_reference_t<T>&&>(t);
}
```

That's it. A `static_cast` to an rvalue reference. The magic is in what happens NEXT — the move constructor or move assignment operator of the destination type sees an rvalue reference and says "I can steal from this!"

---

## Move Constructors and Move Assignment

A class enables move semantics by providing a move constructor and move assignment operator:

```cpp
class Buffer
{
    char* data;
    size_t size;
    
public:
    // Move constructor — steal from 'other'
    Buffer(Buffer&& other) noexcept
        : data(other.data)
        , size(other.size)
    {
        other.data = nullptr;   // Leave 'other' in a valid-but-empty state
        other.size = 0;
    }
    
    // Move assignment — steal from 'other'
    Buffer& operator=(Buffer&& other) noexcept
    {
        if (this != &other)
        {
            delete[] data;          // Free our old data
            data = other.data;      // Steal other's data
            size = other.size;
            other.data = nullptr;   // Leave other empty
            other.size = 0;
        }
        return *this;
    }
    
    // Copy constructor (for comparison)
    Buffer(const Buffer& other)
        : data(new char[other.size])
        , size(other.size)
    {
        std::memcpy(data, other.data, size);  // Actual copy
    }
};
```

**Why `noexcept`?** Move constructors must be `noexcept` so `std::vector` uses them during reallocation instead of falling back to copying. For a full explanation of what `noexcept` is, how the keyword works, and when to use it, see [[RAII, Lifetimes & The Rule of Five#Exception Safety Guarantees]].

---

## RVO and NRVO — The Compiler Is Smarter Than You

Return Value Optimization (RVO) and Named RVO (NRVO) allow the compiler to **elide** (skip) the copy or move when returning a local object:

```cpp
std::vector<int> CreateVector()
{
    std::vector<int> result(1000, 42);
    return result;  // NRVO — no copy, no move. 'result' IS the return value.
}

// ❌ DON'T — std::move prevents NRVO!
std::vector<int> CreateVector_Bad()
{
    std::vector<int> result(1000, 42);
    return std::move(result);  // Forces a move — NRVO doesn't apply
}
```

**The rule:** Never `std::move` a return value. The compiler is smarter than you — it'll use RVO/NRVO if you just `return` the local variable. Adding `std::move` forces an actual move operation, which is worse than zero-cost elision.

**C++17 guarantee:** Since C++17, RVO is **mandatory** for unnamed temporaries — `return T(...)` is guaranteed to construct directly in the caller's space with no copy or move.

---

## std::forward — Perfect Forwarding

`std::forward` preserves the value category (lvalue vs rvalue) of a function argument when passing it through to another function:

```cpp
template<typename T>
std::unique_ptr<Widget> MakeWidget(T&& param)
{
    // forward preserves whether param was an lvalue or rvalue
    return std::make_unique<Widget>(std::forward<T>(param));
}

std::string name = "foo";
auto w1 = MakeWidget(name);           // name is lvalue → forwarded as lvalue → string copy
auto w2 = MakeWidget("bar");          // "bar" is rvalue → forwarded as rvalue → string move
auto w3 = MakeWidget(std::move(name)); // std::move(name) is rvalue → forwarded as rvalue → move
```

**`std::move` vs `std::forward`:**
- `std::move(arg)` — always casts to rvalue. Use when you're DONE with `arg`.
- `std::forward<T>(arg)` — casts to rvalue only if `T` is an rvalue reference. Use in templates to preserve the caller's intent.

> [!danger] Never std::forward the same object twice
> If you `std::forward<T>(x)` to one function, and then `std::forward<T>(x)` to another, the second use might operate on a moved-from object. In templates, treat a forwarded parameter as "consumed" after the first forward.

---

## Universal References vs Rvalue References

This is the most confusing distinction in move semantics. `T&&` means different things depending on context:

```cpp
// RVALUE REFERENCE — binds only to rvalues
void Func(Widget&& param);     // param is always an rvalue reference

// UNIVERSAL (FORWARDING) REFERENCE — binds to ANYTHING
template<typename T>
void Func(T&& param);          // param can be lvalue or rvalue

auto&& var = expr;             // Also a universal reference
```

**The rule:** `T&&` is a universal reference ONLY when:
1. `T` is a template parameter being deduced, AND
2. The form is exactly `T&&` (no `const`, no `volatile`, not `vector<T>&&`)

```cpp
template<typename T>
void f(std::vector<T>&& param);  // ❌ Rvalue reference — not exactly T&&
void f(Widget&& param);           // ❌ Rvalue reference — no type deduction
template<typename T>
void f(const T&& param);          // ❌ Rvalue reference — const qualified

template<typename T>
void f(T&& param);                // ✅ Universal reference
auto&& var = GetWidget();         // ✅ Universal reference
```

**Why this matters:** Universal references are the foundation of perfect forwarding. Without understanding this distinction, you'll accidentally write `std::move` where `std::forward` is needed, or vice versa.

---

## Reference Collapsing — The Hidden Mechanics

Reference collapsing is the compiler rule that makes universal references possible. When a reference to a reference is formed (which can't happen directly, but happens during template deduction), the compiler collapses it:

```cpp
T&  &   → T&      // lvalue ref to lvalue ref → lvalue ref
T&  &&  → T&      // lvalue ref to rvalue ref → lvalue ref
T&& &   → T&      // rvalue ref to lvalue ref → lvalue ref
T&& &&  → T&&     // rvalue ref to rvalue ref → rvalue ref
```

**The rule:** Anything involving an lvalue reference collapses to `T&`. Only `T&& &&` stays as `T&&`.

**How `std::forward` uses this:**

```cpp
// Simplified std::forward implementation
template<typename T>
T&& forward(remove_reference_t<T>& param) noexcept
{
    return static_cast<T&&>(param);
}

// When T = Widget&  (caller passed an lvalue):
//   T&& = Widget& && → Widget&  (collapsed!) → returns lvalue ref
//
// When T = Widget   (caller passed an rvalue):
//   T&& = Widget&&  → returns rvalue ref
```

This is why `std::forward<T>(param)` preserves the caller's value category — reference collapsing does the work.

---

## Perfect Forwarding Failure Cases

Perfect forwarding isn't always perfect. These cases fail:

### 1. Braced Initializers

```cpp
template<typename T>
void Forward(T&& param) { Target(std::forward<T>(param)); }

Forward({1, 2, 3});  // ❌ ERROR — can't deduce T from braced init list

// Fix: use auto
auto init = {1, 2, 3};
Forward(init);  // ✅ T deduced as initializer_list<int>
```

### 2. 0 or NULL as Null Pointer

```cpp
Forward(0);   // T deduced as int, NOT a pointer
Forward(NULL); // T deduced as int or long — implementation-defined

// ✅ Use nullptr
Forward(nullptr); // T deduced as nullptr_t
```

### 3. Overloaded Function Names

```cpp
void Process(int);
void Process(double);

Forward(Process);  // ❌ ERROR — which Process? Compiler can't know

// Fix: explicitly cast to the correct function pointer type
using FnPtr = void(*)(int);
Forward(static_cast<FnPtr>(Process));  // ✅
```

### 4. Bitfields

```cpp
struct Flags { uint32_t isActive : 1; };
Flags f;

Forward(f.isActive);  // ❌ ERROR — can't bind reference to bitfield

// Fix: copy the bitfield first
bool active = f.isActive;
Forward(active);  // ✅
```

---

## Overloading on Universal References — The Trap

Universal references are **greedy.** They match almost everything better than non-template overloads:

```cpp
// ❌ DANGEROUS — the template steals calls from the non-template
void Process(const Widget& w);   // Overload 1: const lvalue ref
void Process(Widget&& w);        // Overload 2: rvalue ref

template<typename T>
void Process(T&& param);         // Overload 3: UNIVERSAL REFERENCE
// This will intercept calls intended for overloads 1 and 2!
```

**Solutions:**

1. **Tag dispatch** — use a tag parameter to route to the correct implementation
2. **`if constexpr`** (C++17) — branch inside a single template
3. **Concepts** (C++20) — constrain the template so it only matches intended types

```cpp
// C++20 Concept solution — cleanest
template<typename T>
    requires std::same_as<std::remove_cvref_t<T>, Widget>
void Process(T&& param);
// Now Process only matches Widget types — stays out of other overloads' way
```

---

## Pass-by-Value + Move — The Modern Idiom

When a function needs to STORE a copy of its parameter, pass by value and move:

```cpp
// ❌ Old way — two overloads for lvalue and rvalue
class Holder {
    std::string name;
public:
    void SetName(const std::string& n) { name = n; }   // Copies
    void SetName(std::string&& n)      { name = std::move(n); } // Moves
};

// ✅ Modern way — one function, works for both
class Holder {
    std::string name;
public:
    void SetName(std::string n) { name = std::move(n); }
    // lvalue: n is copy-constructed → then moved into name (1 copy + 1 move)
    // rvalue: n is move-constructed → then moved into name (2 moves)
    // Cost: 1 extra move vs the two-overload version. Acceptable for most types.
};
```

**When to use:** For parameters you're going to copy anyway. The extra move is negligible for most types (especially with SSO strings). Don't use it for types where move is expensive — in those cases, keep the two-overload approach.
