- Lerp (linear interpolation) on quaternions gives non-constant speed and may produce non-unit quaternions. FastLerp adds renormalization.
- Slerp is mathematically perfect but more expensive. Use Slerp for character animation; use FastLerp for particle systems where slight inaccuracy doesn't matter.

---

## Gimbal Lock — Why It Happens and How Quaternions Fix It

**Why Euler angles lock:** Euler angles are three independent rotations applied in sequence. When one rotation brings two axes into alignment, the remaining rotations both act on the same axis — you lose a degree of freedom.

**How quaternions fix it:** A quaternion stores the entire rotation as one object. When you combine two quaternions, the result is still a single rotation around a single axis. There's never a stage where two axes can merge — the rotation is always an atomic operation.

```cpp
// ❌ Euler: pitch then yaw — can lock
Rot3 rot(90, 45, 0);  // Pitch 90, Yaw 45 — gimbal locked!

// ✅ Quaternion: single rotation, no lock
Quat q = Quat(Rot3(90, 45, 0));  // Same rotation, no locking possible
```

---

## Common Rotation Operations

### Look-At Rotation

Make an object face toward a target:

```cpp
Vec3 toTarget = (Target - Source).GetSafeNormal();
Rot3 lookAt = toTarget.Rotation();  // A game engine's built-in
Quat lookAtQuat = Quat::FindBetween(Vec3::ForwardVector, toTarget);
```

### Rotate Around a Point

```cpp
Vec3 pivotPoint = ...;
Vec3 relative = ActorPos - pivotPoint;
Vec3 rotated = quat.RotateVector(relative);
ActorPos = pivotPoint + rotated;
```

### Smooth Rotation Toward Target

```cpp
Quat current = Actor->GetActorQuat();
Quat target = FRotationMatrix::MakeFromX(ToTarget).ToQuat();
Quat smooth = Quat::Slerp(current, target, rotationSpeed * DeltaTime);
Actor->SetActorRotation(smooth);
```

---

## When to Use Each Rotation Type

| Situation | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Simple per-axis rotation | Rot3 | Intuitive, easy to clamp axes |
| Smooth animation blending | Quat (Slerp) | No gimbal lock, smooth interpolation |
| Combine many rotations | Quat (multiply) | Compact, fast, no accumulating error |
| Physics/rigid body | Quat | Every physics engine uses quaternions internally |
| Camera controls | Rot3 | Players think in pitch/yaw/roll |
| Skeletal animation | Quat (Slerp) | Animation blending between keyframes |
| Editor UI | Rot3 | Humans think in degrees |

---

## Quaternion Pitfalls

### Double Coverage

A quaternion and its negative (-q) represent the SAME rotation. This can cause Slerp to take the \"long way around\" if one quaternion is negated. Use `Quat::Error` or ensure quaternions are in the same hemisphere before Slerp.

### Accumulating Error

Multiplying many quaternions can drift from unit length. Periodically renormalize:
```cpp
quat.Normalize();  // Safe to call periodically
```

### Axis-Angle Edge Cases

`Quat::FindBetween(A, -A)` (vectors pointing exactly opposite) has infinite solutions — any axis perpendicular to A works. game engine handles this with a fallback axis, but the result may not be what you expect.

---


## The Bridge: Complex Numbers → Quaternions

If you understand complex numbers, quaternions are the natural extension:

```
2D Rotations:  Complex numbers (a + bi), where i^2 = -1
               Multiplying by (cos(angle) + i*sin(angle)) rotates by angle.
               2 components: real + imaginary.

3D Rotations:  Quaternions (a + bi + cj + dk), where i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1
               Multiplying by a unit quaternion rotates in 3D.
               4 components: scalar + vector.
```

**The relationship:** Just as multiplying by `i` rotates 90 degrees counter-clockwise in 2D, multiplying by a quaternion rotates in 3D. The extra dimensions (j and k) give us the two additional axes of rotation. The quaternion multiplication rules (ij = k, jk = i, ki = j) are the 3D generalization of i^2 = -1.

**Complex numbers represent 2D rotation perfectly.** Quaternions do the same for 3D. The reason quaternions are 4D (not 3D) is the same reason complex numbers are 2D (not 1D) for 2D rotation: you need one extra dimension to encode the rotation without singularities.


## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Imagine you're holding a ball with arrows painted on it. Euler angles try to describe how you turned the ball by saying \"first you tilted it forward, then you spun it left, then you rolled it.\" The problem: sometimes tilting makes \"left\" and \"roll\" mean the same thing — you're stuck. Quaternions instead say \"you rotated the ball 45 degrees around the arrow pointing up-and-right.\" A single instruction, no confusion. Slerp is the natural way to smoothly go from one rotation to another at constant speed.

### Technical

Quaternions are elements of the SU(2) double cover of SO(3), representing 3D rotations as unit vectors on the 3-sphere in R4. The composition of rotations corresponds to quaternion multiplication (Hamilton product). Slerp interpolates along the great-circle geodesic on S3, guaranteeing constant angular velocity and the shortest rotational path. The two-to-one mapping (q and -q represent the same rotation) requires hemisphere checking to avoid the \"long way\" in interpolation. Quaternions eliminate gimbal lock because they encode rotation as a single axis-angle operation rather than three sequential Euler rotations, avoiding the singularities at pitch=90 degrees in Euler representations.

---


## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Construct a quaternion from axis `Vec3(0, 1, 0)` and angle 90 degrees. Use it to rotate `Vec3(1, 0, 0)`. What's the result?
2. Create a quaternion from `Rot3(0, 90, 0)`. What are the `(x, y, z, w)` values? (Hint: convert to radians first)
3. Explain in your own words why quaternions avoid gimbal lock. Why does representing a rotation as a single axis-angle operation prevent the lock?
4. Use `Quat::Slerp` to blend between identity and a 180-degree rotation. Check that `t=0.5` gives exactly 90 degrees.
5. **Coding:** Make a camera smoothly look at a moving target using `Quat::FindBetweenVectors` and `Quat::Slerp`. Why does Slerp feel better than linear interpolation?

## See Also

- [[Trigonometry & Vectors]] — The math behind the axis and angle
- [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] — How quaternion rotations become matrices
- [[00 — Curves Overview|Curves]] — Slerp in the context of general interpolation
- [[00 — Collision Detection Overview|Collision Detection]] — Rotations in collision transforms

Trigonometry & Vectors.content:
# Trigonometry & Vectors

## Why This Matters
> [!info] Systems Connection
> A `Vec3` is 12 bytes — 3 floats. Because of alignment and cache lines (64 bytes), iterating a `TArray<Vec3>` sequentially is cache-friendly (5 vectors per line). See [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] and [[Memory Hierarchy]] for why iteration order matters.

> [!info] C++ Connection
> `Vec3` is a simple `struct` with `operator+`, `operator-`, and `operator*` overloaded. It's trivially copyable, so `memcpy` and `memmove` are safe. See [[Operator Overloading]] and [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] for the C++ details.


Every 3D game is built on trigonometry and vectors. When your character moves forward, a vector tells the engine which direction \"forward\" is. When you look at something, trigonometry calculates whether you're facing it. When a projectile arcs through the air, sine and cosine trace its path. This is the math that makes games feel real.

---

## Trigonometry — The Language of Angles

### The Unit Circle

Imagine a circle with radius 1 centered at (0,0). Every point on this circle has coordinates (cos(angle), sin(angle)). This is the fundamental insight of trigonometry.

```
sin(angle) = y-coordinate on the unit circle
cos(angle) = x-coordinate on the unit circle
tan(angle) = sin/cos = slope of the line
```

For game dev, the most important angles are:
- 0 radians (0 degrees): cos=1, sin=0 — facing directly right
- pi/2 (90 degrees): cos=0, sin=1 — facing up
- pi (180 degrees): cos=-1, sin=0 — facing left
- 3pi/2 (270 degrees): cos=0, sin=-1 — facing down

### Radians vs Degrees

Computers use radians, humans use degrees. One full rotation = 360 degrees = 2*pi radians (~6.283).

```cpp
float degrees = 90.0f;
float radians = Math::DegreesToRadians(degrees);  // pi/2
float back = Math::RadiansToDegrees(radians);      // 90.0f
```

**Why radians?** Calculus becomes simple in radians (derivative of sin is cos), and all game engines use radians internally. Train yourself to think in radians for angles.

### Sin, Cos, Tan in Practice

```cpp
// Sin — oscillating motion (bobbing, pulsing)
float offset = sin(time * speed) * amplitude;
Actor->SetActorLocation(BaseLocation + Vec3(0, 0, offset));

// Cos — similar to sin but shifted by 90 degrees
// Cos(0) = 1, Sin(0) = 0 — cos starts at peak, sin starts at zero

// Tan — slope, useful for aiming
float angle = atan2(target.Y - source.Y, target.X - source.X);
```

> [!tip] atan2 Is Your Friend
> `atan2(y, x)` gives you the angle from the positive x-axis to the point (x,y), handling all quadrants correctly. `atan(y/x)` fails when x=0. Always use `atan2` in game code: `Math::Atan2(Y, X)`.

### Inverse Trig Functions

When you know a ratio and need the angle:
```cpp
float angle = asin(ratio);   // angle whose sine is ratio  [-pi/2, pi/2]
float angle = acos(ratio);   // angle whose cosine is ratio [0, pi]
float angle = atan2(y, x);   // angle to point (x,y)        [-pi, pi]
```

---

## Vectors — Direction and Magnitude

A vector is an arrow: it has direction AND length. In 2D: (x, y). In 3D: (x, y, z). This is the fundamental data type of 3D graphics.

**Position vector:** where something is. `Vec3(100, 200, 50)`
**Direction vector:** which way something points. `Vec3(1, 0, 0)` = right

### Vector Length (Magnitude)

The length of a vector is sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) — the 3D Pythagorean theorem:

```cpp
Vec3 v(3, 4, 0);
float len = v.Size();  // sqrt(9 + 16 + 0) = 5
float lenSq = v.SizeSquared();  // 25 — faster, no sqrt
```

> [!tip] Use SizeSquared When Comparing Distances
> `if ((A - B).SizeSquared() < range * range)` does the exact same check as comparing sizes but avoids the expensive square root. Use this for distance comparisons in hot loops.

### Normalization — Making Unit Vectors

A unit vector has length 1. It represents pure direction with no magnitude:

```cpp
Vec3 dir = Vec3(3, 4, 0);
dir.Normalize();  // Now (0.6, 0.8, 0) — length = 1
```

**Why normalize?** To combine direction with a separate speed. Movement = direction * speed. The direction says \"which way,\" the speed says \"how fast.\" They're independent when separated this way.

---

## The Dot Product — How Much Two Vectors Point Together

The dot product answers: \"How similar are these two directions?\"

```cpp
float dot = Vec3::DotProduct(A, B);
// = |A| * |B| * cos(angle between them)
```

If A and B are unit vectors (length=1), the dot product simplifies to just cos(angle):

| Dot Product | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | Exactly parallel (same direction, 0 degrees) |
| 0.0 | Perpendicular (90 degrees) |
| -1.0 | Exactly opposite (180 degrees) |
| > 0 | Pointing generally the same way (< 90 degrees) |
| < 0 | Pointing away from each other (> 90 degrees) |

### Practical Uses

```cpp
// Is the enemy in front of me?
Vec3 toEnemy = (Enemy->GetActorLocation() - Me->GetActorLocation()).GetSafeNormal();
float dot = Vec3::DotProduct(Me->GetActorForwardVector(), toEnemy);
if (dot > 0.5f)  // Enemy is within ~60 degree cone in front
    Attack();

// Is the player looking at this object?
float dot = Vec3::DotProduct(PlayerCameraDir, (ObjectPos - CameraPos).GetSafeNormal());
if (dot > 0.98f)  // Almost directly looking at it
    Highlight();

// How much light hits this surface? (Lambert lighting)
float intensity = Vec3::DotProduct(SurfaceNormal, LightDirection);
intensity = Math::Max(0.0f, intensity);  // Clamp — no negative light
```

---

## The Cross Product — Finding Perpendicular Directions

The cross product takes two vectors and produces a third vector perpendicular to both:

```cpp
Vec3 right = Vec3(1, 0, 0);
Vec3 up = Vec3(0, 0, 1);
Vec3 forward = Vec3::CrossProduct(right, up);  // (0, -1, 0) — right-hand rule: right(1,0,0) crossed with up(0,0,1) points backward
```

**Right-hand rule:** Point fingers of right hand along first vector, curl toward second. Thumb points in cross product direction. `A x B` is opposite from `B x A` — order matters!

```cpp
Vec3::CrossProduct(A, B);  // Right-hand: A -> B
Vec3::CrossProduct(B, A);  // Opposite direction — B -> A
```

### Practical Uses

```cpp
// Find the surface normal of a triangle
Vec3 AB = B - A;
Vec3 AC = C - A;
Vec3 normal = Vec3::CrossProduct(AB, AC).GetSafeNormal();

// Build a coordinate frame from one direction
Vec3 forward = Target - Source;
forward.Normalize();
Vec3 right = Vec3::CrossProduct(Vec3::UpVector, forward).GetSafeNormal();
Vec3 up = Vec3::CrossProduct(forward, right);

// Torque (rotational force) — cross product of lever arm and force
Vec3 torque = Vec3::CrossProduct(leverArm, force);
```

---

## Trigonometry + Vectors = Game Math Superpower

### Moving in a Direction

```cpp
// Move forward at a given angle
float angle = Math::DegreesToRadians(45.0f);
Vec3 direction(cos(angle), sin(angle), 0);  // Unit vector at 45 degrees
Actor->AddMovementInput(direction, speed * DeltaTime);
```

### Finding the Angle Between Two Vectors

```cpp
Vec3 A = ...;
Vec3 B = ...;
A.Normalize();
B.Normalize();
float dot = Vec3::DotProduct(A, B);
float angleRad = acos(dot);  // Angle in radians [0, pi]
float angleDeg = Math::RadiansToDegrees(angleRad);
```

### Rotating a Vector

```cpp
// Rotate vector V around the Z axis by 'angle' radians
float cosA = cos(angle);
float sinA = sin(angle);
float newX = V.X * cosA - V.Y * sinA;
float newY = V.X * sinA + V.Y * cosA;
Vec3 rotated(newX, newY, V.Z);
```

---

## Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula | game engine Function |
|---|---|---|
| Vector length | sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) | V.Size() |
| Normalize | V / length | V.GetSafeNormal() |
| Dot product | A.x*B.x + A.y*B.y + A.z*B.z | Vec3::DotProduct |
| Cross product | Perpendicular vector | Vec3::CrossProduct |
| Angle between vectors | acos(dot(normalized)) | via DotProduct |
| Distance | length(A - B) | Vec3::Dist |
| Radians to degrees | rad * 180/PI | Math::RadiansToDegrees |
| atan2 | angle to point | Math::Atan2 |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

Trigonometry is the math of triangles and circles. Sin and cos turn angles into coordinates on a circle. A vector is an arrow — it has direction (where it points) and magnitude (how long it is). The dot product asks \"do these arrows point together?\" The cross product makes a new arrow perpendicular to both. Together, they're how every 3D game knows which way is forward, whether an enemy is in front of you, and how to bounce a grenade off a wall.

### Technical

Trigonometric functions map angular parameters to rectangular coordinates via the unit circle. Vectors in R3 are ordered triples forming a vector space with component-wise addition and scalar multiplication. The dot product (inner product) computes the cosine-scaled projection of one vector onto another; the cross product (restricted to R3) produces a pseudo-vector orthogonal to its operands with magnitude equal to the parallelogram area. Together they enable coordinate frame construction, angle calculation, surface normals, and torque computation — the fundamental building blocks of real-time 3D graphics and physics.

---


## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Given `Vec3 A(3, 4, 0)`, compute its length (`Size()`) and normalize it. Verify that `Size() == 1` after normalization.
2. Find the angle between `Vec3(1, 0, 0)` and `Vec3(0, 1, 0)`. What should the answer be? Now try `Vec3(1, 0, 0)` and `Vec3(-1, 0, 0)`.
3. Write a function that checks if an enemy is in front of the player using dot product. What threshold angle would you use?
4. Compute the surface normal of the triangle with vertices `(0,0,0)`, `(1,0,0)`, `(0,1,0)` using cross product. Which direction does it point?
5. **Coding:** Write a program that moves an object in a circle using `sin()` and `cos()`. Then make it move in a spiral.

## See Also

- [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] — How matrices combine transformations
- [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations]] — Advanced rotation math beyond Euler angles
- [[00 — Curves Overview|Curves]] — Smooth motion using these foundations
- [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] — How Vec3 is stored in memory
- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Where Vec3 lives when you create it

00 \u2014 Curves Overview.content:
# Curves, Interpolation & Kinematics

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Every frame of a game is a snapshot in time. But players don't live in snapshots \u2014 they experience motion. Interpolation is the math that fills in the gaps between frames, creating the illusion of continuous movement. From smoothly moving a camera to blending animations to making a character's hand reach for a doorknob, curves and kinematics are the bridge between discrete data and fluid experience.

---

| Concept | game engine Function / Class |
|---------|---------------------|
| Scalar lerp | `Math::Lerp(float, float, float)` |
| Vector lerp | `Math::Lerp(Vec3, Vec3, float)`, `Math::VLerp()` |
| Stable lerp | `Math::LerpStable()` |
| Inverse lerp | `Math::GetRangePct()`, `Math::GetMappedRangeValueClamped()` |
| Catmull-Rom | `Math::CubicInterp()` |
| Cubic Bezier | Built into `UCurveFloat`, `UCurveVector`, `FRuntimeFloatCurve` |
| Easing | `UCurveFloat` with custom key interpolation (Linear, Constant, Cubic, Auto) |
| Quaternion slerp | `Quat::Slerp()`, `Quat::SlerpNotNormalized()` |
| IK (CCD) | `UIK_CCD` AnimGraph node |
| IK (FABRIK) | `UIK_FABRIK` AnimGraph node |
| Full-Body IK | UE5 IK Rig + Control Rig |
| Timeline curves | `UTimelineComponent` with `UCurveFloat`/`UCurveVector` |
| Spline paths | `USplineComponent`, `USplineMeshComponent` |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
You have two photos and want to create the in-between frame: that's interpolation. Lerp is the simplest — a straight-line blend. Easing functions add "personality" to the blend by making it speed up or slow down, exactly like how real objects move. Bézier curves are like a rubber band stretched between pins and pulled by magnets in between. Splines are many little curves stitched together to make a long smooth path. Slerp is special lerp for rotations that stays on the surface of the rotation sphere. Inverse Kinematics is puppet math: you say where the hand goes, the algorithm bends the arm to match.

### Technical
Interpolation reconstructs continuous signals from discrete samples. Linear interpolation is first-order (C⁰ continuous). Bézier curves use Bernstein polynomials as a basis; de Casteljau's algorithm provides a numerically stable recursive evaluation. Catmull-Rom splines produce C¹ continuous piecewise-cubic curves that interpolate all control points by constraining tangents to central differences. Slerp is a geodesic on S³ (the unit quaternion manifold), computing the shortest path along the hypersphere; nlerp approximates this for small angles. FABRIK solves the IK problem by iteratively enforcing distance constraints through forward-backward projection, which converges to a solution minimizing end-effector error. CCD iteratively minimizes angular error per joint using axis-angle rotations.

---

- [[Trigonometry & Vectors]] \u2014 Vector math foundation for all interpolation
- [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations]] \u2014 Quaternion math underlying slerp and CCD rotations
- [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] \u2014 Matrix representation of spline bases and transforms
- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] \u2014 Memory considerations for spline point arrays
- [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] \u2014 Cache-friendly storage of curve control points


## Folder Map

| # | Note | Quiz | What |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | Lerp & Easing | Yes | Linear interpolation, easing functions, animation curves |
| 02 | Bezier & Splines | Yes | Bezier curves, Catmull-Rom, B-splines |
| 03 | Slerp & Kinematics | Yes | Spherical interpolation, forward/inverse kinematics |

01 \u2014 Interpolation, Bezier & Spline.content:
### Analogy: The Acceleration Pedal

Linear motion feels robotic. Imagine a car: it doesn't instantly jump to 60 mph \u2014 it accelerates smoothly from a standstill (ease-in) and decelerates before stopping (ease-out). Jumping to full speed instantly would slam passengers into their seats. Similarly, UI elements that slide onto screen with \"snap\" feel harsh; ones that ease-in feel natural and polished. Easing functions shape the curve of t so motion mimics the physics our brains expect.

### Why Linear Motion Looks Unnatural

In the real world, nothing moves at constant velocity from a dead stop. Even a light switch has a moment of acceleration. Our brains are wired to perceive natural motion \u2014 things speed up from rest and slow down to stop. Linear interpolation ignores this completely, which is why it feels \"cheap\" or \"robotic\" in games.

### Common Easing Families

#### Power Curves (t^n)

The simplest easing family: raise t to a power.

```
Ease-In (t^2):   starts slow, ends fast     \u2014 like a rocket launching
Ease-Out (1-(1-t)^2): starts fast, ends slow \u2014 like a car braking
Ease-In-Out:      slow \u2192 fast \u2192 slow         \u2014 like a pendulum swing
```

```cpp
// Ease-In Quad
float EaseInQuad(float t) { return t * t; }

// Ease-Out Quad
float EaseOutQuad(float t) { return t * (2.0f - t); }

// Ease-In-Out Quad
float EaseInOutQuad(float t)
{
    return t < 0.5f ? 2.0f * t * t : -1.0f + (4.0f - 2.0f * t) * t;
}
```

**Powers available:** Quad (^2), Cubic (^3), Quart (^4), Quint (^5). Higher powers = more dramatic ease (longer at the slow end).

#### Exponential Curves

Even more dramatic: the value stays near zero for most of the range, then rockets up.

```cpp
float EaseInExpo(float t)
{
    return t == 0.0f ? 0.0f : Math::Pow(2.0f, 10.0f * (t - 1.0f));
}

float EaseOutExpo(float t)
{
    return t == 1.0f ? 1.0f : 1.0f - Math::Pow(2.0f, -10.0f * t);
}
```

#### Elastic

Overshoots and bounces \u2014 like a rubber band snapping into place.

```cpp
float EaseOutElastic(float t)
{
    if (t == 0.0f || t == 1.0f) return t;
    return Math::Pow(2.0f, -10.0f * t) * Math::Sin((t - 0.075f) * TWO_PI / 0.3f) + 1.0f;
}
```

#### Bounce

Simulates a bouncing ball coming to rest.

```cpp
float EaseOutBounce(float t)
{
    if (t < 1.0f / 2.75f)
        return 7.5625f * t * t;
    else if (t < 2.0f / 2.75f)
        return 7.5625f * (t -= 1.5f / 2.75f) * t + 0.75f;
    else if (t < 2.5f / 2.75f)
        return 7.5625f * (t -= 2.25f / 2.75f) * t + 0.9375f;
    return 7.5625f * (t -= 2.625f / 2.75f) * t + 0.984375f;
}
```

### Usage Pattern

```cpp
// Instead of raw lerp:
float RawT = Math::Clamp(ElapsedTime / Duration, 0.0f, 1.0f);
float EasedT = EaseOutCubic(RawT);
Vec3 Position = Math::Lerp(StartPos, EndPos, EasedT);
```

### Visual Description

```
Linear:     |        /
            |      /
            |    /
            |  /
            |/_________

Ease-In:    |         /
            |        /
            |      /
            |    /
            |__/________  (curves upward)

Ease-Out:   |    ___
            |   /
            |  /
            | /
            |/_________  (curves downward)

Ease-In-Out:|      ___
            |     /
            |    /
            | __/
            |/_________  (S-curve, sigmoid shape)
```

---



> [!info] **Game Engine Connection**
> Every engine implements these concepts: Unity's Physics.Raycast, Godot's CollisionShape, Unreal's Box/Sphere/LineTrace. The math is identical — only the API wrapping changes. See [[../UE Math/Collision & Intersection Math]] and [[../UE Math/Curves, Ranges & Random]] for engine-specific implementations.
## Exercises

1. **Frame-rate independent lerp derivation:** The naive lerp `Position = Lerp(Position, Target, 0.1f)` moves 10% of the remaining distance per frame. Derive the exponential decay formula `1.0 - exp(-Speed * DeltaTime)` that makes this frame-rate independent. Start from the differential equation `dx/dt = -k * x` where `x` is the remaining distance. Why does the naive version produce different paths at 30 FPS vs 144 FPS even with the same total elapsed time?

2. **Inverse lerp edge cases:** `InverseLerp(A, B, Value)` returns `(Value - A) / (B - A)`. What happens when A == B? What should the function return and why? Now implement a clamped version that returns 0 when Value < min(A,B) and 1 when Value > max(A,B). What game scenario requires the unclamped version (values outside [0,1])?

3. **Easing function equivalence proof:** Show that `EaseInOutQuad(t)` as defined in the note — `t < 0.5 ? 2t² : -1 + (4 - 2t)t` — is symmetric around t=0.5. That is, prove `EaseInOutQuad(0.5 + d) = 1 - EaseInOutQuad(0.5 - d)`. Why does this symmetry matter for UI animations that enter and exit symmetrically?

4. **Elastic easing dissection:** The `EaseOutElastic` function uses `sin((t - 0.075) * TWO_PI / 0.3)`. Identify: (a) the frequency of oscillation — how many full bounces occur over the full [0,1] range? (b) the amplitude envelope — what function controls how the bounces decay? (c) the phase offset — why subtract 0.075? If you wanted exactly 3 bounces instead of ~3.33, what parameter would you change?

5. **Remap with easing:** You want a health bar that not only maps health [0,100] to screen width [0,500] pixels, but also eases the transition so changes near full health are subtle and changes near death are dramatic. Design a function `HealthToPixelWidth(float Health)` that chains `InverseLerp`, an easing function, and `Lerp`. Which easing function would you use for this effect — and why? Now implement the same with `Math::GetMappedRangeValueClamped` plus an easing curve.

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Lerp is the "find the spot between A and B" function. If t=0 you're at A, t=1 you're at B, t=0.5 you're exactly halfway. It's the straight line connecting two dots. But raw lerp moves at constant speed — natural motion speeds up from rest and slows to a stop. Easing functions reshape t before feeding it to lerp. `t²` makes motion start slow and finish fast (think: rocket launch). `1-(1-t)²` makes it start fast and finish slow (think: car braking). Combining them creates an S-curve: slow→fast→slow, like a pendulum swing. Elastic and bounce easings add personality — overshooting and settling, or bouncing to a stop. `InverseLerp` answers "how far between A and B am I?" — the reverse question. `Remap` chains them: "where am I between input min/max?" → "put me at that same fraction between output min/max." These four building blocks — lerp, easing, inverse lerp, remap — power every smooth transition in your game.

### Technical
Linear interpolation `Lerp(A, B, t) = A + (B-A)·t` produces C⁰-continuous motion. The frame-rate dependence problem arises because iterative lerp `P = Lerp(P, Target, α)` solves `P_n = Target + (1-α)^n · (P_0 - Target)` — the convergence rate depends on `n` (frame count), not elapsed time. The exponential decay formulation `1 - e^(-k·Δt)` derives from the ODE `dx/dt = -k·x` and is frame-rate invariant because the closed-form solution depends only on `Δt`. Easing functions are remappings `f: [0,1] → [0,1]` applied as `Lerp(A, B, f(t))`. Power easings `t^n` produce C^(n-1) continuity at endpoints. Elastic easings combine a decaying exponential envelope `2^(-10t)` with a sinusoidal oscillation, creating damped harmonic motion. Bounce easings use piecewise quadratic segments derived from the physics of a bouncing ball under constant gravity with restitution coefficient ~0.56. `InverseLerp(A, B, V) = (V-A)/(B-A)` is the inverse of lerp, undefined when A=B. `Remap` composes them: `Remap(inMin, inMax, outMin, outMax, V) = Lerp(outMin, outMax, InverseLerp(inMin, inMax, V))`.

# Bezier & Splines

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Curves Overview|Curves]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[01 — Interpolation, Bezier & Splines]] — understand interpolation basics first.




### Analogy: The Flexible Ruler

A single B\u00e9zier curve can only bend so much before you need more control points \u2014 but high-degree B\u00e9ziers get unwieldy (moving one point affects the entire curve). A **spline** is like a flexible ruler bent to pass through (or near) a series of pins. It's made of many small curve pieces (usually cubic) stitched together so the joints are invisible. Each piece is a simple cubic B\u00e9zier; the magic is in how the pieces connect.

### Catmull-Rom Spline

The most popular spline in games: it **passes through** all control points (interpolating spline), unlike B\u00e9zier where points are \"magnets.\" Given 4 consecutive points P\u2080, P\u2081, P\u2082, P\u2083, the curve segment between P\u2081 and P\u2082 is:

$$B(t) = 0.5 \\cdot \\begin{bmatrix} 1 & t & t^2 & t^3 \\end{bmatrix} \\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\\\
-1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\\\
2 & -5 & 4 & -1 \\\\
-1 & 3 & -3 & 1
\\end{bmatrix} \\begin{bmatrix} P_0 \\\\ P_1 \\\\ P_2 \\\\ P_3 \\end{bmatrix}$$

```cpp
Vec3 CatmullRom(const Vec3& P0, const Vec3& P1,
                   const Vec3& P2, const Vec3& P3, float t)
{
    float tt = t * t;
    float ttt = tt * t;
    
    return 0.5f * (
        (2.0f * P1) +
        (-P0 + P2) * t +
        (2.0f * P0 - 5.0f * P1 + 4.0f * P2 - P3) * tt +
        (-P0 + 3.0f * P1 - 3.0f * P2 + P3) * ttt
    );
}
```

> [!tip] game engine Equivalent
> `Math::CubicInterp()` in a game engine computes Catmull-Rom interpolation. For animation paths, A game engine's `USplineComponent` uses cubic Hermite splines internally (closely related).

### B-Spline Overview

B-splines (basis splines) are the mathematical generalization. Unlike Catmull-Rom, B-splines don't necessarily pass through control points \u2014 they create a smooth \"cage\" that the curve follows. Key properties:

- **Local control:** moving one control point only affects nearby curve segments
- **C\u00b2 continuity:** very smooth (continuous curvature, not just continuous slope)
- **Uniform vs non-uniform:** uniform B-splines have equally spaced knots; NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines) add weights for even more control

B-splines are more common in CAD/modeling than real-time games, but they power curve editing tools and are the foundation of NURBS surfaces.

### How Engines Smooth Animation Paths

1. **Keyframe reduction:** Remove redundant keys that lie nearly on a straight line
2. **Spline fitting:** Fit a Catmull-Rom or cubic Hermite spline through remaining keys
3. **Arc-length parameterization:** Re-parameterize so constant speed along the curve means constant distance (not constant t)
4. **Tangent computation:** Use the spline derivative to get smooth velocity direction at every point

```cpp
// Getting velocity direction along a cubic Bezier (the derivative)
Vec3 CubicBezierDerivative(const Vec3& P0, const Vec3& P1,
                               const Vec3& P2, const Vec3& P3, float t)
{
    float u = 1.0f - t;
    return 3.0f * u * u * (P1 - P0) + 6.0f * u * t * (P2 - P1) + 3.0f * t * t * (P3 - P2);
}
```

---

## See Also

- [[01 — Interpolation, Bezier & Splines]] — Next in sequence

02 \u2014 Spatial Audio.content:
# Spatial Audio

> [!note] Why This Matters (Engine Context)
> In a flat 2D mix, all sounds come from "somewhere in the headphones." Spatial audio places sounds in the 3D world: the explosion behind you to the left, the NPC's voice from ahead, the dripping pipe above. This requires simulating how sound waves interact with the listener's head, ears, and environment. The math ranges from simple (inverse-square attenuation) to psychoacoustically complex (Head-Related Transfer Functions). Every game engine — UE AudioVolume, Unity AudioSource, Wwise, FMOD — implements these models. Understanding the math means understanding why a sound at 20m sounds half as loud, why stereo panning isn't enough for VR, and what "3D audio" actually means in your engine's audio settings.

---

## Sound Intensity and Distance — The Inverse-Square Law

Sound radiates from a point source as an expanding sphere. The same energy spreads over an ever-larger surface area:

```
I = P / (4π · r²)
```

- I: sound intensity (W/m²) at distance r
- P: source power (constant)
- r: distance from source

Intensity falls as 1/r². Perceived loudness (sound pressure level, SPL) falls as 1/r:

```
SPL(r) = SPL(1m) - 20 · log₁₀(r)
```

Every doubling of distance: -6 dB. At 1m = 80 dB; at 2m = 74 dB; at 4m = 68 dB; at 8m = 62 dB.

### Attenuation Curves — Game Audio Is Not Pure Physics

Pure inverse-square creates problems: at r=0, intensity → ∞ (divide by zero). At r=1000m, sounds are still theoretically audible at -60 dB (whisper-quiet but present). Games clamp and shape the curve:

```cpp
// UE-style attenuation function with min/max distance
float Attenuation(float distance, float minDist, float maxDist, float falloffExponent) {
    if (distance <= minDist) return 1.0f;  // Full volume within min distance
    if (distance >= maxDist) return 0.0f;  // Silent beyond max distance

    float t = (distance - minDist) / (maxDist - minDist);  // [0, 1]
    return pow(1.0f - t, falloffExponent);  // 2.0=inverse-square, 1.0=linear, 3.0=steep
}

// UE also supports custom curves (Float Curve assets) for arbitrary attenuation shapes
```

- **Min distance (inner radius):** full volume. Prevents the r=0 singularity.
- **Max distance (outer radius):** silent. Culls distant sounds (performance — don't process inaudible audio).
- **Falloff exponent:** 2.0 = realistic inverse-square; 1.0 = gentler linear falloff; 3.0 = steep (sound doesn't travel far).

---

## Panning — Placing a Sound in Stereo

Panning distributes a mono signal between left and right channels based on the source's angle relative to the listener.

**Constant-power pan law:**
```
Left_Gain  = cos((θ + 90°) · π / 360°)   // or equivalently: sqrt(2)/2 · (cos θ - sin θ)
Right_Gain = sin((θ + 90°) · π / 360°)   // or: sqrt(2)/2 · (cos θ + sin θ)
```
where θ = 0° (straight ahead), -90° (hard left), +90° (hard right).

At θ=0 (center): L=R=√2/2 ≈ 0.707. Both channels at -3 dB each → combined = 0 dB (constant power).

**Why constant power?** A sound panned center should be equally loud as one panned hard left. If gains were linear (L=1 at center), the center sound would be +3 dB louder — two speakers both playing the same signal sum acoustically.

---

## 3D Panning — Beyond Stereo

For surround sound (5.1, 7.1, Atmos), panning extends to multiple channels:

**VBAP (Vector Base Amplitude Panning):** the sound source direction defines a vector. Decompose it into a weighted sum of the three nearest speaker vectors.

```
For speakers at azimuth angles φ₁, φ₂, φ₃ and source at φ:
Gains g₁, g₂, g₃ solve: Σ g_i · [cos φ_i, sin φ_i] = [cos φ, sin φ]
                        Σ g_i = 1 (amplitude preservation)
```

For height channels (Atmos), extend to 3D vectors with elevation. This is the math behind every spatial audio renderer — Wwise, FMOD, UE's Audio engine, Dolby Atmos renderer.

---

## HRTF — How Your Ears Locate Sound

The Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF) captures how sound waves are filtered by the listener's head, outer ears (pinnae), and torso before reaching the eardrums. This filtering is direction-dependent — a sound from behind sounds different than from ahead because the pinna shapes the frequency spectrum differently.

An HRTF is a pair of filters (left ear, right ear) for every direction (θ, φ) on the sphere:

```
y_left(t)  = x(t) ∗ HRTF_left(θ, φ, t)
y_right(t) = x(t) ∗ HRTF_right(θ, φ, t)
```

**HRTF cues for spatial perception:**
- **ITD (Interaural Time Difference):** sound arrives at the closer ear first. Δt ≈ (head_radius / speed_of_sound) · sin(θ). For 8.75cm head radius: max ITD ≈ 0.255 ms.
- **ILD (Interaural Level Difference):** head shadow reduces amplitude at the far ear, especially at high frequencies (small wavelength can't diffract around head).
- **Spectral notches:** pinna reflections create frequency dips that shift with elevation — the primary cue for up/down discrimination.

```cpp
// Simplified HRTF approximation (no measured data)
void ApplySimpleHRTF(float input, float azimuth, float elevation,
                     float& outLeft, float& outRight) {
    // ITD: delay based on azimuth
    float itd = (HEAD_RADIUS / 343.0f) * sin(azimuth);  // seconds
    // Simple low-pass for far ear (head shadow)
    float farAtten = 0.5f + 0.5f * cos(azimuth);  // 1.0 at 0°, 0.0 at 90°
    float nearAtten = 1.0f;

    if (azimuth > 0) {  // Source to the right → right ear is near
        outRight = nearAtten * input;  // No delay
        outLeft = farAtten * DelayLine(input, itd);  // Attenuated + delayed
    } else {
        outLeft = nearAtten * input;
        outRight = farAtten * DelayLine(input, -itd);
    }
}
```

Measured HRTFs (stored as FIR filters, 128-512 taps) are applied via convolution (FFT-based for efficiency, see FFT note). UE integrates with audio middleware (Wwise/FMOD) for HRTF processing; the engine passes source position and listener orientation to the audio engine.

---

## Doppler Shift — Moving Sources

When a sound source moves relative to the listener, the perceived frequency shifts:

```
f_heard = f_source · (c / (c + v_relative))

c = speed of sound (~343 m/s at sea level, 20°C)
v_relative = velocity of source TOWARD listener (positive = approaching → higher pitch)
```

A car approaching at 30 m/s (108 km/h): f_heard = f_source · 343 / (343 - 30) = f_source · 1.096 → ~1.6 semitones higher. UE applies a Doppler pitch shift based on relative velocity between `AActor` sound sources and the listener's `APlayerCameraManager`.

---

## Engine Application — UE AudioVolume

```cpp
// UE AudioVolume: defines spatial audio parameters for a region
UCLASS()
class UAudioVolume : public AVolume {
    // Attenuation settings per-sound via USoundAttenuation assets
    UPROPERTY() float MinDistance = 400.0f;    // Full volume within 4m
    UPROPERTY() float MaxDistance = 4000.0f;   // Silent beyond 40m
    UPROPERTY() float FalloffExponent = 2.0f;  // Realistic inverse-square

    // Spatialization method
    UPROPERTY() ESoundSpatializationAlgorithm Spatialization;  // Panning, HRTF, etc.

    // Reverb send — how much of this volume's reverb to apply
    UPROPERTY() float ReverbSendLevel = 0.5f;

    // Doppler
    UPROPERTY() float DopplerFactor = 1.0f;    // 1.0 = physically accurate
};
```

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Sound in 3D is about distance, direction, and environment. Distance: drop 6 dB every time you double your distance — a car horn at 10m sounds half as loud at 20m. Direction: your brain uses three tricks to locate sounds — which ear hears it first (time difference, ~0.0002 seconds max), which ear hears it louder (level difference), and how your outer ear shapes the frequency (spectral notches — why up sounds different from down). Panning blends a sound between speakers using constant-power curves so it doesn't get louder in the center. HRTFs capture all three cues as a pair of ear-filters for every possible direction, stored as short impulse responses. Doppler: a sound moving toward you gets higher-pitched because sound waves bunch up — the same physics as a race car engine whine rising and falling as it passes.

### Technical
Sound intensity follows the inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²), giving SPL reduction of -20·log₁₀(r) (6 dB per distance doubling). Game engines clamp with min/max distance and apply a configurable falloff exponent. Constant-power panning distributes gain as L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°), preserving total power. VBAP extends to arbitrary speaker layouts by solving a linear system for speaker gain weights. HRTF models spatial perception via ITD (Δt ≈ (r_head/c)·sinθ, max ~0.25 ms), ILD (frequency-dependent head shadow), and spectral notches (pinna filtering). Doppler shift: f_heard = f_source · c/(c+v_relative) where v_relative > 0 means approaching. UE implements these via `USoundAttenuation`, `UAudioVolume`, and middleware integration (Wwise/FMOD).

## See Also

- [[01 — Frequency Domain & FFT]] — FFT-based convolution applies HRTF filters efficiently
- [[../Trigonometry & Vectors]] — Vector math for computing listener-to-source direction and distance
- [[../Rendering/03 — BRDF & Lighting Models|BRDF & Lighting]] — The rendering analog: how surfaces reflect light depending on angle

00 \u2014 Geometric Algebra Overview.content:
# Geometric Algebra

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Game development math is fragmented. You learn vectors for positions, quaternions for rotations, matrices for transforms, complex numbers for 2D rotations, and Pl\u00fccker coordinates for lines \u2014 each a separate toolbox with its own rules. **Geometric Algebra (GA)** unifies all of these into one coherent mathematical language. A single algebraic object \u2014 the **multivector** \u2014 can represent points, lines, planes, rotations, reflections, and volumes. Rotations work the same in 2D, 3D, and any dimension. Ray casting becomes a wedge product. Collision detection becomes a geometric product. GA is the \"grand unified theory\" of spatial mathematics.

---

> [!abstract] Advanced \u2014 Can skip on first read
> Geometric Algebra is not required for most game development tasks. Read this when you're comfortable with vectors, matrices, and quaternions, and want to understand the unified mathematical framework behind them.


### Analogy: The Universal Remote Control

Your living room has six remotes: one for the TV, one for the soundbar, one for the streaming box, one for the Blu-ray player, one for the lights, one for the AC. Each has different buttons, different rules, and you keep grabbing the wrong one. Now imagine a **universal remote** that controls everything with the same language. Pressing \"play\" always means play, regardless of the device.

Geometric Algebra is the universal remote for spatial math. Instead of:
- Vectors for direction ( \u2022 add, scale)
- Complex numbers for 2D rotations ( \u2022 multiply)
- Quaternions for 3D rotations ( \u2022 multiply, but different rules)
- Matrices for transforms ( \u2022 multiply, yet another set of rules)
- Pl\u00fccker coordinates for lines ( \u2022 use 6 numbers with a constraint)
- Cross product for oriented areas ( \u2022 only works in 3D!)

...you have ONE algebra where everything is a multivector and ONE product (the geometric product) that handles everything. Lines, planes, rotations, reflections, projections \u2014 all the same operation, all the same data type.

### The Core Unification

GA reveals that several \"separate\" math concepts are actually the same thing wearing different clothes:

| Traditional Math | What It Really Is in GA |
|-----------------|------------------------|
| Complex numbers (a + bi) | Scalar + Bivector in 2D (even subalgebra of Cl(2)) |
| Quaternions (w + xi + yj + zk) | Scalar + Bivector in 3D (even subalgebra of Cl(3)) |
| Cross product (a \u00d7 b) | Dual of the wedge product (a \u2227 b)* in 3D |
| Dot product (a \u00b7 b) | Symmetric part of the geometric product |
| Reflection formula (v - 2(n\u00b7v)n) | Sandwich product: -n v n |
| Rotation by quaternion (q v q\u207b\u00b9) | Sandwich product with rotor: R v R\u0303 |
| Determinant of [u v w] | Magnitude of trivector u \u2227 v \u2227 w |

All of these fall out naturally from the **geometric product** \u2014 no special cases, no dimension limits.

### Why Game Developers Should Care

1. **Rotors are better than quaternions:** Rotors generalize to any dimension, interpolate beautifully (just like quaternions), and handle reflections natively
2. **PGA unifies ray casting:** Points, lines, and planes are all algebraic objects \u2014 a ray-plane intersection is literally a wedge product
3. **Cleaner code:** No switching between vector math, matrix math, and quaternion math \u2014 one unified API
4. **Dimension-independent:** Code that works in 2D works in 3D (and 4D for spacetime)
5. **DAE's curriculum:** Your professor emphasizes GA across multiple courses because it's the foundation of modern geometric computing

---

| Operation | Traditional Approach | GA Approach |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 2D Rotation | Complex number multiplication | Rotor (2D even subalgebra \u2014 isomorphic to \u2102) |
| 3D Rotation | Quaternion multiplication or 3\u00d73 matrix | Rotor sandwich (isomorphic to quaternion) |
| 4D Rotation | 4\u00d74 matrix (no quaternion equivalent!) | Rotor sandwich (8-component rotor) |
| Reflection | Reflected = v - 2(n\u00b7v)n | Reflected = -n v n |
| Line-plane intersection | Solve parametric equations | Meet(L, \u03a0) \u2014 one wedge product |
| Transform interpolation | Decompose: Slerp quat + lerp pos + lerp scale | Motor slerp (one unified interpolation) |
| Distance point to plane | |(p - p\u2080)\u00b7n| / |n|| |Dot(P, \u03a0) or Meet(P, \u03a0) |
| Cross product a \u00d7 b | Only in 3D | *(a \u2227 b) \u2014 dual of wedge, works in any dim |
| Homogeneous coordinates | (x, y, z, w) \u2014 ad hoc rules | PGA trivectors \u2014 algebraic rules |

---

### Libraries and Resources

- **bivector.net** \u2014 The central resource for GA in graphics/games. Includes `pga3d.cpp` (C++ reference implementation of 3D PGA), tutorials, and the SIGGRAPH course materials
- **ganja.js** \u2014 JavaScript GA visualization library for rapid prototyping
- **Klein** \u2014 Jeremy Ong's C++ PGA library for production use
- **Versor** \u2014 Pablo Colapinto's C++ GA library with OpenGL integration

### Simple PGA Type Definition (C++)

```cpp
// Based on bivector.net's pga3d.cpp
struct PGA3D
{
    // 16 components: 1 scalar + 4 vectors + 6 bivectors + 4 trivectors + 1 pseudoscalar
    float m[16];
    
    // Basis: 1, e0, e1, e2, e3, e01, e02, e03, e12, e31, e23, e021, e013, e032, e123, e0123
    
    // Geometric product implementation (16\u00d716 multiply table)
    static PGA3D GeoProduct(const PGA3D& A, const PGA3D& B);
    
    // Meet (vee / regressive product)
    static PGA3D Meet(const PGA3D& A, const PGA3D& B);
    
    // Join (wedge / outer product)
    static PGA3D Join(const PGA3D& A, const PGA3D& B);
};
```

### Learning Path for Game Developers

1. **Start with 2D GA:** Understand that complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA. This grounds the abstract math in something familiar.

2. **Learn the geometric product:** Master `ab = a\u00b7b + a\u2227b`. Everything else builds on this.

3. **Understand rotors in 3D:** See how they map to quaternions. The sandwich product `RvR\u0303` is the key pattern.

4. **Explore PGA:** Once comfortable with 3D GA, add the projective dimension to understand points/lines/planes as algebraic objects.

5. **Experiment:** Implement a simple PGA ray tracer or collision detector. The elegance becomes visceral once you code it.

---


> [!note] Practical Reality
> GA is not yet standard in game engines (though it's gaining traction in research). Most production code uses vectors, matrices, and quaternions. Learning GA gives you a deeper understanding of the underlying math \u2014 rotors are quaternions, the geometric product unifies dot and cross, and PGA makes collision detection algebraic. For everyday gameplay code, stick with vectors and quaternions. For engine-level work (physics, rendering, tooling), GA is the future.

### Why Your Professor Keeps Mentioning GA

Geometric Algebra appears across multiple courses at DAE (Digital Arts and Entertainment) because it's the mathematical foundation underlying nearly every graphics and physics system:

| DAE Course | GA Connection |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Math for Game Dev** | GA unifies dot/cross products, complex numbers, quaternions |
| **3D Graphics Programming** | PGA for projective transforms, homogeneous coordinates, clipping |
| **Physics Programming** | Collision detection via PGA meet/join, rigid body dynamics via motors |
| **Animation Tech** | Motor slerp for bone transforms, FK/IK via PGA |
| **Graphics Programming 2** | Ray tracing, reflection, shadow mapping in unified algebra |
| **Game Engine Architecture** | Designing transform hierarchies and math libraries |

The professor isn't just being thorough \u2014 GA is genuinely the \"right\" way to understand these concepts because it reveals the underlying unity that traditional fragmented math obscures. When you understand that a quaternion IS a rotor and a cross product IS the dual of a wedge product, you see the bigger picture.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Geometric Algebra is the math that says "everything — points, lines, planes, rotations — is just a special kind of number, and you can multiply them all the same way." Instead of learning five different kinds of math for five different geometric problems, you learn one. A rotation is a "rotor" that you sandwich around what you want to rotate. A line is the wedge between two points. A point is the wedge between three planes. Everything connects to everything else through the same multiplication operation. It sounds abstract, but in practice it makes game math code simpler and more elegant.

### Technical
Geometric Algebra (Clifford Algebra) is an associative algebra over a vector space with a quadratic form. The geometric product of vectors decomposes into symmetric (inner/dot) and antisymmetric (outer/wedge) parts: ab = a·b + a∧b. The algebra is graded: scalar (grade 0), vector (grade 1), bivector (grade 2), trivector (grade 3), etc., with dimension (n choose k) at each grade, total dimension 2ⁿ. The even subalgebra is isomorphic to the complex numbers (2D), quaternions (3D), and general spinors in higher dimensions. Projective Geometric Algebra (PGA) uses a degenerate metric (e₀² = 0) to model Euclidean geometry with points as trivectors, lines as bivectors, and planes as vectors. Rigid motions are represented by motors (even-grade elements) acting via the sandwich product, generalizing dual quaternions. The meet (∨) and join (∧) operations implement intersection and connection of geometric primitives as single algebraic products, enabling a unified computational framework for all spatial queries.

---

- [[Trigonometry & Vectors]] \u2014 Traditional vector math that GA unifies and extends
- [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations]] \u2014 Quaternions are the even subalgebra of 3D GA; rotors are the generalization
- [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] \u2014 Matrix transforms as linear representations of GA transformations
- [[00 — Curves Overview|Curves]] \u2014 Rotor slerp, motor interpolation for animation
- [[00 — Collision Detection Overview|Collision Detection]] \u2014 PGA-based collision detection as a unified alternative to SAT/GJK
- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] \u2014 Memory layout considerations for GA multivector types
- [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] \u2014 Cache-friendly SoA storage for batches of multivectors


## Folder Map

| # | Note | Quiz | What |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | Multivectors & Products | Yes | Scalars/vectors/bivectors, geometric product, wedge product |
| 02 | Rotors | Yes | Rotors as double reflections, sandwich product, vs quaternions |
| 03 | PGA & Applications | Yes | Projective GA, points/lines/planes, game dev uses |

00 \u2014 Collision Detection Overview.content:
# Collision Detection

> A collision detection is the silent gatekeeper of every physics engine. Read 01-04 in order.


### Analogy: The Bullet Through Paper

In a movie filmed at 24 frames per second, a bullet fired at 1000 m/s travels 41 meters between frames. If the bullet passes through a 1cm-thick wall, it's on one side in frame 1 and the other side in frame 2 — the collision is **missed**. This is **tunneling**. Continuous collision detection (CCD) sees the bullet's path as a swept volume and catches the wall it passed through. Discrete CCD only checks positions at frame boundaries.

### The Tunneling Problem

```cpp
// Frame N:   Bullet ●──────────── Wall |█|
// Frame N+1:                   Wall |█| ────────────● Bullet
// Discrete check at frame N:   no collision
// Discrete check at frame N+1: no collision
// RESULT: bullet passed through wall undetected!
```

### Solutions

#### 1. Smaller Timesteps (Sub-Stepping)

Run physics at higher frequency (e.g., 240 Hz internally while rendering at 60 Hz).

```cpp
const float SubStepDelta = 1.0f / 240.0f;
while (Accumulator >= SubStepDelta)
{
    PhysicsStep(SubStepDelta);
    Accumulator -= SubStepDelta;
}
```

#### 2. Swept Volumes (Conservative Advancement)

Sweep the entire shape along its trajectory and test against the environment:

```cpp
// Instead of: IsBoxAtPositionOverlapping?(Box, PositionN+1)
// Do:        DoesSweptBoxHitAnything?(Box, PositionN, PositionN+1)

FHitResult Hit;
bool bHit = GetWorld()->SweepSingleByChannel(
    Hit,
    StartPos,       // Position at start of frame
    EndPos,         // Position at end of frame
    Quat::Identity,
    ECC_WorldDynamic,
    FCollisionShape::MakeBox(HalfExtents),
    QueryParams
);
```

#### 3. Ray Casting for Small/Fast Objects

For bullets and fast projectiles, approximate as a ray:

```cpp
// Fire a ray from current position along velocity direction
FHitResult Hit;
bool bBlocked = GetWorld()->LineTraceSingleByChannel(
    Hit, BulletPos, BulletPos + Velocity * MaxCheckDistance, ECC_Visibility
);
```

#### 4. Time-of-Impact (TOI) Calculation

Compute the exact time two moving shapes first touch. GJK can be extended to conservative advancement for TOI, used by Bullet Physics and PhysX.

### game engine Connection: CCD Settings

```cpp
// On a UPrimitiveComponent (or in Project Settings):
// bUseCCD = true  — enables continuous collision detection
// This uses swept shapes internally for fast-moving objects

// For physics bodies:
BodyInstance.bUseCCD = true; // on UPrimitiveComponent or FBodyInstance
```

---

### Analogy: The Airport Security Analogy

At an airport, everyone walks through a metal detector (broad phase — cheap, catches 99% of nothing). If you beep, you go to secondary screening (narrow phase — expensive, precise). Collision detection works the same way: first, cheaply find all object *pairs* that MIGHT collide (broad phase). Then, for those few pairs, run expensive precise tests (narrow phase).

### Broad Phase: Spatial Partitioning

#### Uniform Grid

Divide the world into equal-sized cells. Each object registers in the cells it overlaps. Collision candidates: any two objects sharing at least one cell.

```cpp
// Simple spatial grid
class SpatialGrid
{
    float CellSize;
    TMap<FIntVector, TArray<AActor*>> Cells;
    
    void Insert(AActor* Actor, const Box& Bounds);
    TArray<TPair<AActor*, AActor*>> GetPotentialCollisions();
};
```

**Best for:** Many similarly-sized objects (particle systems, RTS units).

#### Quadtree / Octree

Recursively subdivide space: a 2D quadtree splits into 4 children when a cell contains too many objects; a 3D octree splits into 8. Objects are stored in the smallest cell that fully contains them.

```cpp
// game engine uses octrees internally for light culling and some collision
// You can access via: UWorld->GetControllerIterator(), etc.
```

**Best for:** Static geometry, uneven object distribution.

#### Dynamic AABB Tree (DBVT)

A binary tree of AABBs where each node's AABB contains its children. Objects can move — the tree updates incrementally. This is what UE5 uses internally.

**Best for:** Dynamic worlds with many moving objects.

#### Sort and Sweep

Sort all object AABB min-x values, then sweep across. Objects whose AABBs overlap in sorted x-order are potential collisions (then check y, then z).

**Best for:** 2D physics, or when objects are mostly axis-aligned.

### Narrow Phase

After broad phase identifies candidate pairs, narrow phase runs the precise tests:

| Shape Pair | Algorithm |
|-----------|-----------|
| Sphere-Sphere | Distance check (O(1)) |
| Sphere-Capsule | Point-to-segment distance (O(1)) |
| Sphere-Box | Closest point on box (O(1)) |
| Capsule-Capsule | Segment-to-segment distance (O(1)) |
| Box-Box (oriented) | SAT (15 axes) |
| Convex hull - Convex hull | GJK + EPA |
| Triangle mesh | BVH traversal (AABB tree over triangles) |
| Heightfield | 2D grid lookup + triangle test |

---

| Concept | game engine Class / Function |
|---------|---------------------|
| AABB | `Box`, `Box2D`, `BoxSphereBounds` |
| Sphere shape | `FCollisionShape::MakeSphere()` |
| Capsule shape | `FCollisionShape::MakeCapsule()` |
| Ray cast (line trace) | `UWorld::LineTraceSingleByChannel()`, `LineTraceMultiByChannel()` |
| Sweep trace | `UWorld::SweepSingleByChannel()`, `SweepMultiByChannel()` |
| Overlap test | `UWorld::OverlapAnyTestByChannel()`, `OverlapMultiByChannel()` |
| Hit result | `FHitResult` — Location, Normal, ImpactPoint, Actor, Component, etc. |
| Collision channels | `ECollisionChannel` — ECC_WorldStatic, ECC_WorldDynamic, ECC_Pawn, ECC_Visibility, etc. |
| Collision responses | `ECollisionResponse` — ECR_Ignore, ECR_Overlap, ECR_Block |
| CCD flag | `UPrimitiveComponent::BodyInstance.bUseCCD` |
| Spatial structure | Internal: `FPhysicsInterface`, `Chaos::FImplicitObject` |
| Debug draw | `DrawDebugBox()`, `DrawDebugSphere()`, `DrawDebugLine()` |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Collision detection is the "are you touching me?" problem. First, you wrap everything in simple shapes (boxes, spheres) and do quick checks — that's the broad phase, like a metal detector. If the cheap check says "maybe," you do the expensive check. For boxes, you check if they overlap on all three axes. For any weird shape, you use a clever trick: if you subtract one shape from the other and the result contains the origin, they overlap — that's GJK. For bullets (which move fast), you sweep the shape along its path instead of checking only at the start and end. And if you can shine a light between two objects and their shadows don't overlap, they're not touching — that's SAT.

### Technical
Collision detection operates in two phases. Broad phase uses spatial partitioning (grids, octrees, DBVTs, sort-and-sweep) to reduce the O(n²) all-pairs problem to O(n log n) or O(n). Narrow phase applies geometric algorithms: AABB uses interval overlap on 3 axes (O(1)); GJK iteratively constructs a simplex in Minkowski difference space to test origin inclusion (typically 3-6 iterations for convex shapes); SAT projects convex polyhedra onto candidate separating axes (face normals and edge cross products); Möller-Trumbore solves the ray-triangle system via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors for O(1) intersection. Continuous collision detection uses conservative advancement or swept-shape queries to prevent tunneling for objects moving faster than their own size per frame.

---

1. Implement a sphere-AABB overlap test. (Hint: find the closest point on the AABB to the sphere center, then check distance.)
2. Write a ray-AABB intersection using the slab method. Test with a ray that hits, one that misses, and one that's parallel to an axis.
3. Implement the SAT (Separating Axis Theorem) for two convex 2D polygons. What axes do you test?
4. Why would you use a broad phase before a narrow phase? What data structure would you use for spatial partitioning?
5. **Coding:** Implement a simple 2D physics simulation: bouncing balls with AABB collisions and sphere-AABB overlap. Add gravity and restitution.

- [[Trigonometry & Vectors]] — Dot/cross products, vector projection, normalization (foundation for all collision math)
- [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] — World-to-local transforms for oriented bounding boxes
- [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations]] — Rotating collision shapes
- [[00 — Curves Overview|Curves]] — Swept volumes and movement interpolation
- [[00 — Geometric Algebra Overview|Geometric Algebra]] — PGA unified approach to ray casting and collision detection
- [[03 — Memory/01 — Stack vs Heap|Stack vs Heap]] — Spatial data structure memory management


## Folder Map

| # | Note | Quiz | What |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | AABB and Sphere | Yes | AABB overlap tests, sphere-sphere, sphere-AABB |
| 02 | Ray Casting | Yes | Ray-AABB, ray-sphere, ray-triangle, applications |
| 03 | Separating Axis Theorem | Yes | SAT for convex polygons, OBB-OBB, edge cases |
| 04 | GJK Algorithm | Yes | Minkowski difference, support function, simplex evolution, EPA |

03 \u2014 Constraints & Contact.content:
# Constraints & Contact

> [!info] Prerequisites: [[01 — Rigid Body Dynamics]], [[02 — Numerical Integration]]

---

## Why

Collision detection finds intersecting bodies. Dynamics integrates motion. Constraints PREVENT bodies from doing impossible things — penetrating each other, stretching beyond their length, rotating past a joint limit. Without constraints, a stack of boxes collapses into a single point. With them, you get resting contact, ragdolls, and vehicle suspensions.

**Engine use:** PhysX constraint solver (PGS), UE PhysicsConstraint, any ragdoll or vehicle.

---

## The Constraint Equation

A constraint is a mathematical rule: `C(x) = 0` must hold. For a distance constraint (rope): `C = |p1 - p2| - restLength = 0`.

The solver finds forces (Lagrange multipliers λ) that enforce all constraints simultaneously. Each constraint generates one scalar equation, producing one impulse:

```
J * v + b = 0
```
Where J is the Jacobian (direction of constraint force), v is velocity, and b is the bias (position error correction).

---

## Sequential Impulse (PGS) — How PhysX Works

The most common iterative solver:

```cpp
for (int iteration = 0; iteration < 10; iteration++) {
    for (Constraint& c : constraints) {
        // Compute effective mass along constraint direction
        float effMass = 1.0f / (invMassA + invMassB + 
            Dot(c.jacobian, invInertiaA * c.jacobian) +
            Dot(c.jacobian, invInertiaB * c.jacobian));
        
        // Compute constraint error (how much it's violated)
        float error = ComputeError(c);
        
        // Compute Lagrange multiplier (constraint force)
        float lambda = -(error) * effMass * (1.0f / dt);
        
        // Clamp: contact can only push apart, not pull together
        if (c.isContact) lambda = Max(0, lambda);
        
        // Apply impulse to both bodies
        ApplyImpulse(bodyA,  c.jacobian * lambda, c.anchorA);
        ApplyImpulse(bodyB, -c.jacobian * lambda, c.anchorB);
        
        // Warm-start: accumulate lambda for next frame
        c.accumLambda += lambda;
    }
}
```

**Why iterative:** Solving all constraints exactly requires an N×N matrix solve — O(N³). PGS solves each constraint independently and repeats — converges in ~10 iterations for most scenes. This is what PhysX, Bullet, and Box2D all use.

---

## Resting Contact — Why Boxes Don't Fall Through Floors

When a box sits on a floor, gravity pulls it down, the floor pushes it up — net force is zero. This is "resting contact." The solver must:

1. Detect penetration (body intersecting floor)
2. Apply a normal impulse to separate them
3. Apply friction impulse to prevent sliding
4. Maintain the constraint that they stay separated

```cpp
struct ContactConstraint {
    Vec3 normal;         // direction of separation
    Vec3 tangent1, tangent2; // friction directions (perpendicular to normal)
    float penetration;   // how deep they intersect
    float restitution;   // bounciness (0 = clay, 1 = superball)
    float friction;      // Coulomb friction coefficient
    float accumNormal;   // accumulated normal impulse (warm-starting)
    float accumTangent1, accumTangent2;
};
```

**Restitution:** `v_rel_new = -restitution * v_rel_old`. At 0.5, the body bounces back with half its incoming velocity.

**Friction:** Coulomb model — friction force ≤ μ * normal_force. Static friction (stick) has higher μ than kinetic friction (slide). The solver uses a friction cone: `sqrt(f1² + f2²) ≤ μ * normal`.

---

## Joint Constraints — Ragdolls

A ball joint constrains two bodies to share a point:
```
C = p1 + r1 - (p2 + r2) = 0  // anchor point must stay together
```

A hinge adds an axis constraint — only rotation around one axis allowed. A slider constrains linear motion to one axis. These stack: a ragdoll has ~15 joints, each with 3-5 constraints = 60 constraint equations solved per iteration.

---

## Position Correction — Baumgarte Stabilization

Solving at the velocity level drifts over time due to numerical error. Baumgarte stabilization adds a position correction term:

```cpp
float baumgarte = 0.2f;  // fraction of penetration corrected per frame
float bias = -(baumgarte / dt) * penetration;
lambda = -(error + bias) * effMass * (1.0f / dt);
```

Too high → jitter. Too low → squishy. 0.1-0.3 is typical.

---

## Engine Connection

- **PhysX (UE):** PGS solver, `FConstraintInstance`, configurable position/projection iterations
- **UE Chaos:** PGS + Gauss-Seidel options, constraints as first-class objects
- **Unity:** Configurable solver iterations in Project Settings → Physics
- **Ragdolls:** Ball joints at hips/shoulders, hinge joints at knees/elbows, limits to prevent over-rotation

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Constraints are the rules of the physics game. "Don't go through the floor" (contact). "Stay this far apart" (distance). "Only rotate around this axis" (hinge). The solver is a referee who checks every rule, finds violations, and applies nudges (impulses) to fix them. It loops 10 times because fixing one rule might break another — by iteration 10, everything is close enough. Friction is the referee saying "you can slide, but not too fast."

### Technical
Constraints are holonomic equality constraints: C(x) = 0. Velocity-level: J·v = 0. Lagrange multipliers λ give constraint forces. Sequential Impulse (PGS) solves J·M⁻¹·Jᵀ·λ = -J·v iteratively — each constraint independently, accumulating λ. This is equivalent to Gauss-Seidel on the full system. Restitution uses the pre-solve relative velocity. Friction uses a linearized Coulomb cone (2D approximation). Baumgarte stabilization feeds position error into the velocity-level solve with factor β = 0.1-0.3.

## See Also

- [[01 — Rigid Body Dynamics]] — What the constraints act on
- [[../Collision Detection/04 — GJK Algorithm]] — Finding penetrations
- [[02 — Numerical Integration]] — How the solver interacts with the integrator

03 \u2014 L-Systems & Marching Cubes.content:
# L-Systems & Marching Cubes

> [!note] Why This Matters (Engine Context)
> A single tree model is 50 MB and takes an artist 2 days. A forest of 10,000 unique trees at 50 MB each is 500 GB — impossible. L-systems generate infinite, unique trees from a handful of production rules (a few KB). Marching Cubes converts a 3D scalar field (density values on a voxel grid) into a polygon mesh — the bridge between mathematical functions and renderable geometry. Together, they power procedural foliage, voxel terrain (UE Voxel Plugin, Minecraft-style engines), and organic mesh generation. This note covers the grammar of L-systems and the lookup-table magic of Marching Cubes.

---

## L-Systems — Grammar-Based Generation

An L-system (Lindenmayer system, 1968) is a parallel rewriting system. It has:
- **Alphabet:** set of symbols (e.g., F, +, -, [, ])
- **Axiom:** starting string
- **Production rules:** symbol → replacement string

Applied iteratively, the string grows. A **turtle graphics** interpreter converts the string to geometry.

### Example: Simple Plant

```
Alphabet: F, +, -, [, ]
Axiom: F
Rules:  F → FF+[+F-F-F]-[-F+F+F]
Iterations:
  n=0: F
  n=1: FF+[+F-F-F]-[-F+F+F]
  n=2: (FF+[...]) (FF+[...])+[+(FF+[...])-(FF+[...])-(FF+[...])]-[-...]
  ...
```

Turtle interpretation:
- **F:** move forward by length L, drawing a line
- **+:** turn right by angle δ
- **-:** turn left by angle δ
- **[:** push current position/angle onto stack
- **]:** pop position/angle from stack (return to branch point)
- **X, Y:** no drawing — used for structural rules only

```cpp
struct Turtle { float2 pos; float angle; };
std::stack<Turtle> stack;

void Interpret(const std::string& lstring, float length, float delta) {
    Turtle t = {{0, 0}, 90.0f};  // Start at origin, pointing up
    for (char c : lstring) {
        switch (c) {
            case 'F':
                float2 newPos = t.pos + float2(cos(t.angle), sin(t.angle)) * length;
                DrawLine(t.pos, newPos);
                t.pos = newPos;
                break;
            case '+': t.angle += delta; break;
            case '-': t.angle -= delta; break;
            case '[': stack.push(t); break;
            case ']': t = stack.top(); stack.pop(); break;
        }
    }
}
```

### Adding Realism — Stochastic and Parametric L-Systems

**Stochastic:** multiple productions for the same symbol, chosen randomly:
```
F(0.7) → F[+F]F[-F]F     (70% probability)
F(0.3) → F[+F]F            (30% probability)
```

**Parametric:** symbols carry parameters that affect geometry:
```
F(length, width) → F(length*0.8, width*0.7) [+F(length*0.6, width*0.5)] F(length*0.9, width*0.7)
```
Length and width decrease with each iteration — branches get thinner and shorter.

**Context-sensitive:** production depends on neighboring symbols:
```
F < F > F → F[+F]F   (F with F on both sides — interior branch)
F < F > - → F        (F with nothing on right — terminal branch, stop growing)
```

Realistic tree generators (SpeedTree, UE's procedural foliage) use parametric, stochastic, context-sensitive L-systems with thousands of tuned parameters.

---

## Marching Cubes — From Voxels to Triangles

Given a 3D scalar field f(x, y, z) — a density function evaluated on a voxel grid — Marching Cubes (Lorensen & Cline, 1987) extracts the **isosurface** f(x,y,z) = threshold as a triangle mesh.

### The Algorithm

1. Divide space into cubes (voxels), evaluate f at the 8 corners.
2. For each cube, determine which corners are inside (f > threshold) vs outside.
3. The 8 corners × 2 states = 256 possible configurations → lookup table maps each to a set of triangles.
4. Interpolate triangle vertices along cube edges where the sign changes.

```
Cube corners: v0...v7 (each is inside or outside)
Edge index: 12 edges, each connecting two corners
Lookup table: 256 entries → triangle vertex indices along edges
```

```cpp
// Simplified 2D marching squares (the concept in 2D):
// 4 corners × 2 states = 16 configurations
int config = 0;
if (f(x,   y)   > threshold) config |= 1;  // v0
if (f(x+1, y)   > threshold) config |= 2;  // v1
if (f(x+1, y+1) > threshold) config |= 4;  // v2
if (f(x,   y+1) > threshold) config |= 8;  // v3
// config 0-15 → lookup table → line segment(s)
```

In 3D, the 256-entry lookup table encodes which edges produce triangle vertices. By symmetry and rotation, only 15 unique cases exist.

### Density Functions — What to Feed Marching Cubes

Any function R³ → R can be isosurfaced:
```cpp
// Sphere: f(x,y,z) = R² - (x² + y² + z²)
// Metaballs: f(x,y,z) = Σ 1 / distance² to each ball center
// Perlin noise terrain: f(x,y,z) = y - NoiseHeight(x, z)
// Signed distance field (SDF): exact distance to nearest surface (negative inside)
```

Marching Cubes generates a mesh approximating the surface where f = 0 (or any threshold). Higher voxel resolution = more faithful mesh = more triangles.

---

## UE Voxel Plugin — Marching Cubes in Production

```cpp
// UE Voxel Plugin (simplified architecture):
// 1. Define a density function (C++ lambda or Blueprint)
auto DensityFn = [](float X, float Y, float Z) -> float {
    float Terrain = PerlinNoise3D(X, Y, Z) * 100.0f;
    float Cave = WorleyNoise3D(X*0.5, Y*0.5, Z*0.5) * 50.0f - 25.0f;
    return Y - Terrain + Cave;  // Terrain height - caves
};

// 2. Evaluate on a voxel grid
// 3. Run Marching Cubes → generate mesh
// 4. Optionally: simplify mesh (decimation), compute normals, apply materials
```

The UE Voxel Plugin handles LOD (larger voxels at distance), chunking (streaming grid sections), and collider generation. The core algorithm is Marching Cubes over a configurable density function.

---

## L-Systems + Marching Cubes — Procedural Trees

Advanced pipeline: use an L-system to define a tree's branching structure as line segments, then convert to a 3D signed distance field (each branch = a capsule SDF), then run Marching Cubes to generate a seamless organic mesh.

```
L-system string → turtle geometry → SDF from skeleton → Marching Cubes → mesh
```

This produces trees with smooth branch junctions — no visible seams where branches meet the trunk. SpeedTree uses a similar skeleton-to-mesh pipeline with proprietary improvements.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
An L-system is a sentence that grows itself. Start with "F" (draw a line). Apply the rule "replace every F with F[+F]F" — now you have a branching structure. Apply the rule again to every F in the result — more branches. The `[` and `]` are "remember this spot and come back" — like putting a pin in a map before exploring a side path. After a few iterations, a single letter becomes a complex tree. Marching Cubes turns a 3D "density cloud" into triangles. Imagine a room filled with fog — the fog is thicker in some places. Marching Cubes draws a surface wherever the fog reaches a certain density. It does this by marching through the room cube by cube, looking up the right triangles in a pre-computed table. Feed it noise → terrain. Feed it metaballs → organic blobs. Feed it an L-system skeleton → a tree mesh.

### Technical
An L-system (Lindenmayer system) is a formal grammar with parallel string rewriting: axiom ω, alphabet V, production rules P: V → V*. Turtle graphics interprets the string: F=draw, +/−=rotate, []=push/pop stack for branching. Stochastic L-systems use probabilistic rule selection; parametric L-systems attach continuous parameters to symbols; context-sensitive rules depend on neighboring symbols. Marching Cubes extracts an isosurface from a 3D scalar field by classifying the 8 corners of each voxel as inside/outside, indexing into a 256-entry lookup table, and interpolating triangle vertices along edges where the sign changes (15 unique topological cases by symmetry). The density function can be any mapping R³→R (noise, SDF, metaballs, implicit surfaces). UE Voxel Plugin applies Marching Cubes to user-defined density functions for procedural terrain with LOD and chunking.

## See Also

- [[01 — Noise Generation]] — Noise functions provide density fields for Marching Cubes terrain
- [[02 — Sampling & Placement]] — Scatter objects on the surfaces generated by L-systems
- [[../Trigonometry & Vectors]] — Turtle graphics uses vector math for movement and rotation

00 \u2014 Rendering.content:
# Rendering Math

> [!info] Overview of the rendering math pipeline. Start here before diving into individual notes.

## The Rendering Pipeline — Math at Every Stage

Every frame, the GPU transforms millions of triangles from 3D world space to 2D screen pixels. Math is the engine at every stage:

```
3D Model Space          [[01 — Projection & Clip Space|Projection]]
    ↓ Model Matrix      (local → world coordinates)
World Space
    ↓ View Matrix       (world → camera-relative)
Camera Space
    ↓ Projection Matrix (perspective/orthographic)
Clip Space
    ↓ Perspective Divide (w-divide)
NDC (Normalized Device Coordinates)
    ↓ Viewport Transform
Screen Space (pixels)

For each pixel of each triangle:
    ↓ [[02 — Barycentric Coordinates|Barycentrics]]
Interpolated Attributes (color, UV, normal)
    ↓ [[03 — BRDF & Lighting Models|BRDF/Lighting]]
Lighting Calculation
    ↓ Tone Mapping, Gamma
Final Pixel Color
```

## Folder Map

| # | Note | What It Covers |
|---|---|---|
| 01 | [[01 — Projection & Clip Space]] | Perspective divide, NDC, homogeneous coordinates, frustum math — how 3D becomes 2D |
| 02 | [[02 — Barycentric Coordinates]] | Triangle interpolation, UV mapping, why GPUs rasterize with barycentrics |
| 03 | [[03 — BRDF & Lighting Models]] | Lambert, Blinn-Phong, Cook-Torrance, Fresnel, GGX — physically-based rendering math |
| 04 | [[04 — Spherical Harmonics]] | Basis functions, irradiance probes, encoding lighting as 9 floats |
| 05 | [[05 — Monte Carlo & Path Tracing]] | Importance sampling, PDF, Russian roulette, variance reduction |

## Why This Matters

Every visual effect in a game — shadows, reflections, ambient occlusion, global illumination — is math. A shader is just a math function that runs millions of times per frame. Understanding projection means understanding why distant objects appear smaller. Understanding BRDF means understanding why metal looks different from plastic. Understanding Monte Carlo means understanding why ray-traced images start noisy and converge.

**Engine use:** Every rendering engine — UE's Deferred Renderer, Unity's HDRP/URP, Godot's Vulkan renderer, custom engines.

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Rendering math is a camera taking a photo of a 3D scene and developing the film. Projection is the lens — it flattens the 3D world onto the 2D sensor. Barycentrics is the pixel interpolation — what color should this pixel be based on the triangle's corners? BRDF is the film chemistry — how does light bounce off different materials? Spherical harmonics is a compression trick — store complex lighting in just 9 numbers. Monte Carlo is taking many photos with slightly different settings and averaging them for the perfect shot.

### Technical
The rendering equation: L_o = L_e + ∫ f_r·L_i·(n·ω_i)dω_i — outgoing light = emitted + integral of BRDF × incoming light × cosine over hemisphere. Projection: 4×4 P matrix maps view-space to clip-space; w-divide maps to NDC [-1,1]³; viewport maps to [0,W]×[0,H]. Barycentrics: any point in triangle = α·V0 + β·V1 + γ·V2 where α+β+γ=1. BRDF Cook-Torrance: f_r = D·F·G/(4·(n·l)·(n·v)) — normal distribution, Fresnel, geometry. SH: f(θ,φ) ≈ Σ c_lm·Y_lm — project function onto orthogonal basis, reconstruct with dot product. MC: E[f] ≈ (1/N)·Σ f(x_i)/p(x_i) — samples weighted by probability.

## See Also

- [[../From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations]] — The big-picture map of all game math
- [[../UE Math/FVector, FRotator, FQuat — The Core Types]] — UE's implementation of these concepts

01 \u2014 Projection & Clip Space.content:
# Projection & Clip Space

> [!note] Why This Matters (Engine Context)
> Every vertex your game renders makes a journey: model space → world space → camera space → **clip space** → NDC → screen pixels. The projection matrix and perspective divide are the gatekeepers of this pipeline. Get your projection math wrong, and objects warp, clip incorrectly, or vanish entirely. Understanding the homogeneous `w` coordinate — what it stores, why the GPU divides by it, and how it enables perspective — is fundamental to every shader you'll write. This note traces a 3D point through the entire projection pipeline to a 2D pixel.

---

## The Pipeline: One Vertex's Journey

```
Model Space (local coordinates, relative to object origin)
    │  Model Matrix (M): scale → rotate → translate
    ▼
World Space (global coordinates)
    │  View Matrix (V): rotate/translate so camera is at origin, looking down -Z
    ▼
Camera/Eye Space (camera at origin)
    │  Projection Matrix (P): scale, perspective warp
    ▼
Clip Space (homogeneous coordinates, before divide)
    │  Perspective Divide (÷ w): convert from 4D homogeneous to 3D
    ▼
NDC — Normalized Device Coordinates (cube [-1,1]³)
    │  Viewport Transform: scale + translate to pixel coordinates
    ▼
Screen Space (pixel x,y + depth)
```
