- ✅ **UPROPERTY() member variables** — the GC already tracks these via reflection
- ✅ **Local variables** in functions (temporary stack references)
- ✅ **Function parameters** when the lifetime is guaranteed by the caller
- ✅ **Interfaces** (`IInterface*`) which cannot be wrapped in `TObjectPtr`

### GC Implications

A raw `T*` is **only GC-tracked** if it's a `UPROPERTY()` member. Stack variables and non-UPROPERTY members can become dangling after GC.

> [!warning] Common Crash: Raw Pointer in Non-UPROPERTY Member
> ```cpp
> class UMyComponent : public UActorComponent
> {
>     AActor* CachedOwner;  // ❌ NOT a UPROPERTY — GC can destroy this!
> 
>     void Tick(float DeltaTime)
>     {
>         CachedOwner->GetActorLocation();  // ?? CRASH after GC
>     }
> };
> ```
> 
> **Fix**: Use `TObjectPtr<AActor> CachedOwner;` or mark it `UPROPERTY()`.

---

## TSharedPtr\<T\> — Reference-Counted Smart Pointer

### What It Is

`TSharedPtr<T>` is a **non-intrusive, reference-counted** smart pointer modeled after `std::shared_ptr`. It is NOT GC-aware — it manages its own lifetime through reference counting.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Core/Public/Templates/SharedPointer.h, lines 691-696
```cpp
// SharedPointer.h lines 691-696
/**
 * TSharedPtr is a non-intrusive reference-counted authoritative object pointer.
 * This shared pointer will be conditionally thread-safe when the optional Mode
 * template argument is set to ThreadSafe.
 */
template< class ObjectType, ESPMode InMode >
class TSharedPtr { ... };
```

### Thread Safety Modes

`TSharedPtr` takes an `ESPMode` template parameter:
- **`ESPMode::NotThreadSafe`** — Faster, single-thread access only (default for `TSharedPtr`)
- **`ESPMode::ThreadSafe`** — Atomic reference counting, safe across threads (default for `TSharedRef`)

### Key Methods (all from SharedPointer.h)

| Method | Line | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `Get()` | 1089-1092 | Returns raw pointer, or nullptr |
| `IsValid()` | 1109-1112 | Returns `Object != nullptr` |
| `operator bool()` | 1099-1102 | Explicit bool, same as `IsValid()` |
| `operator->()` | 1131-1134 | Arrow operator with nullptr check |
| `Reset()` | 1141-1143 | Releases reference, potentially deletes object |
| `GetSharedReferenceCount()` | 1152-1155 | Debug: number of shared references |
| `IsUnique()` | 1164-1167 | True if this is the only shared reference |

### TSharedRef — The Non-Nullable Variant

```cpp
// SharedPointer.h lines 146-153
/**
 * TSharedRef is a non-nullable, non-intrusive reference-counted authoritative
 * object reference.
 */
template< class ObjectType, ESPMode InMode >
class TSharedRef { ... };
```

> [!summary] TSharedRef vs TSharedPtr
> Use `TSharedRef` whenever possible — it guarantees non-null, eliminating nullptr checks. The engine documentation says: *"Use TSharedRef instead of TSharedPtr whenever possible -- it can never be nullptr!"* (SharedPointer.h line 64).

### MakeShared — Single Allocation

```cpp
// SharedPointer.h lines 2110-2114
template <typename InObjectType, ESPMode InMode = ESPMode::ThreadSafe, typename... InArgTypes>
[[nodiscard]] inline TSharedRef<InObjectType, InMode> MakeShared(InArgTypes&&... Args)
{
    SharedPointerInternals::TIntrusiveReferenceController<InObjectType, InMode>* Controller =
        SharedPointerInternals::NewIntrusiveReferenceController<InMode, InObjectType>(Forward<InArgTypes>(Args)...);
    return UE::Core::Private::MakeSharedRef<InObjectType, InMode>(
        Controller->GetObjectPtr(),
        (SharedPointerInternals::TReferenceControllerBase<InMode>*)Controller);
}
```

Prefer `MakeShared<T>(...)` over `MakeShareable(new T(...))` — it allocates the object and reference controller in a single memory block (line 67).

### TWeakPtr — Non-Owning Observer

```cpp
// SharedPointer.h lines 1323-1328
/**
 * TWeakPtr is a non-intrusive reference-counted weak object pointer.
 */
template< class ObjectType, ESPMode InMode >
class TWeakPtr { ... };
```

Key methods:
- **`Pin()`** (line 1565-1568) — Promotes to `TSharedPtr` if the object is still alive
- **`IsValid()`** (line 1588-1591) — Returns `Object != nullptr && WeakReferenceCount.IsValid()`

> [!warning] Do NOT call `Pin()` in hot loops
> SharedPointer.h line 76: *"For best performance, minimize calls to TWeakPtr::Pin (or conversions to TSharedRef/TSharedPtr)"*

### When to Use TSharedPtr / TSharedRef

- ✅ **Non-UObject classes** — structs, managers, pure C++ types
- ✅ **Systems** — render resources, audio buffers, custom allocators
- ✅ **Cross-thread ownership** — use `ESPMode::ThreadSafe`
- ❌ **UObject-derived classes** — the GC manages these already; using `TSharedPtr` creates a second, conflicting ownership system

> [!warning] Common Mistake: TSharedPtr to a UObject
> ```cpp
> TSharedPtr```<UMyObject>` Ptr = MakeShared`<UMyObject>`(); // ❌ UMyObject is GC-managed!
> ``
> This creates **two ownership systems** (GC + ref-count). The GC may destroy the object while `TSharedPtr` still holds a reference → use-after-free. **Never do this.**

---

## TUniquePtr`<T>` — Exclusive Ownership

### What It Is

`TUniquePtr<T> is a **single-ownership, non-copyable** smart pointer in the vein of `std::unique_ptr`.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Core/Public/Templates/UniquePtr.h, lines 16-27
```cpp
// UniquePtr.h lines 16-27
// Single-ownership smart pointer in the vein of std::unique_ptr.
// Use this when you need an object's lifetime to be strictly bound to the lifetime
// of a single smart pointer.
//
// This class is non-copyable - ownership can only be transferred via a move operation:
//
// TUniquePtr`<MyClass>` Ptr1(new MyClass);    // The MyClass object is owned by Ptr1.
// TUniquePtr`<MyClass>` Ptr2(Ptr1);           // Error - TUniquePtr is not copyable
// TUniquePtr`<MyClass>` Ptr3(MoveTemp(Ptr1)); // Ptr3 now owns the MyClass object
```

### Key Methods (all from UniquePtr.h)

| Method | Line | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `Get()` | 291-294 | Returns raw pointer without releasing ownership |
| `IsValid()` | 250-253 | Returns `Ptr != nullptr` |
| `operator bool()` | 260-263 | `explicit operator bool` (contextual conversion) |
| `operator->()` | 270-273 | Arrow operator |
| `operator*()` | 280-284 | Dereference operator |
| `Release()` | 301-306 | Releases ownership, returns pointer, nulls TUniquePtr |
| `Reset(T* InPtr)` | 313-322 | Destroys old object, takes ownership of new one |
| `GetDeleter()` | 329-332 | Returns reference to the deleter object |

### Custom Deleters

`TUniquePtr` supports custom deleters as a second template parameter (line 96):

```cpp
// UniquePtr.h line 96
template `<typename T, typename Deleter = TDefaultDelete<T>`>
class TUniquePtr : private Deleter { ... };
```

The default deleter (`TDefaultDelete<T>`, lines 29-65) simply calls `delete` on the pointer.

> [!note] Deleter difference from std::unique_ptr
> UniquePtr.h lines 25-27: *"If you provide a custom deleter, it is up to your deleter to handle null pointers. This is a departure from std::unique_ptr which will not invoke the deleter if the owned pointer is null."*

### When to Use TUniquePtr

- ✅ **PIMPL idiom** — hide implementation details in .cpp files
- ✅ **Exclusive ownership of non-UObject types** — managers, buffers, resources
- ✅ **Factory functions** — return a uniquely-owned object
- ✅ **RAII resource management** — files, sockets, GPU resources
- ❌ **UObject-derived classes** — same problem as TSharedPtr

---

## TWeakObjectPtr\<T\> — Weak UObject Reference

### What It Is

`TWeakObjectPtr<T>` is a **weak reference** to a UObject. It does NOT prevent garbage collection, and returns `nullptr` when the object is destroyed.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/WeakObjectPtr.h, lines 40-47
```cpp
// WeakObjectPtr.h lines 40-47
/**
 * FWeakObjectPtr is a weak pointer to a UObject.
 * It can return nullptr later if the object is garbage collected.
 * It has no impact on if the object is garbage collected or not.
 * It can't be directly used across a network.
 *
 * Most often it is used when you explicitly do NOT want to prevent
 * something from being garbage collected.
 */
```

### How It Works: ObjectIndex + SerialNumber

`FWeakObjectPtr` stores an **ObjectIndex** and **ObjectSerialNumber**. When you call `Get()`, it looks up the object by index and verifies the serial number hasn't changed (meaning the object slot hasn't been reused for a different object).

```
Source: WeakObjectPtr.h lines 410-505
```cpp
// WeakObjectPtr.h line 414-425 (serial number validation)
inline bool SerialNumbersMatch() const
{
    checkSlow(ObjectSerialNumber > FUObjectArray::START_SERIAL_NUMBER && ObjectIndex >= 0);
    int32 ActualSerialNumber = GUObjectArray.GetSerialNumber(ObjectIndex);
    return ActualSerialNumber == ObjectSerialNumber;
}
```

### Key Methods (all from WeakObjectPtr.h)

| Method | Line | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `Get()` | 212 | Returns nullptr if object is gone/GC'd |
| `Get(bEvenIfGarbage)` | 212 | Returns object even if marked as garbage |
| `IsValid()` | 290-300 | True if `Get()` would return non-null |
| `IsStale()` | 308 | True if used to point to a live object but no longer does |
| `IsExplicitlyNull()` | 314-326 | True if explicitly assigned to null or never initialized |
| `Pin()` | 238 | Returns `TStrongObjectPtr<UObject>` — keeps the object alive |
| `Reset()` | 123-132 | Resets to null state |

### No Implicit operator bool!

```cpp
// WeakObjectPtr.h line 276
explicit operator bool() const = delete;
```

This is **intentionally deleted** to prevent accidental resolution of weak pointers. Use `IsValid()` explicitly.

> [!warning] IsValid() Can Be Expensive
> WeakObjectPtr.h lines 280-281: *"This should be done only when needed as excess resolution of the underlying pointer can cause performance issues."*

### TStrongObjectPtr — The Pin Result

Calling `Pin()` on a `FWeakObjectPtr` returns a `TStrongObjectPtr<UObject>`, which increments the object's internal ref-count, preventing GC:

```
Source: WeakObjectPtr.h lines 238-244
```

### When to Use TWeakObjectPtr

- ✅ **Delegates / callbacks** — you want to observe a UObject without keeping it alive
- ✅ **Caches** — hold a reference to something that may be destroyed independently
- ✅ **Editor tools** — referencing objects the user might delete
- ✅ **Cross-system observation** — one system watching another without ownership
- ❌ **Primary ownership** — use `TObjectPtr` instead

> [!warning] Common Bug: Forgetting to Check IsValid()
> ```cpp
> TWeakObjectPtr<AActor> WeakActor = SomeActor;
> // ... time passes, GC runs ...
> AActor* Actor = WeakActor.Get();  // May be nullptr!
> Actor->GetActorLocation();        // ?? CRASH if Actor was GC'd
> ```
> **Always check**: `if (WeakActor.IsValid()) { WeakActor->DoThing(); }`

---

## Garbage Collection Mechanics

### Incremental Reachability (UE 5.4+)

UE 5 supports **incremental GC** via the `GIsIncrementalReachabilityPending` flag:

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/GarbageCollectionGlobals.h, line 13-14
```cpp
namespace UE::GC
{
    /** true if incremental reachability analysis is in progress */
    extern COREUOBJECT_API TSAN_ATOMIC(bool) GIsIncrementalReachabilityPending;
}
```

When this flag is true, `TObjectPtr` assignments call `MarkAsReachable()` via the GC barrier (ObjectPtr.h lines 337-361), ensuring that newly referenced objects aren't collected during incremental marking.

### MarkAsReachable

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/ObjectFwd.h, line 27
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Private/UObject/GarbageCollection.cpp, line 605-608
```cpp
// ObjectFwd.h line 27
COREUOBJECT_API void MarkAsReachable(const UObject* Obj);

// GarbageCollection.cpp lines 605-608
void MarkAsReachable(const UObject* Obj)
{
    Obj->MarkAsReachable();
}
```

### GC Scope Guard

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/GarbageCollection.h, lines 116-134
```cpp
/** Prevent GC from running in the current scope */
class FGCScopeGuard { ... };

class FGCScopeTryGuard
{
    bool LockSucceeded() const { return bLockSuccessful; }
};
```

### How the GC Finds References

The GC uses **reflection** (`UPROPERTY()`) to trace object references. Every `UPROPERTY()` of type `TObjectPtr<T> or `T*` is automatically visited. `TWeakObjectPtr` properties are **not** automatically traced — use them when you intentionally don't want to keep an object alive.

---

## Decision Flowchart

```
I have a reference to X. Which pointer do I use?
│
├─ Is X a UObject (AActor, UActorComponent, UTexture, etc.)?
│  │
│  ├─ Am I the owner / do I need to keep X alive?
│  │  ├─ YES → TObjectPtr<X> as UPROPERTY() member
│  │  └─ NO (just observing) → TWeakObjectPtr<X>
│  │
│  ├─ Is this a function parameter or local variable?
│  │  └─ Raw X* is acceptable (GC won't run during the call)
│  │
│  └─ Is this an interface pointer?
│     └─ Raw IX* (TObjectPtr doesn't work with interfaces)
│
└─ Is X a non-UObject (struct, pure C++ class)?
   │
   ├─ Does X have a single clear owner?
   │  ├─ YES → TUniquePtr<X>
   │  └─ NO → 
   │     ├─ Shared ownership → TSharedPtr<X> / TSharedRef<X>
   │     └─ Non-owning observer → TWeakPtr<X>
   │
   └─ Does X need thread-safe reference counting?
      └─ TSharedPtr<X, ESPMode::ThreadSafe>
```

---

## Quick Reference: Operators and Methods

| Pointer | `Get()` | `IsValid()` | `operator bool` | `operator->` | `Reset()` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TObjectPtr```<T>`** | Line 697 | Line 755-758 (`explicit`) | Contextual only | Line 736 | via `= nullptr` |
| **Raw T\*** | N/A (it IS the pointer) | `!= nullptr` | Implicit | Built-in | `= nullptr` |
| **TSharedPtr`<T>`** | Line 1089 | Line 1109 | Line 1099 | Line 1131 | Line 1141 |
| **TUniquePtr`<T>`** | Line 291 | Line 250 | Line 260 | Line 270 | Line 313 |
| **TWeakObjectPtr`<T>`** | Line 212 | Line 290 | **Deleted** (line 276) | Line 225 | Line 123 |

---

## Common Crashes and Bugs

### 1. Dangling raw T\* after GC (non-UPROPERTY member)

```cpp
// ❌ Crash waiting to happen
class UMyComponent : public UActorComponent
{
    AActor* SomeActor;  // Not tracked by GC
public:
    void DoWork() { SomeActor->GetName(); } // ?? Use-after-free
};
```

**Fix**: `TObjectPtr<AActor> SomeActor;` or `UPROPERTY() AActor* SomeActor;`

### 2. TSharedPtr to a UObject

```cpp
// ❌ Two ownership systems fight each other
TSharedPtr<UMyObject> BadPtr = MakeShared<UMyObject>();
// GC may collect UMyObject while TSharedPtr still references it
```

**Fix**: Use `TObjectPtr<UMyObject>` for UObject types.

### 3. TWeakObjectPtr without null check

```cpp
TWeakObjectPtr<AActor> WeakActor = GetSomeActor();
// ... GC runs ...
WeakActor->GetActorLocation(); // ?? CRASH
```

**Fix**: `if (AActor* A = WeakActor.Get()) { A->GetActorLocation(); }`

### 4. Forgetting TWeakPtr::Pin() before use

```cpp
TWeakPtr<FMyStruct> WeakStruct = SharedStruct;
// WeakStruct->DoThing(); // ❌ Won't compile — TWeakPtr has no operator->
TSharedPtr<FMyStruct> Pinned = WeakStruct.Pin();
if (Pinned.IsValid()) { Pinned->DoThing(); } // ✅ Correct
```

### 5. TObjectPtr to an interface

```cpp
TObjectPtr<IInterface> Ptr; // ❌ Won't compile
```

Interfaces in UE don't derive from `UObject`, they use multiple inheritance. Use raw `IInterface*`.

---

2. Why is TSharedPtr`<UObject>` a bug? What system manages UObject lifetimes instead?
3. What happens if you store a raw UObject* in a UPROPERTY? What does the GC do?
4. Write a struct with TObjectPtr`<AActor>` members. Does the struct need to be a USTRUCT?

UE Inheritance & UObject System.content:
# UE Inheritance & UObject System

## The UObject Hierarchy

Every class in UE that needs reflection, serialization, or garbage collection inherits from `UObject`. This single base class is the foundation of the entire engine.

```
UObject                   ← Base of everything: reflection, GC, serialization
├── UActorComponent       ← Attached to actors: movement, rendering, audio
│   └── USceneComponent   ← Has a transform — can be placed in the world
├── UGameInstance         ← Persists across level changes
├── UDataAsset            ← Designer-configurable data containers
├── UBlueprintFunctionLibrary ← Static utility functions callable from BP
│
AActor (inherits UObject) ← Can be placed in the level, has Tick, replicates
├── APawn                 ← Can be possessed by a controller
│   └── ACharacter        ← Walking, jumping, capsule collision built-in
├── AController           ← Possesses pawns, handles input
│   ├── APlayerController ← Human input
│   └── AAIController     ← AI-driven behavior
└── AGameModeBase         ← Game rules
```

> [!info] Source
> UObjectBase.h (CoreUObject/Public/UObject), Actor.h (Engine/GameFramework)

---

## GENERATED_BODY() and Super

Every `UCLASS` must contain `GENERATED_BODY()`. This macro generates a `Super` typedef that points to the parent class:

```cpp
UCLASS()
class UMyClass : public UObject
{
    GENERATED_BODY()
    // Compiler sees: typedef UObject Super;
};
```

Now you can call parent functions without spelling out the full class name:

```cpp
void UMyClass::BeginDestroy()
{
    // Clean up your own stuff first...
    Super::BeginDestroy();  // Calls UObject::BeginDestroy()
    // The parent's cleanup runs
}
```

---

## The Lifecycle of a UObject

UObjects have a well-defined lifecycle with virtual functions you override:

```cpp
// Creation order:
UObject::UObject(FObjectInitializer&)    // Constructor — set default values
  → PostInitProperties()                 // After properties are initialized
    → PostLoad()                         // After loading from disk (not on new objects)
      → BeginPlay() (AActor only)        // Gameplay start
        → Tick(float DeltaTime)          // Every frame (if enabled)

// Destruction order:
BeginDestroy()                           // About to be destroyed — last chance to clean up
  → FinishDestroy()                      // Actually freeing memory
    → IsReadyForFinishDestroy()          // Can we free now? (async loading check)
```

> [!tip] Constructor vs BeginPlay
> Set default values in the constructor. Access other actors/components in `BeginPlay()` — they don't exist yet during construction.

---

## Virtual Function Override Pattern

UE virtual functions follow specific patterns:

### Standard Override
```cpp
// AActor::BeginPlay is virtual
void AMyActor::BeginPlay() override
{
    Super::BeginPlay();  // ALWAYS call the parent first for BeginPlay
    // Your init code...
}
```

### BlueprintNativeEvent — Dual Implementation
```cpp
UFUNCTION(BlueprintNativeEvent)
void OnDamageReceived(float Amount);

// C++ default implementation (in .cpp):
void AMyActor::OnDamageReceived_Implementation(float Amount)
{
    // Default C++ logic — Blueprint can override
}
```

When called, UE checks if the Blueprint overrode `OnDamageReceived`. If yes, runs the BP version. If no, runs the `_Implementation` version.

### BlueprintImplementableEvent — BP-Only Body
```cpp
UFUNCTION(BlueprintImplementableEvent)
void OnDeath();

// No _Implementation! The body is ONLY in Blueprint.
// Do NOT write a C++ body — it won't compile.
```

---

## UObject's Built-In Features

Every UObject automatically gets:

| Feature | What It Does |
|---|---|
| **Reflection** | `GetClass()`, `IsA<T>()`, `GetName()`, property iteration at runtime |
| **Serialization** | `UPROPERTY()` members save/load automatically |
| **Garbage Collection** | UObjects are tracked and collected when unreferenced |
| **Networking** | `IsSupportedForNetworking()`, `GetLifetimeReplicatedProps()` |
| **Editor Integration** | Details panel, Blueprint nodes, data validation |

> [!warning] UObjects are GC-tracked, raw C++ objects are not
> A plain `class MyData { }` (not UObject) gets no garbage collection. You manage its lifetime with `new`/`delete` or smart pointers. UObjects are managed by UE's GC — you never `delete` them manually.

---

## CDO — Class Default Object

Every `UCLASS` has a **Class Default Object** — a template instance that holds default property values:

```cpp
UCLASS()
class UWeaponData : public UDataAsset
{
    GENERATED_BODY()

public:
    UPROPERTY(EditDefaultsOnly)
    float Damage = 10.0f;  // This value lives in the CDO

    UPROPERTY(EditDefaultsOnly)
    float FireRate = 0.1f;
};

// Getting defaults:
const UWeaponData* Defaults = GetDefault<UWeaponData>();
float baseDamage = Defaults->Damage;  // 10.0f
```

**Why CDOs matter:** When you spawn 1,000 enemies, they don't each store \"Damage = 10.0f\" — they inherit it from the CDO until you change it. This saves memory and enables class-wide defaults that editors can modify.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

UObject is the parent of every engine class. It gives you superpowers: your properties save automatically, the garbage collector cleans up after you, and Blueprints can talk to your code. `GENERATED_BODY()` is the membership card — without it, none of this works. `Super::` is how you say \"now let the parent do its thing\" when you override a function. The CDO is the blueprint for all instances — it holds the default values.

### Technical

UObject (UObjectBase.h) provides runtime type information via `UClass` reflection objects, serialization through `FArchive`, and GC participation through the `FUObjectArray` reachability graph. `GENERATED_BODY()` (ObjectMacros.h:800) expands to friend declarations for the `FObjectInitializer` constructor chain and the static `StaticClass()` member. The CDO is constructed once at engine init via `GetPrivateStaticClassBody()` and cloned for Blueprint defaults. BlueprintNativeEvent dispatch checks `UFunction::HasAnyFunctionFlags(FUNC_BlueprintEvent)` to determine the execution path.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. What's the difference between BeginPlay and the constructor? When should you use each?
2. Write a C++ class that logs to console at each stage: constructor, PostInitProperties, BeginPlay, Tick, BeginDestroy. Spawn it and observe the log order.
3. What happens if you access GetOwner() in the constructor? Why?
4. Write a BlueprintNativeEvent. Where does the _Implementation suffix come from?


## UActorComponent Lifecycle

UActorComponent's lifecycle is distinct from AActor's. Components are attached to an owner and follow this sequence:

```
Constructor
    ↓
RegisterComponent()        ← Owner adds to component list
    ↓
InitializeComponent()      ← Called after owner's Init
    ↓
BeginPlay()                ← After all components initialized
    ↓
TickComponent(DeltaTime)   ← Every frame if bCanEverTick
    ↓
UnregisterComponent()      ← Owner removes from list
    ↓
DestroyComponent()         ← Final cleanup
```

**Key rules:**
- Don't access other components in the constructor — they may not exist yet.
- `InitializeComponent()` is the first point where all sub-objects are available.
- `BeginPlay()` guarantees the world is ready and all actors are spawned.
- `TickComponent()` only fires if `PrimaryComponentTick.bCanEverTick = true` AND `PrimaryComponentTick.bStartWithTickEnabled = true`.


## See Also

- [[04 — OOP & Generics/01 — Inheritance & Virtual Functions|Inheritance]] (C++) — Standard C++ inheritance
- [[UHT, Macros & Reflection]] — How GENERATED_BODY and UCLASS work under the hood
- [[Casting/01 — Cast Internals & Variants|Casting]] — Type-safe casting in the UObject hierarchy

UE Templates & T-Containers.content:
# UE Templates & T-Containers

## Why UE Has Its Own Containers

UE doesn't use `std::vector` or `std::map`. It has its own: `TArray`, `TMap`, `TSet`. The reasons:

| Reason | What It Means |
|---|---|
| **Reflection** | UE containers integrate with UPROPERTY and garbage collection |
| **Serialization** | TArray saves/loads automatically when inside a UPROPERTY |
| **Network replication** | Containers with `Replicated` sync across the network |
| **Blueprint exposure** | TArray and TMap appear as native types in Blueprint graphs |
| **Memory tracking** | UE allocators report to engine memory profilers |
| **Deterministic** | Same inputs = same behavior on all platforms (no STL implementation variance) |

---

## `TArray<T>` — The Workhorse

```cpp
// Source: Containers/Array.h

TArray<int32> Numbers = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};

// Core operations
Numbers.Add(6);                          // Push to end
Numbers.Emplace(10, \"hello\");            // Construct in-place
Numbers.Remove(3);                       // Remove first occurrence
Numbers.RemoveAt(0);                     // Remove by index
Numbers.Pop();                           // Remove last
int32 val = Numbers[2];                  // Index access (no bounds check)
int32 safe = Numbers.IsValidIndex(2) ? Numbers[2] : 0;

// Search
int32 idx = Numbers.Find(4);             // Returns INDEX_NONE (-1) if not found
bool exists = Numbers.Contains(4);
int32* ptr = Numbers.FindByPredicate([](int32 N) { return N > 3; });

// Sorting
Numbers.Sort();                          // Default ascending
Numbers.Sort([](int32 A, int32 B) { return A > B; });  // Custom predicate
Numbers.HeapSort();                      // Heap sort (faster for mostly-sorted)

// Memory
Numbers.Reserve(100);                    // Pre-allocate for performance
Numbers.Shrink();                        // Free overallocated slack
int32 num = Numbers.Num();              // Element count
int32 max = Numbers.Max();              // Allocated capacity
```

### TArray Memory Model

Like `std::vector`, TArray grows exponentially (growth factor ≈ 1.5-2x). Unlike `std::vector`, TArray uses UE's allocators — so `TArray::Add` calls `FMemory::Realloc` instead of `::operator new`.

```cpp
// UPROPERTY() makes TArray serializable + GC-tracked
UPROPERTY()
TArray<TObjectPtr<AActor>> TrackedActors;  // GC follows pointers inside the array
```

### TArray vs std::vector

| | TArray | std::vector |
|---|---|---|
| Allocator | UE's FMemory (profilable) | std::allocator |
| Reflection | ✅ (with UPROPERTY) | ❌ |
| Growth factor | ~1.5-2x (configurable) | Typically 2x |
| `Emplace` | `Add` already emplaces, `Emplace` for complex types | `emplace_back` |
| `Remove` | `Remove` / `RemoveAt` / `RemoveAll` | erase-remove idiom |

---

## `TMap<K, V>` — The Dictionary

```cpp
TMap<FName, int32> Scores;
Scores.Add(FName(\"PlayerHealth\"), 100);
Scores.Emplace(FName(\"PlayerAmmo\"), 30);

int32* val = Scores.Find(FName(\"PlayerHealth\"));  // nullptr if not found
if (val) { UseValue(*val); }

int32& ref = Scores.FindOrAdd(FName(\"PlayerMana\"));  // Adds if missing
scores.Remove(FName(\"PlayerHealth\"));

for (const auto& [Key, Value] : Scores) { }  // Structured bindings work!
for (auto It = Scores.CreateIterator(); It; ++It) { It->Key; It->Value; }
```

---

## `TSet<T>` — Unique Collection

```cpp
TSet<FName> ActiveTags;
ActiveTags.Add(FName(\"Burning\"));
ActiveTags.Add(FName(\"Burning\"));  // No-op — already present

if (ActiveTags.Contains(FName(\"Burning\"))) { }
ActiveTags.Remove(FName(\"Burning\"));

// Set operations:
TSet<int32> A{1,2,3}, B{2,3,4};
TSet<int32> Union = A.Union(B);            // {1,2,3,4}
TSet<int32> Intersection = A.Intersect(B); // {2,3}
TSet<int32> Diff = A.Difference(B);        // {1}
```

---

## Other Key T-Containers

| Container | Equivalent | Use For |
|---|---|---|
| `TArray<T> | `std::vector` | Dynamic array — default choice |
| `TMap<K,V> | `std::unordered_map` | Key-value storage |
| `TSet<T> | `std::unordered_set` | Unique collection |
| `TQueue<T> | `std::queue` | FIFO — async task queuing |
| `TLinkedList<T> | `std::list` | Rare — TArray beats it for most things |
| `TStaticArray<T,N> | `std::array` | Fixed-size stack array |
| `TBitArray`<>` | `std::bitset` | Bit-level operations |
| `TSparseArray<T>` | — | Optimized for sparse indices (UE's object array uses this internally) |

---

## UE Template Patterns

### `TIsIInterface<T>` — Template Type Traits

```cpp
// SFINAE: only enable if T is a UInterface
template<typename T>
typename TEnableIf<`TIsIInterface\\<T\\>`::Value, T*>::Type GetInterface();

// Used extensively in Cast<T>() to select the interface path
```

### `TSubclassOf<T>`

```cpp
UPROPERTY(EditDefaultsOnly)
TSubclassOf<AActor> EnemyClass;  // Editor shows dropdown of AActor subclasses

AActor* Spawned = GetWorld()->SpawnActor<AActor>(EnemyClass);
```

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Write a function that enqueues 5 items via TQueue, then dequeues and processes them. Why use TQueue instead of TArray for this pattern?
2. Write a TSubclassOf`<AActor>` UPROPERTY. What editor widget appears?
3. When is TSparseArray better than TArray?

## See Also

- [[TArray Internals]] — Deep dive into TArray's memory model and growth
- [[04 — OOP & Generics/05 — Templates & Concepts|Templates]] (C++) — Standard C++ templates
- [[03 — Memory/06 — std vector & Container Internals|std::vector]] (C++) — How std::vector compares

UHT, Macros & Reflection.content:
# UHT, Macros & Reflection

## Why UE Has So Many Macros

Every UE class starts with `UCLASS()`, every property with `UPROPERTY()`, every function with `UFUNCTION()`. These aren't just decoration — they feed the **Unreal Header Tool (UHT)**, which generates an entire hidden layer of C++ code that makes the engine's runtime systems work: serialization, garbage collection, Blueprint exposure, networking, and the editor UI.

```cpp
UCLASS(BlueprintType)
class PROJECTSTORM_API UMyActor : public AActor
{
    GENERATED_BODY()  // ← This is where the magic happens

public:
    UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, BlueprintReadWrite)
    float Health = 100.0f;  // ← Automatically serialized, replicated, GC-tracked

    UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable)
    void TakeDamage(float Amount);  // ← Automatically callable from Blueprints
};
```

> [!info] Source
> `UCLASS` / `UFUNCTION` / `GENERATED_BODY`: `CoreUObject/Public/UObject/ObjectMacros.h:779-808`
> `ENUM_CLASS_FLAGS`: `Core/Public/Misc/EnumClassFlags.h:8`

---

## What UHT Actually Does

### The Pipeline

```
YourHeader.h (with UCLASS/UPROPERTY/UFUNCTION)
    │
    ▼
UHT parses the header — extracts all macro-marked declarations
    │
    ▼
UHT generates: YourClass.generated.h, YourClass.gen.cpp
    │
    ▼
The generated code adds reflection data, serialization, GC tracking
    │
    ▼
The compiler compiles everything together into the final binary
```

**UHT is a separate program** that runs before the C++ compiler. It's similar to the C++ preprocessor but understands UE's specific macros. It reads your headers, finds all `UCLASS`/`UPROPERTY`/`UFUNCTION` markers, and generates the glue code that makes Unreal's runtime systems work.

---

## GENERATED_BODY() — The Entry Point

```cpp
UCLASS()
class UMyClass : public UObject
{
    GENERATED_BODY()  // REQUIRED in every UCLASS/USTRUCT

    // This macro expands to generate:
    // - A static UClass* pointer (StaticClass())
    // - Type information (GetClass(), IsA<T>())
    // - Constructor boilerplate
    // - Serialization operator declaration
    // - Super typedef (enables Super::FunctionName)
    // - CDO (Class Default Object) support
};
```

> [!tip] `Super::` Comes from GENERATED_BODY
> When you call `Super::BeginPlay()`, the `Super` typedef was generated by `GENERATED_BODY()`. It's `typedef ASuperClass Super;` — a convenience that links to your parent class.

---

## UCLASS() — Making a Class Visible to UE

```cpp
// Source: ObjectMacros.h:808

UCLASS(BlueprintType, Blueprintable, Abstract)
class UCharacterBase : public UObject
{
    GENERATED_BODY()
};
```

### Common UCLASS Specifiers

| Specifier | What It Does |
|---|---|
| `BlueprintType` | Can be used as a variable type in Blueprints |
| `Blueprintable` | Can be subclassed in Blueprints |
| `Abstract` | Cannot be placed in levels — exists only as a base class |
| `NotBlueprintable` | Cannot be subclassed in Blueprints |
| `HideDropdown` | Not shown in class picker dropdowns |
| `Deprecated` | Marks as deprecated — shows warnings |
| `Within=ClassName` | Can only be placed inside instances of ClassName |
| `MinimalAPI` | Only export the type information, not all functions |
| `CustomConstructor` | Suppress auto-generated constructor call |

```cpp
// AActor-specific specifiers:
UCLASS(Config=Game, hidecategories=(Rendering, Physics))
class AMyActor : public AActor { };

// Config=Game: Settings saved to Game.ini
// hidecategories: Hide property categories in the editor
```

---

## UFUNCTION() — Exposing Functions to the Engine

```cpp
// Source: ObjectMacros.h:779

UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable, Category = \"Combat\")
void TakeDamage(float Amount);

UFUNCTION(BlueprintPure, Category = \"Stats\")
float GetHealthPercent() const;

UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable, WithValidation)
void ServerRequestHeal();

UFUNCTION(BlueprintImplementableEvent)
void OnDeath();  // Implemented in Blueprint — no C++ body!

UFUNCTION(BlueprintNativeEvent)
void OnSpawn();  // Has C++ body (_Implementation suffix) + can be overridden in BP
```

### UFUNCTION Specifiers Reference

| Category | Specifiers |
|---|---|
| **Blueprint** | `BlueprintCallable` (can call from BP), `BlueprintPure` (no side effects, no exec pin), `BlueprintImplementableEvent` (BP-only body), `BlueprintNativeEvent` (C++ default + BP override) |
| **Network** | `Server` (client → server), `Client` (server → client), `NetMulticast` (server → all), `Reliable` (guaranteed delivery), `Unreliable` (fire-and-forget), `WithValidation` (server validates client request) |
| **Execution** | `Exec` (console command), `CallInEditor` (callable from editor details panel) |
| **Meta** | `Category = \"Name\"` (organize in BP node menu), `DisplayName = \"Name\"` (rename in BP), `Keywords` (search terms) |

---

## UPROPERTY() — Exposing Properties

```cpp
UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, BlueprintReadWrite, Category = \"Combat\")
float MaxHealth = 100.0f;

UPROPERTY(VisibleAnywhere, BlueprintReadOnly)
int32 CurrentAmmo;
```

### UPROPERTY Specifiers Reference

| Category | Specifiers |
|---|---|
| **Editor** | `EditAnywhere` (edit in BP + instances), `EditDefaultsOnly` (edit in class defaults), `EditInstanceOnly` (edit per-instance), `VisibleAnywhere` (read-only in editor) |
| **Blueprint** | `BlueprintReadWrite`, `BlueprintReadOnly` |
| **Network** | `Replicated` (replicate across network), `ReplicatedUsing=Func` (call OnRep) |
| **Memory** | `Transient` (not serialized), `SaveGame` (included in save games), `SkipSerialization` |
| **Misc** | `Category`, `AdvancedDisplay` (hidden behind \"Advanced\" dropdown), `meta=(...)` |

---

## What the Generated Code Actually Does

When you write `UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) float Health;`, UHT generates:

1. **Reflection data** — a `FProperty` entry that stores the name, type, offset, and metadata
2. **Serialization code** — `operator<<` that saves/loads Health with the object
3. **Garbage collection tracking** — if Health is a `TObjectPtr<UObject>`, the GC knows to follow it
4. **Editor integration** — the details panel knows to show Health as an editable float
5. **Network replication** — if marked `Replicated`, Health is compared and sent across the network

All of this is C++ code that you never write — UHT writes it for you.

---

## Common UHT Errors and What They Mean

| Error | Cause |
|---|---|
| \"Missing GENERATED_BODY()\" | You have `UCLASS()` but forgot `GENERATED_BODY()` in the class body |
| \"Unrecognized type\" | A type used in `UPROPERTY()` isn't a UHT-recognized type |
| \"Include 'X.generated.h' must be the last include\" | `.generated.h` must be the LAST `#include` in the header |
| \"Cannot find class\" | Forward-declared class needs `class` prefix: `TSubclassOf<class UMyClass>` |

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

UE macros (`UCLASS`, `UPROPERTY`, `UFUNCTION`) are instructions to a code generator. You write a class with these markers, and before compilation, a tool called UHT reads your headers and writes extra C++ code behind the scenes. That generated code is what lets your variables show up in the editor, your functions be called from Blueprints, and your data be saved and loaded. You never see this code — it's the hidden plumbing that makes Unreal's tools work.

### Technical

UHT processes header files in a pre-compilation pass, parsing UE macro annotations to generate reflection data (`UClass`/`UFunction`/`FProperty` objects), serialization operators, network replication descriptors, and GC reference graphs. `GENERATED_BODY()` (ObjectMacros.h:800) expands to a `friend` declaration of the compiled-in `FObjectInitializer` constructor and the `StaticClass()` static member. The generated `.gen.cpp` file contains `IMPLEMENT_CLASS`/`IMPLEMENT_FUNCTION` macros that register type metadata with UE's reflection system at static initialization time.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Write a UCLASS with UPROPERTY and UFUNCTION. What does GENERATED_BODY() actually generate? Look at the .generated.h file.
2. Why must .generated.h be the LAST include in your header?
3. What happens if you forget GENERATED_BODY()? What's the EXACT compiler error?
4. How does IMPLEMENT_CLASS register your class with UE at static initialization?

## See Also

- [[02 — Syntax & Types/08 — Preprocessor & Macros|Preprocessor]] (C++) — How #define and macros work in standard C++
- [[UE Inheritance & UObject System]] — How UE classes inherit and override functions
- [[Casting/01 — Cast Internals & Variants|Casting]] — How `Cast<T>()` uses UHT-generated type information
- [[Pointer Ecosystem]] — UPROPERTY with TObjectPtr vs raw pointers

00 \u2014 Casting Overview.content:
# Casting in Unreal Engine

> [!info] This is the overview. For the full content, read 01-04 in order.


> [!info] C++ Connection
> `Cast<T>()` replaces `dynamic_cast` in UE. Unlike `dynamic_cast` (50-200+ cycles via vtable walk), UE's `Cast<T> uses a bitwise AND on `EClassCastFlags` (1-2 cycles). See [[04 — OOP & Generics/03 — C++ Casting & RTTI|Casting & RTTI]] for the standard C++ versions.


In ordinary C++, you have three casts for pointer/reference types:

| Cast | Mechanism | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| `static_cast` | Compile-time; no runtime check | Zero |
| `dynamic_cast` | Compiler RTTI (`typeid`/vtable walk) | Expensive — linear in hierarchy depth |
| `reinterpret_cast` | Bit-level; no check at all | Zero (and zero safety) |

Unreal Engine replaces `dynamic_cast` with its own `Cast<T>()` template. This isn't a wrapper — it's a **custom RTTI system** that completely sidesteps the compiler's `dynamic_cast` machinery.

> [!summary] The One-Liner
> `Cast<T>(Obj)` uses UE's custom reflection system (bit-flag walk or `StaticClass()`) instead of C++ RTTI. It's faster, works in editor and cooked builds, and returns `nullptr` — never throws.

---

When you write:

```cpp
UCLASS()
class AMyActor : public AActor
{
    GENERATED_BODY()
};
```

UHT generates (approximately):

```cpp
// GENERATED_BODY expands to:
constexpr static EClassCastFlags StaticClassCastFlags() 
{ 
    return CASTCLASS_None; // or a unique flag if configured
}

// Inherited from DECLARE_CLASS:
static UClass* StaticClass();
constexpr static EClassCastFlags StaticClassCastFlags();
consteval static EClassCastFlags StaticAllClassCastFlags()
{
    return StaticClassCastFlags() | Super::StaticAllClassCastFlags();
}
```

For common engine types (AActor, APawn, etc.), Epic **manually assigns** cast flags in `Casts.h`:

```cpp
DECLARE_CAST_BY_FLAG(AActor)
DECLARE_CAST_BY_FLAG(APawn)
DECLARE_CAST_BY_FLAG(USceneComponent)
// ...
```

This expands to an explicit `TCastFlags_V<T> = CASTCLASS_AActor` specialization. Without this, `Cast<AActor> falls back to the `IsA`<T>`()` slow path — still correct, just slightly slower.

---

### For someone new to UE C++

Imagine you're holding a box labeled "Fruit." You want to know if it's specifically an Apple. You can:

1. **Rip it open without checking** (`static_cast<Apple*>`): If it's actually a banana, you have a mess (undefined behavior / crash).
2. **Use the official scanner** (`Cast<Apple>): It reads the box's barcode (cast flags / IsA) and either hands you an `Apple*` or nothing (`nullptr`). Costs a few CPU cycles. Always safe.
3. **Use a high-security scanner** (`CastChecked<Apple>`): In development, it blares an alarm if the box isn't an apple. In shipping (the store), it assumes the label is right and just hands you the box — fast but dangerous if wrong.

UE's `Cast<T>` is the scanner. It uses UE's own labeling system (not the compiler's), so it works identically in the editor and in the shipped game.

### For someone who knows C++

UE's `Cast<T> is a **policy-based static-dispatch type checker**. At compile time, `if constexpr` selects one of three paths based on:
1. Whether `TIsIInterface<From>` is true (interface source)
2. Whether `TCastFlags_V<To> != CASTCLASS_None` (bit-flag fast path)
3. The fallback `IsA<T>()` (inheritance chain walk via precomputed `StructBaseChainArray`)

Unlike `dynamic_cast`, which walks the vtable pointer to `type_info` and performs string comparisons, UE's system is purely integer-based: either a single bitwise AND or an O(1) array index lookup. The cost is constant regardless of hierarchy depth.

The `TCopyQualifiersFromTo_T` return type — implemented via `std::remove_cvref_t`-style type manipulation in `Core/Public/Templates/LosesQualifiersFromTo.h` — ensures that `const`/`volatile` qualifiers from the source are preserved on the result, something manual `static_cast` doesn't do automatically.

---

1. Why is Cast```<AActor*>`(Obj) faster than dynamic_cast`<AActor*>`(Obj)?
2. What happens when CastChecked fails in a shipping build?
3. When does Cast`<T>` use the bit-flag fast path vs the IsA slow path?
4. Write a Cast-like function for a non-UObject class using static_cast.

- [[04 — OOP & Generics/01 — Inheritance & Virtual Functions|Inheritance]] (C++) — Standard C++ virtual dispatch
- [[UE Inheritance & UObject System]] — UE class hierarchy and lifecycle

- `Cast<T> full implementation: `Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/Templates/Casts.h` (lines 88–167)
- `CastChecked` implementation: `Casts.h` (lines 196–277)
- `dynamic_cast` override: `Casts.h` (lines 534–594)
- `EClassCastFlags` enum: `Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/ObjectMacros.h` (lines 343–415)
- `DECLARE_CLASS` macro: `ObjectMacros.h` (lines 1895–1945)
- `HasAnyCastFlag` implementation: `Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Private/UObject/Class.cpp` (lines 586–589)
- `IsA<T> template: `Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/UObjectBaseUtility.h` (lines 632–657)
- `GetInterfaceAddress`: `UObjectBaseUtility.h` (line 675)
- `IsChildOf`: `Engine/Source/Runtime/CoreUObject/Public/UObject/Class.h` (lines 807–834)
- `TCopyQualifiersFromTo` / `TLosesQualifiersFromTo`: `Engine/Source/Runtime/Core/Public/Templates/LosesQualifiersFromTo.h` (lines 1–21)
- [[Explicit & Implicit Conversion]]
- [[06 — Bridge/01 — C++ vs UE|C++ vs UE]]

03 \u2014 When to Use & Pitfalls.content:
# When to Use and Pitfalls

> [!info] Part of the [[00 — Casting Overview|Casting]] learning path.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[02 — Interface Casting & References]] — understand all cast variants first.


### Decision Flowchart

```
Is the conversion known safe at compile time?
├─ YES (upcast, same type, void*)
│  └─ Use static_cast<T> — it's zero-cost, the compiler verifies correctness
├─ NO
│  ├─ Do you need the EXACT type (not a child)?
│  │  └─ Use ExactCast<T>
│  ├─ Do you have a UObject pointer and need runtime safety?
│  │  ├─ Are you 100% sure it's the right type?
│  │  │  └─ Use CastChecked<T> — validates in dev, strips to static_cast in shipping
│  │  ├─ Is null acceptable if the cast fails?
│  │  │  └─ Use Cast<T> — returns nullptr, check it
│  │  └─ Are you checking without actually converting?
│  │     └─ Use IsA<To>() — cheaper than Cast if you don't need the pointer
│  ├─ Are you casting to/from a UINTERFACE?
│  │  └─ Use Cast<T> — it handles GetInterfaceAddress automatically
│  └─ Is this NON-UE code (no UCLASS/GENERATED_BODY)?
│     └─ Use dynamic_cast<T> — UE's Cast won't work
```

### Concrete Rules

| Situation | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Upcast: `AActorDerived*` → `AActor*` | `static_cast` | Zero-cost; the compiler verifies it |
| Downcast: `AActor*` → `AActorDerived*` | `Cast<AActorDerived> | Runtime type check, returns `nullptr` |
| Interface query: `UObject*` → `IInterface*` | `Cast<IInterface> | Uses `GetInterfaceAddress` |
| Check without converting | `Obj->IsA<To>()` | Cheaper — doesn't construct result pointer |
| Must succeed; dev guard | `CastChecked<To>` | Logs error in dev; strips to static_cast in shipping |
| Exact type match | `ExactCast<To> | `GetClass() == StaticClass()` |
| Non-UObject hierarchies | `dynamic_cast` | UE's `Cast` doesn't work here |
| Flat C-style pointer conversion | `(T*)ptr` | ⚠️ Don't. Just don't. |

---

### 1. "I Cast<>'d and didn't check null"

```cpp
// ❌ CRASH BUG
APlayerController* PC = Cast<APlayerController>(GetOwner());
PC->SetViewTarget(NewTarget); // nullptr if GetOwner() is not a PC

// ✅ CORRECT
if (APlayerController* PC = Cast<APlayerController>(GetOwner()))
{
    PC->SetViewTarget(NewTarget);
}
```

> [!danger] Why UE's Cast Never Throws
> Unlike `dynamic_cast` with references (`dynamic_cast<T&>(ref)` throws `std::bad_cast`), UE's `Cast<T>()` **always returns `nullptr`** on failure. There is no reference version. This means every `Cast<T>()` call site must null-check the result. The pattern `if (auto* X = Cast<T>(Y))` is idiomatic UE C++.

### 2. Using static_cast for Downcasts

```cpp
// ❌ COMPILES, WRONG AT RUNTIME
AActor* SomeActor = GetOwner(); // Actually an APawn
ACharacter* Ch = static_cast<ACharacter*>(SomeActor); // Undefined behavior

// ✅ CORRECT
ACharacter* Ch = Cast<ACharacter>(SomeActor); // Returns nullptr if wrong type
```

`static_cast` on a wrong type is undefined behavior. The pointer is silently wrong, and accessing it will corrupt memory or crash. Always use `Cast<T>()` for downcasts.

### 3. Casting Across Worlds / Garbage Collection

```cpp
// ❌ UNSAFE
AActor* A = MyWeakPtr.Get();     // Might be garbage-collected between Get() and Cast
AMyActor* M = Cast<AMyActor>(A); // Could be dangling

// ✅ SAFER
if (MyWeakPtr.IsValid())
{
    AMyActor* M = Cast<AMyActor>(MyWeakPtr.Get());
    if (M) { /* use M */ }
}
```

> [!warning] Cast Does NOT Pin Objects
> `Cast<T>()` doesn't prevent garbage collection. If you only have a raw pointer (no `UPROPERTY`/`TObjectPtr`), the object can be GC'd between the `Cast` call and your use of the result.

### 4. Editor-Only Behavior Differences

```cpp
// In editor with -game: CastChecked logs an error and continues (non-fatal)
// In shipping: CastChecked is a raw static_cast — no error, no null, UB if wrong
```

`CastChecked` behaves **differently in editor vs shipping**. In the editor, a failed cast logs an error but the code continues (the function returns `nullptr` — yes, even though it's decorated `FUNCTION_NON_NULL_RETURN`). In shipping, all checks are stripped and it becomes a raw pointer cast.

### 5. Cast`<T>` with UStruct (Non-UObject)

```cpp
// ❌ Cast doesn't work on UStructs at all
FVector* Vec = Cast<FVector>(SomePtr); // static_assert fails — not a UObject

// UStructs don't have GetClass() or CastFlags — they use the FStruct hierarchy
// Use IsChildOf on UScriptStruct for struct type checking
```

`Cast<T>()` only works on `UObject`-derived types and `UINTERFACE` types. `UStruct` (value types like `FVector`, `FHitResult`) can't use `Cast`<>`.

### 6. Casting from void*

```cpp
// ❌ WRONG
void* RawPtr = SomeUObject;
AActor* A = Cast<AActor>(RawPtr); // static_assert fails: void is not a UObject

// ✅ CORRECT
AActor* A = static_cast<AActor*>(static_cast<UObject*>(RawPtr));
// But prefer: use GetTypedOuter or similar if possible
```

---



## Exercises

1. **Cast vs IsA decision:** You're iterating 1000 actors every frame looking for a specific type. Write two versions: one with  inside the loop, one with  first. Benchmark both. When does checking  first beat direct ?

2. **Null dereference trap:** Write . What happens if Cast returns null? Why is this the #1 Cast bug in UE? Write a  version and a safe version with null guard.

3. **Cross-world Cast:** You have a reference to an actor that was destroyed last frame.  returns true (pending kill hasn't flushed). What does  return? What about ?

4. **Template Cast wrapper:** Write a  that returns null if the Cast fails and logs a warning with the object name. Add a  variant that calls  on failure.

5. **Performance regression case:** Find a UE forum post about Cast performance. Common mistake: calling  on  in Tick. Why is  wasteful? What should you use instead? (Hint:  comparison.)

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Cast is the right tool 90% of the time — it's safe (returns null), fast (1-2 cycles), and clear. But 10% of the time, you want the other tools:  when null is a programmer error that should crash immediately (engine startup, BeginPlay);  when you need the exact type, not a child pretending to be its parent (serialization, type identity checks);  when you just need a boolean answer without the pointer (filtering, validation). The #1 pitfall is dereferencing without checking null — Cast can return null, and  crashes.

### Technical
Cast performance characteristic: bitwise AND on  (1-2 cycles).  is the same operation minus the cast — slightly faster if you don't need the pointer.  adds  which crashes in debug/shipping with  level — use only when the cast MUST succeed (invariants). Common performance traps: Cast inside tight loops (use  +  caching), Cast on  (use , no cast needed), Cast before  (Cast can crash on garbage pointers).

## See Also

- [[Pointer Ecosystem]] — TObjectPtr, TWeakObjectPtr, and GC safety
- [[UE Inheritance & UObject System]] — UPROPERTY and GC reachability

GAS Networking & Prediction.content:
# GAS Networking & Prediction

## The Core Problem GAS Solves
> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[Network Roles]], [[RPCs in Unreal Engine]], and basic GAS concepts (see the \"What You Need to Know About GAS\" section in this note).


> [!note] What You Need to Know About GAS
> GAS (Gameplay Ability System) is UE's ability framework. Key concepts:
> - **GameplayAbility:** A single ability (punch, fireball, jump) with activation logic
> - **AbilitySystemComponent (ASC):** The component managing abilities on a character
> - **GameplayEffect:** A temporary or permanent modifier (damage, heal, buff, debuff)
> - **GameplayTag:** A hierarchical label (e.g., `Ability.Magic.Fire`) for categorization
> - **PredictionKey:** A unique ID correlating a client's predicted activation with the server's response
> 
> This note focuses on the NETWORKING layer of GAS. For GAS fundamentals, see the UE Core folder.


In a networked fighting game, when a player presses the \"punch\" button, several things need to happen:

1. The client needs to **feel responsive** — the punch should start immediately
2. The server needs to **validate** the punch — is the player allowed to punch right now?
3. The server needs to **apply the punch** — deal damage, apply knockback
4. Other clients need to **see the punch** — play the animation on the opponent's screen

GAS solves this with a **prediction-first, server-authoritative** model. The client predicts the ability will succeed, starts playing it immediately, and the server validates afterward. If the server disagrees, the client rolls back.

> [!summary] The GAS Networking Flow
> Client predicts → Client sends Server RPC → Server validates → Server executes → Server replicates state back → Client receives RepNotifies → Client reconciles
>
> Every step uses either an RPC or a RepNotify. There are no other mechanisms. Understanding this flow is understanding GAS networking.

```
Source: Engine/Plugins/Runtime/GameplayAbilities/Source/GameplayAbilities/
```

---

## The Full Ability Activation Flow — Step by Step

Let's trace a complete ability activation from button press to all clients seeing the result:

```
1. CLIENT — Button Press
   Player presses \"Fireball\" key.
   UAbilitySystemComponent::TryActivateAbility(Handle)
   
2. CLIENT — Local Prediction (IMMEDIATE)
   CanActivateAbility() passes locally
   Ability is predicted — starts playing locally
   PredictionKey is generated (unique ID for this activation attempt)
   Animation starts, cooldown timer starts — player sees instant response
   
3. CLIENT — Server RPC
   CallServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey)
   This is the generated thunk for:
   UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable, WithValidation)
   void ServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey);
   
4. NETWORK — RPC travels to server
   
5. SERVER — Validation
   ServerTryActivateAbility_Validate(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey)
   Returns true (GAS defers validation to the ability itself)
   
6. SERVER — Execution
   ServerTryActivateAbility_Implementation(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey)
   → InternalServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey, nullptr)
   → Server checks: is ability granted? is it on cooldown? does it cost resources?
   → If valid: activates the ability on the server
   → Applies GameplayEffects (damage, cooldown, cost)
   
7. SERVER — Replication
   Server's ASC replicates state changes:
   - Activated abilities list → Replicated property
   - GameplayEffect changes → Replicated GameplayTagCountContainer
   - Attribute changes → Replicated via OnRep on AttributeSet
   
8. CLIENT — Receives Replication
   Client receives replicated state:
   - OnRep_ActivateAbilities() fires — client reconciles predicted vs actual
   - GameplayEffect replication updates tags/attributes
   - If server rejected the ability: ClientActivateAbilityFailed() RPC is sent back
   
9. OTHER CLIENTS — See The Result
   Other clients receive:
   - Replicated animation montage → OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage() fires
   - The opponent's ASC updates → health bar changes, hit reaction plays
```

**The key insight:** The client's prediction and the server's execution both run the SAME ability code. The client predicts what WILL happen. The server decides what ACTUALLY happens. If they match (which they should, for non-random abilities), the client's prediction seamlessly becomes the real state. If they differ, the client rolls back.

---

## GAS RPCs — Complete Catalog

### Server RPCs (Client → Server)

```
Source: AbilitySystemComponent.h
```

| RPC | Purpose | Lines |
|---|---|---|
| `ServerTryActivateAbility` | Client requests ability activation | 1250 |
| `ServerTryActivateAbilityWithEventData` | Same, with gameplay event data | 1254 |
| `ServerSetReplicatedTargetData` | Client sends targeting data to server | 1311 |
| `ServerEndAbility` | Client requests to end an ability | 1554 |
| `ServerSetReplicatedEvent` | Client sends a generic ability event | 1558 |
| `ServerAbilityRPCBatch` | Batched RPC for multiple ability calls | 1618 |

```cpp
// AbilitySystemComponent.h:1250-1254
UFUNCTION(Server, reliable, WithValidation)
void ServerTryActivateAbility(FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle AbilityToActivate, 
    bool InputPressed, FPredictionKey PredictionKey);

UFUNCTION(Server, reliable, WithValidation)
void ServerTryActivateAbilityWithEventData(FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle AbilityToActivate, 
    bool InputPressed, FPredictionKey PredictionKey, FGameplayEventData TriggerEventData);
```

**The implementation** (AbilitySystemComponent.cpp:2016-2034):
```cpp
void UAbilitySystemComponent::ServerTryActivateAbility_Implementation(
    FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle Handle, bool InputPressed, FPredictionKey PredictionKey)
{
    InternalServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, PredictionKey, nullptr);
}

bool UAbilitySystemComponent::ServerTryActivateAbility_Validate(
    FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle Handle, bool InputPressed, FPredictionKey PredictionKey)
{
    return true;  // GAS defers validation to InternalServerTryActivateAbility
}
```

**Why validation returns true?** GAS validates inside `InternalServerTryActivateAbility`, not in the `_Validate` function. The validation checks things that require game state (cooldowns, resource costs, tags) which are only available inside the ability activation code. The `_Validate` function just ensures the handle and prediction key are well-formed.

### Client RPCs (Server → Client)

| RPC | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ClientTryActivateAbility` | Server tells client to activate an ability (for server-initiated activations) |
| `ClientActivateAbilityFailed` | Server tells client their prediction was wrong — roll back |
| `ClientSetReplicatedEvent` | Server sends a generic ability event to client |
| `ClientAbilityRPCBatch` | Batched RPC for multiple ability calls |

```cpp
// Client receives server-initiated activation
void UAbilitySystemComponent::ClientTryActivateAbility_Implementation(FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle Handle)
{
    FGameplayAbilitySpec* Spec = FindAbilitySpecFromHandle(Handle);
    if (!Spec)
    {
        // Handle can arrive before the spec on the same frame
        // Queue it for when the spec arrives
        FPendingAbilityInfo AbilityInfo;
        AbilityInfo.Handle = Handle;
        PendingServerActivatedAbilities.AddUnique(AbilityInfo);
        return;
    }
    InternalTryActivateAbility(Handle);
}
```

---

## The Prediction System — Prediction Keys

The Prediction Key is the mechanism that connects a client's prediction to the server's response. Without it, the client wouldn't know which activation the server is confirming or rejecting.

```
Source: GameplayAbilities/Source/GameplayAbilities/Public/GameplayPrediction.h
```

**How it works:**

```
1. CLIENT generates PredictionKey
   FPredictionKey Key = FPredictionKey::CreateNewPredictionKey(ASC);
   // Key.Current = unique ID for this activation
   // Key.Base = the last acknowledged prediction (for chaining)

2. CLIENT predicts the ability
   ASC->TryActivateAbility(Handle, &Key);
   // The ability runs locally with this prediction key
   // Any GameplayEffects applied during prediction are tagged with this key
   // \"These effects are PREDICTED — they might be rolled back\"

3. CLIENT sends RPC with the key
   ServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, Key);
   // The key travels with the RPC so the server knows which prediction this is

4. SERVER executes with the key
   InternalServerTryActivateAbility(Handle, InputPressed, Key, nullptr);
   // Server runs the ability with the SAME prediction key
   // GameplayEffects applied on server also get tagged with this key
   // Server replicates: \"Key X is now confirmed\"
   
5. CLIENT receives confirmation
   // Server's replicated state arrives
   // Client sees: \"Key X effects are now AUTHORITATIVE\"
   // Removes the \"predicted\" tag from those effects
   // If the server rejected the ability: ClientActivateAbilityFailed(Handle, Key.Current)
```

**The prediction key enables:**
- **Reconciliation:** The client knows which prediction to confirm or roll back
- **Effect deduplication:** The client doesn't double-apply effects (once from prediction, once from replication) because they share the same key
- **Chained predictions:** `Key.Base` allows abilities to predict subsequent activations before the first is confirmed

---

## GAS RepNotifies — The Replication Side

GAS uses RepNotifies extensively for state that must be reactive on clients:

### OnRep_ActivateAbilities

```cpp
// AbilitySystemComponent.h:1667
UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing = OnRep_ActivateAbilities, BlueprintReadOnly, Transient)
FGameplayAbilitySpecContainer ActivatableAbilities;

// AbilitySystemComponent.h:1722
virtual void OnRep_ActivateAbilities();
```

**What it does:** When the server adds/removes abilities (e.g., granting a new ability from a GameplayEffect), this RepNotify fires on clients. The client updates its local ability list to match.

**Why it matters:** Without this, the client wouldn't know it has a new ability until it tries to use it and the server rejects it. This is the mechanism that keeps the client's ability bar in sync with the server.

### OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage

```cpp
// AbilitySystemComponent.h:1777
UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage)
FGameplayAbilityRepAnimMontage ReplicatedAnimMontageInfo;
```

**What it does:** When an ability plays a montage on the server, the montage information replicates to clients. `OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage` plays the same montage on all clients so everyone sees the animation.

**Why a RepNotify and not an RPC?** Montages are state (they have a duration, current position, play rate). A RepNotify ensures late-joining clients see the montage at the correct position. An RPC would only fire once and late joiners would miss it entirely.

### OnRep_OwningActor

```cpp
// AbilitySystemComponent.h:1488-1504
UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing = OnRep_OwningActor)
TObjectPtr<AActor> ReplicatedOwningActor;

void OnRep_OwningActor();
```

**What it does:** When the owning actor is set on the server (during `InitAbilityActorInfo`), this replicates to the client so the client knows which actor this ASC belongs to.

### AttributeSet OnRep Functions

AttributeSets use RepNotifies per-attribute for health, mana, stamina, etc.:

```cpp
// AttributeSet.h:396 — documentation example
// void UMyHealthSet::OnRep_Health(const FGameplayAttributeData& OldValue)
```

Each replicated attribute gets its own `OnRep` that fires when the server updates it. This is how the client's health bar stays in sync.

---

## Prediction — What Gets Predicted vs What Doesn't

| System | Predicted? | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Ability activation** | ✅ Yes | Client runs ability locally, server confirms/denies via RPC |
| **GameplayEffects applied by abilities** | ✅ Yes | Tagged with prediction key, reconciled on confirmation |
| **Attribute changes from GEs** | ✅ Yes | Predicted locally, overwritten by server replication |
| **GameplayCues (visuals/sounds)** | ✅ Yes | Triggered locally on prediction, server-triggered on confirmation |
| **Cooldowns** | ✅ Yes | Predicted locally so the UI updates immediately |
| **Costs (mana, stamina)** | ✅ Yes | Predicted locally, reconciled with server values |
| **Target data (aiming)** | ✅ Yes | Client predicts targeting, sends to server via `ServerSetReplicatedTargetData` |
| **Ability task state** | ⚠️ Partial | Some tasks predict, some don't — depends on the task |
| **Montage position** | ❌ No | Replicated from server via `OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage` |

**The prediction rule:** Anything the player would notice a delay on gets predicted. Anything only other players need to see is server-driven.

---

## The Prediction Window

Between when the client predicts an ability and when the server confirms it, the client is in a **prediction window.** During this window:

1. GameplayEffects are tagged as \"predicted\" — they show their effects but can be rolled back
2. The client can't activate another ability that depends on this one's results (chained predictions use `Key.Base`)
3. If the player disconnects during the window, the prediction is discarded — the server's state is the truth

The window closes when:
- The server confirms the prediction (client receives replicated state matching the prediction key)
- The server rejects the prediction (`ClientActivateAbilityFailed` RPC)

---

## Common GAS Networking Mistakes

### 1. Calling Server RPCs Directly Instead Of Using The Generated Thunk

```cpp
// ❌ DON'T — bypasses prediction, local execution, etc.
ServerTryActivateAbility_Implementation(Handle, true, Key);

// ✅ Use the public API which handles prediction + RPC internally
TryActivateAbility(Handle);
// This calls: local prediction → CallServerTryActivateAbility (thunk) → RPC
```

### 2. Not Understanding That Effects Are Applied Twice

```cpp
// On client during prediction:
// GameplayEffect is applied with PredictionKey
// Health bar updates to show predicted damage

// On server:
// Same GameplayEffect is applied with same PredictionKey
// Server replicates the effect → client receives it

// Client: \"I already have an effect with this PredictionKey\"
// Client deduplicates — doesn't double-apply
// Client: \"This effect is now AUTHORITATIVE\" (removes predicted tag)
```

**You don't need to handle this.** GAS deduplicates automatically using the prediction key. If you're seeing double-application, you're probably applying effects outside the prediction system.

### 3. Relying On Server State During Prediction

```cpp
// ❌ During client prediction, the server's replicated state hasn't arrived yet
void UMyAbility::ActivateAbility(...)
{
    // ActorInfo->AbilitySystemComponent->GetGameplayAttributeValue(HealthAttribute)
    // This is the CLIENT'S value — it might be out of date
}

// ✅ Use predicted values during prediction
// GAS handles this internally for most cases
```

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple

When you press \"punch,\" your game doesn't wait for the server's permission. It throws the punch immediately (prediction). Behind the scenes, it sends a message to the server: \"I'm punching.\" The server checks: \"Are you allowed to punch? Are you stunned? Is punch on cooldown?\" If yes, the server makes it official and tells everyone. Your client hears back: \"Your punch was confirmed\" — and since you already started the animation, everything transitions seamlessly. If the server says no, your client cancels the punch (rollback).

### Technical

GAS implements client-side prediction using `FPredictionKey` as a correlation token between local prediction and server confirmation. The client calls `TryActivateAbility()`, which generates a prediction key, runs the ability locally (applying predicted GameplayEffects with the key), and sends `ServerTryActivateAbility` RPC with the same key. The server validates via `InternalServerTryActivateAbility` (not the `_Validate` function, which returns `true` unconditionally — AbilitySystemComponent.cpp:2023), executes the ability, and replicates the results. The client receives replicated effects matched by prediction key, reconciles predicted vs authoritative state, and handles rejection via `ClientActivateAbilityFailed` RPC. RepNotifies (`OnRep_ActivateAbilities`, `OnRep_ReplicatedAnimMontage`) handle state that cannot be predicted — ability lists and montage positions that must match the server exactly.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Trace a predicted ability activation from button press to server confirmation.
2. What is a `FPredictionKey` for? Why does the server need the client's PredictionKey?
3. What happens when the server's execution differs from the client's prediction?
4. Write a `_Validate` for a GAS Server RPC. What should it check? What NOT?

## See Also

- [[RPCs in Unreal Engine]] — How Server/Client/NetMulticast RPCs work
- [[RepNotifies & OnRep]] — How GAS replicates state changes
- [[Network Roles]] — Why only AutonomousProxy can call Server RPCs
- [[PushModel & Dirty Marking]] — How the ASC marks replicated properties dirty
- `Engine/Plugins/Runtime/GameplayAbilities/Source/GameplayAbilities/Public/AbilitySystemComponent.h`
- `Engine/Plugins/Runtime/GameplayAbilities/Source/GameplayAbilities/Private/AbilitySystemComponent.cpp`

Iris Filtering & Prioritization.content:
# Iris Filtering & Prioritization

## The Core Problem
> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[Iris Replication System Overview]] — understand Iris architecture first. [[Network Roles]] — understand which connections need which data.

> [!info] Math Connection
> Grid filter uses 2D spatial hashing — O(1) per pair vs O(n) for distance checks. Distance prioritization uses `FVector::DistSquared`. See [[00 — Collision Detection Overview|Collision Detection]] for spatial partitioning.


Once Iris knows an object has dirty properties (PushModel), it faces two questions:

1. **Who needs this?** — Not every connection needs every object. Sending a monster on the other side of the map to a player who can't see it wastes bandwidth.
2. **How urgent is this?** — Even among objects a connection does need, some are more important than others. The player's own health bar update matters more than a distant tree swaying in the wind.

> [!summary] The Path of an Object
> Dirty → Filter (does Connection #4 need this?) → Prioritize (how urgent for Connection #4?) → Serialize → Send
> 
> Each stage can eliminate the object. If it fails filtering, it stops. If its priority is too low, it may be deferred. Only objects that pass ALL stages get serialized and sent.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/Filtering/
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/Prioritization/
```

---

## Stage 1: Filtering — \"Does This Connection Need This Object?\"

### The Filter Pipeline

Filters run as a **chain**. Each filter gets a chance to say ALLOW or DENY for each (object, connection) pair. The first DENY stops the chain — later filters are skipped for that pair. If all filters ALLOW, the object proceeds to prioritization.

```
(Connection #4, Object 0x7F3A)
   │
   ▼
Filter 1: AlwaysRelevant?      → Not always relevant. Continue.
   │
   ▼
Filter 2: GridFilter           → Connection is 500m away, cull distance = 200m.
                                  → DENY. Stop.
                                  → Object 0x7F3A will NOT be sent to Connection #4.
```

**Why the chain matters:** You can stack filters. An object might need to pass BOTH a spatial check (is it nearby?) AND a gameplay check (is it the right team?) AND a state check (is it currently active?). Each filter handles one concern. Together they form a complete relevancy system.

### Built-in Filters

| Filter | What It Does | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **AlwaysRelevantNetObjectFilter** | Object passes for ALL connections, always | `AGameState`, `AGameMode` — things every player needs |
| **FilterOutNetObjectFilter** | Object passes for NO connections | Dormant actors, destroyed-but-not-yet-garbage-collected objects |
| **NetObjectGridFilter** | Spatial grid: only nearby connections | Most AActor types — enemies, pickups, other players |
| **NetObjectParentRelevancyGridFilter** | Same as grid, but child components inherit parent actor's relevancy | Actor components on grid-filtered actors |
| **NetObjectConnectionFilter** | Per-connection override list | Exceptions — \"this specific connection should always/never see this object\" |

### The Grid Filter — How Spatial Relevancy Actually Works

The grid filter is the workhorse. Here's how it operates:

```
1. WORLD IS DIVIDED INTO A GRID
   Each cell is typically 50-100 meters across.
   An object at position (1234, 5678) → cell (12, 56).
   A connection at position (1250, 5700) → cell (12, 57).

2. EACH OBJECT REGISTERS ITS CELL
   When an actor begins replicating, the grid filter records its cell.
   When the actor moves to a new cell, the filter updates the mapping.
   Cost: O(1) — just checking if world position / cellSize changed.

3. EACH CONNECTION HAS A VIEW
   The connection's PlayerController provides:
   - ViewLocation (where the camera/pawn is)
   - CullDistance (how far can this connection see?)
   These map to a set of grid cells within the cull distance.

4. FILTER CHECK
   For each (object, connection) pair:
   Is the object's cell within the connection's visible cell set?
   Yes → ALLOW. No → DENY.
   Cost: O(1) — just checking if cell index is in a set.
```

**Why a grid and not per-object distance checks?** Computing distance between every object and every connection is O(objects × connections). For 1000 objects and 50 connections, that's 50,000 distance calculations per frame. A grid lookup is O(1) per pair — the object's cell index is precomputed, the connection's visible cells are precomputed, and the check is a set membership test. That's the difference between a server handling 20 players and 200 players.

> [!tip] The Cull Distance
> Cull distance is per-actor, set in the actor's networking settings. A tree might have 50m cull distance. A mountain might have 500m. A bullet might have 2000m (you need to see it coming). The grid filter uses the connection's view cull distance as an upper bound — even if the tree says 50m, if the connection can only see 200m, the tree is still culled at 200m. The tighter of the two wins.

---

## Stage 2: Prioritization — \"How Urgent Is This Right Now?\"

Once an object passes filtering for a connection, it enters the prioritization stage. Here, Iris assigns a **floating-point priority score** to each (object, connection) pair. Higher priority objects get serialized first. Lower priority objects may be deferred to a later frame if bandwidth runs out.

### How Priority Scores Work

Priority scores are **relative** — there's no absolute \"good\" or \"bad\" score. Iris sorts all objects by priority for each connection, then serializes from highest to lowest. When the frame's bandwidth budget is exhausted, remaining objects wait for the next frame.

**Example:** A connection can receive 50 objects per frame. 80 objects are relevant. Iris sorts them by priority: the player's own pawn (0.98), a nearby enemy attacking them (0.85), a distant enemy (0.23), a swaying tree (0.05). Iris serializes the top 50. The bottom 30 wait until the next frame, when they might have higher priority (the enemy got closer).

**The critical insight:** A bandwidth-limited server doesn't skip objects — it **defers** them. The tree that got priority 0.05 today will get priority 0.05 again tomorrow. If nothing more important comes along, it eventually gets sent. This is fundamentally different from filtering (which permanently blocks objects for a connection).

### Built-in Prioritizers

| Prioritizer | How It Computes Priority | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **SphereNetObjectPrioritizer** | Priority = 1 / distance from connection view. Closer = higher. | Open-world games where distance is the primary factor |
| **SphereWithOwnerBoostNetObjectPrioritizer** | Same as sphere, but objects owned by the connection get a multiplier (typically 2x) | Games where the player's own stuff should always update fast |
| **FieldOfViewNetObjectPrioritizer** | Priority by angular distance from view center. Objects in front of the camera > objects behind. | First-person shooters — what you're looking at matters more than what's behind you |
| **LocationBasedNetObjectPrioritizer** | Priority by world location without distance falloff | When you need explicit per-location priority (objectives, capture points) |
| **NetObjectCountLimiter** | Caps the total number of objects replicated per frame. Beyond the cap → priority 0. | Preventing burst overload when a player teleports into a crowded area |

### SphereWithOwnerBoost — The Default Prioritizer in Detail

Most games use `SphereWithOwnerBoost`. Here's its formula:

```
Raw Priority = BasePriority / Distance(object, connection view)

If object is owned by this connection:
    Priority = Raw Priority × OwnerBoostMultiplier (default: 2.0)
Else:
    Priority = Raw Priority
```

**Walkthrough example — three objects, one connection:**

| Object | Distance | Owned? | Raw Priority | After Boost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Player's own pawn | 0m (same location) | Yes | 1.0 / 0.01 = 100.0 | 200.0 |
| Nearby enemy | 10m | No | 1.0 / 10 = 0.10 | 0.10 |
| Distant pickup | 200m | No | 1.0 / 200 = 0.005 | 0.005 |

The player's pawn always wins. Even if the enemy is closer than the pawn (impossible — pawn is at 0m), the boost ensures the pawn's state changes arrive first.

**Why the boost matters:** During a death/respawn sequence, the player's pawn might briefly be far from their camera (spectating). Without the boost, the pawn's priority would drop and its replication would be deferred. With the boost, it stays prioritized through the transition.

### NetObjectCountLimiter — Preventing Bandwidth Floods

When a player teleports into a new area, suddenly 100 objects become relevant. Without a limiter, Iris tries to serialize all 100 immediately — blowing the bandwidth budget and causing packet loss.

```cpp
// The limiter says: \"Max 50 objects per connection per frame\"
// 100 objects want to replicate → top 50 by priority get sent
// Bottom 50 wait until next frame
// Over several frames, all 100 eventually get sent
```

This is a **soft cap**, not a hard block. Objects aren't permanently skipped — they're just queued. This prevents the \"teleport lag spike\" while still eventually delivering everything.

---

## The Per-Connection View — Where Filtering and Prioritization Get Their Data

Both systems need to know where the player is. The connection's `PlayerController` provides this each frame:

```
Source: Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/ReplicationView.h
```cpp
struct FReplicationView
{
    FVector ViewLocation;     // Camera or pawn location
    FVector ViewDirection;    // Camera forward vector
    float CullDistance;       // Maximum distance to consider
};
```

**What uses what:**
- `ViewLocation` → Grid filter (which cells are nearby?), Sphere prioritizer (how far is the object?)
- `ViewDirection` → FOV prioritizer (is the object in front of or behind the camera?)
- `CullDistance` → Grid filter upper bound (objects beyond this distance are never relevant)

The view is updated every frame from the `PlayerController`. If the player is spectating, the view comes from the spectated pawn. If the player is in a vehicle, the view comes from the vehicle. The system doesn't care where the numbers come from — it just needs a location and direction.

---

## Filtering vs Dormancy — Two Different Tools

Confusing these is common. They solve different problems:

| | Filtering | Dormancy |
|---|---|---|
| **What it does** | Decides per-connection relevancy dynamically | Freezes replication globally for an actor |
| **When it changes** | Every frame (as objects and players move) | On explicit state changes (wake/sleep events) |
| **Granularity** | Per-connection — Alice sees it, Bob doesn't | Global — nobody sees it until woken |
| **Use case** | \"This enemy is too far for Bob to see\" | \"This completed quest item never changes — stop checking it\" |
| **Iris mechanism** | `UNetObjectFilter` pipeline | `ENetDormancy` (unchanged from legacy system) |

**You can use both together.** An actor can be dormant (not checked at all) AND when awake, use grid filtering (only nearby connections see it). Dormancy eliminates the object from consideration entirely. Filtering reduces its audience. They stack.

---

## Custom Filters and Prioritizers — When To Write Your Own

Both systems are designed to be extended. You create a subclass and register it.

### When To Write a Custom Filter

Write a custom filter when spatial proximity isn't enough:

```cpp
// Example: \"Only players on the same team can see this objective marker\"
class UTeamRelevancyFilter : public UNetObjectFilter
{
    virtual void Filter(FNetObjectFilteringParams& Params) override
    {
        // For each connection, check if they're on the same team as the object's owner
        // If not → DENY
    }
};
```

Other good candidates:
- Gameplay-phase filtering (\"only show boss health bar during boss fight\")
- Frustum culling (add on top of grid for even tighter culling)
- LOD-based relevancy (\"don't replicate detailed state for objects beyond LOD1 distance\")

### When To Write a Custom Prioritizer

Write a custom prioritizer when distance isn't the best urgency metric:

```cpp
// Example: \"Enemies shooting at the player get priority over patrolling enemies\"
class UThreatPrioritizer : public UNetObjectPrioritizer
{
    virtual void Prioritize(FNetObjectPrioritizationParams& Params) override
    {
        // Boost priority for enemies whose target is this connection
        // Lower priority for idle/patrolling enemies
    }
};
```
