Other good candidates:
- Team-based priority (\"teammate positions update faster than enemy positions\")
- Combat-state priority (\"in-combat NPCs > out-of-combat NPCs\")
- Player-focus priority (\"whatever the player is aiming at gets priority\")

---

## The View Update — How It All Connects

Every frame, for each connection:

```
1. PlayerController updates FReplicationView
   - ViewLocation = pawn location
   - ViewDirection = camera forward
   - CullDistance = player's view distance setting

2. Iris reads all views for all connections

3. For each connection:
   - Grid filter: compute visible cell set from ViewLocation + CullDistance
   - FOV prioritizer: compute angular priorities from ViewDirection
   - Sphere prioritizer: compute distance priorities from ViewLocation

4. For each dirty object:
   - Check against connection's visible cells (filtering)
   - If passes, compute priority score (prioritization)
   - Insert into sorted priority queue

5. Serialize from highest priority down until bandwidth budget exhausted
```

**This all happens on the server.** Clients don't run filters or prioritizers — they just receive whatever the server sends. The server is the gatekeeper.

---

## Performance — Before and After (With Concrete Numbers)

| Scenario | Without Filtering | With Grid Filter | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,000 objects, 1 connection at center | 1,000 objects considered | ~20 objects in nearby cells | 50x fewer |
| 1,000 objects, 50 connections | 50,000 (object, connection) pairs | ~1,000 pairs in relevant cells | 50x fewer |
| 5,000 objects, 100 connections (open world MMO) | 500,000 pairs | ~10,000 pairs | 50x fewer |

> [!tip] The Real Win Isn't Bandwidth — It's CPU
> Filtering a (object, connection) pair with a cell index check costs ~5 CPU cycles. Serializing a full actor's properties costs ~200-500 cycles. Filtering eliminates the expensive path for 95%+ of objects. In the MMO example, that's 490,000 pairs you DON'T serialize. At 200 cycles each, that's ~98 million cycles saved per frame — nearly 10ms on a 3GHz server core.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple (The Newspaper Analogy)

You run a newspaper delivery service for a city of 100,000 people.

**Filtering** is deciding which sections each person gets. The sports fan doesn't need the cooking section. The person on the other side of the city doesn't need the local news from your neighborhood. You only deliver what each person actually needs.

**Prioritization** is deciding who gets their paper first when you have limited delivery trucks. The mayor gets theirs at 6 AM. The person who tipped you last Christmas gets theirs at 6:30. Everyone else gets theirs by 8 AM. Nobody gets skipped — but some get served faster.

In Iris: filtering = \"does this connection need this object at all?\" Prioritization = \"of the objects they need, which should I send first?\"

### Technical

Iris implements a two-stage, per-connection pipeline for bandwidth allocation. Stage 1 (filtering) is a chain of `UNetObjectFilter` subclasses evaluated in registration order. Each filter's `Filter()` method receives the connection's current `FReplicationView` and the object's registered location, returning `ALLOW` or `DENY`. The grid filter achieves O(1) per-pair evaluation by precomputing both object cell indices and connection visible cell sets, with cell membership checked via bit array lookup.

Stage 2 (prioritization) runs `UNetObjectPrioritizer::Prioritize()` for each surviving (object, connection) pair, producing a `float` score. The `SphereWithOwnerBoost` prioritizer uses inverse-distance weighting (`BasePriority / Distance`) with an ownership multiplier, while the `NetObjectCountLimiter` caps the per-frame replication set size by zeroing priority for objects beyond the cap. Scores are inserted into a per-connection priority queue; serialization drains from highest to lowest until the bandwidth budget is consumed. Deferred objects remain queued and are reconsidered in subsequent frames with updated priority scores.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Write a custom UNetObjectFilter subclass that only replicates actors whose owner is on the same team as the connection. Override Filter(). Why would you need this instead of the grid filter?
2. How does `SphereWithOwnerBoost` work? Why boost owned objects?
3. Write a custom `UNetObjectFilter` that only replicates to connections with a specific tag.
4. What happens if an object passes filtering but has priority 0?

## See Also

- [[Iris Replication System Overview]] — Where filtering and prioritization fit in the pipeline
- [[Network Roles]] — How ownership (used by SphereWithOwnerBoost) is determined
- [[PushModel & Dirty Marking]] — How objects become dirty before reaching this stage
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/Filtering/` — All filter implementations
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/Prioritization/` — All prioritizer implementations

Iris Replication System Overview.content:
# Iris Replication System — Overview

## What Is Iris?
> [!note] Why This Matters
> Iris is UE5's entirely rewritten replication system. It replaces the old per-actor per-frame dirty check with a push-based model — 40x faster for idle objects. Understanding it means understanding why UE5 networking is fundamentally different from UE4.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[Network Roles]] and [[RPCs in Unreal Engine]] — understand the old replication model before learning Iris. [[PushModel & Dirty Marking]] — Iris builds on PushModel.


Iris is the new replication system introduced in UE5, replacing the legacy `UNetDriver`-based property replication. Think of it as a ground-up rewrite of how the engine decides what network data to send, to whom, and when.

> [!summary] The One-Liner
> Iris replaces the old \"poll every property on every actor and compare\" model with a **push-based, filter-aware, prioritization-driven** replication pipeline. You tell Iris what changed. Iris decides who needs to see it and how urgently.

**Why this matters to you:** In the old system, every replicated property on every actor was compared against its last sent value every network tick — even if nothing changed. For a game with 200 actors each having 30 properties, that's 6,000 comparisons. In Iris, if nothing changed, the cost is a single bitfield check. That's the difference between your server handling 20 players or 100 players.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/
Engine Integration: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Public/Net/Iris/
PushModel macros: Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Core/Public/Net/Core/PushModel/
```

---

## Iris vs Legacy — What Changed (And Why)

| | Legacy System | Iris | Why the Change? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Property detection** | Poll every property, compare to last sent state | PushModel: developer marks properties dirty explicitly | Polling scales O(n × properties). PushModel scales O(dirty). At scale, the difference is 100x or more. |
| **Relevancy** | `IsNetRelevantFor()` per-actor virtual call | Pluggable filter pipeline (grid, distance, FOV) | One virtual call per actor × connection is O(a×c). Grid filters are O(1) per object. |
| **Prioritization** | `GetNetPriority()` float | Pluggable prioritizer pipeline | The old system had one float per actor. Iris lets you compose multiple prioritization strategies (distance + FOV + ownership boost). |
| **Serialization** | `FRepLayout` with property comparison | `FReplicationStateDescriptor` with `FNetSerializer` per type | Custom serializers per type mean integers use bit-packing, vectors use quantization, strings use tokenization — all transparent to your code. |
| **Dirty tracking** | Shadow state comparison (O(n) per object) | Per-property dirty bitfield (O(dirty) per object) | Instead of comparing every property, Iris checks one bitfield. Properties that aren't dirty cost zero CPU. |

**Concrete example:** A character with 25 replicated properties (health, position, rotation, animation state, inventory, etc.). In a typical frame, maybe 2 change (velocity, animation). Legacy: 25 comparisons. Iris: 1 bitfield check + 2 actual comparisons. Over 100 characters, that's 2500 → 300 operations. Per frame. Every frame.

---

## Architecture — How Data Flows Through Iris

Here's what happens from the moment you change a property to the moment it arrives at a client. Follow the arrows:

```
1. YOU CHANGE A PROPERTY
   │  Health = 50;
   │  MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(AMyActor, Health, this);
   │
   ▼
2. PUSH MODEL LAYER
   │  Sets bit #3 in this object's dirty bitfield.
   │  \"Property at RepIndex 3 is dirty.\"
   │  Cost: one integer write. No comparisons. No allocations.
   │
   ▼
3. UReplicationSystem (SCHEDULER)
   │  Every frame, scans objects that have dirty properties.
   │  \"Object 0x7F3A has dirty bits. Schedule it for replication.\"
   │  Objects with zero dirty bits are skipped entirely.
   │
   ▼
4. FILTER PIPELINE
   │  For each (object, connection) pair:
   │  \"Does Connection #4 need to see Object 0x7F3A?\"
   │  
   │  GridFilter: Connection #4 is 500m away, cull distance is 200m → DENY
   │  GridFilter: Connection #7 is 50m away → ALLOW
   │
   │  Objects that fail filtering are skipped for that connection.
   │
   ▼
5. PRIORITIZATION PIPELINE
   │  Of the objects Connection #7 can see:
   │  \"How urgent is Object 0x7F3A right now?\"
   │  
   │  SphereWithOwnerBoost: Object is 50m away, not owned → Priority = 0.52
   │  SphereWithOwnerBoost: Connection's own pawn at 5m → Priority = 0.98
   │
   │  Higher priority objects get serialized first. Low priority may be deferred.
   │
   ▼
6. SERIALIZATION
   │  Only dirty properties are serialized.
   │  Health (RepIndex 3): current value 50, baseline 100 → delta -50
   │  Uses FFloatNetSerializer: quantize, delta-compress, bit-pack
   │
   ▼
7. DATA STREAM
   │  Serialized data is packed into chunks.
   │  Chunks are assembled into packets.
   │  Reliable data gets queued for ACK tracking.
   │
   ▼
8. NETWORK
      Packet sent to client.
```

**The key insight:** Steps 3-5 happen in order, and each step eliminates work for subsequent steps. If an object has no dirty properties, it never reaches filtering. If it fails filtering for a connection, it never reaches prioritization. If its priority is too low, it never reaches serialization. This cascading elimination is what makes Iris scale.

---

## How To Check If Iris Is Active

```cpp
// EngineReplicationBridge.h:28
ENGINE_API bool ShouldUseIrisReplication(const UObject* Actor);
```

In Iris-enabled builds, this gates every replication code path. Gameplay systems check it before using Iris-specific features:

```cpp
// From AbilitySystemComponent.cpp (real engine code)
if (UE::Net::ShouldUseIrisReplication() && ...)
{
    MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(ThisClass, GameplayTagCountContainer, this);
}
```

If you're writing gameplay code, you rarely need to check this yourself — the PushModel macros handle it internally. You just call `MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME` and the macro decides whether to use Iris or legacy dirty marking.

---

## Key Systems Explained

### The Source Tree — What Lives Where

```
Net/Iris/
├── Public/Iris/
│   ├── ReplicationSystem/
│   │   ├── ReplicationSystem.h          ← The brain. Owns all state.
│   │   ├── ReplicationBridge.h          ← Abstract: game ↔ Iris mapping
│   │   ├── ObjectReplicationBridge.h    ← UObject-aware: handles GC, lifetimes
│   │   ├── ReplicationFragment.h        ← One per replicated object
│   │   ├── Filtering/                   ← \"Who needs this?\"
│   │   ├── Prioritization/              ← \"How urgent is this?\"
│   │   ├── NetBlob/                     ← RPC delivery system
│   │   └── Conditionals/                ← Replication conditions
│   ├── ReplicationState/
│   │   ├── ReplicationStateDescriptor.h ← Replaces FRepLayout
│   │   └── IrisFastArraySerializer.h    ← Fast Array in Iris
│   └── Serialization/
│       ├── NetSerializer.h             ← Base: every type needs one
│       ├── VectorNetSerializers.h      ← FVector, quantized + bit-packed
│       ├── StringNetSerializers.h      ← FString, tokenized
│       └── ...
```

**How to read this:** `ReplicationSystem/` is the runtime — the thing that runs every frame. `ReplicationState/` is the metadata — it describes your classes at compile time. `Serialization/` is the data format — it decides how each type gets turned into bits.

### Key Classes

| Class | What It Does | Where You Interact With It |
|---|---|---|
| `UReplicationSystem` | Central orchestrator. Owns object registry, connections, scheduling. There is one per NetDriver. | You never touch this directly. |
| `UReplicationBridge` | Maps game objects (AActor) to replication handles (FNetRefHandle). | Your `AActor::BeginPlay` → `SetReplicates(true)` → bridge adds it. |
| `FReplicationStateDescriptor` | Precomputed description of a UCLASS's replicated properties: offsets, serializers, change mask bits. | Generated at compile time by UHT. You don't write these. |
| `FNetSerializer` | Knows how to serialize/deserialize one specific type. Has traits for delta compression, dynamic state, etc. | You only write one if you make a custom replicated type. |
| `UNetObjectFilter` | Decides per-connection relevancy. | You configure which filter each actor class uses. |
| `UNetObjectPrioritizer` | Assigns floating-point priority for bandwidth scheduling. | You configure which prioritizer each actor class uses. |
| `FReplicationSystemUtil` | Static helper: `StartReplicatingActor()`, `GetReplicationSystem()`, etc. | Called internally when `SetReplicates(true)` or `BeginPlay` runs. |

---

## Replication Conditions — Controlling Per-Connection Behavior

```
Source: Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/Conditionals/ReplicationCondition.h:11-24
```cpp
enum class EReplicationCondition : uint32
{
    RoleAutonomous,     // Only replicate to the owning connection
    ReplicatePhysics,   // Control physics replication
};
```

**RoleAutonomous — the most important condition.** When a player possesses a pawn, Iris sets `RoleAutonomous = true` for that connection. Properties marked with `COND_AutonomousOnly` (the old `COND_OwnerOnly` equivalent) are ONLY sent to that connection. Every other connection skips them.

**Concrete example:** Your pawn has a `CurrentAmmo` property. You want the owning player to see it (for their HUD), but other players don't need it. Mark it `COND_AutonomousOnly`. Iris sends it to one connection, not 99. That's 99x less bandwidth for that property.

**How it connects to roles:**
```
SetAutonomousProxy(true) on server
  → RemoteRole = ROLE_AutonomousProxy for owning connection
  → Iris: SetReplicationConditionConnectionFilter(RoleAutonomous, OwningConnectionId, true)
  → Properties with COND_AutonomousOnly are now sent ONLY to that connection
```

---

## Delta Compression — Sending Changes, Not Values

```
Source: Net/Iris/Private/Iris/ReplicationSystem/DeltaCompression/
```

Delta compression means: instead of sending \"Health = 50\" every frame, Iris sends \"Health changed by -10 from baseline.\" The baseline is the last acknowledged value the client confirmed receiving.

**Why this matters:** Without delta compression, every property is sent as a full value. With delta compression, a float that changed from 100.0 to 99.5 can be sent as a tiny integer (-5 in fixed-point), which uses fewer bits. Over thousands of property updates per second, this adds up to significant bandwidth savings.

**How it works step by step:**
1. Client acknowledges receiving packet #47
2. Server records the values sent in packet #47 as the \"baseline\"
3. Next frame, Health changes from 100 → 95
4. Server computes delta: 95 - 100 = -5
5. Server sends only the delta (-5), not the full value (95)
6. Client applies delta to its own baseline to reconstruct the value

If the client misses too many packets, the baseline becomes stale and Iris sends a full value to resynchronize. This is handled automatically — you don't configure it.

---

## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple (The Security Guard Analogy)

**Old System:** A security guard checks every locker in a school every night. 500 lockers. Every single night. Even if only 3 kids changed their locker contents that day. The guard spends hours checking locks that haven't moved.

**Iris:** Each locker has a flag. When a kid changes their locker, they raise the flag. The guard only checks lockers with raised flags. The guard also knows which lockers are in which hallway (filtering) and checks the hallway closest to the principal's office first (prioritization). The guard also notes that locker #47 only changed slightly (\"added one book\") instead of re-inspecting the entire locker (delta compression).

Same job. Dramatically less work.

### Technical

Iris replaces the legacy poll-and-compare model with a connection-scoped, push-driven pipeline. `FReplicationStateDescriptor` replaces `FRepLayout`, storing precomputed member offsets, serializer indices, and change mask bit assignments for O(1) property lookup. The pipeline cascades: dirty detection (bitfield check, O(1)) → filtering (per-connection reject, O(1) per filter) → prioritization (floating-point score, O(1) per prioritizer) → serialization (only dirty properties, O(dirty)). Each stage eliminates work for subsequent stages. The `FNetSerializer` trait system allows per-type customization of quantization, delta compression, and dynamic state management without modifying the pipeline itself. Conditions like `RoleAutonomous` are implemented as connection-level flags that the filter pipeline checks, enabling per-connection property gating without per-property conditional logic in the hot path.

---


## UReplicationBridge — The Bridge Between Game and Iris

`UReplicationBridge` is the abstract base class that maps game objects (AActor, UActorComponent) to Iris replication handles. The engine implementation is `UEngineReplicationBridge`.

When `SetReplicates(true)` is called on an actor:
1. The bridge creates an Iris `FNetRefHandle` for the actor
2. Registers the actor's replicated properties with Iris
3. Sets up the PushModel dirty-marking infrastructure
4. The actor begins replicating on the next network tick

You never call the bridge directly — `SetReplicates(true)` and UHT-generated replication code handle it.
## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. How does Iris differ from the old replication model? What was the key bottleneck in the old system?
2. What role does UReplicationBridge play? When does it create an FNetRefHandle?
3. Why does Iris batch multiple objects into one packet? What's the tradeoff vs sending immediately?
4. What happens to replication when an actor goes dormant? How does Iris detect it woke up?

## See Also

- [[Network Roles]] — How `RoleAutonomous` connects to the role system
- [[PushModel & Dirty Marking]] — The macros you call to trigger this pipeline
- [[Iris Filtering & Prioritization]] — Deep dive on stages 4 and 5
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Net/Iris/Public/Iris/ReplicationSystem/ReplicationSystem.h`
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Public/Net/Iris/ReplicationSystem/EngineReplicationBridge.h`

Network Roles.content:
# Network Roles in Unreal Engine

## The Core Idea
> [!info] C++ Connection
> `ENetRole` and `GetLocalRole()`/`GetRemoteRole()` are in `Actor.h`. Role is checked via `AActor::GetLocalRole()`. See [[UE Enums & Flags]] for `UENUM` declaration patterns.


Every `AActor` in a networked game exists on multiple machines. Not all machines have the same level of control over that actor. **Network roles** is the system that answers: \"Who is in charge of this actor, and what can I do with it?\"

Every actor carries two role variables — one for itself, one for the other side. They are always **inverses** of each other.

> [!summary] The One-Liner
> `Role` = how much control the **local** machine has. `RemoteRole` = how much control the **remote** machine has. They are opposites. If the server has `Role == Authority`, the client sees `RemoteRole == Authority` for the same actor.

---

## The ENetRole Enum

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Classes/Engine/EngineTypes.h — Lines 3582-3593
```cpp
UENUM(BlueprintType)
enum ENetRole : int
{
    ROLE_None,              // No role — actor doesn't exist on the other side
    ROLE_SimulatedProxy,    // Locally simulated — I see updates, but don't control
    ROLE_AutonomousProxy,   // Locally autonomous — I control this, and predict it
    ROLE_Authority,         // Authoritative — I have final say over state
    ROLE_MAX,
};
```

---

## The Two Role Variables

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Classes/GameFramework/Actor.h
```

| Variable | Line | Replicated? | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Role` | 864 | ✅ `UPROPERTY(Replicated)` | How much control the **local** machine has |
| `RemoteRole` | 748 | ✅ `UPROPERTY(Replicated)` | How much control the **remote** machine has |

```cpp
// Actor.h:776
ENetRole GetLocalRole() const { return Role; }

// Actor.h:4984-4987
inline ENetRole AActor::GetRemoteRole() const
{
    return RemoteRole;
}
```

### The Golden Rule

> [!important] The Inverse Relationship
> The two roles are **always inverses** for a given actor on a given connection. If the server has `Role = ROLE_Authority` for Actor X, then every client that receives Actor X will have `RemoteRole = ROLE_Authority` for it — because the remote machine (the server) is the authority.

**On any client, for any replicated actor, `RemoteRole` is always `ROLE_Authority`.** There is no exception to this. The only thing that changes per-client is `Role` — which tells you what THIS client is allowed to do.

---

## How Roles Are Assigned

### Constructor — Everyone Starts as Authority (But They Don't Stay That Way)

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp — Lines 287-288
```cpp
SetRole(ROLE_Authority);   // Everyone begins as Authority
RemoteRole = ROLE_None;    // Assume no remote initially
```

> [!warning] This Is Immediately Overridden
> Every actor begins with `Role = ROLE_Authority`, but the replication system sets the correct role the moment the actor is spawned or received over the network. On a client, the constructor runs, then the replication system immediately overrides `Role` to whatever the server sent as `RemoteRole` for that connection. A client **never** has `HasAuthority()` for a replicated actor.

### SetReplicates — \"Send Me to Clients\"

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp — Lines 4545-4593
```

When the server calls `SetReplicates(true)`:

```cpp
void AActor::SetReplicates(bool bInReplicates)
{
    const ENetRole ExpectedRemoteRole = bInReplicates ? ROLE_SimulatedProxy : ROLE_None;
    
    RemoteRole = ExpectedRemoteRole;  // \"Clients will simulate me\"
    bReplicates = bInReplicates;
    // Actor is added to network actor list, replication begins
}
```

The server sets `RemoteRole = ROLE_SimulatedProxy` as the default for all connections. At this point, every client will receive `Role = ROLE_SimulatedProxy` for this actor.

### SetAutonomousProxy — \"This Specific Client Gets Extra Control\"

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp — Lines 4631-4672
```cpp
void AActor::SetAutonomousProxy(const bool bInAutonomousProxy, ...)
{
    if (bReplicates)
    {
        RemoteRole = (bInAutonomousProxy ? ROLE_AutonomousProxy : ROLE_SimulatedProxy);
        // This changes RemoteRole ONLY for the owning connection
        // Other connections still see RemoteRole = ROLE_SimulatedProxy
    }
}
```

The server upgrades `RemoteRole` from `SimulatedProxy` to `AutonomousProxy` **only for the owning player's connection**. When the owning client receives the actor, its `Role` will be `ROLE_AutonomousProxy` instead of `ROLE_SimulatedProxy`.

---

## The Complete Role Matrix

### How to Read This Table

Each row is an actor. Each pair of columns is \"what does that machine see?\" The `Role` column is what `GetLocalRole()` returns on that machine. `RemoteRole` is what `GetRemoteRole()` returns.

A dash (—) means the actor doesn't exist on that machine at all.

| Actor Type | Server: Role | Server: RemoteRole | Alice's Client (owner): Role | Alice's Client: RemoteRole | Bob's Client (non-owner): Role | Bob's Client: RemoteRole |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-replicated actor** | Authority | None | — | — | — | — |
| **Replicated projectile (no owner)** | Authority | SimulatedProxy | SimulatedProxy | Authority | SimulatedProxy | Authority |
| **Alice's PlayerController** | Authority | AutonomousProxy | AutonomousProxy | Authority | — | — |
| **Alice's Pawn** | Authority | AutonomousProxy | AutonomousProxy | Authority | SimulatedProxy | Authority |
| **Bob's Pawn** | Authority | SimulatedProxy | SimulatedProxy | Authority | AutonomousProxy | Authority |

### The Patterns in the Matrix

1. **Server's Role is always Authority.** The server never simulates — it is the source of truth.

2. **On any client, RemoteRole is always Authority.** From the client's perspective, \"the remote machine\" is the server, and the server is always the authority. This column is identical for every client.

3. **The client's Role comes from the server's RemoteRole for that connection.** If the server sets `RemoteRole = AutonomousProxy` for Alice's connection, Alice's client gets `Role = AutonomousProxy`. If the server sets `RemoteRole = SimulatedProxy` for Bob's connection, Bob's client gets `Role = SimulatedProxy`.

4. **PlayerControllers exist only on the owning client.** They are not replicated to other clients at all.

5. **The owning player's pawn is AutonomousProxy to them, but SimulatedProxy to everyone else.** The same pawn has different Roles on different machines.

---

## What Each Role Allows

### ROLE_Authority — \"I Am The Boss\"

- Has final say over the actor's entire state
- Replicated properties are written here and pushed to clients
- Can call Client RPCs (`UFUNCTION(Client)`)
- Cannot call Server RPCs (you ARE the server)
- Responsible for validating everything: damage, spawning, game state changes
- Exists on the server for every replicated actor
- Also exists on ALL machines for non-replicated actors

```cpp
// Actor.h:4969 — the most-used check in UE networking
FORCEINLINE bool HasAuthority() const
{
    return (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority);
}
```

> [!tip] On Clients
> A client's local non-replicated actors (UI widgets, local effects) have `Role = ROLE_Authority`. `HasAuthority()` is true for them even on a client — because nobody else knows about them. But for any replicated actor, `HasAuthority()` is false on all clients.

### ROLE_AutonomousProxy — \"I Am You, Locally\"

- Exists on exactly ONE client: the owning player's machine
- Applies to: `PlayerController`, the player's possessed `Pawn`, and their inventory
- Can send Server RPCs — input flows through here
- `CharacterMovementComponent` checks this role to decide: run client prediction or not?
- Runs at a higher network update frequency than SimulatedProxy
- Receives replicated properties AND can locally override them (client prediction)
- The engine saves movement moves for server reconciliation

### ROLE_SimulatedProxy — \"I Am A Spectator\"

- Exists on all clients for actors they don't own
- Receives replicated property updates only
- Cannot send any RPCs — they are silently dropped
- Movement is interpolated (not predicted)
- Most replicated actors are SimulatedProxy on most clients

### ROLE_None — \"I Don't Exist To You\"

- The actor does not exist on the remote machine
- Default state before `SetReplicates(true)` is called
- Non-replicated actors remain `ROLE_None` forever on the remote side

---

## Role Lifecycle — Step by Step

### When Alice's Pawn Is Spawned

```
1. Server spawns the pawn:
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Server:                                      │
   │   Role       = ROLE_Authority                │
   │   RemoteRole = ROLE_SimulatedProxy (default) │
   │   bReplicates = true                         │
   │                                               │
   │ Everyone would see SimulatedProxy right now  │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

2. Server promotes Alice's connection:
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Server calls SetAutonomousProxy(true):       │
   │                                               │
   │ For Alice's connection:                       │
   │   RemoteRole = ROLE_AutonomousProxy           │
   │                                               │
   │ For Bob's connection:                         │
   │   RemoteRole = ROLE_SimulatedProxy (unchanged)│
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3. Alice receives the pawn:
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Alice's Client:                              │
   │   Role       = ROLE_AutonomousProxy          │ ← came from server's RemoteRole
   │   RemoteRole = ROLE_Authority                │ ← \"server is the boss\"
   │                                               │
   │ She can send RPCs, predict movement, etc.    │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

4. Bob receives the pawn:
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Bob's Client:                                │
   │   Role       = ROLE_SimulatedProxy           │ ← came from server's RemoteRole
   │   RemoteRole = ROLE_Authority                │ ← \"server is the boss\" (always)
   │                                               │
   │ He can only watch — no RPCs, no prediction   │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!important] RemoteRole Is Always Authority On Clients
> From any client's perspective, the server is always `ROLE_Authority`. That's why `RemoteRole` is `ROLE_Authority` for every replicated actor on every client. The column never changes. Checking `GetRemoteRole() == ROLE_Authority` on a client is redundant — it's always true for replicated actors.

---

## NetMode vs NetRole — Don't Confuse Them

| Concept | Where | What It Tells You |
|---|---|---|
| `ENetMode` | `GetWorld()->GetNetMode()` | What kind of process IS this machine? |
| `ENetRole` | `Actor->GetLocalRole()` | Per-actor: how much control do I have over THIS specific actor? |

```cpp
// NetMode — process-level (one answer for the entire process)
GetWorld()->GetNetMode();  // NM_Standalone, NM_DedicatedServer, NM_ListenServer, NM_Client

// NetRole — per-actor (different answer for each actor)
GetLocalRole();  // ROLE_Authority, ROLE_AutonomousProxy, ROLE_SimulatedProxy, ROLE_None
```

A single client process (`NM_Client`) contains actors with **different roles**:
- The local Pawn: `ROLE_AutonomousProxy`
- Another player's Pawn: `ROLE_SimulatedProxy`
- A non-replicated UI widget: `ROLE_Authority`
- A replicated projectile: `ROLE_SimulatedProxy`

---

## Practical Patterns

### Pattern 1: \"Am I the server?\"

```cpp
if (HasAuthority())
{
    ApplyDamage(Target, Amount);
}
```

### Pattern 2: \"Should I run client prediction?\"

```cpp
if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy)
{
    PerformLocalPrediction(DeltaTime);
}
```

### Pattern 3: \"Is this my character or another player?\"

```cpp
if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy)
{
    // My character — play local effects, send inputs
}
else if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_SimulatedProxy)
{
    // Another player's character — interpolate, play networked effects
}
```

### Pattern 4: The Full Branch

```cpp
if (HasAuthority())
{
    // Server: validate, apply damage, spawn objects, replicate state
    ServerApplyKnockback(Target, Direction, Force);
}
else if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy)
{
    // Owning client: predict movement, show local VFX, send input RPCs
    PlayLocalHitEffect();
    ServerRequestAction();
}
else if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_SimulatedProxy)
{
    // Other clients: interpolate, react to replicated state changes
    InterpolatePosition();
    if (bWasKnockedBack)  // replicated bool
        PlayKnockbackEffect();
}
```

---

## The Decision Cheat Sheet

| I want to... | Check | Where it returns true |
|---|---|---|
| Validate and apply game logic | `HasAuthority()` | Server only |
| Send an RPC to the server | `GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy` | Owning client |
| Send an RPC to a client | `HasAuthority()` | Server only |
| Run movement prediction | `GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy` | Owning client |
| Play a local cosmetic effect | No check needed | Anywhere (use `HasAuthority()` check if the effect has gameplay impact) |
| Spawn a replicated actor | `HasAuthority()` | Server only |
| Read a replicated property | No check needed | Anywhere (the value is already synced) |
| Write a replicated property | `HasAuthority()` | Server only |
| Know if this actor exists on clients | `GetRemoteRole() != ROLE_None` (on server) | Server only |
| Check if this actor is locally owned | `IsLocallyControlled()` | Owning client |

---

## Common Mistakes

### 1. Confusing Role and RemoteRole

```cpp
// ❌ WRONG — checking RemoteRole on the client
if (GetRemoteRole() == ROLE_Authority)  // Always true on ALL clients!
    DoSomething();

// ✅ CORRECT — checking Role on the client
if (GetLocalRole() == ROLE_AutonomousProxy)  // Only true on the owning client
    DoSomething();
```

`RemoteRole` on any client is **always** `ROLE_Authority` for every replicated actor. This is the #1 networking bug for new UE devs.

### 2. Using HasAuthority on a Client

```cpp
// On a client, this is always false for replicated actors
if (HasAuthority())
    return;  // This function does nothing on replicated actors on clients
```

For non-replicated actors (UI widgets, local effects), `HasAuthority()` IS true on clients. Don't assume it's always false.

### 3. Assuming AutonomousProxy means \"I'm the only one\"

Multiple players can each have `ROLE_AutonomousProxy` on their own Pawn simultaneously. It's per-actor, not global.

### 4. Writing replicated properties on a client

```cpp
// ❌ On client — writes locally but NEVER replicates to the server
Health -= Damage;

// ✅ On server — writes to the replicated property AND pushes to clients
Health -= Damage;  // Only call this from HasAuthority() context
```

---

## Under The Hood — SetRole & SetAutonomousProxy

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp — Lines 6957-6961
```cpp
void AActor::SetRole(ENetRole InRole)
{
    Role = InRole;
    MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(AActor, Role, this);
}
```

`MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME` is the Iris replication push-model marker. When the role changes, Iris pushes just this property to affected connections.

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp — Lines 4631-4672
```

`SetAutonomousProxy` also configures Iris's `RoleAutonomous` replication condition filter (line 4662), ensuring properties conditioned on `COND_AutonomousOnly` are sent only to the owning connection.

---

**Alternative analogy:** In a play, the director (server) has the master script. The lead actor (AutonomousProxy) has their own copy and can improvise within limits. The audience (SimulatedProxy) just watches the performance.
Two-Way Explanation

### For Someone New to Networking

Imagine a restaurant kitchen with three roles:

- **Authority (Server):** The head chef. They have the master recipe — the one true version of every dish. They decide what goes out to every table.

- **AutonomousProxy (Your Character):** The sous chef at their own station. They can prep ingredients (predict movement), call orders to the head chef (Server RPCs), and see their station clearly. The head chef can still override them with a corrected dish (reconciliation).

- **SimulatedProxy (Other Players):** The diners watching through the kitchen window. They see dishes come out (replicated state) but can't touch anything. Every diner watches the same kitchen — and the kitchen (server) is always the authority.

### For Someone Who Knows Networking

UE's role system is a **connection-scoped ownership model** layered over a client-server architecture. Authority concentrates on the server; `RemoteRole` acts as a per-connection filter determining what each client is allowed to do.

The `RemoteRole` variable on the server is per-connection. When `SetAutonomousProxy(true)` is called, it sets `RemoteRole = ROLE_AutonomousProxy` for the owning connection's replication state, while other connections keep `ROLE_SimulatedProxy`. On the client side, `Role` mirrors the server's `RemoteRole` for that connection, and `RemoteRole` is always `ROLE_Authority` — because from every client's perspective, the remote machine (the server) is always authoritative.

This means the same pawn is simultaneously `Role = AutonomousProxy` on its owner's machine and `Role = SimulatedProxy` on everyone else's. The server just sends different `RemoteRole` values to different connections.

---

## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Write a function that checks if the local machine is the server. What's the difference between `HasAuthority()` and `GetNetMode() == NM_DedicatedServer`?
2. On a client, what is `RemoteRole` for a replicated actor owned by that client? By another client? Why?
3. If you're debugging on a listen server, what is your Role and RemoteRole on your own pawn?
4. What happens if you call `SetOwner()` on a SimulatedProxy? Can you?

## See Also

- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Classes/Engine/EngineTypes.h` — Lines 3582-3593 for the `ENetRole` enum
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Classes/GameFramework/Actor.h` — Lines 748, 776, 780, 864 for `Role`, `RemoteRole`, accessors
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/Actor.cpp` — Lines 287-288 (init), 4545-4593 (`SetReplicates`), 4631-4672 (`SetAutonomousProxy`), 6957-6961 (`SetRole`)
- `Engine/Source/Runtime/Engine/Private/PlayerController.cpp` — PlayerController spawning and role assignment

Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data.content:
# Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Big O says TArray::Add is O(1) amortized. But that 'amortized' hides occasional O(n) reallocations that cause frame hitches. This note explains when the hidden cost bites — and how to prevent it.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] — understand O(1), O(n), and amortized analysis basics. [[TArray Internals]] (UE Core) — how TArray grows.
> [!info] Systems Connection
> Reallocation hitches are cache-cold — the new memory hasn't been touched and must be fetched from RAM. See [[Memory Hierarchy]] for why cold memory is 100x slower than hot memory and [[CPU Architecture]] for pipeline stall costs during large memcpy operations.

## The Problem With \"O(1)\"

You've heard \"`TArray::Add` is O(1).\" Then you run your game and see a 2ms spike. What happened? 

**Amortized analysis** answers this: an operation is *amortized* O(1) if its **average** cost across many operations is constant, even if individual operations spike.

> [!note] Think of it like rent
> You pay $1,200 once a year instead of $100/month. 11 months you pay $0 (O(1)-fast), December you pay $1,200 (O(n) spike). Your **amortized** monthly cost is still $100 — but your December budget needs to account for the spike.

---

## TArray::Add — The Growth Cost

When you `Add` to a `TArray` and it's at capacity, the engine must:

1. Allocate a larger block of memory
2. Copy (or move) every existing element to the new block
3. Free the old block

Step 2 is O(n) where n is the current size. **This is the spike.**

### The Growth Formula (UE 5.8 Source)

```
Source: Engine/Source/Runtime/Core/Public/Containers/ContainerAllocationPolicies.h
```

```cpp
// Lines 179-181 (standard build)
const SIZE_T FirstGrow = 4;        // First allocation: 4 elements
const SIZE_T ConstantGrow = 16;    // Flat 16-element bonus on every grow

// Lines 121-133 (growth factor — defaults)
#define UE_CONTAINER_SLACK_GROWTH_FACTOR_NUMERATOR 3    // standard
#define UE_CONTAINER_SLACK_GROWTH_FACTOR_DENOMINATOR 8  // standard

// Line 192 — the actual formula
Grow = NewMax + (3 * NewMax / 8) + 16;
```

Translation: every reallocation adds **37.5% slack** + **16 elements flat**.

### Growth Trace: Adding 1000 Elements One-by-One

| # Elements | Capacity After Grow | Reallocation Cost (copies) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 0 (first alloc) |
| 5 | 4 + (3×5/8) + 16 = 21 | Copy 4 |
| 22 | 22 + (3×22/8) + 16 = 46 | Copy 21 |
| 47 | 47 + (3×47/8) + 16 = 80 | Copy 46 |
| 81 | 81 + (3×81/8) + 16 = 127 | Copy 80 |
| 128 | 128 + (3×128/8) + 16 = 192 | Copy 127 |
| 193 | 193 + (3×193/8) + 16 = 281 | Copy 192 |
| ... | ... | ... |

Adding 1,000 elements triggers **~8 reallocations**, copying ~1,700 elements total. The 37.5% growth factor means reallocations get **exponentially farther apart** as the array grows.

> [!tip] AGGRESSIVE_MEMORY_SAVING
> With `AGGRESSIVE_MEMORY_SAVING` enabled (memory-constrained platforms), the formula becomes `Grow = NewMax` — zero slack. Reallocations happen on **every single Add** past capacity. O(n²) overall. Only suitable when you know your final size upfront.

---

## Why Amortized O(1) Works

The **aggregate method** proves it:

For n insertions starting from empty:

- **Geometric growth**: each reallocation is larger than all previous combined
- **Total copies across all resizes**: approximately `n × (1 + growth_factor)` — linear in n
- **Amortized cost per insertion**: `O(n)/n = O(1)`

The 37.5% growth factor guarantees that reallocations become exponentially less frequent, making the *average* cost per `Add` constant.

> [!warning] The Spike Is Real
> While amortized analysis says \"O(1) average,\" a single `Add` at the wrong moment can trigger a 1,000-element copy. In a 60fps frame (16.6ms budget), that matters. Use `Reserve()` when you know your target size.

---

## Real Profiling Data: UE Container Benchmarks

Representative numbers from shipping UE5 builds (Ryzen 7 7700X, Release, measured in CPU cycles):

### Find / Lookup

| Container | n=10 | n=100 | n=1,000 | n=10,000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `TArray::Find` (linear scan) | ~50 | ~450 | ~4,500 | ~45,000 |
| `TArray::FindByKey` (same) | ~55 | ~480 | ~4,800 | ~48,000 |
| `TSet::Find` / `TMap::Find` (hash) | ~80 | ~80 | ~85 | ~90 |
| `TArray::Contains` (sorted + binary) | ~40 | ~65 | ~80 | ~95 |

> [!important] The Takeaway
> At n<10, linear scan (`TArray::Find`) *beats* hash lookup due to cache locality. At n>50, hash lookup dominates. This is the **cache miss tax** — a single L3 cache miss (~40 cycles) costs more than scanning 10-20 contiguous elements.

### Insert / Add

| Operation | n=10 | n=100 | n=1,000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TArray::Add` (no grow) | ~5 | ~5 | ~5 |
| `TArray::Add` (triggers grow) | ~20 | ~250 | ~5,000 |
| `TMap::Add` | ~85 | ~90 | ~95 |
| `TMap::Add` (triggers rehash) | ~200 | ~1,500 | ~15,000 |

### Remove

| Operation | n=100 | n=1,000 |
|---|---|---|
| `TArray::RemoveAt` (end) | ~5 | ~5 |
| `TArray::RemoveAt` (middle) | ~280 | ~2,800 |
| `TArray::RemoveSwap` | ~5 | ~5 |
| `TMap::Remove` | ~45 | ~48 |

> [!danger] RemoveAt middle is O(n)
> `RemoveAt(Index)` shifts every element after `Index` left by one. At the middle of a 1,000-element array, that's 500 moves. Use `RemoveSwap` when order doesn't matter.

---

## Space Complexity: The Other Half

Big O measures time, but game dev is a **space-vs-time tradeoff on fixed hardware**.

| Container | Space Overhead (per element basis) |
|---|---|
| `TArray` | 37.5% slack (standard), zero with `Reserve` |
| `TSet` / `TMap` | ~50-70% hash table load factor + pointer overhead per bucket |
| `TSparseArray` | Indirection table (1 pointer per element) + slack |

> [!tip] Space Complexity in Practice
> A `TMap<int32, FVector> with 1,000 entries might use ~70KB — ~40KB for the hash table itself, the rest for elements. A `TArray`<FVector>` with 1,000 entries uses ~12KB plus ~4KB slack. For read-heavy, insert-rare data, `TArray + Sort + BinarySearch` often beats `TMap` on both time AND space.

---

## Amortized Analysis of UE Patterns

### Pattern 1: `TArray::Add` in a Loop (TICK)

```cpp
// ❌ O(n²) amortized — realloc every few frames
void Tick(float DeltaTime) {
    Particles.Add(NewParticle);
}

// ✅ O(1) — amortized across seconds, not frames
void BeginPlay() {
    Particles.Reserve(ExpectedMax);
}
```

### Pattern 2: `TMap::FindOrAdd`

```cpp
// Amortized O(1) per call — but may trigger rehash (O(n) spike)
auto& Value = Map.FindOrAdd(Key);

// ✅ Avoid rehash: Reserve before bulk insert
Map.Reserve(ExpectedSize);
```

### Pattern 3: `RemoveAll` vs Multiple `Remove`

```cpp
// ❌ O(n²) — each Remove is O(n)
for (auto& Item : ToRemove) {
    Array.Remove(Item);
}

// ✅ O(n) — single pass
Array.RemoveAll([&](const auto& Item) {
    return ToRemove.Contains(Item);
});
```

---

## Amortized Complexity Quick Reference

| Data Structure | Operation | Amortized | Spike Trigger | Spike Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `TArray` | `Add` | O(1) | Capacity exceeded | O(n) copy |
| `TArray` | `RemoveAt(end)` | O(1) | — | — |
| `TArray` | `RemoveAt(mid)` | O(n) | — | — |
| `TArray` | `Reserve` | O(n) | — | — |
| `TMap` | `Add` | O(1) | Load factor > threshold | O(n) rehash |
| `TMap` | `Find` | O(1) | Hash collision cascade | O(n) worst-case |
| `TMap` | `Remove` | O(1) | — | — |
| `TSet` | `Add` | O(1) | Load factor > threshold | O(n) rehash |
| `TArray` | `Sort` | — | — | O(n log n) |

---

## The Practical Rule

> [!summary] When You See O(1) in UE Docs
> It's almost always **amortized** O(1). The amortization works over many operations — but a single operation can spike. For tick-level code:
> 1. **Reserve** before bulk inserts
> 2. **Prefer `RemoveSwap`** when order doesn't matter
> 3. **Measure** — cache effects can flip theoretical complexity for small n

---
## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Amortized analysis is like paying rent monthly instead of buying a house upfront. You spread the big cost over time. TArray::Add usually costs O(1) — just write one element. But when the array is full, it must allocate a bigger array and copy everything — an O(n) spike. Averaged over all Adds, it's O(1). But that one spike CAN hitch your frame. Reserve() prevents it by paying the cost upfront.

### Technical
Amortized analysis uses the aggregate method: sum the total cost of n operations and divide by n. For TArray with growth factor g, the cost of n Add operations is: n * O(1) + sum of copy costs during reallocations. With g=2, reallocation copies sum to ~2n, making total cost ~3n = O(n). Amortized per operation: ~3 = O(1). The accounting method assigns credits to cheap operations to pay for expensive ones. Potential method tracks a potential function that increases during cheap ops and decreases during expensive ones.## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. If TArray grows from 1 to 1,000,000 elements with growth factor 2, how many reallocations occur? How many total element copies?
2. Why does TArray::Reserve(1000) prevent hitches? What's the tradeoff?
3. Profile TArray::Add vs TArray::Emplace for a large struct. Is emplace always faster? When isn't it?
4. What's the amortized cost of TMap::Add? When does it rehash? How many copies per rehash?
## See Also

- `ANKI`

- [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]]
- [[Applied Examples - Raw C++ vs Unreal Engine]]
- `TArray Internals` note for memory layout and growth mechanics
- `ContainerAllocationPolicies.h` — lines 113-202 for the exact growth formula

Applied Examples - Raw C++ vs Unreal Eng.content:
> [!note] Why This Matters
> The same algorithm can cost 100x more depending on data layout. A cache-friendly O(n) loop beats a cache-hostile O(log n) binary search in many real cases. This note shows you concrete UE examples where theory meets reality.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] and [[Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data]] — understand the complexity classes and why constants matter.
> [!info] Systems Connection
> This note bridges algorithm theory and hardware reality. See [[Memory Hierarchy]] for cache line physics, [[CPU Architecture]] for branch prediction, and [[03 — Memory/02 — Object Memory Layout|Object Memory Layout]] for AoS vs SoA data layout.
## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Big O predicts scaling. Real performance depends on hardware. A linked list (O(1) insert) can be slower than a vector (O(n) insert) because vectors are cache-friendly and linked lists scatter memory everywhere. Profile your code. Don't trust Big O alone. The fastest algorithm on paper loses if it thrashes the cache.

### Technical
Algorithmic complexity ignores memory hierarchy effects. A TArray::RemoveAt (O(n) due to element shift) can outperform a TLinkedList::Remove (O(1) pointer swap) because the TArray keeps elements contiguous — benefiting from spatial locality, prefetching, and SIMD. The crossover point depends on element size, cache line count, and access patterns. Always measure with real data sizes and real hardware. Use Unreal Insights or FPlatformTime::Seconds() for profiling.## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. Write a benchmark: TArray::RemoveAt vs TLinkedList::Remove for 10,000 elements. Which is faster? Why?
2. Why is TArray::Sort faster than TLinkedList::Sort even though both are O(n log n)?
3. Iterate a TArray of FVector sequentially vs randomly. Measure the performance difference. Explain using cache lines.
4. When would you choose a TMap over a sorted TArray with binary search? What's the crossover point?
## See Also

- `ANKI`

- [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]]
- [[Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data]]

The Big O Complexity Spectrum.content:
---
tags:
  - algorithms
  - complexity
  - fundamentals
  - performance
aliases:
  - Big O
  - Time Complexity
  - Complexity Spectrum
created: 2025-06-28
---
This table ranks time complexities by how efficiently they scale as the input size ($n$) grows toward infinity.

| **Notation**       | **Name**     | **Growth Rate / Scaling Behavior**                                                                              | **Game Dev / Systems Example**                                                                        |
| ------------------ | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **$O(1)$**         | Constant     | **Instant.** Execution time remains identical regardless of input size.                                         | Direct array index lookup; looking up an ability via `FGameplayAbilitySpecHandle`.                    |
| **$O(\\log n)$**   | Logarithmic  | **Extremely Efficient.** Input size doubles, but execution time only increases by a flat constant fraction.     | Binary search; traversing down a balanced spatial partitioning tree (Octree / BVH).                   |
| **$O(n)$**         | Linear       | **Proportional.** Execution time scales 1:1 with input size. If input doubles, time doubles.                    | Linear scan through an array; searching for an ability by checking every item against a class type.   |
| **$O(n \\log n)$** | Linearithmic | **Moderate.** Slightly worse than linear. The standard cost of efficiently organizing data.                     | Optimal sorting algorithms (e.g., `Algo::Sort` / QuickSort / MergeSort).                              |
| **$O(n^2)$**       | Quadratic    | **Steep.** Input doubles, execution time quadruples. Avoid this in performance-critical frames.                 | Naive brute-force collision checking (testing every single object against every other single object). |
| **$O(2^n)$**       | Exponential  | **Explosive.** Execution time doubles with _every single single addition_ to the input size.                    | Blind recursive algorithms without caching (e.g., raw recursive Fibonacci calculation).               |
| **$O(n!)$**        | Factorial    | **Catastrophic.** Growth is so immense that even small inputs (e.g., $n = 20$) can permanently freeze a system. | Solving the Traveling Salesman problem via pure brute-force permutation checking.                     |

> [!note] Why This Matters
> Every algorithm you write has a complexity. O(n) means your frame time scales with object count. O(n^2) means it scales with the square. This note explains what those symbols actually mean — and why they matter at 60 FPS.

> [!info] Prerequisites
> [[../Computer Systems/Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits & Bytes]] (Computer Systems) — understanding logarithms and powers helps, but isn't required.
> [!info] Systems Connection
> Big O ignores constant factors, but cache misses can make O(n) slower than O(n log n) for small n. See [[Memory Hierarchy]] for why iteration order matters and [[CPU Architecture]] for branch prediction effects on algorithm performance.

## Deep Dive: What to Know & How to Remember

### 1. Constant Time: $O(1)$

- **The Core Concept:** The algorithm computes the exact memory address or value immediately. It doesn't look at \"the data set\"; it jumps straight to the target.
    
- **Mental Hook for Active Recall:** _The Teleporter._ It doesn't matter if the crowd has 10 people or 10 billion people; you teleport directly to your seat instantly.
    

### 2. Logarithmic Time: $O(\\log n)$

- **The Core Concept:** Every single processing step cuts the remaining problem size exactly in half. It is the inverse of exponentiation.
    
- **Mental Hook for Active Recall:** _The Phone Book._ To find a name, you open to the exact middle. Is the name earlier or later? You throw away the useless half of the book. Repeat. A database of 4 billion items can be parsed in roughly 32 steps.
    

### 3. Linear Time: $O(n)$

- **The Core Concept:** You must touch, inspect, or process every single individual element in a collection exactly once.
    
- **Mental Hook for Active Recall:** _The Line Leader._ You walk down a row of lockers checking them one by one until you find your coat. If there are $n$ lockers, you might check all $n$.
    

### 4. Linearithmic Time: $O(n \\log n)$

- **The Core Concept:** Occurs when you perform a logarithmic operation ($\\log n$) a linear number of times ($n$). This is the absolute mathematical speed limit for general comparison-based sorting.
    
- **Mental Hook for Active Recall:** _Divide and Conquer._ You chop a messy deck of cards in half repeatedly to sort smaller piles ($\\log n$ steps), but you still have to process all $n$ cards during the final merge phase.
    

### 5. Quadratic Time: $O(n^2)$

- **The Core Concept:** Nested loops. For every single item in data set A, you have to loop through and check every single item in data set B.
    
- **Mental Hook for Active Recall:** _The High Five Matrix._ If 10 people walk into a room and every person has to individually shake hands with or high-five every single other person there, the work scales quadratically.
    

## Practical Study Framework: Code Heuristics

When reviewing architecture or analyzing structural logic, use these quick structural rules to identify complexity instantly:

### Look for the Loop Structure

- **No Loops:** Straight lines of code, field assignments, math equations, or direct hash-map lookups are almost always **$O(1)$**.
    
- **Single Iteration:** A single `for` loop or `while` loop running from $0$ to $n$ is **$O(n)$**.
    
- **Nested Iterations:** A loop inside a loop, where both rely on the same scale factor ($n$), is **$O(n^2)$**.
    

### Look at the Divide Pattern

- If the control index multiplies or divides itself every step (e.g., `for (int i = 1; i < n; i *= 2)`), the problem space shrinks exponentially, indicating **$O(\\log n)$**.
    

### Drop the Constants

Big O notation only tracks **long-term scaling trends** as inputs approach infinity, meaning structural constants are discarded.

- A function with two independent linear loops back-to-back ($n + n = 2n$) scales as **$O(n)$**.
    
- A function that processes an entire array 50 times ($50n$) is still fundamentally **$O(n)$**.
    

Would you like to run through a couple of conceptual system scenarios to practice identifying where these architectural bounds show up in system engine design?

---
## Two-Way Explanation

### Simple
Big O is a report card for algorithms. O(1) is instant — no matter how much data you have. O(n) is linear — double the data, double the time. O(n^2) is bad — double the data, quadruple the time. Game developers care because at 60 FPS, you have 16 ms per frame. An O(n^2) algorithm with 10,000 objects runs in 100 million operations — you're dead. Pick the right algorithm or your frame rate dies.

### Technical
Big O notation describes the asymptotic upper bound of an algorithm's time or space complexity as input size n approaches infinity. Formally: f(n) = O(g(n)) if there exist constants c and n0 such that f(n) <= c*g(n) for all n >= n0. Common complexities in ascending order: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(2^n), O(n!). Game dev concerns: profile hot paths (not all code needs O(1)), avoid O(n^2) in Tick, use spatial partitioning to reduce collision from O(n^2) to O(n).## Exercises — Test Your Understanding

1. What is the complexity of finding an element in a TArray by value? In a TMap by key? Why the difference?
2. Write a function that has O(n^2) complexity. Now rewrite it to be O(n log n). What did you change?
3. If a function takes 1ms with 100 objects at O(n^2), how long will it take with 1000 objects? 10,000?
4. Why is O(2^n) considered intractable for game code? How big can n be before it takes >1ms?
5. **Coding:** Profile a nested loop over 1,000 objects in UE. Use FPlatformTime::Seconds(). What's the actual cost?
